likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: The labs in the class are so time consuming. Even working in a group, it can be upwards of 10 hours spent on labs.

EA4 is the worst EA there is. I do not understand why you have homework AND lab. The labs aren't bad as long as you find a good group. (Also, I get that the lab is supposed to be hard, but I think you know there's a problem when even the TAs are saying that they don't know what's going on. But you struggle through eventually and the median for the labs are always in the 90s.) The content is difficult – and sometimes I feel like they could be presented in a way that is easier to understand. I tried very hard but got on the lower quartile in the first exam. I have just accepted that it's something that can't be helped if I don't really understand the concept.
Interesting subjects but labs were very long.

Honestly, Professor Bayliss is great, he teaches the course very clearly and makes it very clear what he wants you to learn. However, there's so much extra work in this course. The labs are so long and the homeworks can take more than 4 hours. This allowed me to learn a lot but makes the course overall pretty brutal. Would definitely recommend the professor, but the workload not so much.

The course is generally very time–consuming between the homework, homework quizzes, labs, and exams, and I felt that it was the worst out of the entire EA4 sequence in terms of how much time I spent on the class (especially on the weeks with labs due). It's important that you find a good lab group and also try to engage in the coding aspect because understanding that will help in the exam. I also recommend signing up for a PGSG because I would've been so lost in the class had I not signed up for one.
You have to take this course anyways, it's kinda a combination of Math 228–2 and EA 1 and 3

This EA is kind of a combination of the rest of the EAs in different ways. It's challenging and quite time consuming due to the lengthy homeworks and the labs. The labs were absolutely the worst part of this course they require mathematical derivations, MATLAB, and analysis write ups. It's so much work and time and checking with TAs (the line to see the TAs is generally out the door so I recommend getting there a lil early). Prof Bayliss is very sweet, his class moves relatively slowly and he's great at asking/answering questions. He does use a chalkboard though, so a lot of important is only spoken and not written out. It's easy when he's moving slowly to get distracted, but try to keep listening because he'll say something important that you might miss and then the rest of what's on the board is confusing.
I did not enjoy this class in any aspect

Honestly my favorite EA of the sequence. Course material is super interesting and the problem sets were very rewarding to work through. Bayliss is a fantastic lecturer and great at encouraging and answering questions. The class is serious work though. You must put in a lot of time consistently to succeed. The labs could be pretty intense and took an insane amount of time (often 10+ hours per person in a team of 4... I think my personal max was around 16 hours). Always start early. Exams were often lengthy and challenging, but Bayliss published a comprehensive list of practice problems before each exam that were fairly representative of what you need to be able to do for the exam, so if you find a way to set aside the time to work on it, you'll probably do just fine.

The labs are literally the worst part. They take so long and seem pretty unnecessary to me. The exams ask questions about the MATLAB codes within the labs. If you want to do well in those labs make sure you pick really good people for your lab group, otherwise you'll regret it.

EA 4 was definitely one of my favorites of the EA classes. While the labs were long and tedious, they helped contextualize what we were learning in class. Exams were a bit of a wildcard; 2/3 were probably too long for the time given. All in all, I actually really enjoyed this class; it was an interesting way to teach diffeq.
this class was really hard at first for me, but i buckled down and practiced with a lot of problems and it started to click better
class is 2x as much work as a normal class. Get a good lab group

A very solid class and by far my favorite of the EAs! Professor Bayliss is wonderful, his lectures are clear and structured and I consistently came out of each one feeling like I wrote solid notes and learned something new. I will say the homeworks could get rather long, and if a lab was also due in the same week, the workload could get quite bad. I would definitely recommend forming a lab group with people you know and trust to do their work well.

The exams were very fair content–wise, but oh lord could they be a time crunch. I couldn't even get to some of the questions on the final because of time constraints. That's the major thing I would watch out for. Otherwise, solid class, great professor!
This course had heavy focus on practice which is why I like doing so I can feel a sense of mastery on the topic.

I really liked the way Prof. Bayliss taught this class, and he explained tough concepts in a way that helped them stick. He would sometimes rush through things the first time introducing them, but he would circle back next lecture and review it before going on. I found the homeworks to be a bit too mechanical and repetitive. The labs nearly took me out, so be mentally prepared for them (especially lab 2) and really try to have a good group because it makes a huge difference. Lab office hours were always super duper packed and it was very difficult to get a question answered. It may be a good idea to have there be discussion posts on Canvas or Piazza to make answering questions about the lab more efficient. The exams are challenging, so focus on having a great conceptual understanding of everything instead of memorizing problems. That may seem unnecessary to mention, but we often went over one to two problems over the course of 3–4 lectures so you'd think you'd be directly assessed on that, but the exams truly focus on applying concepts.

Maybe I'm just not good at math but this course was literally hell. I get it, its the last course to weed out engineers but I genuinely don't understand why it has to be so hard. Don't even think about reading the textbook, watch videos on the subjects you feel weak on and MAYBE you'll understand the content. Also, lets not forget to mention the LABS. Why are we making these labs due the WEEK OF THE EXAMS like it takes wayyyy to much time off your hands. Another thing is THERE IS NO CURVE, yes they might have curved previous years but THERE WILL BE NONE GOING FORWARD. So if you do poorly on the exams and you think you can make it out if you put all your effort in, don't even try, just drop and take the trailer because Bayliss and Wick WILL FAIL YOU!!!

Course breakdown: Weekly ish HWs 10%, Group Labs 30%, 3 Exams 20% apiece. According to upperclassmen it's very important to internalize this material because you will use it for the rest of your college career. Bayliss is a sweet, dedicated professor who is very knowledgeable and patient–– he answers questions very well. ATTEND CLASS. His lecture style is slightly slow but he brings up everything you need to know about exams and even outlines labs. The labs are MATLAB heavy and very dense so make sure to choose a good group.

Good class sometimes deadlines are a little bit crazy though. For example in one of the first few weeks there was a hw due on the Friday then lab on Tuesday and then midterm on Friday still doable but a little tough considering other classes. Overall learn a good amount in this course and it is quite practical.

This class is fairly difficult but completely manageable. The content is going to see completely repetitive and somehow still not make sense, but if you got office hours and do the homeworks, everything works out. Bayliss is an incredible professor and genuinely wants to see each of his students succeed. Ask questions about what doesn't make sense and communicate what you need! I will say the labs are by far the worst part of this class. Your group may be great, but it will probably still have problems finding times to meet or communicating who will complete each part. My only recommendation is to start on them early and try to clear the majority of your time on the final due date. I had plenty of times where my group thought things were done but on the final day, we realized something needed to be tweaked or double checked in office hours.

This is a fair course. I had Alvin Baylis for this class and he definitely has his quirks but the faster you adapt to this class and the method of teaching the better you will do. People will say that this is the hardest EA and while it definitely has a case for being so, I found it exceptionally fair, and got an A myself. The best advice I can give is get a responsible lab group, join a peer guided study group, and spend time with the content. Take the quizzes with people if possible but do the homework alone. Exams are decently challenging, but nothing crazy or unexpected (although there is some time pressure).

This course covers differential equations, which mainly include 1st–order ODEs, 2nd–order ODEs, 1st–order systems, numerics, and applications. The homework was graded by completeness: this was great because we focused on doing the problems rather than go to office hours or friends every time to do hours. The homework quiz was still important and tested our understanding. The labs were manageable as long as you formed a reliable group, delegated early, and went to lab hours after trying oneself.
The course was paced really well and I felt like I learnt all of the content really well. I also thought that the content was really interesting and pretty intuitive.
The labs were very strenuous but also very eye opening. It made it easy to visualize the subject matter. A lot of your time will be spent working in the labs so chose a group you can count on.
Really difficult time crunch tests

I thought overall EA4 was fine. I feel like it has a reputation for being the hardest EA, but honestly I thought the content wasn't too difficult, and the exams were very fair. With that said, there was the typical time crunch and if you're someone who makes small mistakes on exams, you might get penalized in EA4. Also Bayliss is getting a bit old to be honest, and I think sometimes that detracts from his teaching. The chapter notes and hw psets were the most useful resource for studying for exams. Also, relative to other EAs, the homework will take longer, especially when you add in the time on each lab. HW 3 or 4 was genuinely an 8 hour slog.

This class is fine. Most of the class is straightforward, however the difficulty comes in through the exams which are more of time– sensitive exams rather than hard exams. Bayliss is a bit on the older side, but the simplicity of the class allows for his teaching style to be effective at reinforcing topics instead of making it hard to understand. The biggest downside of this class is the 4 labs which can be tedious, but are made manageable by doing it with a group of friends who splits the work equitably.

This course wasn't as hard as I had expected it to be based on what people had told me. I thin joining a PGSG was helpful and made it easier to learn the concepts. I also think having a good lab group helped since the labs were a lot of work and took a lot of time. The labs were also due the same week as the midterms which made it annoying, but if you start early and collaborate with your group they're doable.

This was my least favorite class this quarter and EA course overall. Professor Bayliss is unorganized with how he writes lecture notes on the board, and half the time I can't even tell when he's doing an example or not. It becomes hard to follow, especially when he erases things quickly. The labs are very lengthy and time consuming, which I didn't really like. I do appreciate how Bayliss would sometimes dedicate a class to reviewing what the lab is about. However, I didn't so too well on the first two exams and the only thing stopping me from dropping the class was the fact that I had to do those previous labs. Bayliss switches up the notation from the textbook, so cross referencing both was something I found a bit hard. We would get practice problems, but they ended up not being like the actual exams; this made me feel like no matter how much I studied I'd still do bad.

Expect EA4 to be a hard class since it is the last in the sequence meant to weed out students. That being said your grade souly depends on your own commitment. From the perspective of midterm and final content, make sure to attend every lecture. It is purely Alvin Bayliss writing on a chalkboard how to solve problems step–by–step and if you pay attention and take notes throughout the lecture you will do well on the exams. I believe that he is a great teacher, but the main struggle in this class stems from the labs and the exams. Regarding labs, find a solid, smart group of four friends who you can rely on to do their section of work. The labs are time consuming and complex. I would say that the labs are the hardest part of the class because of the sheer amount of work that is expected, but if it is distributed throughout your group you will get it done, just start early (over the weekend) and get help from the TA's. Regarding the exams, you need to know the content like the back of your hand since they include a lot in a short period of time. You need to be able to see a problem, identify it, and solve it without thinking to hard. Luckily as I said none of the content is too hard and Bayliss does a great job of teaching it. He is also very accomodating if a group needs an extension. Don't skip lecture, start the homework early, and pay attention in class and you will get an A.
Not as bad as I thought. Get a good group for the labs. HW is pretty chill. Exams not too bad.
Kind of rough, not terrible though. Homework isn't too bad and subjects are understandable if you study

A lot of how this course goes is dependent on your group. It was helpful for me to work with my friends, because we actually were able to get work done when we were together and we did a good job about splitting up tasks and getting started on the lab earlier than most groups. Office hours were also super important.

This class is A LOT of work. You have weekly homework (that are around 30 problems from the book, yes 30 weekly) and quizzes with every homework. You also have 4 labs and 3 exams (non cumulative). The homework would take around 10 hours and the quiz wasn't too bad, however labs were really bad. They're very lengthy and you don't learn much from them other than how much group work sucks. You have to coordinate with 3–4 other people on tasks that build on each other and you only have a week to do it, which is especially bad in the middle of the quarter when everyone is so busy. There's a ton of coding and a bunch of write–ups that are graded nicely enough. Other than that exams are pretty hard but if you know what you're doing getting a decent grade is feasible. Prof Bayliss is a great teacher and such a nice prof. He really cares about student learning and loves teaching. He is always very open to questions and really takes his time to answer them. He knows the content inside and out and I would recommend taking the class with him.

I though Bayliss was a great and knowledgeable lecturer. He explained things clearly and always ensured questions were answered. The labs were a lot of work, but there were only four of them. The homework were long but useful for exam preparation. The exams themselves were very faired and tested content taught in class and emphasized in the practice exam problems. I liked that Bayliss posted his notes to Canvas. They were very useful for exam preparation.
This course is hard especially since nothing gets recorded so you have to use the textbook or pay a lot of attention during class.

Yeah I'm sure you've heard about this class from others. I consider this similar to EA I with how comfortable the content will feel to most people. The labs are infamous and I was super worried about them coming into EA. The first 2 kind of sucked but after than it wasn't actually too bad. The grading was actually not too bad on them either. I will say while the class stinks Bayliss is a super nice prof. He may not be the most engaging lecturer but I think they were fine and he was always happy to answer questions and seemed to genuinely care about the students.

One of the hardest EA's, Bayliss knows what he is talking about and I felt like I was actually learning when I attended class. Supplement going to class with reading the chapter notes he posts it is really helpful

This material for this class isn't actually that difficult if you just pay attention and study. The labs you should make sure you get a good group, and the bigger the better so each person can do less work. As long as you go to lab hours, the labs will be fine, they just take time. Bayliss is hard to pay attention to but he's really nice and a pretty good teacher if you can pay attention. The biggest issue with this course is the lab hours. They are extremely busy during the days right before the lab is due because obviously that's when everyone has their answers to check. As a result, all of EA4 is trying to check their answers, and there isn't enough TAs, so the room is so crowded and you have to wait for like 1.5 hours in line just to check your stuff. I hope they make a better system going forward but it is what it is.

EA4 covers differential equations and then finishes up the last bit of linear algebra that EA1 didn't get to. Prof. Bayliss is a great instructor and does a really good job explaining course material, but wow, this class is hard. In addition to ~weekly HW and three exams, there are four 5–person lab assignments. The labs are frustratingly difficult and take about ~50–60 hours per group, per lab. Be sure to start them early and go to office hours early, because OH is packed––like waiting in line for 90 minutes type packed––leading up to the due dates. EA4 was my most time consuming class this quarter, and I would recommend trying to take it during a lighter quarter if you can. But as far as instructors go, I'm very glad I took it with Bayliss, and I did enjoy the content we learned. Note that Bayliss does not record lectures, but as far as I know, no EA4 profs record.

Overall, this course is not that tough. I thought that Bayliss taught the course very well and felt very prepared to do all of the homework and tests just based off of lectures and study materials. The labs are pretty rough but you get your own lab group of 4–5 people that you get to choose the people.

The lectures, homeworks, quizzes, and exams are fairly reasonable and straightforward. Professor Bayliss was a good lecturer and easy to follow. He's receptive to questions and predictable.

On the other hands, the labs were a nightmare. I even had a good team. The labs are long and confusing. The lines for office hours were over an hour long, and the TAs had conflicting (or no) answers. There were always multiple typos in the labs that made us have to redo parts of the labs. The TAs often did not know what to do and were completely useless. It was very stressful and high pressure, considering that the lab tasks were related and built off of each other. I would suggest going to office hours as soon as possible to avoid the lines and make sure to get a good team!

This course is not too difficult concept wise, but the amount of work every week is insane. Labs can take between 10 – 15 hours per person and the homework would take up to 10 hours a week. Some weeks, we would have a lab, homework, and a quiz all due. The amount of time spent on the homework for only a 0.25% completion grade felt way too little. Bayliss is wonderful, however the amount of work was insane and there was not enough TA office hours. In the two days before each lab was due, there would be a line of 100+ people out of the door. Bayliss posted practice questions for each exam, but they often did not reflect the concepts actually tested.
Quizzes and exams are very fast paced

Bayliss is such a sweetheart and a good professor. Exams are hard and averages were relatively low but mostly because they were really long for the 50 minutes we were given. Labs are tedious and take a long time but definitely do help you understand the material at a deeper level

I really enjoyed the content of this class. I thought solving the problems was actually quite interesting (aside from when you do springs and pendula at the end of the class).

I had Bayliss and thought he was decent at explaining concepts. Sometimes his writing was hard to interpret, even at row 4 or 5 back. People would zoom in with their camera to try and figure things out.

Homeworks were quite fair (5 of them throughout the quarter).

The really rough part are the 4 labs. Basically, hope your group is good, and go to TA office hours to check your answers. Worst part is that sometimes the due dates align with exam weeks, which is just a lot. Also, sometimes lab content is on the exams, but like... your group splits up what they work on, so sometimes one person is very strong in the part that is on the exam (just kind of luck)

The course is very well paced. You are constantly reviewing in class and nothing seems to be too fast. The only difficult part is the labs as they are incredibly long and are hard to balance with other exams going on at the same time.

They say that your lab groups make or break your experience in the class...yes that is 100% true. Labs were assigned irregularly, sometimes having them back to back, so the workload between 5 people is quite a lot. Many people also dropped the class so groups can become smaller. The labs aren't necessarily difficult, but they are a lot of work, and I recommend always going to office hours to check results and figures. In class lectures are great, Prof. Bayliss does a great job of applying the material from the textbook in relevant manner to engineering, with many examples done in class as well. Homeworks are completion based, but the weekly quizzes are not. I highly recommend always sitting in lecture, the material is well explained.

Overall, a complicated and frustrating course. Hard exams, very, very, very long labs, several hours of waiting at the lab office hours for a single clarification or simple question, exam practices that do not really align with the exams, harsh grading that does not award almost any partial work, and difficult regrade policies. My fall nightmare...

Oh boy oh boy. Yes, this was by far the most challenging EA course I've taken. At times At times the difficulty felt appropriate and even rewarding, but more often it felt challenging in ways that were honestly annoying at best and malignant at worst.

Although the courses labeled as "engineering analysis", the content is essentially a math class and they should say so from the getgo. It's all about differential equations – first and second order ODEs, eigenvalues/eigenvectors, systems, and related methods. You’ll learn a great deal from this material, and for most engineering majors is pretty relevant. When the concepts click, especially in real–world applications, the class can be genuinely interesting. Unfortunately, a lot of these “nicer” moments are more than offset by a number of issues:

LABS – everyone is probably gonna talk about this, so I need not say much. Yes, labs are extremely long – often reaching 50 pages per report – and often feel more time–consuming than necessary. This is a very common student concern. Starting early is essential.

HW + HW QUIZZES – this was only worth 10% of our grade. It's usually manageable (though there were one or two that were quite difficult) and graded for completion. What isn't graded for completion, however, are the HW quizzes – they are timed, due the same night as the homework, and worth significantly more points (8 points, while the actual HW is just 2). They generally mirror the last few problems in your homework, but they still just add a considerable weekly pressure. I do not miss this at all.

PROF. BAYLISS – opinions will vary by student, but personally his instruction never really clicked. I will say, Bayliss is kind. Perhaps a bit callous, but he is always willing to take questions and is never ever dismissive of students. He will frequently asks the class if there’s any questions on the material, and does a pretty good job at answering. Definitely take advantage of this and ask questions early on – do NOT let pride slow you down.

But everything else about his teaching I honestly found subpar – it was honestly a challenge to leave class with any meaningful grasp of the material. The man’s old. He’s quiet. He’s not engaging as a teacher. And he operates under the philosophy that he can just explain something once and expect you to understand it all, which just doesn’t work for most people. Many students benefit from reinforcement or rephrasing, and the lack of repetition made the lectures difficult to follow. Important equations or steps seemed to appear without enough context, making the content feel more intimidating than it actually is once understood. Especially after learning the material on my own later, I’d look back to Bayliss’ teaching and scratch my head as to why it felt so inaccessible in lecture… Because when you get it, it actually isn’t so bad, but Bayliss somehow managed to make it seem so advanced/intimidating. At times I’ve just thought in my head “you know, he could have literally just phrased this concept like ______ and it would’ve been so much simpler and made more sense.”

I was also concerned by several mismatches between expectations and reality with Prof. Bayliss – he has told us things that felt near–deceitful. For one, at the beginning of the quarter, he said the course was “not intended to be a weed–out class” – it absolutely is; many aspects of the workload and assessments felt 100% consistent with a weed–out structure – our Test 1 average literally rose by over 5 points because so many students dropped the class (63.4 mean & 67 median to 68.8 mean and 72 median). And similarly, just before Test 3 we were reassured that “all of this is content we’ve covered before, there shouldn’t be any tricks or anything like that, etc.” – and sure, the test technically covered the content we learned (duh), but so many tricks were thrown at us with the algebra – squaring then adding fractions, having to use quotient rule out of nowhere, and just an unfamiliar formatting of certain problems that introduced difficulty beyond what had been emphasized (like hehehe, what if I replaced this x with an x’’ and it ends up completely changing the equation). You should be motivated to look elsewhere for your EA4 professor – if Prof. Wick is teaching, other students say they found his section somewhat more accessible.

And of course, for seemingly no reason, lectures aren’t recorded. So this makes it impossible to revisit explanations that were hard to follow the first time. I see absolutely no reason for it to be this way (There's an active recording system in the room and you can see the camera monitoring us live at the lectern), and I am convinced it's done to make the course artificially difficult – when in reality it would help us learn the material better and literally make us better engineers. The posted lecture notes do exist, but they are quire rough, hard to read, and not at all sufficient for learning new material independently. They are more useful for exam review than for first exposure.

Which brings me to my next point:

TESTS –these are the most challenging aspect of the course for me. Historically, that Test 1 is pretty hard, Test 2 is the most manageable, (try to be above average on that, should pay off), and Test 3 is the hardest. My main concern is that the exams fundamentally didn’t reward you for conceptual mastery as much they rewarded speed, and algebraic position under pressure. Even when you understand the material thoroughly, the sheer amount of algebra, trickiness of formatting, and time constraints can significantly affect performance (and yes, making simple mistakes can absolutely cost you a sizable fraction of your exam grade). The difficulty often felt reminiscent of EA2 – huge time pressure, grind–heavy, and sometimes unpredictable. Yes, there’s the classic EA method of giving you limited practice problems/tests that you just know aren’t going to be enough to prepare you. The exams will always be harder, always will have stuff in there written in ways you’ve never seen. I’m not sure exactly what they want us to do – think up of new problems in our heads like Nostradamus? This fundamentally wasn’t “engineering analysis,” it felt more so an IQ test with diffeqs built into it. You can just feel it was designed punitively. Never before have I seen an exam where you can master the material and see how someone can score so well, yet you still score so low.

RECOMMENDATIONS FOR FUTURE STUDENTS – learn the concepts first, then practice exam style problems in the days leading up to the exam. Trying to practice test questions while still learning the material is overwhelming. – CHOOSE YOUR LAB GROUP INTENTIONALLY. Choose your own friends, especially if you know them to be hard workers. Don’t take the chance of going random – every “random” group experience I’ve heard of often turned into a nightmare. – Go to office Lab Office Hours EARLY. The TAs will confirm whether your approach is correct, but going closer to the deadline means the line to talk to them can literally be out the door. – And this is the biggest one: JOIN A PGSG! They are a crucial tool for solidifying the material and can really help offset subpar teaching. In particular, they REALLY make a difference in preparing you for exams, and that’s worth solid gold.

Overall, be prepared to sink LOTS of time into this one. It’s hard, but it is NOT impossible. Good luck!
