categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: If you can take this class with Professor Gardner. She is one of the sweetest human beings ever. I honestly only took this class to complete a foundational discipline but i'm so glad i did. It's honestly a pretty easy A. I probably did the least of what was expected from us and did fine. I'd recommend attending lecture as most of the midterms are based on what's covered during lecture. The remaining content (20%) comes from the textbook which Professor Gardner will give you a study guide covering the chapters that are expected from us. I'd also highly recommend doing the extra credit activities. They're honestly pretty fun and quick to finish and can highly help your grade. Would highly recommend taking this class and would take it again if possible!

I had to spend a lot of time outside of class reading the textbook and studying, which was exhausting and at sometimes felt really defeating. I think the material was extremely interesting and fun to learn about, I just wish that the amount of stress that seem to be involved in this course wasn’t as prominent so I could’ve enjoyed learning about the material more.
Great course! Extremely engaging and not very time–consuming.

This was a very good intro psych class. We covered a lot of topics in a short amount of time but as long as you managed your work well it was definitely doable. The weekly quizzes were very helpful and were pretty easy if you use the lecture notes and looked at the textbook sections. The tests weren't terrible, only 50 MC questions but you definitely need to study. I think the discussion boards weren't super helpful but it was interesting to see what you classmates had to say. Overall good course and learned a lot.

This course was amazing and professor Gardner is very effective at explaining the course content! My biggest advice is to come to class because 80% of all the test content comes from lectures with only 20% from the textbook. If you take good notes in the lectures and do all the textbook readings you should have no trouble doing well on the tests by studying your notes. Since there are only 3 tests the whole quarter, each test covers a lot of content, so be sure to give yourself at least a few days to study; don't try and do it all last minute. One of the best ways to study for prof Gardner's tests is to study with people so you can quiz each other and practice recalling the information. If you study well, the tests aren't very hard, but you do have to study WELL. Also, if you have any questions about what you're responsible for knowing for the test go to office hours and ask because prof Gardner and the TA's are very willing to clarify what is fair game for the exams.
The professor is absolutely adorable–– the best! The class is fun too. There isn't really much homework, the hours spent outside of class was only for studying for the midterms.
It was an okay introduction class but definitely not something that can be pushed to the side when it comes to the MCQ exams.

I felt like this class was very well put together and organized. It felt like I was able to learn everything well and understand the information better than other classes because of the interactive learning.
I really enjoyed the course, and it makes me want to continue exploring psychology. I actually enjoyed the format of the tests, although challenging.

The Prof. was very engaging. Her teaching style allowed me to be fully immerse in the lecture and her use of examples and images allowed for me to understand more complicated and difficult concepts easily.

Liked the class overall. Go to lecture, do the suggested readings, and you'll do good. The only aspect of the class I strongly disliked is the 10 page paper requirement for minors who can't consent to participate in the research studies. It felt like an inequitable penalty for being a minor. 5–6 pages max would probably be more fitting for an equal paper requirement.
I really enjoyed this class. It is fairly memorization based, but I found that it was pretty easy to remember most things because they are all related to me. I think I learned a lot about myself too.
An awesome course that focused on many aspects of psychology at a shallow level. Tough tests but if you study enough, an A is definitely doable.

Great class! Professor Gardner is great and the TAs were all very passionate and helpful. The most time spent outside of class was on studying for exams. There are weekly quizzes and discussion boards, and extra credit opportunities in the forms of papers where you are explaining concepts. The beginning of class can be kind of boring but as time moves on the topics get more interesting. Exams can be somewhat difficult because it is reviewing such a large amount of content in a short time so studying is super important. Not super easy, but very doable if you put in the effort and is ultimately enjoyable.

Professor Gardner is such a passionate teacher, and you learn so many various aspects about psych through this course. You definitely need to put in some work to get an A, but as long as you spend enough time memorizing the lecture and textbook content, you'll be fine!
Lecturer is very good, breaks down complex subjects pretty well.

I absolutely loved this course. It was the perfect introduction to psychology, with clear and easy–to–understand lecture slides and a structure that made it accessible and enjoyable. I rarely needed to attend lectures because the slides were so effective, and I appreciated the stress–free exams and opportunities for extra credit. The course made me even more interested in psychology, and I found myself discussing what I learned with my family and friends outside of class. I can’t wait to take more psychology courses!
This course had very interesting material to learn, little homework to do, and a light amount of reading.
This is a great class that everyone should take. Prof gardner is a very fun and fair professor

I loved this class so much. Professor Gardner is an AMAZING professor, covering everything efficiently and with such passion and humour. Even though the class has over 300 people, she does an amazing job at keeping us all engaged. Turning up to class is not a must, but missing out on one lecture can be a lot of missed content, and not going can mean that you miss out on her ad– libbed examples about a concept that can help you remember it easier. While I do think that the class did not place much importance in encouraging students to ask questions about the content, it meant we could learning a huge amount of factual knowledge and apply that to interesting real life scenarios. I found the content very self–reflexive, so it meant that a lot of it was intuitive to learn and appreciate within a real life context. The exams, although being multiple choice, require a good understanding of all the content on the slides and the 10 sections of textbook reading the professor assigns. This can be quite taxing and so shouldn't be left until the night before if you want to get a good grade, but not impossible. This is a relatively easy class to get an A if you keep on top of the weekly discussion posts and quizzes, but if you don't you can only really afford to lose 19/300 points to get an A, so be organized. Overall, I adored this class and will be taking PSYCH 213 with Professor Gardner next quarter because of it!

3 mid–term exams (all multiple choice) – 240/300 points 9 weekly discussion posts (you get 100% as long as you just write something on time) – 18/300 points 9 weekly online mini–quizzes (open book so easy to get 100% but I'd recommend trying to do them without notes first) – 42/300 points

Lectures go relatively fast as you cover a lot of material in a short period of time, but Prof Gardner keeps the lectures interesting with a variety of examples and demonstrations. Open note mini quiz and discussion posts weekly are an easy way of keeping your grade up for when exams come around. Textbook is dense but are given specific sections of the chapters to focus on for the exam which is helpful. Overall, very low maintenance class and would recommend.

I did not enjoy this class whatsoever. I have never taken psych during HS and so I entered this class thinking that it would be a good introduction to Psych as a field. While the lecture content was enjoyable, I hated the exams. They were so specific for no reason, and there was just too much content to cover.

I took this class thinking it would be an easy A which is NOT THE CASE. The average of the first exam was a C and the second was a B–/B. Into classes should never be this difficult and it definitely deterred me from taking another psych class again.
I wasn't interested in the topic beforehand but I loved this class so much I want to take more psych classes. The lectures were always the highlight of my week.
I found this course really interesting and it was not too difficult. You might find yourself studying a lot for the exams, but if you put in the work it is not hard to get an A.

TAKE THIS CLASS! Professor Gardner made every class engaging and wanted everyone to succeed. She gave a limited amount of homework (discussion post, response, and quiz weekly) and she gave multiple extra credit opportunity. It is a lot of memorizing but it is worth it!
Great prof, great class, fair grading, and resources/materials/TAs/extra credit opportunities are made very accessible to students, would definitely recommend this course.
I really liked this class. I wish that the lectures had been recorded but overall it was very good.

Prof. Gardner was great, she's a fun lecturer and I never get bored listening to her. The class is a good intro to psychology, and was easy to follow. Homework is minimal and exams are straightforward to study for if you paid attention in class and took good notes.

Professor Gardner is awesome. She has so much passion for teaching and makes the discussions really engaging and fun. However, there is a lot of information that we learn in this quarter so you have to be studying always to stay on top of it or you will fall behind. She offers a lot of resources to prepare for the exams and extra credit assignments. I enjoyed taking this class and would recommend taking it with her.
Gardner is incredible, very engaging and fun to learn from. Class isn't too awful to get an A in.
The aspects that were most important to me were learning more about the way the human mind functions, such as learning attachment styles

This course was pretty straightforward since the exams were essentially the same concepts we learned during lecture. In my opinion, it was definitely necessary for the most part to attend class in order to understand what was being taught on the slides. The questions on the exams were based on applying the concepts during class which I felt we didn't learn a lot about during lecture so it was kind of up to you to find examples to study off of.
This class was great. It was definitely stressful at times, but very manageable. If you put in effort, listen to lecture and study for the exams, an A is achievable.

One thing that was challenging about this course is much of the grade is weighted on three multiple choice exam throughout the quarter. If you aren't the strongest test taker, then I definitely recommend brushing up before this course.

Gardner is my favorite professor of all time. There is nothing I wouldn't do for her. She makes a 300 person class so engaging and fun! I wish she taught all my classes. By far, my favorite class of the quarter. I will say though, STUDY FOR THE EXAMS. They are not difficult, but are designed to make sure you actually know the material so review!

This course was not difficult and very easy to follow. There are open note quizzes due every Sunday and they usually take less than 2 minutes and they were typically very easy. There are only three midterms throughout the quarter and as long as you study for them you will be ok. The professor was really good keeping the lecture engaging and I really enjoyed the course 100% recommend taking.
This class was just memorizing facts before exams and nothing else. Very little work and can be somewhat boring. Professor is great though.

The course was enjoyable. The professor was very interesting and kept a very large class interested and engaged during the quarter. The workload is very manageable and I enjoyed the contents. There were 3 non–cumulative exams during the quarter which was nice.

The professor does a good job teaching and she does help you understand the material in lecture. Its a big lecture but even so she does a great job explaining things. This class is mostly AP Psych (covers less than AP Psych in fact) and its all rote memorization. The course material itself is not hard to remember or understand but the exam questions can be a little tricky. The grading is something I don't like though. While there are extra credit opportunities, it seems almost impossible to get above a 90 on exams and theyre weighed pretty heavily.

TAKE THIS CLASS WITH PROF GARDNER she's so so good. Genuinely interesting to listen to and made the hour and twenty minutes not painful. She breaks the concepts down effectively and makes them applicable in an engaging way.

The class consisted of 3 non–cumulative exams. I wouldn't say that this was an easy A course, but it was very interesting. This class is very fast–paced, and as long as you start studying a few days before the exam and go through all of the slides and the textbook before the exam, you should be good.

The course was pretty interesting and lectures were nice. Some might enjoy more this kind of lectures than I personally did, as they are not necessarily theoretically intensive. The grading is based on one take home open book quiz (~15%), 3 exams with 50 multiple choices questions (80%) and some comments to make on the canvas (honestly not the best exercise, but makes sense for freshmen). 2/3 exams were difficult, especially if you don't master English perfectly as they might have some hidden meanings. As a non–native English speaker it was not exactly great. Aside from that, a good class, requires some work but not too much.

Professor Gardener was amazing! She was very passionate about the material, and did a great job of teaching it in an engaging way. I've always found psychology to be interesting and professor Gardener defiantly made me want to take more psych classes in the future

Psych is a wonderful class to take as a freshman, and super interesting as a Psych major. If you can take it with Prof. Gardner, please do, she is INCREDIBLE and so engaging even as you're struggling to stay awake.

This course throws so much information at you. You better keep up with it if you want to do well in this class. I definitely struggled and felt overwhelmed when I would study the night before the exam. It was sometimes over 200 terms to memorize and you needed to read chapters from the book because she pulls questions from there. I took this class with Orgo and really thought I could dedicate the time to doing well but I was wrong. I tried creating flashcards and doing spaced repetition which helped but wasn't enough for the way the exams were written. The class is honestly just graded on the 3 exams you take which means you have to be consistently above the average. The exams have select all that apply, none of the above, or (A and B) (C and B)( C and A). You have to get use to that type of answer choice and really know the content. The other thing about this class is the psych labs you have to attend. I thought it was 3 surveys you had to complete but it's 14 credits and you fail the class if you don't complete all 14. They sometimes took a lot of time around an hour but don't contribute to your grade. Honestly if you have the time, you can do well in the class but if your just here to learn try to take it with a lighter workload. Oh there are extra credit opportunities so make sure to do those!

I loved this class and Professor Gardner! Her lectures were super engaging and interesting and I learned a lot! The class was pretty doable too with extra credit opportunities and open note quizzes every week to bump your test grades, but even the tests were very fair and with a couple hours of studying you could do well.
concepts are not difficult, just a lot of content. Recommend studying early
This class isn't too difficult, just a lot of reading.

BEST CLASS IVE TAKEN SO FAR!!!!! If you are even slightly interest in psychology take this course and take this course with Gardner FOR REAL!! She is the best lecturer I've seen here so far and she WILL keep you engaged for 90 minutes straight, make you laugh, AND teach you in such a fun way about psych. Decided to declare a minor in psych right after taking it. Be prepare to REALLY study and even memorize extremely detailed concepts though. Could not recommend enough seriously! Special shout out to TA Lourdes who was the best TA ever!

Take this class! Prof Gardner is awesome!! Definitely not an easy A, but as long as you study ahead of time (I recommend finding friends to study with), everything will be alright! This class is very rewarding and the content is super interesting!
I was slightly bored
Take it if you're interested in psych. Not an easy distro because I took about 12000 words of notes. Pretty much every part was tested.

This was a great overview of psychology for me since I have never taken a psychology course before. Professor Gardner was an engaging lecturer and she was very clear about what would be on the exams. She was also very helpful during office hours. In her lectures she did a great job of balancing conceptual explanations with plenty of real world examples and studies and fun in–class puzzles and activities.

I found the class extremely interesting and looked forward to Professor Gardner's lectures each day. However, I found some of the exams to be particularly difficult in the application of the teachings. The quizzes and readings definitely helped in my preparation, though.

Not really sure who said this was an easy distro, it really isn't. Lots of necessary memorization to do well, and there is barely a curve when it comes to midterms. If you're looking for an easy distro, this is not going to be it (especially with no prior psychology experience).

Take this class with Professor Gardner!!! She is a phenomenal lecturer and even though our classes were over an hour long, I was able to be engaged the whole time. This class is hard to get an A in because of the grading scale, but if you make flashcards, review the material thoroughly, and do the extra credit, it is definitely doable. You will do well in this class if you are good at memorizing –– a lot of the examples she brings up in class are also applicable to the midterm.

TAKE THIS CLASS! Prof Gardner is amazing and so great at explaining complex concepts. The class isn’t too much work, just studying for the three multiple choice exams in the quarter, and prof Gardner offers multiple extra credit opportunities.

I really liked this class, I felt like there was a lot of content and I would have probably benefited from going to office hours a little more, especially considering the exams were on the lecture slides and the textbook. As a heads up, if you are under 18 at the time of taking this course you will have to write a 10–15 research paper as a lab requirement while everyone gets to do the labs. I feel like it isn't really equivalent but just keep that in mind.

Take this class! The professor is incredible and very engaging in her lectures. The weekly quizzes are easy, and the tests are all multiple choice. With proper studying, it's definitely possible to get an A.
It was fun and interesting

Going into this course, I expected it to be an easy A, but don't underestimate it. While it's definitely possible to end with an A, I struggled a bit at the start because I didn't study too hard as I expected to effortlessly do well (which was not the case). After studying more and more for the next two exams, I found the course to be so much easier. Definitely take with Prof. Gardner, she was super engaging and makes concepts easy to understand. It's also a big pro that there is no cumulative final, just three exams on three different units split up throughout the quarter.

This was a great introductory level course to psychology. The instruction was thorough and engaging and genuinely prepared me for what would be on exams. The class had minimal work outside of lectures and the work given was not difficult to understand or complete. Overall, this course was a very stress free introductory class that really encouraged my interest in psychology.

Good intro class. Wendi is great. The exams are legit hard. I came in thinking I would have no trouble at all. I'd never gotten consistent Bs in college but I got a B or B+ on every exam and studied by butt off for all of them. Prepare with other students. That worked best for me.

If you've taken the AP Psych course and got a 3 or 4 (meaning it doesn't count for a credit here), then this course is perfect. However, I'm not sure I can recommend this course to those who haven't taken a psych course before. A lot of material is covered in a short amount of time. In addition to this, having to participate in studies to pass the course can also be a bit of a pain. However, if you've taken AP Psych before, take notes, and actually read the book chapters, you should be perfectly fine.

This course is very interesting and I learned a lot thanks to Professor Gardner. There are three tests over the course of the class that are non–cumulative and multiple choice so as long as you study the concepts you can do well. :)
Not an extremely easy A. You will have to study but if you space it out, memorization will be easier. Also probably go to lectures.

For an intro level class, this was not easy. The lectures got dry at times and it was very easy to lose focus. If you stay on top of everything, you will get by, but the exams can be a pain considering that some questions may have multiple answers and that type of stuff.
This class is very interesting, a nice introducing class. I learnt lot about Psychology, and I would recommend this class.

This is an amazing class! The class is extremely interesting and gives a great overview of introductory psychology concepts and fields. The class consists of lectures and short readings from the textbook that help for the exams. The classwork consists of weekly short open note quizzes and discussion boards (both grade boosters) and three exams (no final). The exams are NOT easy, but are manageable if you study well. Prof. Gardener is an amazing professor and has very engaging and funny lecture style. Overall, the class is not an easy A, but it is totally doable and is a great class with an even better professor.

If memorization is your strength, there's not much to worry about. Setting aside enough time to memorize content (some of which is near common sense). All tests are non–cumulative and multiple–choice, but some MC options are like "A and C" or "all of the above". The material content is pretty easy, and prof Gardner is a very engaging lecturer. Textbook selections can seem long but you can skim most of it, making notes of only the important parts. Personally, it saves me the most time when I make handwritten notes during lecture (of both the slides and what prof Gardner says).

Exams are harder than you think. READ THE TEXTBOOK and learn everything it says there. Not much work on non exam weeks but make sure to memorize EVERYTHING from lectures and textbook sections (only the ones she assigns) before taking the exam

I really enjoyed this course not only because of the content but also because of the enthusiasm from Professor Gardener. The exams were recently spaced out and tested what we learned, so long as you read the required textbook sections and read the slides you would do fine.
The course was extremely intriguing. I enjoyed the visually stimulated type of learning.
the extra research you're forced to partake in are so annoying and a waste of time. I just randomly answered all of them to get them over with.

Was really interesting. The exams are tricky and need to memorize a lot of material. Go to lectures as they are interesting and Prof Gardner explains topics by using a lot of examples that are not on the slides. Not an easy A
I love how I can apply many of these psychology references into the real world––such as the learning and memorization techniques discussed in lecture.

This class is what you would expect of an Intro to Psychology course (there's a lot of breadth and not a lot of depth). If you've taken psychology before, this will be a breeze. Tests and quizzes are fair as long as you study the slides (everything on the test is from the slides and readings). The best part is that the final isn't cumulative, and if you work hard, you'll get an A. Professor Gardner is really engaging, and the lectures are not boring. Definitely take this if you're a premed because it's a fun break from other classes and will be on the mcat!
Great course to take. Professor Gardner is great at capturing interest and her lectures are fun. The content is not too complex and definitely a great intro course.

This class was amazing! Professor Gardner is really good at teaching this course and ensuring that students are engaged during lectures. The topics taught are relevant to the tests and as long as you study, you should do very well in this class. Group/partner studying is the best!

Overall, I enjoyed my time in PSYCH 110. This was both attributed to the content and Prof. Gardner. The lectures were well structured and engaging (for the most part), short discussion posts w/replies due every weekend. and open–note mini quizzes were easy to manage throughout the quarter, When the exams came, it did require a few days of studying to fully cover all the content tested on the exam, but it was definitely doable if you manage your time well and study with friends. The exams were also very fair and most of the class didn't have trouble getting a passing score. If you study hard enough on the exams and take advantage of the easy points on the weekly tasks, there's a high chance you'll come away with an A or A– in the class. Highly recommend the course if you are pre–med and taking other challenging courses like chemistry or calculus, also a good FD if you're not planning on taking any more social science courses. The content was also really interesting and applicable to daily life, so it was a fun course to take just starting out at NU. Remember the research study requirement! Easy but a bit time consuming at points, or opt for the essay.

I really enjoyed this class. It was my favorite class by far this quarter. Professor Gardener was extremely engaging and enthusiastic. I looked forward to each lecture as I found them quite interesting. The tests were all multiple–choice, and they were a bit tough. I would not recommend studying last minute for these exams. Definitely do not cram for them last minute as everything on the slides is fair game and sometimes she chooses minute details on the slides/reading to test on.

Take Psych 110 with Professor Gardner! Literally the best prof ever! Extremely enthusiastic about the material and very helpful. Make sure to study well for all three exams. I did very bad on the first one and had to dedicate a lot of time to studying for the other two exams. Not an easy A class but if you are good at memorization, this class is for you.
idk mixed feelings, extra credit was nice, exam style hard to get used to

I thought that this course was structured well; even though it was very content–heavy and had a lot of information to learn (and memorize!), it was paced well and I definitely found it manageable. The content was also interesting on its own, so I was motivated to learn.
The course covered a wide variety of topics and helped guide an understanding on how psychology plays a role in everyday life.

Not a bad distribution requirement to take. I'd never taken AP Psych but I still did well in the class. Professor Gardner is a great teacher, and there are plenty of office hours if you don't understand concepts. But make sure to study still, a lot of people did poorly on the first exam.

This class was great and despite taking an intro to psych class in high school, it expanded my knowledge about psychology. The class felt interesting without being overwhelming difficult. I will say, the majority of your grade comes from exams, but they are non– cumulative and multiple–choice. There was a bit of a learning curve on the first exam but after I understood how questions were asked I did fairly well.

This class is not a lot of work; however, the slides were really wording, making it really hard to concentrate during the lecture at times. Also, the exams were challenging and definitely required more studying than expected. Overall, the course content was very interesting, but applying it was difficult.

Professor Gardner – there are not enough positive adjectives in the English language to describe her. I so appreciated her engaging teaching style, and her obvious care/interest for her students and that they understand the information she's teaching. I found most of the curriculum quite interesting, and I thought the exams were fair. There is a decent amount of memorization require for the exams, but they were not too difficult and as long as you pay attention in lectures—especially to the examples and topics she emphasizes—you should be fine! Also, there is not too much reading involved; all you really have to do is read and take notes on the few sections she specifically points out in the study guide before an exam.

Yes! I had no prior exposure to psychology before college, and this course was incredible. Wendi is an outstanding instructor—her passion for teaching is unmatched, and she’s probably my all–time favorite teacher! That said, you should start studying from day one. It’s well known that grades on the first exam tend to be lower, so early preparation is key. Also don't think this is one of those classes you can skip, if you do be ready to be lost, as she covers a lot in lecture with in–class elaborations and demos that are referenced in the exam as well

The course is organized into three non–cumulative exams with no final. There’s an extra credit opportunity before each exam and the possibility of a curve, which is helpful. However, don’t fall into the trap of poor performance on the first exam and rely on improvement over time, as many students do. The material builds consistently: everything you learn from the start goes toward the first exam, and once that’s done, the next set of material begins immediately for the second exam, and so on. The pace can feel intense, but I retained so much and genuinely enjoyed applying what I learned to real–life situations. Don't think you need to read the entire text book, as long as you thoroughly study the lectures, your notes, and the sections she points out in every exam study guide from the textbook, you'll be okay!

This was by far my favorite course of the quarter. The only reason I got a low grade was due to having to spend a lot more time on my other courses. Wendi is an amazing professor and you can really see that she enjoys what she does.
This course was incredibly interesting and engaging. Wendi Gardner is an amazing professor.

Professor Gardner is an amazing person and lecturer! I'm always excited to come to class because the lectures were also so fun. I love how the professor engages with us, so 120 minutes pass by so fast. The first exam was honestly the hardest out of the three, so make sure you study hard for that one. Also, go to office hours, because the TAs (shoutout to Lourdes) were so helpful.

This is definitely not an easy distro class if you are looking for one. There is a lot of memorization required so don't be surprised if you fail the test with no studying. If you are good at that then this class is actually not too difficult for you. The content is very interesting and I highly recommend it.

Professor Gardner is AMAZING!! She kept lectures engaging and entertaining. I walked away from class with a new thing every single time. I most definitely recommend taking this course whether its for your major/minor or just as an elective.

This course was so amazing! Professor Gardener was so genuinely interested in what she was teaching that it made me so interested as well. The content was super interesting and I was always so excited to go to class!

People told me this class would be an easy A. This class is NOT an easy A. This class is not as hard as organic chemistry, but you really need to study for the exams. The exams are 50 MC and they are challenging. At times, I felt that I was being tricked into certain answers. Further our first midterm average was around a 76% and the second exam was an 83%. Further, this class requires you to study a lot. Also, I think Dr. Gardner is a good professor, but her slides are a mess at times. She tries shortening phrases to the point where they do not make sense. Also, when you are tested, you are REQUIRED to know the book chapters. She does not teach them in class but you will be tested on it. Further, this class was a 6/10 for me. The last unit was the toughest about clinical disorders.
Fantastic class! Professor Gardner is a great lecturer, and the difficulty was manageable while still being interesting. I definitely recommend it!

Prof Gardner is awesome. She's super engaging and knows what she's talking about. Not a lot of outside work is required, but the exams are not easy. The way the exams are formatted basically make it so every question is a trick. Once you recognize that, you will be okay. But the exams are hard.

Prof is great. Very big class so unless your a pet dont create much connection with prof. If you pay attention and do proper studying then you can get an A. Its really dependent on how much effort you put. I should've put more but oh well, you live with what you do. Lectures are easy to understand. Textbook studying is a little annoying as it can be easily incorporated into the slides but i guess they just don't want too.

Having taken AP Psych in high school, I would say this was a refreshingly better version of it (and I found AP Psych to be pretty good as it is). Professor Gardner is also one of the best professors I’ve ever had! She really cares about your fit in the class, and is always open to help or answer questions as needed (she’s also just very knowledgeable). Overall, this course was very good, you just have to be willing to sit down and study for the 3 exams mainly.

Professor Gardner is a great professor who truly cares about her students. She takes time to explain concepts in the lectures and makes them easy to digest by relating the topics to real examples. The material is very dense, but if you stay on top of each lecture and take thorough notes, it will make it manageable.
Overall great class with stimulating material and engaging lectures. Tests are doable with some group study.
The lectures were very engaging and the work outside of class was not only manageable but also helped understand the topics from lecture better.

Wendi is amazing. So engaging and made the class to interesting and fun. The class is very easy with discussion posts every week and simple hw quizzes, etc. However the exams are HARD. You have to study A LOT and read the textbook sections she tells you too. The class is very worth it though despite the exams.

This is a phenomenal course. Wendi Gardner is AMAZING – so sweet and you can tell she genuinely is so passionate about the subject material and cares about us learning it. Every unit has been fascinating, and this course has made me interesting in taking more psychology classes. There are three non–cumulative, multiple choice exams throughout the quarter. They are very fair – they cover lecture and textbook material, and Prof Gardner tells us what textbook sections we are expected to know for the exam. As long as you put in the time to study and learn the material, you will do fine on the exams – the questions themselves aren't that hard. There are three extra credit opportunities – none of which are hard. There are weekly open–note mini quizzes and discussion posts, which also help boost your grade. Overall can't recommend this course enough – take it with Wendi Gardner!!!!!!!
The topics covered in the course were always very interesting and Prof. Gardner was always enthusiastic and approachable which encouraged learning.

I LOVED this course. Professor Gardner is clearly an expert at engaging her students with the course material. There was a variety of teaching methods such as engaging students in real life experiments, weekly mini quizzes for review, discussions, extra office hours for exam prep, and textbook readings. The course is very clearly structured, helping you know exactly what to expect for each week in terms of course load and study time. I learned so much in a very short span of time and now I know I have a strong interest in psychology.
Definitely a memorization–heavy course. Often had 400+ flashcards before each exam. Big plus that there's no final exam. Aside from exams, very minimal work is required each week.

This course was a great intro to the field of psychology. The professor is great — she's a great lecturer. However, you should definitely try pacing out the textbook readings because they can really catch up to you.

Professor Gardner is so amazing!! Definitely take this class with her. I looked forward to going to class every day. Each lesson was super engaging and there were a lot of little assignments that basically, if you do them, it'll boost your grade. There are three midterms that are non cumulative, and you have to participate in 14 lab credits in order to pass. That is something to definitely keep in mind before choosing your class, because some of them fill up quickly and are long.

TAKE THIS CLASS!! Wendi Gardner is one of the best teachers ever, she's extremely engaging and passionate. The topics are very interesting, especially since you can see them applied to the real world. Only thing to consider is that you need to study a decent amount for the exams, but reading through the book chapters thoroughly and going to every class will help a ton.

Took this expecting an easy intro course, it definitely is not easy. The grading is quite lopsided and there is very little room for error, 3 midterms make up 80% of your grade. The rest of the 20% is very easy and there are 3–4 extra credit opportunities that help, but only a little. The tests aren't as easy as they look and they often test you on very small details of lecture/textbook content that is already quite dense. But lectures are super engaging and Prof. Gardner is fantastic

This class is a must–take for any student! I learned so much about myself, but it also me more easily understand others, their thought processes, and the ways they might function. The lecturer was incredible and did a great job at engaging the students and providing examples that would help me remember information for the exams.
I found it a good bit more mentally engaging than most of my high school classes, and I found that I had to study more.
I would 100% taking this course! The professor makes it VERY engaging and hands out treats to those who participate in her experiments!

This class was pretty good, but more difficult than expected for an introduction/survey psychology class. Professor Gardner is an engaging lecturer, but it just a lot of content to write down and remember, which gets tedious. The 3 exams are pretty difficult, but there are a number of extra credit opportunities and lecture quizzes which boost your grade. One annoying thing was that we were tested on some material that wasn't presented in the lecture, so you would have to read the textbook in depth (some sections) to prepare for exams.
Gardner is really good professor

This class was really enjoyable. Prof. Gardner's enthusiasm is clear, and she is able to make already interesting concepts very palatable. Overall a very interesting and pretty easy class that is great for both requirements and just for pure interest.
It was a good introductory class to psychology; I feel like I learned a lot about psychology in general

Prof. Gardner was super sweet and gave great lectures – the course was a solid basic intro Psych Course without too much work. Exams were straightforward and related exactly to what we talked about in class. Would definitely recommend
LOVE WENDI GARDENER LOVE HER SO MUCH ugh what an incredible lecturer

I loved this course. I'm honestly going to miss it next quarter. This was my fun, easy course my first quarter and it definitely met that. I highly highly recommend this course because you will learn a lot about psychology which I feel we should all know the basics of, and it is fun! Professor Gardner is also a very good professor. She is very animated and enthusiastic about the psychology which is very clear in lectures and in her office hours. This is appreciated. She also allows for questions in class which is nice and people ask some good questions. She also does examples in class which make the class more engaging/interactive since it makes us all participate individually even though it is a big class in a large lecture room. You will learn a lot in this course and you dont' even have to do much work. The assignments are easy to complete. Even though this class is easy, you do have to study and memorize a lot. Don't neglect it! I do recommend studying in a group as that is the best way to study for psych and it does help.
I loved gardner – she is SO fair in her exams and is engaging during lecture
Good class! Not as easy as people say it is, but definitely manageable. The prof is super sweet and nice.

Professor Gardner is amazing and the best Psych 110 professor. No outside work out of class, just pay attention and you will be fine. Not an easy A but not super hard to get a B if you study before exams. The class was super interesting and I enjoyed the way it was taught a lot, one of my favorites information–wise.

It's not a hard class if you have good study habits or if you know how to study for your learning abilities, but if you don't, you should learn before the first test because if not, you will not do good.

This course was fun to take. It wasn't too hard, and the professor kept it engaging by using real–world examples and doing small experiments during class that made lessons and concepts more memorable. She was also very approachable if you had questions or needed help.

The exams were meh, and there were no such things as practice problems from the department whatsoever unless you got assigned a TA who would give you stuff. I did not like how tests were formatted, as they were conceptual in ways we were not thinking in class, so it was hard to transfer over. Some multiple choice (it was all multiple choice) had answer options like A&B, B&C, all of the above, but wayyyy to many of them. I thought the quizzes were unfair, with answer options being so similar it was hard to tell them apart, or demonstrating one aspect of the topic or a similar one, without it being accurate.

Otherwise I really liked the class. Prof Gardner is a super good lecturer, fun, engaging, and smart. I really liked my TA personally too, though sometimes the concept explained was still a bit ambiguous. I learned a LOT in this course. However, if you find yourself to not be the best test taker, and you start to fall behind, there aren't that many ways to improve on your grade even with all the extra credit.
Important aspects of this course consisted of absorbing the content taught in lectures and following the guided readings that were posted to Canvas.

Great Class! Great Professor! Material is very interesting. I had never taken a PSYCH class and I could still understand the material. The office hours really helped. Professor Gardner is very funny and a communicates content very clearly during lecture. Make sure to pay attention during lecture and to read the parts of the book that are meant to come up on the exam. Studying for this class was fun but took time. It give a basic and broad understanding of Psych and opens other courses.

I really really enjoyed this class. You can tell that Professor Gardner is very passionate about the material that she teaches, which makes her use many really cool examples in class. It is not a difficult class but there is definitely a lot of memorization.

I loved this! I was already interested in psych to begin with, and she made me like it even more. She’s a very good teacher who keeps you engaged. She’s also very organized and a great person to talk to.

I thought this was a good course with lots of information taught at a pace that made it very manageable. While the tests were challenging, the expectations of what should be known were made clear and therefore studying was simple if long at times.

Take this class. Easy A if you put in the effort. Simply memorization. Take it with Prof. Gardner, she is so funny and engaging, class was always laughing and her slides are so easy to follow along. She posts them after class so I would recommend just writing down what she says that might not be so clear on the slides instead of writing everything down because she goes at a fair pace so it's not possible and honestly not worth it. You just need a day or two to study and cram all the info in your head. Three exams, all multiple choice, and not cumulative. But trust me, everything that is on the slides and the textbook reading she says to read for the tests will be on the exam so you will not do good in this class unless you study. The material is not hard at all, just please make flashcards and go over the slides and readings.

This class is amazing. Wendi is so passionate! It definitely isn't an easy A, you have to work for it, but the topics are so interesting you aren't going to mind spending so much time learning. The readings and slides are easy to understand. This class is mainly about studying and remembering.

I think this class is fairly easy if you put in the time to study for it. The exams are 100% based on the slides, and any information on there are fair game for the exam. There are 3 exams, weekly quizzes on canvas, discussion posts, and extra credit assignments. Gardner gives a lot of opportunity to raise your grade, so I honestly think this class was fairly structured. Everyone did not do super well on the first exam and improved a lot in the last two, so I would stick it through with the class. There are also studies that you have to do, in order to be considered that you completed the class. I would suggest completing it early on in the quarter, so that you do not have to worry about it later.
super easy lectures are fun
Very good class and well taught. Lectures were interesting and taught tons of interesting stuff. Tests are mostly applying memorized information.

THIS CLASS IS AMAZING BUT... you need to put the work in. Take notes and if you miss a topic focus on it and go over it at least twice outside of class before you even start reviewing. There is A LOT of content. Get a study partner and quiz each other before each exam. Do ALL extra credit. This is class is easy with effort. Do the readings or you will get best a mid 80 on an exam.
Lectures were engaging and the content was interesting to me

Not a very difficult or time consuming class overall, as it is a general overview of many different psychology concepts. I would add that unless you study pretty hard, the tests are fairly difficult but the homework each week takes less than half an hour.

absolutely love wendi. she is an amazing lecturer and so so nice. she gives opportunities for extra credit! the only thing is that the exams are hard. this class is definitely not an easy A but if you put in effort you can definitely get an A–. super interesting material too! made me want to be a psych minor!

This course was great! The lectures were definitely super helpful so make sure you attend those! The professor gives many interesting and memorable examples which make the class a great experience and also really helps with recalling the material during an exam. While the extra credit opportunities are optional, taking advantage of them encourages you to go through all the material which allows you to review and truly understand the concepts learned. Definitely focus on the slideshows, take notes during lecture, and make sure you read the sections of the textbook that are listed on the study guide that the professor releases since those are the most important concepts to know!
Very baseline level class that doesn't go to in–depth about any concepts. Provided a good overview of many topics and good for gauging your interest in the field

I took this class as an engineer. I was always interested in pysch so I thought it was pretty cool. The lectures were stimulating. The weekly workload was very light. And the tests were not especially dififcult.
I think this is a great class to take. While exams are on the harder side, I found materials to be accessible and lectures to be very interesting.

If you're thinking of doing psychology as your major, do not base your judgement of Northwestern Psychology on this course. PSYCH 110 is a great course if you want to learn some practical knowledge and understand how some things influence your behavior, BUT the course itself literally goes through every major field in psychology within 3 months which is very very tiring. There is a lot of information and some of it goes into a lot of detail which you have to remember, and this can be stressful at times. If you do not have super strong background in psychology or are not memorization genius, you might have to work harder on some parts of the course, if you want an A. Also, there is a lab component which is really fun because you get to meet researchers and work with them, so you get some connections and also experience in the psych lab. Also, professor Gardner has 3 exams, all worth the same amount of points and they are non–cumulative because it is a lot of information to keep. She also is a great instructor, and she gives students real–life examples of the material learned. She also teaches you some practical things like how to recognize guilt–tripping, how framing of information can manipulate your decision making or how people make some life choices. I also liked how she always finished more serious lectures with a very positive and deep quote which really helps you put things in the perspective. So, make sure to listen during the class, do your readings from the study guide and honestly take this course with prof. Gardner and you will learn a lot, even if you do not get an A in the end.
Professor Gardner is an exemplar professor. I thourghly enjoyed her lectures whilst learning a lot at the same time.

This course rules. Wendi was an amazing professor who made class engaging, interactive, and extremely entertaining. She taught the material in an extremely helpful and easy–to–learn manner, explaining things in detail and teaching us tricks to remember them. I feel I learned so much in this class and it was a wonderful course. The workload is extremely manageable and the exams are nothing to worry about if you study properly.
The way this course was taught was great and I was genuinely excited to go to that class the most.

Its an easy course if you pay attention to the slides, read the textbook, and do the homework. It has a lot of different deadlines for different assignments, so it would be helpful to plan those dates ahead before you start the course so you don't miss any which will damage your grade. Take care of your lab requirement / research paper as soon as possible and don't try to cram it at the end of the semester.

This class was very interesting and Professor Gardner is an amazing professor who is very passionate about the material. Our grade was comprised of 80% exams which included 3 non–cumulative all multiple–choice midterms with the last one being before thanksgiving (no final!) and the other 20% of our grade was weekly online discussion posts and mini quizzes. There were also 4 opportunities for extra credit that I would recommend doing as they aren't very hard. Overall this class was a great introductory course to psychology and I would recommend taking it even if psych isn't the field you want to go into!
The course has a lot of reading though the lectures were pretty fun.

I really enjoyed the class and I think psychology is so important for everyone to take. The exams can be tricky, and test the slides and lectures thoroughly, as well as information from the textbook. Professor Gardner was a great lecturer, and I was engaged every day of class. I want to take more psychology classes now.
Wendi Gardner is an excellent professor. Her lectures are engaging and fun. I would definitely recommend her if you want to take this class.

The course was really good in making me interested in psychology and the behavior of people and the extra credit papers really helped me learn and consolidate my knowledge on the topics. The course really demonstrated the importance of applying what you have learned and not only memorizing.

If you are interested in the class, I would 10000 PERCENT RECOMMEND IT! Professor Gardner is a fantastic lecturer who keeps the class engaged. The structure of the class is very reasonable and designed to help you learn. I never was overwhelmed and felt office hours and reaching out for questions was very reasonable. There are 3 multiple–choice tests all weighted the same and she also offers extra credit opportunities –– grade–wise it feels like it averages out. Also, this is a solid class to make friends in and study with. Take this class to learn about yourself and the world!!!

source_file: Student Report for Simon Freedman (GEN_ENG_205-1_20  Engineering Analysis I)_9d46186c-9503-4d88-8840-eb257cd9fad4en-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: This course assumed a lot of coding knowledge that I didn't necessarily have. This made the coding homework assignments seem almost impossible. The linear algebra concepts and homeworks were much easier than the coding parts and doable with enough YouTube and the textbook (the textbooks were very helpful and I would definitely recommend them).

The class was just the professor rehashing the textbook, so I stopped going. Honestly, a badly designed course (why teach linear algebra and matlab in one quarter?) The TA ran things pretty much, but he didn't really help with much of anything and generally was way too harsh. Literally only take this course if you have to.
I think this class is a little challenging after the first midterm (during linear algebra). However, I think there are a lot resources to prepare for exams.

It is very hard. There was more homework with Freedman than people who took it with Ilya remembered doing. Also, much of the homework was very time consuming and difficult to complete with the resources we were given in class.

EA is one of those classes that you just have to get over it, however, I did not think EA 1 was that bad. Since this is the trailer sequence, class sizes are smaller and there is more individualized attention which is really nice. Freedman is a good professor; he was also there to help and answer any questions.

I liked linear algebra and MATLAB. As someone who took multivariable calculus before this class, I had already learned some of these things through a different lens so I have the feeling that it was easier for me than for others. Simon could improve as a teacher in many ways. He knew us all by name and it was clear that we has trying to teach a good class, but sometimes it felt like he thought we were dumb when explaining some things. For example, once I asked him about a linear algebra problem that touched on derivatives and he asked in a rude way if I had taken calculus before. He also does not prepare well for class since he gets confused when he is explaining something, which makes us even more confused.

While the trailer sequence is easier than the normal EA sequence, the quality of instruction and amount of help you receive is lower. Office hours were extremely unhelpful and disorganized, and the TA was sometimes rude. I often had to resort to asking my friends who took EA in the previous quarter for help.
I had a Linear Algebra background going into the course which really helped. The matlab was more challenging.
Concepts that seem confusing at first will become clear with practice and study, don't panic. Ask professor if need help.

everyone says trailer ea is easier but this professor proved that statement wrong. simon freedmans lectures were very confusing and he started the course assuming you already know the matlab basics. generally unorganized class. TA was completely useless

This class is hard. You will have to go to office hours and be sure to make sure you understand the coursework. I joined a PGSG and it really helped better my understanding but if you go to every office hours, it will help so much.
I retook this after barely passing with Randy Freeman and can confidently say Simon Freedman is 10000% better. Learned a lot from Ilya’s videos too when I got stuck
Assessments were fair.
professors voice was pretty monotone so it was hard to focus, in addition, I definitely feel like he can improve his teaching style holistically.

Lectures were good. I think the content is really good. You learn a lot of useful things like linear algebra and basics of programming. However, if it is your first time programming you can struggle a little. I think the course should contain a better intro to programming language. Despite professor Friedman's lectures were good, I ended up using online resources, which were much more straightforward and easy to understand.

This class made the content a lot harder and more confusing. It overcomplicated things when the concepts were not as hard or challenging. The tests were also harder than practice test and the questions on tests could be very confusing. The code for the homework were also long, confusing and challenging.

This course was so hard for no reason. I was not taught how to use matlab I was just expected to already have an entire foundation in computer science, which I did not. I struggled through every assignment and had to ask for help wherever I could find it.

5/7

source_file: Student Report for Alvin Bayliss (GEN_ENG_205-4_21  Engineering Analysis IV)_c6e4dd0d-b51b-4787-a7b1-21300a80821aen-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: Good
Alvin is such a sweet & kind old man but has to be the worst lecturer I've ever had

This course is taught well. Although Prof. Bayliss doesn't record lecture, his notes are posted on Canvas, and his lectures follow a very logical flow that is easier to understand than the textbook. The TAs (Noah and Elisheva) are SOOOO helpful and amazing at explaining concepts. The labs can be tedious, but as long as you're working with a big group, they're manageable.

Awful experience. Bayliss is not very understanding when discussing about regrades, the lectures are structured in a disorganized way. Some homeworks and labs required a disproportional amount of work (like more than 15 hours). The class felt very formulaic, like just following a set of formulas to solve certain types of problems. I really enjoyed learning about differential equations in the past, but this class removed all of my excitement from the topic from how bad it was. Felt like a lot of busy work, not intelectually challenging at all, and Bayliss is not engaging.
Topics such as resonance and van der pol oscillators are fundamental topics for electrical engineering students. (Those were important for me)

Bayliss is an amazing professor, explains really well, answers any questions and is very kind and accomodating. It is hard to hear him sometimes if you sit in the back though. Labs can be time consuming at times so start early.

Class was very difficult but Prof Bayliss was a great teacher (maybe even the best I have had at Northwestern). He answered any and all questions in class, explained things extremely well, and gave help on the labs. He is crazy smart. He would give review sessions before every exam, going over all of the material on the exams which was very helpful. He wants you to succeed and that is reflected in his lectures and exams. There are 3 exams which were very fair and 4 labs. The labs were very very hard and time– consuming but you get to work in groups of 5 max. I recommend having 5 people so it is less work each and going to office hours. The TAs were so so helpful and saved us. Start the labs early because they always take longer than you think. This was the only EA I trailered and I recommend it! From what I have heard it was better then in the fall.

This class didn't have the most difficult content, it was just a ton of work with the labs and homeworks. The labs were incredibly long and tedious and were incredibly dependent on your group. My group was a mess, so labs were a big source of stress, but if you manage to get everything done in the lab, you'll be fine–– everyone got at least a 90 in the labs. Just make sure to go to class where bayliss/the ta's would walk through the lab. Exams weren't too bad–– there were 3 with no final. The first exam was of average difficulty and the second two were quite easy. Bayliss is sometimes hard to understand in lecture and his notes on the board aren't great, but overall he did a good job explaining. The textbook is also pretty good.

EA4 is not as bad as everyone says –– mostly an extension of math concepts and is doable with all of the resources. Textbook is great unlike the other EA classes, so read that if you get confused. Exams were tricky and only 25% get As in Bayliss's class. TAs host review sessions before exams. Worst part of the class is the 4 labs – very time consuming.

I feel like this course isn't as bad as people make it out to be, but I definitely recommend joining a PGSG for this class. Sometimes Bayliss doesn't fully explain a topic, but reading the textbook will usually help. I feel like this class is definitely a good amount of work, but going to office hours was so helpful.

EA4 was fun for me. Just a lot of math and problem solving. Professor Bayliss is extremely good at answering questions, very passionate, kind, and smart. He makes you feel like an engineer because he always puts things into context. You can tell he cares about the class and is passionate about teaching. He has some goofy mannerisms which make class a little silly sometimes.
It's a course we all have to take... it's tough. Sit in the first two rows so you can see Prof Bayliss write on the board.
This course is pretty intense and time consuming, but you learn a lot

This is one of the worst classes for engineering majors. The labs take forever to complete despite being in groups of 5 people and prof. Bayliss makes the exams and quizzes extremely tough and lengthy. The only good part were the TAs Elisheva and Noah who were really helpful.

I like this class better than some of the other EA classes and think that the material was presented in a clear and organized way. The projects would sometimes take awhile, but it depends on your group, and the TAs were very helpful in office hours.
It's not that bad if you get a good lab group.
By far the most time consuming EA, but also my favorite one! Make sure to join a PGSG
Absolutely helped. I actually learned new things

This course was pretty challenging especially due to the matlab projects every other week! It really forces you to seek knowledge independently outside the classroom to succeed. However, you do learn valuable problem solving skills as an engineer and you finally see the direct correlation between physics, math and programming to engineering applications. Very vital course for mccormick students!

Contrary to most, I actually loved this course. I think a big part of me liking it was the fact that I was in PGSG (STRONGLY RECOMMEND), and I knew what was going on in class all the time. The content itself is actually pretty simple, there is just a lot to do. The homework is usually not too bad and graded for completion. The weekly quizzes are also similar to the homework problems and super doable. I took trailer, so that may be why the exams also seemed to be relatively easy. Although the class averages on the first two exams were like 59 and 74, I was always able to do at least 20% above that because of PGSG. The labs are long so I recommend starting them early and going to see the TAs. Once you solve the math and get the code running, it's not bad. Make sure you have a good group that likes to work ahead, otherwise have fun doing it yourself lol. People will make this course sound like hell, but if you're in engineering, I am sure there have already been worse classes that you had to take–– this is one is not that bad, and actually pretty interesting.

The tailer EA4 with Professor Bayliss was descent – definitely a lot easier than the regular sequence with him. The class consists of very tedious homework that are only two points, and then a quiz to test on your knowledge of the homework. There are four labs that take a descent amount of time but the TAs were very helpful with that. There are three Exams worth 50% of the grade which is a lot more fair compared to the other EAs; I think Exam 1 was the most difficult but none of them were bad at all. I'd say I enjoyed this class more so than the other EA classes. Take the trailer though, everything is easier but it's still a challenging class – just more manageable.

Professor Bayliss is the best part of this course. He is a strong lecturer and incredibly kind in answering questions both during and after class; you can tell he is very invested in helping every student understand differential equations.

Still, jamming so much content into a short quarter makes this course super challenging. The homework assignments are very long, and the lab reports take a good deal of time and effort –– make sure you build a group that is strong in both coding (MATLAB) and math, and go to TA lab office hours to check your progress! All of this really does help you build a thorough understanding of differential equations, though.

TAKE THIS CLASS IN TRAILER!!!

The trailer sequence is the best thing ever. Bayliss is actually a really good teacher and wants to explain things really well and is really nice. The actual course content is not really bad at all (if you didn't mind a more math focused class like ea1 you might like this too). Honestly probably my favorite EA so far. The labs are kind of extensive and annoying, but the rest of the class is pretty chill.

The killer with EA4 is the curve but trailer helps out a lot with that. Overall not as bad as I thought it was going to be at all.

I love professor bayliss. He is old but such a good teacher and really cares for his students. He will give extensions in labs and exams if you need them so I recommend. Honestly he was the only reason I actually learned and understood EA 4 content.
Holy crud this class.

The grading is pretty interesting. 50% of the grade is 3 midterms, 35% 4 labs, and15% 5 homework completion and quizzes.

Definitely the hardest class I’ve taken so far. I enjoyed learning about the content though. I left EA3 knowing how to apply some math to very specific situations not at all understanding what any of that math meant or why I was doing it. EA4 makes you actually learn and understand that math. Bayliss is a fantastic lecturer that explains stuff pretty intuitively. He’s also very responsive to emails. He has a reputation for being this brutal unyielding professor, but if you have legitimate complications going on in your life, he’s pretty accommodating if you reach out to him. The TAs were also lovely. Shoutout to Elisheva and Noah carrying my lab and exam grades with their in lecture reviews and office hours. They were absolutely so kind and made this class a lot more bearable.

This class’s reputation comes from the workload; it is BRUTAL. Bayliss expects a lot out of his students, and I personally think it’s too much for engineering sophomores. The content is difficult, and some weeks you have a homework + quiz, a lab, and a midterm all in the same week. The midterms are difficult, the homework takes a long time, and the labs are just a lot. There is no such thing as coasting in EA4; you have to work to get above a D. (Historically I’ve been told Bayliss is not a fan of curving, and he actually curved down in Fall 2023 because one of the midterm averages was abnormally high.) I know a couple people who switched to industrial engineering so they could skip/drop this class.

My number one advice for going into this class is FIND A BALANCED LAB GROUP EARLY. Your group will make or break your experience. I had a good group, and I still had to put 18+ hours in the last lab due to unexpected problems. These labs take a lot of effort, and you want to make sure you have people who can work through difficult math, code in MATLAB, write well, and format lab reports. A lot of the time I had to put into this class was completely these labs.
loved learning computational analysis and finally real world applications . Bayliss is so so sweet

Coming out of EA 3 with the absolutely worst experience and understanding of differential equations, I was worried for EA 4, but Bayliss made all the concepts VERY straightforward and clear. He told you exactly what you need to know for exams, and all the examples he did in–class and assigned for homework were similar to what you would see on exams. There are weekly (ish) homeworks accompanies with a short quiz, 4 labs involving MATLAB that you work on and write a report about with a group of up to 4 other people you can choose (MAKE SURE YOU HAVE PEOPLE WHO ARE GOOD AT MATLAB IN YOUR GROUP), and 3 midterms (no final!!). I HIGHLY RECOMMEND DOING PGSG– it literally saved my grade in this class.
It challenged me but in a good way. He is a great proffesor and truly cared about his students and answered all our questions.
I mean... its EA so obviously the class isn't great but the trailer is so much better – the labs are the worst part and exams are very hard but it honestly isn't worse than EA1
The professor is super nice and always open to questions during class. I think he explains concepts very clearly, but I really wished his lectures were recorded.
You have to put the work in but Bayliss is a very great professor. Do not be afraid to ask questions – he doesn't mind them at all and does his best to try to clarify/communicate his ideas.
Challenges you intellectually.
It is very difficult, you must be careful not to fall behind.

The labs are a huge pain but interesting if you can get into it. A lot of coding. The exams and homework assignments were fair. The instruction takes a little to get used to, as a lot of the integral infromation is spoken in passing and you must catch it the first time.

I am glad I did trailer, although I thought the class was nearly going to fill up before I could register which is always the big risk. Just like the fall, three50 minute exams adding to 50 % of ur grade + 5 problem sets every other week that come with a quiz (15% of grade). Then you have the 4 grueling MATLAB projects throughout the quarter (35% of grade). The only reason this class is brutal is because of the curve. Apparently the bottom 50–55% get a C. It's crazy. Other than that, it probably isn't that much harder than other courses.

Definitely recommend taking trailer if you can. Think the curve was a lot more forgiving and exams weren’t too bad. They're a little harsh with the grading and the no partial credit stuff, so try to argue for points back if you got them. Bayliss was a great professor and lecturer, he was very straightforward and taught us everything you needed to know for the exams. Labs are pretty hard and time consuming– recommend starting early and going to TA office hours before they get crowded the day or two before the lab is due.

Very challenging course. The labs are pretty heinous –– get started on them a week in advance. Rest of the course is pretty straight forward. Do the hw understand the material you can do well on the tests.

This class is hard but it is a requirement. The lectures are innately painful, but there are a lot of resources to help you. The TAs host OH a lot for hw for lab. The labs are quite painful but if you get help from the TAs then your life becomes a lot easier. The homeworks are long but it is just based off completion and they are helpful for the weekly quizzes. Just learn the material well enough to get through the class and you'll be ok. Although Bayliss says that only 1/4 of the class gets an A, 1/3 gets a B, and 1/3 gets a C. idk where he is coming up with these numbers but that feels kinda harsh and a lot of students end up getting curved down.
n/a

I thought this course was challenging but also interesting in real world applications. The problem with this class is the instruction. Professor Bayliss is a lovely man and I think he is very smart and knowledgeable. Unfortunately, he is getting older and it is difficult to understand and hear him in class. For him, the information he teaches is automatic. He basically has each lecture memorized from start to finish: what he is going to say, write on the board, and examples he will provide. Unfortunately, this means it is sometimes difficult to follow his line of reason (as we are not math experts), especially when it is difficult to hear him.
It was very challenging at first but going to the TA's office hours really helped, I highly suggest signing up for a PGSG!

In my personal opinion, this is the best out of all the EA's. Prof Bayliss is so kind and patient, making sure to always stop and answer our questions, no matter how simple they may seem. He lectures in a clear, straightforward and concise manner at a pace that is easily followed. He truly is a sweetheart and is doing what he can to ensure our success. If you attend lectures, do the homework, and the review problems, there's no doubt you'll be sufficiently prepared for the exams. The labs are a bit heavy, especially in the midst of exam season, but if you divide the tasks up well enough, you should finish by the deadline with only a bit of struggle. At this point in the EA sequence, everyone is rooting for you to do well and finish. This has been my favorite math class I've taken yet and I hope Prof. Bayliss knows the positive impact he is making on us engineering students.
EA–4 isn't horribly hard but you need to go to class or else you won't succeed. Reading the textbook isn't enough.

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: Because of the structure of the class, you can't expect to just succeed by paying attention in lecture and doing the homework. It is a very conceptual class, and you will find questions very difficult if you don't understand all aspects of the course. The professor was very nice, and she answered every question that was asked; however, it did feel like the questions being asked took up teaching time, and I felt my time was wasted a lot. Otherwise, the professor was great at explaining the concepts and breaking them down for everyone.

Class is fine, exams are hard. It starts really slow so take that time to make sure you really understand the fundamentals because everything builds on itself. The last month of the course will make you wish were a communications major but you'll get through it. Honestly, a lot of people struggle in this course, but if you practice the recitation worksheets and actually understand how to solve the problems you will do well on the quizzes and exams. Don't take for granted what you know, and join a peer–guided study group if you think chemistry will be difficult for you.

This course is genuinely awful, but that is not Professor Nemr's fault. Chem 110 is 100% a weed–out course, even though it is an introduction course. I came in with knowledge of chemistry from Sophomore Year of HS, and we essentially learned everything I learned in that class, and parts of AP Chem in 10 weeks. The frustrating part of this course though,h was that we essentially spent the first 2 weeks doing nothing besides "study tactics" and basic math. Then it just abruptly became knowledge dumping every single lesson, and then the quizzes and exams had us apply the concepts in ways that we truly had not been taught to think about. The expectation of the course is to teach you to fully understand the concepts and be able to apply them to any situation, but there just is not enough time to learn everything they want you to know and be able to understand it to the extent that is expected of you. You will spend hours studying for every quiz and test and get mediocre grades on them, and it gets to a point where you can study as much as you want and still get nothing out of it. I attended office hours almost every week, joined an PGSG, and went to tutoring and its never going to be enough. The lectures just dump a bunch of new information every single time, and then are often not connected to previous topics in class.
