The issue with this is that they would connect it to previous topics on exams and it just made no sense. The class is terrible and very very hard to earn an A in.

This is definitely a challenging class, especially considering it's an entry–level chemistry course. Professor Nemr is excellent, and I would highly recommend her section. Don't get too caught up in the numbers/grades; remember that it is just as challenging for everybody else, too. Really focus on participating in lectures and understanding them rather than taking notes; all the slides are posted online, so it's not super helpful for you to copy down everything you see on the board word for word. Make an effort to show up to every class, as it moves pretty fast. Do not be deceived by the first week of easy content; it ramps up quickly after that.
It was very hard and I was upset that the last chapter, the one that felt hardest to me, was rushed through so fast even though it contained the most content

Professor. Nemr is a great lecture and very kind. She is always willing to make time for he students outside of office hours, lecture, exams. She comes up with great ways to learn the course material, and cares of students well being. She realizes the course material is difficult and doesn't make it easier but guides students to learn on their own with a little help

This is a super hard course. It is a weed out for sure. Lock in first week. After each lecture do at least 5 practice problems. You can get through this course! Talk to TAs – most helpful, go to office hours, and there is a list of chem tutors if anything. Prof. Nemr wants you to succeed. If you have little to no chem experience it hits you HARD as this is your first stem college class. Lock in first week, DO PRAC. PROBLEMS EVERYDAY, go to office hours, and you can get through this! Your study skills will change a lot. Be patient with yourself.

honestly i knew it would be challenging taking chem in college but the way it was taught made it even more difficult. lectures went by quick and weren’t really explained as good as they could have been. i would go back to the slides myself to review before quizzes and exams. the recitations were very helpful in getting more practice in with new content.

This was a course of all time. As much as I think that the experiences students will have in this course are instructor dependent, the exams are all the same, and they are not easy. The Exam Averages will be exam averages of all time, and unless you walked into this class with a 5 in AP chemistry or some equivalent, expect to spend a lot of time studying if you want to see good results. That being said, good luck :)

This is a very hard course and to do well you really have to do all the practice questions given in the textbook. Lectures go through material very fast and you're expected to do most of your learning outside of class. I would recommend to buy the textbook they give you and do every problem you're given because they copy their lectures directly from it.
Do as many practice problems as possible if you want to be successful in this course, go to office hours, and study the material before you even start learning it.

For all students new or familiar with chemistry, this is an expansive course that covers the foundational mechanics of chemistry. This course is most likely required for the majority of students that take it, so be sure to use all resources available to you for help even if you don't think you need it. These resources include drop in tutoring, office hours, etc. If you prefer to have a more upbeat and lively prof be sure to take it with Prof Nemr. Shes the goat but both Northrup and Nemr are good.

This course genuinely made me reconsider my major. Very grateful for learning how much I dislike this subject and looking forward to not continuing the sequence next quarter! However, professor Nemr was amazing and still kept lectures engaging. It was overwhelming at times but her support made the course feel survivable. You can tell how passionate she is; she’s also always willing to answer questions and is very understanding! MSP, PGSG, and chem tutor hours were also helpful.

I thought this course was well taught and well structured, Prof Nemr was an excellent lecturer and incredibly personable. The exams were extremely difficult and could be discouraging, but it is a curved class so its unclear quite what success will look like. The provided practice problems through homework and recitation were great resources.
Chem 110 will always be my opposition, but Prof Nemr is one of the best profs to teach chem!!
I thought the exams could be difficult but nothing crazy and they were always fair, just tough timing.
I think it was good overall. There was a lot we needed to know and at times it was hard to keep up. It’s important to keep going to office hours and engage in recitation

This chemistry course is amazing. Coming into the class, I was not sure of what to expect as previous reviewers did not have similar experiences. Coming in having taken a strong chemistry class in high school was extremely helpful as there were a good amount of review topics that were tested differently. There are definitely some more challenging units, but once you get the hang of the way to best study and prepare for quizzes and exams it definitely gets easier. If you are able to, TAKE THIS COURSE WITH PROF NEMER. She is a fantastic lecturer and really engages the class to best teach the chemistry topics. Her office hours are also great if you are struggling so I really recommend going if you can. Paying attention and going to class are the key to success in this class for sure. When starting this course, you might be scared away or think that it is easy in the beginning, but do not get to comfortable as the pace is fast and you will always be learning new material. The first midterm was daunting but it was definitely a learning experience and the reflections better prepare you to approach the second midterm and the final. Overall, I had a positive experience taking Chem 110.

Chem 110 is a very, very, VERY hard class. If you can avoid it protect your peace and do NOT take this class. As a sophomore, I already knew this was one of the hardest classes here and it definitely lived up to that. If you can definitely take it with Nemr, I liked her she seemed nice. I went to Northrop’s office hours once and to be honest he wasn’t that helpful to me (he likes to ask you questions to try to get you to figure it out yourself but I was totally lost). This is an extremely fast paced class (we learned thermochem in a week) that is heavily application based and you have to know how to navigate through these very long word problems. There is a quiz every week (except for midterm weeks) and they drop the two lowest quizzes, although the first quiz is to “see where you’re at” so if you have no prior experience like me one of the dropped quizzes is automatically gone. The weekly homeworks were pretty evil and a lot of them covered content we learn at the end of the week, so you’re pretty much out of luck if you need help (you can go to the peer tutoring, but when I went they literally just used chatgpt because it’s so difficult). Unfortunately this class will literally have you eating, sleeping, and dreaming chem just for the average on the midterm/quizzes to be in the 40–50s. It is kind of cool to learn about chemistry too I guess.

Professor Nemr is always animated during her lectures. She's patient with questions and encouraging of her students. That being said, this class is definitely a weeder; the test averages were crazy low. My advice would be to keep pushing through, though. I'm expecting 131 to be difficult, but not a weeder based on those ctecs
I wish this class focused more on our learning than the grades. Professor's like to make it seem like they are not but in reality they really have things set that would greatly affect your grade.

If you are reading this you likely have to take chemistry regardless. This is a course that requires a lot of effort in order to succeed. I would recommend taking time to review all bailable practice problems and gain a proficient understanding of the course content. The first midterm was not that bad, and the second midterm was a bit harder. The final was most definitely the hardest. Professor Nemr is defiantly the teacher to take this course with. She does a great job covering the important material in her lectures.
This course was very challenging, you need to study or you will be behind. Go to office hours and ask questions and do lots of practice problems.
This course was very difficult and fast–paced. You need to make sure you are actively practicing and doing questions from the book.

First, when I read CTEC’s about Nemr, I saw that she was a good instructor but was condescending at times when responding to questions. I agree with these reviews, although sometimes I didn’t blame her. Oftentimes students would ask questions before Nemr got to the main points of her lecture, and she ended up falling behind the other professor, Northrup, so she oftentimes had to play “catch–up”. With that being said, there were many times where people asked genuine questions and she gave an unfriendly response, and I couldn’t tell if she felt annoyed or if that’s just how she responds to questions. Second, the class. The major criticism I have of this class is that we didn’t learn as much chemistry as we should have. In other words, a lot of the class is memorization because they did not want to teach use the chemical reasoning behind certain topics. The discussion section (which is just doing practice problems for two hours) was helpful, especially since I found a good group to complete problems with. The homeworks took a decent amount of time because they aimed to challenge you, which I appreciated. Overall, if you want a good grade, you’re going to have to spend a good chunk of time outside of class understanding the concepts. In my experience, the midterm 2 and the final will be the most important parts of the course, and for all assessments, expect them to contain questions that are super conceptual.

This course is difficult, but if you're taking this course it is likely it's a major requirement. The content is confusing and it becomes really easy to get lost fast. This class is almost entirely math. My recommendation is to go to Nemr's office hours and your TA's office hours. Personally, I found Nemr to be engaging and helpful, and I would highly recommend taking this course with her if you are able to. Quizzes, midterms, and finals are all very difficult. The practice tests they give out are not good measures of how difficult the actual test would be. Almost all of the content builds on each other, so definitely attend all required classes and recitations if you want a decent grade in this class. Even then, you need to dedicate a decent amount of your own time to be able to understand and solve the types of problems they give you.
Even though only part of the hw is required, be sure to do all of it; it's good practice. Not only that, but stay on top of the content because it builds.
This course was challenging. My hardest class on the pre–med track. The hardest part was the problem–solving aspect.

I had taken chemistry at the very beginning of high school so I was very nervous to take CHEM –110 , but I will forever be grateful to Prof. Nemr for making the content easily digestible and fun to understand.

I believe it is a difficult course if you struggle to learn how to apply formulas. I feel like you need a base level/good understanding of math concepts to do well in this class. You need to learn how to decipher problems.

DO NOT GET ME STARTED ON THIS CLASS A.K.A. RAGEBAIT 101. This class is like drinking water from a fire hydrant. I do not say this to scare you but to prepare you. This is the hardest class I have ever taken in my whole entire life. The subject matter wasn't even that hard. I took AP Chem in high school, so I had a decent foundation of the concepts. This class doesn't even test you on what you learned, or what the instructors say the test is based on; it tests you beyond that. The exams will be anxiety–inducing, and you will most likely not do well on them, but do not fret. You will still pass. This course is the pinnacle of weed–out courses. You just gotta tough it out and not let it steal your whimsy. Without further ado, here is my advice to survive this course: 1. GO TO OFFICE HOURS: I didn't start going until after the first midterm, and I wish I started going earlier. They are actually helpful and will help you get a better understanding of the course content. Even if you don't have any questions, other people will, and you can work together on problems. 2. BECOME BESTIES WITH YOUR TA: My TA was an actual angel (Big shout out to Mia). They make the quizzes and know what you are gonna be tested on, so please get to know them. They also have office hours, which are better than the professor's office hours because you get more personalized help. 3. FOCUS ON TEXTBOOK PROBLEMS FOR PRACTICE: Textbook problems are what quizzes, homework, and recitation packets are based on. Understanding them will give you a leg up in the class. Also, with this being said, you don't have to read the textbook. Even my Professor reccommended not reading the textbook. Watch the Organic Chemistry tutor or Professor Dave Explains or some other Chem goat, and you should be good. 4. DO NOT, I REPEAT, DO NOT NEGLECT YOUR SOCIAL AND EMOTIONAL HEALTH FOR THIS CLASS: You would think this advice is obvious, but the hustle culture of this school will make you feel guilty for not studying during every waking hour. I fed into this and cut off my friends, family, hobbies, sleep, and laundry to do well in this class. That plan definitely did not work. It probably made me perform worse because I was running on fumes. PLEASE make sure you do something nice for yourself every day. Even if you don't do well as you hoped, you will still have your whimsy left to hold onto.

I think it's really important to practice free response question in preparation for midterms and finals. Regardless of professor go to office hours because other students there will help you when it gets too packed.
Everything in this course builds on each other. Make sure to understand the foundations early on.

Prof Nemr is a really good teacher and always willing to answer any question, I would highly suggest going to office hours if you can (especially if you don't understand the concepts since this class is so fast paced). The class is composed of weekly graded homeworks, 2 midterms and a final (ATTENDANCE FOR CLASS IS MANDATORY). The midterms and the final were especially hard. I would not recommend taking this class as a distro but if you have to take it I would take it with Nemr.

This was a course that challenged my ability to analyze and solve intricate problems. The concepts themselves are easy, but learning to put a bunch of concepts together to solve a problem took time to get used to.

I feel like the pace was very inconsistent with this course. The first half felt very slow and I believe we spent too much time on the beginning topics. After the first midterm, the pace picked up almost immediately and I often felt behind and confused about the material since we would go over it so quickly. 2 hour recitations were very long and hard to get through.

This course is definitely challenging. Coming into it, I had never done AP Chemistry so my previous knowledge was very limited and much of the content was new. I would definitely choose Professor Nemr, because her explanations in lecture are very clear and she annotates all of her slides. Also from my friends who had the other professor he was not nearly as clear when going through slides. The quizzes are hard but doable if you do the homework/recitations and put in effort. The most challenging exam was by far the final. It synthesized concepts from the entire quarter into single questions and not a single person I talked to completed the full exam. The largest problem with this course is the practice material rarely represents what a real exam problem would look like; the questions in the practice exams were significantly easier than anything that was on any midterm or final. If you are taking this course, make sure to do as much practice material as possible and make sure you understand each concept rather than memorizing how to solve questions. Also the class meets 5x a week so it moves very quickly.

This course is HARD. You will be spending hours pouring over concepts you don't understand, and even when you think you do, you will still get less than 50% on quizzes. However, I learned so much and Nemr is a good professor. She is a lively lecturer which helps keep you engaged in class. I will say that the free response on quizzes/midterms/finals is SO much harder than any practice the teachers give you, which is really frustrating and definitely my least favorite part of the course. Nothing can really prepare you for the free response they ask you, but doing a lot of different practice problems is probably the best way to prepare because you will have to apply concepts you learned in ways you never have before. But all in all it is a weeder class, and it is worthwhile to try your best and not let the class completely take you out.
This course consisted of general chemistry content, and the lectures were informative. However, the quizzes and exams are tricky with their word problems.

The exams were a great deal harder than the material on the quizzes, homework, or recitations. Because of this, you have to understand the content to a very great extent in order to do well. If you can apply a concept but only to simple/straightforward questions, chances are you will not do well on the exam/quiz.

So I came in expecting this to be terrible because of other people's CTECs, but it really wasn't that bad. Definitely take it with Nemr if you can, the 9am start time is kinda brutal but worth it to take it with her because it makes a huge difference imo. Midterm 1 was fine, midterm 2 was evil, final exam was evil, but the final has mostly content from the 2nd half of the course which I wish I would have known. There is just a lot of content in a short amount of time that you have to learn and memorize so it can be overwhelming.

If you are going to take chemistry 110 you have to take it with professor Nemer. She was extremely kind and always open to answer any questions. Additionally, she truly wants you to succeed and always puts in the effort to help you do so.

Going into this course, I definitely was intimidated based on what I heard, but I think as long as you go in expecting it to be difficult, you can handle it a lot better. I would recommend finding a TA or one of the professors that you find helpful, and go to their office hours whenever you have a point of confusion. I found having those one–on–one conversations were super helpful when dealing with some confusing points in the class. I would ABSOLUTELY recommend taking this course with Professor Nemr. She tried to make class fun everyday, clearly cared about our success, and you could just tell the amount of effort she was putting in to ensure that we had the best experience possible.

At times the exams will be more a measure of your ability to read and dissect their word puzzles they throw at you rather than a test of your knowledge on the content. This can be very frustrating along with very low averages of midterms and quizzes. The practice exam problems are very helpful in preparing for exams, and attending office hours after recitations proved to be a very effective way to clear misconceptions. Overall this course is very fast paced with quizzes every week, midterms every two weeks, classes everyday, 2 hour recitations every week, and homework every week. I think one time we covered chapter 3 chapter 4, and moved onto chapter 5 in one week. Just keep pushing and studying, make time for yourself, and it will be over in no time.
I learned a lot from this course and I liked my professor, but the course overall was challenging and very time–consuming. And the exams were extremely difficult.
Overall this class was hard. It was very tedious and required a lot of outside class time. I would recommend Prof Nemr. She is very kind and teaches the material so well.

This class is hard. The speed is fast and there is just so much to remember and learn. You have to do a lot of outside studying and plenty of practice problems to get a hang of the materials. The exams are always harder than the practice exams.

Chem 110 is a great course to get the basics of chem down for anyone with little to no chem experience (like myself). Prof Nemr is a great lecturer who really tries to make the material understandable, and also genuinely wants to see you succeed. People like to doompost this class quite a bit, but I found it was very manageable so long as your recall ability was strong. Oh yeah, the single most important thing about 110 is that YOU MUST BE ABLE TO MEMORIZE CONTENT!!!!!!!!! The profs will say it's not memorization based, but I couldn't disagree more. If you go into this thinking you can squeeze out an A without having to nail these concepts into your brain, YOU WILL DO POORLY IN THIS CLASS. That being said, the memorization you will do is light (at least for me); knowing polyatomic ions and rules of reaction by heart will get you so far, especially considering how much the content builds on itself. Aside from that, do as much practice as you can. This is a math–centric class, so more practice problems will only help you on the exams! Your discussion section is also great for working out any struggles with content, and take advantage of office hours. I had schedule conflicts with Nemr's but the general TA office hours in the basement were very helpful. Overall, I think this class is great at what it's meant to do, it just requires a bit of effort to see yourself succeed.

God, this course. I shall keep my more colorful words to myself. This class hands down is the most grotesquely difficult class I have ever taken in my life. This is the only time in my life when I have been in a class where I felt like studying didn't help me all too much. The volume of information you have to learn is immense, and the speed at which you have to learn it is more obscene. Quizzes are weekly. Which is fine, I guess, but there are only 4 questions, so god forbid you don't know the content you just finished learning 2 days before to the maximum. This class also likes throwing lots of "study material" at you, which I believe is the most bad faith part of this course. Honest to God, he may strike me down if I am lying. I have taken advantage of every question bank/study source provided to me, and yet what we are tested on during the midterms and the final are so starkly different it feels worthless. Personally, the professor I had was amazing, she did the absolute best she could with this beast of a class. If this is supposed to be the chemistry class for people with "no experience," then I doubt it's going to leave an even remotely satisfying taste in their mouth when they finish.

This class is the hardest I have ever had to take in my life. In summary do, ALL of the practice problems in the textbook, a lot of the practice problems they give you to do stem from this and the textbook is a good source to gather understanding that are not intuitive in lectures.

I thought the class was really good for students who haven't taken AP chem. If you have taken AP chem, this course will be a waste of your time; however, if you haven't, this course is amazing and slowly introduces you to chem.

I think this course had what I mostly expected from it. Nothing was extremely confusing to understand; it just takes more time for some things to click than others. My philosophy: the amount of time you put into it is the grade you will get out of it.
This course was very difficult especially if you have no chem background. However, if you dedicate time outside of class you should be fine.

This course is very rigorous, that being said I did learn a lot and my chemistry skill got to a moderately proficient level. The exams are far too hard for our level, with the average being discouragingly low (feels like they try to scare people into dropping). There is a curve at the end, however it was not clear what the curve is and you have no idea what your final grade is going to be.

Overall this course was very okay, it would have been excellent if the grading had more structure especially because it is an intro class.
This course was very challenging yet taught me a lot about chemistry. Exams were very challenging.

Nemr's class is not any different than the other professors, so do yourself a favor and take the 10am or 11am section if possible. READ THE TEXTBOOK. Most of the exam questions and homework questions will be 10x easier if you do the problems in Petrucci's textbook. This is definitely a weed out class, so just make sure you're putting in the right amount of time. The second midterm is much harder than the first so try your hardest to ace your first one as cushioning. Overall, I would say it's not the worst thing in the world and the workload is quite manageable. If you've never gotten a B, it will probably be your first so don't kill yourself trying to obtain perfection.

I was really into polyatomic ions, and when we went over them, it was only for like 10 minutes and we were just told to memorize a few and move on. Same thing with element naming and the elements themselves, it feels like they just will throw out random obscure ones you are 'supposed to know' even though the homework and quizzes only cover like a few of them. Deceitful if im being honest.

source_file: Student Report for Jeremy Keys (GEN_ENG_205-3_21  Engineering Analysis III)_24e74eb3-8b89-4d2e-aa1a-adf360e7346fen-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: Great professor! I felt like the exams were very fair and challenged you to understand the problems well while still being similar enough to what we covered in class.
The class is manageable and organized. Do homework and practice problems and you should be good.

This quarter of EA 3 was a lot more manageable than I had thought it would be. Keys was a great professor for the most part and the homework was a bit difficult but doable if you go to office hours to ask for help. The exams were all super straightforward and Keys was very nice about letting us know how many questions and what exactly was going to be on the exam if we had asked during class. The average for our class was pretty high as well.

Took this trailer after dropping EA3 last spring after the first quiz. Take the trailer! The course is so much better as a trailer and the quizzes are so much easier. Keys is a very good lecturer and I felt like the material this time was a lot easier to understand with him. As well as his quizzes in the Fall compared to the ones he gives in the Spring are definitely easier and fair. He also allows corrects for 30% of lost points which is very helpful.
This class was pretty chill, HIGHLY recommend taking during Fall Quarter. Interesting topics, although some people may not care since it is all circuits and mechanical systems.
Very thoughtful teacher in regard to student interest and participation.

Professor Keys is a wonderful professor who realy cares about his students and their learning. I think that this course did a good job in helping us understand the topics and materials in an easy–to–understand way. The coursework is honestly very manageable and fair, the exams were very fair and to–the–point and reflected what we learned in class very well.

Quite an easy class during the trailer sequence, 3 exams worth 25% each, and then weekly homework for the other 25. Exams were allowed to do corrections for up to 30% of the lost points which is an insane curve. Very relaxed and Keys is a great lecturer.
I highly recommend taking this course in the fall (trailer is much more manageable than the regular class). Keys was also wonderful!

A bit too much percentage on the three exams. 75% exams , 25% hw, 3 exams and 8 homeworks with one drop. Keys does let you earn 33% back on points you missed for each exam which is nice. Overall good course that is very straightforward if you study what the professor suggests.

EA3 has been my favorite engineering class so far, and I think a lot of it has to do with Prof. Keys' teaching. He does tend to go through example problems quickly, but he has recorded lectures which are super helpful to go through again if a concept is confusing. We had quiz corrections in this class which were extremely beneficial. It is still a tough class but was manageable in difficulty.

As a CS major who's not particularly interested in physics, taking the EA3 trailer was one of the best decisions I've made. The content is not necessarily easy, but the exams were significantly easier than the previous quarter's. Professor Keys is really understanding and a great lecturer. He also told us what each question would broadly be, which made studying a lot easier. The homework can be a bit challenging, so definitely work with others on it.
Take the trailer sequence with Keys. Exams were fair and grading was also very lenient. Lectures are recorded
Keys is a great professor, he explains the concepts well and provides great examples in class. The midterms were straightforward and assessed what he taught. Take EA3 with Keys!
Definitely take this with Keys – his lectures are engaging and exams are fair. .
Prof. Keys is a very fun professor to have and is very well organized on his stuff!! Do recommend taking it with him

Jeremy Keys is a wonderful wonderful man. He picked me up from the trenches of the normal sequence of EA and he carried me through trailer. He personally cured my trauma from EA 2. Trailer EA is probably the best decision I ever made as a McCormick Engineering student. Keys allows you to do test revisions where you get 30% of your lost points back if you just redo the problem. The tests were also so much easier than the tests from spring quarter in normal sequence. He also recorded lectures.
I loved EA 3 because it helped me visualize and understand all the physics mechanics and electrical systems around me to a certain degree.

In my opinion, the most enjoyable EA class I've taken so far. I think even if it wasn't trailer that would still be the case because of Professor Keys's instruction and style of the course. The best part about this class is still the test corrections as it boosts your grade but also helps you learn the existing material to apply to the next exam.

Really amazing teaching!! The course was really fair and I think the Professor did a great job explaining the concepts in a digestible way. It is clear he is passionate about teaching and cares about his students!! Will miss this class

Favorite course I took this quarter. Keys is an amazing instructor and this being the only Engineering Analysis class where I attended every lecture is a testament to that. Although the MATLAB homework assignments are annoying they are doable.

Keys is a great professor. This class was so much better since he was the only instructor. Keys organized the material in the class in a way that made way more sense and created exams that really emphasized the big points of the class. Taking this class trailer was a much better experience than taking it in the normal sequence. Keys really knows what he is doing.

Taking trailer EA3 was amazing. Keys was very helpful at explaining topics thoroughly. The tests we the right amount of difficult. Retakes were helpful, and homework's were positively catered to the test.
Keys is a great lecturer. He moves at a steady pace, and his exams mirror the lecture content.
Solid class, very well–structured and clear lecturing style. I like the incorporation of example walk–throughs for each topic, it helps tremendously for learning.
Professor Keys was a good professor, he explained what we were learning in class very well.

To start things off I would like to say that Jeremy Keys is a Hall of Fame professor, His ability to break down complex examples and to understand questions is in his class. I had had some experience with the the content before because I had taken AP Physics C in high school, however, considering I took that class a year ago I couldn't remember anything. However, that was alright because as I said in the first sentence Keys is a Hall of Fame level professor. Keys fostered a welcoming environment so nobody was questioned, furthermore, His slides and notes were great so if I had ever missed a lecture it would be easy for me to catch up. His office hours and final review session were wonderful and his tests were extremely fair in the sense that he would only test you on concepts and problem types that we did in class or in the homework so all in all this class was great all around and I am proud to say that I ended off my EA experience on a high note

Professor Keys is an excellent professor – absolutely take the class with him if you can. He's a great lecturer and always takes time to answer questions (no matter how basic) and makes himself super available to students if they ever need help. I really struggled with EA2 but really liked EA3 because of his teaching style. Super fair tests (at least in the trailer) and gives opportunity for test corrections to gain back 30% of lost points which is very nice. The class is not the worst – go to class/put in some effort and you should be fine.

Its a good class. Learned a lot, well organized, good notes and good slides. The topics weren't exactly difficulty and neither were the exams. It would have been nice to have more challenging conceptual questions in the future.
An excellent class that clearly taught the core EA3 concepts. Prof. Keys can be hard but is a great and understanding professor.
Taught me to be analytical

It's a very straightforward class. Almost identical format to EA2, go to class and discussions(if you need it), do the homework(lowest is dropped), study for the 3 quizzes(no final). Anyone who is an engaged student will get a good grade and learn a decent amount.
W teacher
Take the trailer sequence, it's significantly easier. This section's average was around an A–, despite EA usually being curved to around a 3.1. Keys was also great, and the tests were very fair.

Professor Keys is the GOAT, no other way to say it. Take EA3 with him. He really knows what he is doing and is able to answer any and all questions. Responds to emails quick, very helpful during office hours. 10/10 Prof. If I had Keys for every class, life would be amazing. 3 exams, 8 homeworks. All equally weighted. I actually really enjoyed going to his class.
Keys is the goat. Explains material really well and is willing to provide any help needed.

Keys is good. I think he became more lenient this quarter, but in my opinion he taught the class well. His instructions were clear, and he simplified problems well for us. The course is honestly not that hard, content–wise. It's similar to EA2 in that all the content in the respective class all relates to one another.
Keys is an ok instructor. 50% of the time he does not understand my questions or appear to be confused to why I have these questions. The practice problem during class is helpful though
Professor Keys is really fair and pretty nice. The MATLAB portion of some of the homework assignments were pretty annoying, but I have no complaints otherwise.
Prof. Keys made the content really easy to learn and the homework was not too time consuming. Trailer seemed way easier than the regular course based on what I’ve heard from my peers.

Keys is great! Highly recommend taking EA with him. He genuinely cares about his students and their learning, and his exams were fair. You really have to study, but he's not trying to trick you but rather really make sure you studied and understand the material. He's one of the kindest and best professors I've ever had at NU. I don't know if he has tenure, but if the university cares about the quality of the teaching they would do everything to ensure he doesn't leave.

Professor Keys is a very effective instructor and his lectures will help you learn. Go to them and attend office hours whenever you can. The contents were not rocket science, although the algebra does get ugly at times. The MATLAB codes were honestly unnecessary but you just have to do them. The tests were pretty simple but that also means a simple mistake can set you back in the curve by a lot.

Take EA3 trailer with Professor Keys! He's a great lecturer with fair exams and gives you everything you need to get an A. The class was 3 midterms worth 25% each and 25% homework and exam averages were high 80s and even low 90s. He also offers quiz revisions where you can get back up to a third of your points lost on your exam which is amazing! The class average overall was probably an A– or an A. It's a lot easier than non trailer and taking it with Keys made it even better!
First midterm was okay ish but once we got to levers it started ramping up in difficulty. Professor Keys is the GOAT. Really wants to see you succeed.
This course is challenging but the tests are exactly what you expect them to be, no surprises. As long as you go to class and keep up with the material you will be fine.
It allowed me to understand more about how systems are analyzed and built upon from electrical to mechanical.
It incorporates a simple way of looking at dynamic systems, which I enjoyed learning about.

If you want an A in EA3, I suggest taking it Fall with Professor Keys. He teaches the content well, gives you enough study material, and his office hours are very helpful. He doesn't curve the class down and since he makes the exams slightly easier for the trailer sequence, I want to say over 60% of us got an A (might be overshooting). There is really nothing I can point out that is wrong with this class and for an EA sequence class, this is the best it is going to get.

Keys had a pretty bad reputation last quarter for making his tests crazy intense, but I think he found a really nice medium this quarter! He might cook us on exam 3, but regardless, I think he's definitely found his footing. His lecturing is by far the best out of all the other EA 3 profs, and I love that he is truly passionate about the topics he teaches and makes sure all his students feel supported in the class. I came into this class super scared cause EA 2 was def not my thing, but EA 3 has been super fulfilling! Since he's a relatively new professor, I think he's a bit all over the place with exam difficulty bc he's trying to find the proper vibe of the class, so I can't really tell you how hard or easy the class is gonna be in the spring rip. But, fs attend his lectures instead of any other prof haha.

source_file: Student Report for Dayne Swearer (CHEM_151-0_01  General Chemistry I)_e266c6c9-c663-4e40-b082-cfb1b531f933en-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: It felt very fast at times, but attending lectures and doing the homework definitely helped

The main problem with chem 151 is the glaring disconnect between the professors, TAs, super TAs, and textbook. Everyone seems to have a different idea about which content is important to teach, and which content is even correct, and no seems to care to remedy these contradictions. Don't be surprised if, within the span of a week, you are told three completely different things about a chemical concept and when you go to check the textbook to see which version is right, you somehow find a fourth distinct interpretation of that same concept.

The content of the course was pretty interesting however, the department doesn't do that a great of a job ensuring that concepts are taught clearly. The practice midterms we received were pretty close to what expect however, you have to make sure that you're actively looking for resources to learn the material. Simply, attending lectures isn't enough. The first midterm was awful but the content get slighltly better. The course goes further in depth than AP Chem so some new material is learned however, a lot of it is overcomplicated. The quizzes were extremely difficult compared to past years.

This course was definitely my biggest challenge this quarter. To really understand all of the material would be a monumental task that very few people I know where able to do. I do appreciate that we learn real deal chemistry and some real world applications, it didn't just feel like a teacher telling us how to solve problems that we would see on the test. Overall, its a hard class but didn't feel like a total waste of time. I think prof Swearer needs to improve his lecturing. A lot of his in–class examples didn't make sense, and he often misspoke. He did a lot better when the class was more engaged and asking questions. He is the kind of prof who I think would do well in teaching a class of 15–25 rather than like 100 kids. He needs to approach the lectures more like Bethel, who goes through the steps of solving problems rather than just regurgitating formulas and notions about chemistry and thermodynamics.

Beginning with the lectures, they are poorly paced and organized. Some days you would spend the entire time on like perfect gas law and others you would breeze through all of the orbital stuff with important topics literally being mentioned once in the middle of some tangent. Taking notes in the class felt impossible (as it was moving way too fast) a week into class and I eventually found zero value in going to class or watching the lectures. Essentially the only thing I found useful was reading the textbook. The lectures would actively confuse me too. The textbook, on the other hand, while the ONLY useful part of the course was also poorly utilized. First of all, you're advised to get the 12th generation of Petrucci's textbook while the actual course is based off the 11th generation (which is more accessible ;)) which actually lead to some discordant lecture structuring with some lectures referencing chapters that just didn't exist in the 12th gen version. Further, the book is tedious to read and likes to indulge in providing lots of context that, while interesting, is really not all too helpful. The other lecture materials such as the homework, quizzes, and practice tests are fairly useless. The homework is tedious and ends up being the same "plug into x equation and solve" over and over taking around 6 hours to get through. The quizzes are few and far between in terms of practice and its more of a weed out tool than it is good practice. 10 points total, you mess up a question its a 75%. Its only fortunate that the quizzes, homeworks, and participation are worth nearly nothing in the final grade. The practice tests are way too easy and do not reflect the actual exam whatsoever.
I enjoy chemistry but I didn’t know how to study for the midterms, and I wish we were given more resources like recommended problems from the textbook.

This class was definitely really tough. After taking an advanced chemistry (AP–adjacent) course in high school, most of the content was somewhat familiar to me, but 151 required a much deeper understanding of each topic. I spent hours each weekend working on homework and/or studying, and up to 20 hours per week studying on exam weeks. As someone who generally enjoys chemistry, the content was interesting, but there are still a few topics that I couldn't even begin to grasp. I'm also not a great test taker, so the fact that exams are worth 65% of the overall grade made it tough to be successful (even when I felt like I had a solid understanding of the material we were tested on). I highly recommend working through problem sets and quizzes with classmates.

Was moderately difficult class with sometimes confusing lectures. However, weekly homework was very helpful at learning how to solve problems for the class. Exams required a decent amount of studying. Prof not always the best at explaining things, but did take time to answer all questions in class. Class definitely moves fast and I found it hard to keep up.

Don't take this class unless you have to, it's not pleasant. You have to learn all of the content on your own because the lectures are pretty much useless and the profs teach as if you're already familiar with the content. The quizzes are so difficult and they're out of very few questions so you'll end up with very low scores, but at least they're not worth too much of the overall grade. The midterms average in the 60s so don't expect to do well, even if you study. This class is not fun but you won't fail it as long as you put in some effort.

Do not take this course if you don't have to because it is a lottt of unnecessary stress. However, if you do have to take it, don't stress too much but definitely STUDY CONSISTENTLY!! I went into this course only having taken freshman year chemistry in high school and I have gotten a little over the average on midterms (finals haven’t come out yet but praying). Find people to do the quizzes with and to study with, do practice problems, watch Youtube videos, anything else that helps you, and you should be. Look over the slides of both lecturers because there is slightly different info on both. Midterms are okay, 1 is harder than 2. Final is super hard, prepare.
The course content is very packed. If you did not learn chemistry beforehand, I don't think this is a course for you to get started with... Even if you did, you have to be prepared for the fast pace.

This course is difficult and fast–paced but the structure of the assignments is very conducive to learning the material. The weekly homework assignments and quizzes are very helpful because they force you to study for the exams a bit each week. The quizzes are pretty hard but I usually had enough time to figure things out, as there were typically 3–4 questions in 60 minutes. Midterms 1 and 2 were difficult, but fair in my opinion. However, the final exam I took last night was absolutely horrific. I did very well on the first two midterms, and I studied way more for the final, but this test was just not possible to do well on in my opinion. The questions on the exam did not remotely mirror the practice problems we did in class and in the TA review session. Also, they did not provide a practice final even though they provided practice tests before each midterm. Very disappointing end to my Chem 151 experience, because other than that, I enjoyed the class. I liked Prof. Swearer but he sometimes answered questions in a roundabout way that made me more confused. Bethel is a bit more time–efficient and straightforward with his lectures, but Swearer was still a great prof. and very enthusiastic about the material.
The course is very challenging, especially the weekly quizzes and final exam. I wish we solved more problems in class as this would have prepared us better for the quizzes and exams.

The exams are difficult, particularly the final because there wasn't a practice one made. The concepts can be interesting, but several times in discussion really hard questions will be asked that are never thought of again. If you are interested in Chemistry then this can be a fun class, but you will spend a lot of your studying time here.
This course is difficult and requires a lot of work. Must be willing to go to office hours frequently for help.
It is difficult content and designed to create stress.

The course is very challenging, especially since lectures were hard to follow. Due to the fast paced nature of the class, several key concepts are covered every lecture, making it hard to deeply understand them during lecture. Reading your textbook and learning from homework is crucial to gaining a better understanding of key concepts.

I took AP Chem in high school so it was somewhat easier to follow along the course. There are some concepts that the professors don't go too in–depth with that show up in tests/quizzes which was super frustrating.

Chem 151 is HARD!! It’s a difficult class. It was especially difficult for me because I did not take AP chem. There were many student who took AP or IB chem and that gave them a better foundation for learning the material (because it’s a very fast paced class). Definitely a weed out class.
Lectures, recitation worksheets, and homework did not prepare for weekly quizzes or final exam.
151 is just one of those things you gotta thug out. Nothing's that difficult it's just a lot of busy work. The final sucks.

The class moves incredibly fast, making it extremely difficult to keep up with reading with a McCormick workload. The weekly quizzes are challenging, so try to find a group of friends to work through the problems even though some specific values may differ. The exams are quite challenging, but the practice exams do a descent job of preparing you for what ends up being on the exams. Big picture: I would highly advise against taking this course unless you absolutely must as a major requirement.

It isn't easy at all, and I feel like they aren't as helpful as I would hope when taking such an important course. I feel like there should be a more personal relationship between the professor and the student, but in this course there really isn't unless you force it.

The course content is interesting, and Professor Swearer does a moderately good job of explaining things. He sometimes tends not to understand questions students ask him. Regardless, the major issues in this class are out of his hands—namely, the exams, which tend to be exceedingly difficult for no apparent reason. The first exam was difficult, and while the second was less so, the final exam was much more difficult than the others. An average score of around 60% should be cause for alarm for everyone involved in instruction, but they seem to take it as a point of pride. Overall, this course is interesting, if not very difficult. You will have to put in a lot of effort to succeed, but it is far from impossible.
I think this course is relatively hard but if you try your hardest and make sure you are familiar with concepts on a deeper level, it will help you succeed.
Thia course sucks, but if you pay attention you will get through. Do your homework and go to office hours and pray for a curve
This course was not overly difficult but instruction time was not very useful in learning content. To understand topics thoroughly, additional resources were needed.
Tough class, just have to get through it.

This class was not good. We were not given a practice final exam, but 75% of the material on the final was material we learned during reading week and the week before that. They asked questions that we had zero experience or practice with, especially since we were not given a practice final, nor was there a recitation sheet for the last chapter. Regardless of how much outside time you put into this class, the profs and TAs put out of context information on the assessments. Not to mention you have to balance lab work as well. The weekly quizzes were open book and take home, which helped, but some of the questions were quite literally things we did not learn in lecture or in our textbook. If you have to take this class, be ready to dedicate a lot of time for studying and practicing.

This course is very fast paced which comes with being in the quarter system. The way assignments are structured does a good job of ensuring students understand the topics that are covered in each week's set of lectures, but can sometimes feel unfair. The exams are challenging.

It was somewhat boring as I had felt as if a lot of the material taught was review for me. On occasion the instructor did not explain elements of the course in ways that I found particularly easy to understand, but the material is not too hard to pick up on your own outside of class.

I always felt I understood what was happening during lectures, and maybe sometimes on the homeworks too. Once I got to the quizzes and exams I felt like I had never done chemistry before, no matter how much I studied.

This class made me cry.... No but seriously, it's a weed out class for a reason. This was probably the class I put the most amount of time and effort into, yet the one I received the poorest grades back on. The final was diabolical, literally no amount of reading would have prepared me for that, but the midterms were more doable. Note, for review, read Professor Swearer's slides but watch Bethel's lecture videos. I read the textbook for the first midterm and it didn't help at all. Overall, if you don't need to take chemistry as a requirement, then please don't.
The class was very hard and requires a strong understanding of chemistry coming in. The tests were very difficult and required more than just memorization.

This course is definitely one to take seriously! I would say this course was the hardest I had this year. I was surprised by the difficulty of the weekly quizzes: being prepared and making sure you have problem–solving questions answered is key! I liked how the content built on itself with each chapter, but I think it was hard for me to grasp some of the concepts at first because they were more abstract and based on definitions. Aside from the quizzes and homework and exams, I have to say that what I learned was really interesting, especially the more conceptual parts! I also really appreciated the demos that would come every now and then. While the course didn't take as much time away from my day as some of the other ones, it definitely took the most brainpower. I really enjoyed Professor Swearer's energy and openness to answer questions. I think I would have understood the content more if the content included more physical rationalizations and justifications for why things happened, or why certain rules are true. This was definitely a very, very big challenge to get through, but it is definitely doable with help from the professors in office hours and from friends!

This class is very difficult, but not unmanageable. Prof Swearer is very nice and makes an effort to get to know all of his students if you go to office hours. I had already been introduced to most of this content in my high school chem class, so it's hard for me to say how effective the teaching was, but I think that the structure of the class was definitely helpful in my learning. The weekly homeworks were a good resource for me to check my understanding, but the weekly quizzes sometimes seemed disproportionately difficult – make sure you have a few friends in the class you can collaborate with on those. I didn't use the textbook too much, but sometimes exam content came from the textbook and wasn't covered in lecture, so I'd definitely recommend reading through it. The super TAs write all the tests and quizzes, so go to their office hours and especially their review sessions before each exam.
The course is difficult but if you study you will be ok.

It's definitely not an easy A, but the class was well taught and it is very possible to do well if you show up to lecture and study effectively. Quizes and exams do make up about half the grade, but the rest of the points are completion and participation. Prof. Swearer did a good job explaining material, although sometimes it felt like we lagged behind or glossed over things compared to the other sections. TA office hours are available every day of the week and are incredibly helpful.

This course is extremely difficult. It moves very quickly, has very hard and tricky exams, and penalizes you harshly for missing class. The grading system is also not very forgiving, so you need to stay alert and aware of what assignments come up.

I took this course as a pre–med prerequisite. This is among some of the only reasons that one should take this course. It is extremely challenging as a lot of background knowledge is expected and the class moves extremely quickly through a lot of material. This class requires you to study hours a week to even keep up with the material, let alone the exams. Prof. Swearer is a nice guy and is very helpful in office hours, however, his lectures can be quiet confusing and very hard to follow. In general, this class is extremely challenging and will make you realize if you want to do what you're doing bad enough to get through the class and take Chem 152.
Very demanding and requires a lot of studying.
The biggest problem of the course for me was the discrepancy between both instructor's classes, as I felt like there was always something missing in my classes

this is the hardest class i have ever taken. not because the content is necessarily hard, but because there is SO MUCH of it. we go way too fast, and learn sm more than other schools. a good experience though, taught me good study skills.
This course was fine. It is just important to stay on top of the material, which is very difficult to do. The amount of work does pile up and it can make it really easy to forget to study.

I don't think 151 is as bad as most people say, but it certainly isn't easy. You have to stay on top of lectures, do all the homework, and make sure you know how to apply every single concept in a timed exam, often in situations we never talked about in class. The quizzes don't get too hard as long as you have a good group of friends, and if you put in the time, you'll get a b+ or a– at least. With that said, don't take it if you don't have to –– it's a big distraction from other classes.

Swearer is a good professor, he just sometimes makes mistakes in lecture or covers topics quickly. Everything is recorded though, so lectures aren't a huge issue. Go over the textbook if you don't understand something, it generally goes more in depth.
As a biochemistry major, I took this course to fulfill my major and distro requirements for WCAS, and the content itself was intriguing and helpful.

When it comes to assignments, the homework isn't terrible neither the quizzes. They are manageable but can take a while to complete. During lectures, I felt like Swearer didn't thoroughly explain some concepts so I would have to look it up on youtube. I would recommend the Organic Chemistry tutor. Also when it came to answering questions, sometimes he wouldn't be able to answer it, which I guess is understandable.

This course is incredibly difficult. The weekly quizzes only contain 4 questions, so getting one wrong each time can really hurt your grade. The midterm exams and final exams are difficult as well, often covering content that was never covered in class. However, I don't think the course needs to be as difficult as they make it. I think the Achieve homework we are given each week is not helpful for understanding the content covered in the weekly quizzes and exams, because the questions are much easier than you will see on a weekly quiz or midterm/final exam. I think by not requiring students to do Achieve homework and by giving them practice worksheets made by the professors or TAs that are much more focused on content we will see on a midterm/final exam, that are more challenging than the Achieve homework, and that include integrative questions that cover content across different chapters, students would be much more prepared for exams.

For students taking this course, I would recommend reading the textbook over before each lecture and rewatching lecture recordings if you missed something during a lecture. It will give you a much better understanding of what content might show up on quizzes or exams, since not all of it is covered explicitly in class. I would also recommend trying to attend office hours.

Overall, if this course is not a requirement, I would not recommend taking this course.

The way the course was set up prevented more communication between professors and students, leaving the students to struggle alone. The recitation was very unhelpful, depending on who your TA was, because they wouldn't be able to communicate the knowledge we learned properly even though they knew the material. This class is very fast–paced, with little time to ask questions or even be confused.

I felt that the workload and amount of content was immense, much more than what I had initially anticipated. I focused a great deal on getting outside help, much more than other classes. In the end, I'm proud of the amount of effort I put forward for the class itself.

It's quite a difficult course to do well–in. I spent a lot of time studying and was always above the average on tests but my grade in the class was still low (like B). Also, the weekly quizzes are insanely hard sometimes for some reason. I know a lot of kids that would do really badly on the quizzes just because the questions were so difficult.

This class requires a huge portion of your time which sucks, especially as a first year student trying to learn good college study habits. Meeting 6 times in 5 days (including 161 which is required to take alongside 151) is hard enough, but also having a weekly homework assignment and quiz is rough too. The exams are 60% multiple choice, and because there are so few multiple choice, getting one wrong lowers your score by 3%. The textbook is fine, but has a lot of unnecessary information. The professors recommend reading it before class, which does familiarize you with the material, but also there's derivations and a lot of math in there we never cover, so taking notes on the textbook sometimes is pretty hard.
The course was made easier by the professor as it took time to explain

Reading the textbook and attending lectures were the most important parts of this class. I think that lectures were important but if you were in Prof. Swearer's section you need to also read Prof. Bethel's slides before quizzes. There were a bunch of things in Prof. Swearer's slides that did not always line up with what Prof. Bethel was teaching, which led to confusion on tests or quizzes. I think that communication between class sections could have improved so that it did not feel like other students had an advantage based on what section they were in. Overall the class was helpful for me and required for my major, but you have to put a lot of effort in otherwise you will not do well.
I thought it was a good class and it did a good job covering everything that showed up on the exams.

This course focused well on the topics that it had to cover, particularly understanding atomic and molecular structures which was a large focus during the middle portion of the class. I feel like more time should be dedicated to thermodynamics, since some of the content felt rushed towards the end so thermodynamics can be a large concept in the final.

This course was challenging mostly because it was not taught effectively. I had to teach myself the content most of the time because the professor would talk about each section in a way that complicated the topic further. The quizzes were always far more challenging than the homework and practice problems we went over.

There is a lot of work that comes with this class, but nearly all of it is very helpful. The concepts are also not very difficult, but in order to do well on the quizzes it is crucial that you understand the concepts extremely well.

Look, it's a weed out chemistry class at a rigorous school. It's hard to explain how difficult it can be to adjust to the class rigor and hours of work outside of the lectures, but it is absolutely manageable if you stick with it. The lack of curves takes a heavy toll on the grades that are attainable without sacrificing every moment of free time, and the clarity as to whether or not there is a curve to the overall grade at the end keeps you on your toes. By the end everything feels like it can be done with enough practice, and then the final exam slaps you in the face after containing questions in formats never before seen that are loosely related to the way materials covered are applied at best.
This is a difficult class. I found myself having to consistently learn the material outside of class. The lectures were an OK start to the content, but not nearly enough for the exams.

Came into chem thinking I knew most of the content but was quickly humbled– there is much more depth and conceptual understanding needed to succeed in the class compared to high school (but that's to be expected). The homework assignments aren't too bad, but the exams definitely stress the importance of conceptual understanding of most of the content so make sure you are thoroughly rehearsed in the information. I enjoyed chem in high school and generally still enjoyed 151 (minus the exams, they definitely make them harder than they need to be). Hardest part was probably keeping up with how fast we moved through content since you learn things that will be on the exam right up until the day of the test. I would also brace myself for the final, I felt semi– confident walking into the exam but it was much harder than I had anticipated (walked out praying I passed).

It’s a hard class but that’s a given. If you study enough, keep up with the textbook, and really think deeply about the content and go to office hours to try and get a full grasp of every concept, you’ll be fine. I didn’t take AP chem and was nervous coming into the class, but it’s very doable with the right amount of studying. Also Swearer is such a chiller great guy and helpful in office hours. I’d recommend him he’s a cool dude and does a good job making you interested in the topics.

This course is pretty challenging and I do not think I would be able to succeed in this class if I had not taken AP Chemistry in high school. I do think it is possible to succeed without having taken ap chem but it would be very hard. The final exam was extremely difficult and I felt like it was a little unfair as a lot of the material on the test was taught very briefly during the last week of instruction which happened to be reading week also. I wish the test covered more of the content from throughout the quarter which is what a lot of people study more of. I also wish they taught that material that was primarily on the test in a more thorough manner.

Although the lectures can be quite confusing at times, the homework allows you to completely understand the topics. The homework load is not much, but the exams are where this course is challenging, especially the final as it was a lot harder than the two midterms.
I liked this course because it taught me many things however this class was challenging and time consuming but overall worth it

This course was a significant learning experience, both academically and personally. I appreciated the in–depth focus on understanding why concepts work the way they do, which enhanced my ability to think critically about chemistry. It also taught me important skills like managing a heavy workload and seeking help when needed, both of which will benefit me in future courses.
For the most part the progression of the course made sense, but there a few times where I felt like what we were learning wasn’t very relevant to gen chem.

this course was challenging. I did not take AP chemistry in high school, and I had to work much harder to stay on par, as many students had more experience. stay on top of the readings, and take Bethel's class if you can
The course was quite challenging, but I think it was manageable if you had some experience with Chemistry before.

Even if you took AP Chem before this the class is still difficult! We dive much deeper into topics than what I learned in high school and the expectations are much higher than before. The "quizzes" are quite difficult and should be treated as a test that you have to study or review for especially because of how they are graded. At times the quizzes were harder than the exam.

I felt the course material was in–depth but easy to understand at times. However, exams really covered all details and you really needed to know the 'why' behind each concept instead of just memorizing content.

This course was confusing to me at times. Overall I enjoyed it, however there is a lot of material, and it is clear that sometimes the professor had to rush through a concept in order to get through everything on time. My largest problem with the course was my first week or two, in which I felt I was not warned to assisted in catching up with the Chemistry knowledge that others had from high school that I myself did not have.

This course is not easy at all. Some topics are covered more thoroughly than others and overall, the system of the course was just not great. I feel as though my professor went through this class in a skewed way and didn't teach us everything we needed to know for the exams. The first midterm was hard, and the second midterm wasn't terrible, but the final was the hardest exam I have ever taken. Learning a whole unit during the week of reading week did not allow for anytime to understand and study the material and it was not a very cumulative test either. Sure, there was more focus on the latest units, but it was still not evenly displayed throughout the exam. Overall, this course is extremely challenging and requires a lot of outside time.

This class is certainly challenging and will require a lot of effort in order to grasp the material and do well in the class. Professor Swearer was a pretty good lecturer and was able to clearly explain the material, but he did leave out or gloss over some really important content that the Bethel included in his lectures, so it left us more behind in some of the material. It was hard to visualize and soak in all the material thrown at us when we need to move so fast.

I wouldn't take this class if it is not an explicit requirement for you just because it is definitely a time–intensive course that demands your attention. You are better off taking something else. However, if you do take it, it is difficult but is manageable. The resources like the Peer Guided Study Group, Drop–In Tutoring, and the plenty of TA office hours are your best friend.

If you are reading these CTECs, you probably will have to take chemistry. If you are in Mccormick and got placed into chem 151, then I recommend sandbagging and taking chem 131/141 for an easier time instead if your major allows it. This course was painful from beginning to end, I studied hard and still did terribly.
Lots of material with not a lot of time spent going over the topics in detail.

This class was a whirlwind. There is so much material to be covered in so little time. The instructors recognize this, but there isn't much they can do because concepts need to be covered. That being said, the lectures were helpful at clarifying concepts covered on exams. Reading the textbook is the most important thing you can do. Lectures do not provide too many practice examples so that they can touch on all of the main topics, but practice examples are important to show minor details that might get passed over otherwise. The homeworks are not too long but do help solidify information. This class is definitely hard, mostly because of the constant influx of information, but as long as you keep up with the daily material and are willing to study/practice a decent amount on your own, it is very manageable.
Its a hard class but most people have to take it. Make sure you put in the hours from the very start.
Average genchem experience, if you pay attention you'll be fine
This course was really challenging. Even after putting in a lot of hours for homework, quizzes, and exams, I never felt really confident about the material but what I learned was very interesting.

The course succeeded in instilling a good work ethic and study habits, as it expects its students to be fairly independent. The lectures and discussion sessions are great resources, but at a certain point, it is necessary to use the textbook and get comfortable with self–learning. Dr. Swearer and the TAs are always willing to put aside a few minutes to discuss topics you are passionate about or confused by.

I had a terrible experience in this course, originally chemistry had interested me but this class discouraged me from the content vs. the exams. I felt like I put in a lot of effort, and understood the content, but then what we were tested on did not match that
This course was tough. There is a lot of content presented very quickly.

This is a challenging, but doable, course. Through this course, I learned I did not want to continue down pre–med. Really evaluate why you're taking chemistry before signing up, because it is a very time intensive course.
This is the hardest gen chem class I have taken, please do not wait until the end to study, because the course moves fast and you must be able to retain/recite all information for the exams!

Having this class first thing in the morning was a bad decision. It has an Iclicker requirement (attendance and accuracy based) meaning I could not skip this 9AM class. This class is very manageable, weekly homework and quizzes. I will say, I would recommend taking the class with the prof who writes the tests (Bethel). I watched his lectures on Panopto because I was unsure that Swearer was covering everything with proper depth, and at times I was right. I would learn something new from Bethel. He has also just been doing this for a while, and understands how to teach this material in a simple way.

TLDR: Take Bethel's section because he writes the exams and take the 10 or 11AM section because there is a geolocation attendance tracker.

It's a Gen Chem class, it's going to be hard. Don't expect to get an A in this class. The weekly recitation and quiz problems are useful for studying, but more outside studying is still necessary to do well. The class moves too fast as well (not necessarily Swearer's fault, there is just way too much content to squeeze into ten weeks), so I often had to go back and rewatch lectures to catch what I couldn't write down during class. Overall, not a fun time, but everyone suffers together at least.

I find this course challenging and had difficulty understanding certain topics of the course whenever I had to take the midterms and finals because they weren't talked about in the lessons, so I had a hard time understanding them. However, on the bright side, this course is a roller coaster since some parts are actually enjoyable.

source_file: Student Report for Yonggang Huang (GEN_ENG_205-2_21  Engineering Analysis  II)_9d3293de-52a5-4594-b628-47bd4a9d701aen-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: Prof Huang really is the best, please take EA2 with him. While the exams kind of sucked, especially the final, I think that the content itself is nothing too complicated, they just pull out really difficult questions for the final. This course was felt quite chill compared to EA1.

Professor Huang was amazing. He is very funny and the way he speaks makes his lectures very engaging. The weekly homework isn't bad if you work with people and go to office hours. We got cheat sheets on the exams which was comforting, but they weren't very helpful other than to remember the occasional formula. Each section is curved to a B average, so you won't really know your grade until final grades are released.

The workload outside of class is not so bad, and Professor Huang does a very good job keeping students engaged in lecture. Professor Huang also excels at covering all material needed for the exams in a timely fashion.
This class is similar to physics but there is new content that can be difficult. The grades are very dependent on class and relative to the averages.
The homework was difficult but not too hard and the midterms covered exactly what you would think would be on them.

EA2 is entirely analzing static equilibrium in 2D and 3D. Conceptually, equilibrium is the only thing covered, and problems are all solved by using systems of equilibrium equations. Weekly homework isn't too challenging or time consuming, exams are similar in difficulty to homework and practice problems, the design projects are quite tedious but not too difficult. Huang is a good professor, and his lectures are mostly covering examples which are usually pretty helpful. The TA recitations cover more practice problems, similar to the homework.

EA2 is so much easier than EA1 – especially if you have some introductory physics experience. As many people have said, everything just boils down to sum of forces and moments = 0. I just took the final exam, and that was indeed the main takeaway. Do take this course with Huang. He is so unintentionally funny and actually a good teacher. You will learn funny phrases such as "ONE rope ONE force" and "Making Sense". Do hope for your sake that everything does make sense, as the course does move pretty fast. He also has insane aura, and will tell you how good of a teacher he is. The class is curved to a B, so no matter how brutal the test – you just have to do better than most people, which if you put in some extra time trying to understand the material, is very achievable. Yonggang is absolutely the GOAT. He finishes the course about 2 weeks early which is really nice when other classes like DTC start to ramp up.
Similar to the basic statics you took in high school, except with problems that are 5x more complicated. If you want to do well, read the textbook.

If you can, I would highly recommend taking EA2 with Huang. I found his lectures to be very clear and easy to follow, and it seems as though he's usually the one to write the final exam which is another bonus. He does tend to skip the algebraic steps after setting up the main equations, so if you have trouble visualizing how to get to the solution the lectures may be harder to follow.
