comments: I would not recommend this course, Dr. Unger starts by saying why past ctecs were wrong and moves on to assigning vague assignments which he grades absurdly hard.

it was a pretty easy A, and a decent seminar to take. there were quite a few writing assignments that were 750–1000 words but it wasn't too challenging. i don't think i earned higher than a 60 on any of the writing assignments but i did decent on presentation and got an A in the class. make sure you are participating a decent amount + ask enough questions during presentations because that can effect your grade a lot. you can rewrite old writing assignments to improve your grade too, so you are basically guaranteed an A if you want to put in the effort
A lot more challenging than I expected, but not very serious

This class is not the easiest seminar in the world, but it isn't a ton of work. That being said, be prepared to receive a bad grade on the first essay. It's hard to know exactly was the professor is looking for, but once you figure it out, it is easy to conform to the right style. Understanding how to make real–life connections/comparisons is important in this class.

If you're interested in biology (especially if you're also taking Bio 201) take this seminar! I would even say that those who don't like biology should consider this seminar. As this is a seminar, it's basically a writing class centered around biological concepts.

Prof. Unger is very enthusiastic about biology and his lectures were always very interesting. He presented the various concepts from an unique perspective (like how do we fit all of our DNA inside of our cells, how/why does DNA replicate the way it does, etc.). This perspective helped me appreciate what we were learning in Bio 201.

Altogether, there were three essays (all 750–1000 word count, and one of them is about the final presentation), two shorter assignments, and one 10–minute final presentation (+ 5 mins of open questions from the class) about any biological "wonder" you want to present about. Also, participation throughout the quarter and during the "question" portion of the presentations counts for 25% of your grade, so keep that in mind. The class ended well before reading week, which I appreciated since it alleviated a lot of stress.

Some of the expectations were different than what I was used to, but Prof. Unger was always willing to zoom call/email to clarify anything. As long as your writing is coherent and flows well (and ideally you check it with the Prof. before the due date) you will be fine, as the grade cutoff for an A is very generous.

Coming into this course, I expected a science–based seminar to be significantly more lenient with the writing assignments as compared to a literature or history–based seminar. That being said, the grading scale is curved to 80/100 for an A, which is necessary given the way he grades. However, he is always willing to work with students after class on writing and wants each student to get as much out of the class as they are willing to put in. Overall, I believe this class truly helped me to improve my writing skills as well as teaching me about some of the wonders of biology. If you are looking for an easy seminar, this is not it. But if you have a fascination with biology and a desire to get better at writing, give this seminar a try.

This course was so unnecessarily difficult. Compared to the other biology seminar that I had taken, the writing in this class was graded incredibly harsh. Essay prompts are often very unclear and vague, and you have to assume a lot of things when you actually get to writing. Despite having little clue what you're writing about, you have to be incredibly precise and eloquent in your writing to receive a relatively decent grade. Because it is so unnecessarily hard to get a good grade in this class, it detracted from my interest in biology as a whole. Instead of letting my desire to learn more about the different topics of biology, I was too focused on following every minute constriction he made for the essays and would still do poorly. Also, I found it odd for one of the writing assignments in a literal writing class to have a word limit of 4...and that it was graded based on one's graphic design skills. Anyways, if you're reading this, don't bother taking this class, it's not worth the headache and there's so many interesting seminars out there.

This class everyone bashes on, but professor V is honestly one of the most open professors on campus. There are only four major assignments (3 essays, 1 assignment) which is not a lot of work whatsoever, but the grading can be a little harsh. Professor V provides a lot of feedback and if you communicate your needs to him, you will do just fine in the class. ALSO PARTICIPATE!!! Participation weighs the most for your overall grade.

5/7

comments: The content of the class isn't too bad but the exams can be hard if you don't pace yourself well. I really liked Wick's lecturing style that kept me engaged. The labs take quite a bit of time and I would recommend starting them as soon as possible so you can go to office hours to check answers as you go along.
3 midterms no final, 4 labs are very not chill
N/A
Probably the easiest EA course if you study hard enough. The labs are horrible if your lab mates aren't helpful though.

Literally, the only thing that matters in this class is if you have a good group for the labs (which are hellish). I had a group of 3 and went random to get 2 more people because we heard that the labs were difficult. We got stuck with a terrible lab member who did not complete their parts which led to incomplete lab submissions. We went to Bayliss about this issue but got little to no support from him, despite being told the opposite by TAs (and by him in a meeting). The content is fine as long as you go to class since notes and lectures are NOT posted. Homework is completion–based but long and if you don't do a problem there is no partial credit (0 on entire assignment).
Get a good group who will work hard and attend office hours. Try the homework yourself and then work through it with the answers to really absorb material. Exams are pretty straightforward.
The labs are horrendous, but everything else is alright.

Classic diffeq class, isn't super hard but also isn't a walk by any means. Make sure you get a good group for your lab because if you don't you're screwed and make sure that you spend time on the homework and quizzes since theyre important moving forward in the class
ODEs, professors are good

Great course, I probably will never apply the things I learned here to anything in my life but it was a good challenge. This class should definitely come before EA3. Both instructors are fantastic, but Stuart Wick in the greatest teacher on campus.
Everyone is praying for a favorable curve once the class concludes.

My reaction to this course in terms of learning and challenging myself was the hardest class that I've taken here. The constant hours spent on this class were incredibly valuable and cool to see myself learn.

The course material itself is fine, but I'm not a fan of the class structure. A mountain of homework problems are assigned, but they make up a very small portion of your homework grade. The real homework grade is a 25–minute timed quiz of 2 questions based off the homework, which just allows the class to assign an insane load of homework for us without the TAs having to deal with it. Furthermore, there are a lot of "gotchas" in the lab setup so that they can give people automatic zeroes. For example, if you use the wrong font, you get an automatic zero for the lab. Essentially, the course staff wants to be able to do the least work possible, so by giving automatic zeroes, they can decrease their workload.
This course takes a lot of time for the labs which involve a lot of writing, MATLAB, and math. I did not particularly enjoy doing the labs but Professor Wick's lectures were very enjoyable

Professor Wick is the best professor I've ever had. He makes the content so much more interesting than it would otherwise be. The class itself wasn't bad at all. Very easy if you just go to class and ask questions when you don't understand something. The labs were terrible though. My group wasn't great, so I found myself doing much more work, and losing points on parts of the lab that other members of my group did. While I disagree with being graded in groups, the rest of the class was solid.

This course was the hardest EA to me, not necessarily because of content but because of the amount of time you have to put in the class, especially with the labs. I think it is important to find a good lab group and try to finish/ start the labs as soon as possible because they take so much work. Content wise though it's not bad even though the toughest part is that the tests are pretty long, and you only have one hour to complete them, so having enough time to finish is usually the toughest part.

comments: I felt like this course focused a lot on conceptual info whereas chem 110 focused more on calculations. Overall, I think I enjoyed the material taught in this course more than that of chem 110.

If it wasn't for his tenure, I'm sure professor Weitz would not be teaching this course. The lack of passion for our learning along with an overall inability to explain concepts made this class unbearable. After years of negative CTECs, I would not be surprised if Weitz still doesn't change his methods. I strongly suggest you do not take this class unless you are required for your major.

Overall this course was good, there were sometimes tricky questions on like the quizzes or tests, but never anything too bad. Only thing to keep in mind is that the textbook can be really confusing, I had to reread things over a few times and in small chunks to get anything out of it (and you're expected to read the textbook before each lecture), and that sometimes it felt like professor Weitz would forget that we were students and was instead teaching to other experts who already knew chemistry. I got confused a lot. Usually though the TAs make up for that and are always answering questions in chat, so overall okay experience.

This class was very hard. Weitz is nice but the powerpoint is all over the place and he goes very fast. It is also very complicated content wise. Tests are very hard, but good curves as everyone is lost.

This class is going to want to make you scream and curl into a ball. It takes so much time out of your week and just to learn uninteresting material. Weitz is very monotonous and a little boring but the TA's are very helpful and useful.
Gen chem in general sucks, doubly so during an international pandemic where you're learning too much material.

Weitz is clearly very knowledgable in his field but does not teach well. He reads directly off the slides (which are very word heavy) and seems much more interested in deriving equations and going on side tangents than explaining crucial material.

It is ESSENTIAL that you read the textbook before class. If you don't, you will be very confused. If you put in a little bit of practice every day, it's manageable. Material is challenging but if you just keep with it, it won't get out of control.

Use the TAs!!! They are wonderful and probably the reason most of the class was able to succeed.
Did not enjoy the course since lectures were just the professor reading off the slides and not answering students’ questions clearly.

Difficult class to get the hang of, will most definitely need to study and review often. It can be hard to tell what is on the exams at times since they do tend to look different than the practice materials but TAs are so helpful, most of the time more helpful than the instructor.

4/9

Weitz is by far the worst professor I've ever had; it was actually painful to attend his lectures. I joined a PGSG which saved me but I am still using credit/no credit for this class because exams are insanely difficult. One MC can be worth 14 points which is ridiculous.

This class is bad. This is a mandatory class in the Gen Chem sequence or else I would highly recommend against it. We spent a vast majority of lecture time doing derivations of formulas we barely use and end up talking about the important concepts, that are tested on, for minimal time. The textbook is not great but it is the closest you will get to understanding any concept in this course, lectures just confused the issue. Be prepared, exams have an emphasis on concepts rather than problem solving.

The course content is much more advanced than in Chem 110. In addition, it is taught much worse. Professor Weitz rushes through topics and slides without giving students an adequate amount of time. He also does a poor job answering questions, and will sometimes even get annoyed with students for asking about things that seem simple to him.

Professor Weitz is passionate about the course. However, the lectures are not very engaging and I often found it difficult to pay attention. I also feel that the lectures contained a lot of extraneous details that weren't needed to know for the exams. I found myself reading the textbook way more than I thought I would. The homework for this class was not difficult. Practice exams help greatly with the actual exams. Utilize your TAs and officer hours!

Lecture in this class is brutal. The slides are confusing, poorly organized, and often different from the ones provided on canvas. Prof Weitz talks down to his students and generally seems like he doesn't want to be teaching.

This course had poor instruction. The in class lectures always seemed disorganized and I didn't feel like I learned much information from them. The most useful resources were the homework, the textbook, and google.
Very difficult hard to focus
Do the reading. It will make learning in lecture so much easier. Being prepared for class will help you be prepared for quizzes and exams.
This class was challenging but at times rewarding after putting in large effort

this course is horrible. they have the potential to make it so much better than it is, but instead they focus on making exams with trick questions and blaming the students for not understanding the material. youtube and the amazing tas taught me everything in this course, weitz's lectures are incoherent and the book makes no sense. taking this course made me so unhappy.

This course is very different from 110, because it is more conceptual. A lot of emphasis on electrons and how they behave. We did not have to use calculations until the final. Weitz put a chapter we never went over on the second midterm, and he told us it was going to be on the midterm a couple days before the exam. The exams are majority multiple choice which is unfair for students who do better on free response. This felt extremely unfair and unnecessary. Weitz lectures very quickly and the lecture slides he posts do not always match the slides he presents in lecture. The TAs are very helpful and seem to care a lot which is nice. Definitely go to TA review sessions before exams.

The lectures are not very well–explained. The slides are disorganized as well. Sometimes Weitz would explain or place emphasis on equations or things that are irrelevant to what we need to know for the exam, causing confusion.
This course was very challenging, especially after the first midterm. I feel like each part of the class gets harder and harder as the quarter progresses on.
Go to the TAs, watch chem tutor videos online, and do lots of practice.

This class was unnecessarily hard. Despite being mostly conceptual and less mathematics–based than Chem 110, I found that it was even more difficult. I felt that I had to teach everything to myself after lecture because of the way it was taught. Professor Weitz sometimes goes over a topic for a whole lecture and then says at the end that we don't need to know any of it and that it was just context for the actual content he would be teaching the next day. After taking notes for an hour, hearing that was somewhat frustrating. To do well in this class, ask questions during chem lab lecture and during recitation. The TAs are great at explaining content that felt confusing during lecture. The exams are tough but if you study enough an A or B is very achievable. Read the textbook.
This course was challenging just as any general chemistry course. It covered a lot of content and a lot of the burden is put on the student to figure it out.
this class was so hard. If you don't HAVE to take chem don't. just don't. find a way around it. It was not worth it online. I feel like I just crammed for exams and lost it all right after.

The lectures were confusing and there was a lot of information presented that had nothing to do with what we actually needed to know for the exams and other assignments. Professor Weitz definitely cared about our success, but the fact that I had to read the book in order to actually understand the topics he tried to go over in lecture wasn't great. However, the exams were much more fair than the ones in 110.
Very difficult course, make sure to go to office hours and do practice problems.

This class was hard because of the complex topics in this section of chem including quantum mechanics and thermochemistry. I did feel like I had to teach myself the concepts and spent a lot of time outside of the class studying to understand the material. I felt that the quizzes and exams were unfair at times and did not reflect the practice exam questions or things covered in class.

5/9
The course is okay if you're into chem. NU chem is hard in general. Sapling is pretty annoying. especially since you never know if there's more to a question.
Overall, I did not enjoy this course. I do not feel like I was engaged or that my engagement was encouraged by the instructor.
This course was challenging but it helped me learn a lot. Reading the textbook is very important.

The material taught in this class was well structured in a sequence that enhanced my understanding of pervious and incoming topics. However, sometimes the professor's explanations were unclear and not much class time was set aside for questions to be answered. This was partially fixed by the TAs answering questions in chat during class but it was sometimes difficult to follow class lecture and also be aware of the useful questions being asked and answered in chat.
The course was more annoying because I am not the biggest fan of chemistry and it felt like the course was going on for forever.

The course is hard work, period. But you can make it. Just read the book, watch Youtube videos on the material and have a group of friends you can work with and you'll be solid. The tests are hard. Don't worry about a super high grade. There's a curve, and here you'll learn that failure is honestly relative.
This course was taught poorly and I had a hard time grasping the material from the lectures. Not a lot of time is spent on the material that is tested so I would recommend going to office hours.
It was fast paced which made it easy to fall behind. There was a lot of conceptual things at the beginning that were unclear if they needed to be memorized or not.

The course itself is kind of difficult, and most of the knowledge you get will come from the textbook. If you read beforehand as recommended, the lecture section is just repetition but usually not anything new. The course itself is kind of bland, but the TAs are great!
The class wasn't the best. At times the method of instruction made it difficult to understand the topics.
It truly felt that the professor did not care about my learning of the materials at all. You will be teaching yourself gen chem in this class and the TA's will be your only saving grace.
I did not really enjoy this course. The book is not interesting to read whatsoever, the instruction was subpar, and lectures were confusing.

This course is very difficult. If you do the readings ahead of time you should be okay, but the readings themselves take hours if you actually want to understand them. Often, Prof. Weintz's lectures are just more confusing. The course isn't impossible just be prepared to meet with TAs (who are usually the best and most helpful resources) and complete the readings to learn the material.
It covers some more of the basics of chemistry but its pretty difficult and I would not recommend taking it unless needed for a major.

This class was not homework heavy, however you had to put in a lot of time to review the material on your own. You have to ensure you don't fall behind in your review as well, because the content picks up really quickly.

this class is MUCH more intense than 110 was so prepare yourself. the professor's lectures aren't very helpful in understanding the material in an efficient manner, so I recommend that you supplement that with office hours or reading the textbook.
This class was very challenging and the pace was way too fast.
Hard class but if you did well in 110 you;ll be fine
Very fast–paced, but good professor and interesting material. It is a hard class though.

The lectures often gave information in a confusing manner, and some days would be completely spent on information that was ultimately unimportant. Much of the time I could only figure out what information was important and what wasn't was from the homework assignments. The TAs were super helpful in answering questions and clearing up any confusion though.

I learned most, if not all the material by myself from reading the textbook, and from the TA's. The lectures by the professor were confusing, and not helpful at all in learning the material. The TAs were amazing in helping with review materials and sessions, but the professor's lectures were not helpful at all. I learned the entire quarter by reading the book and taking notes. The end of the quarter was super fast in fitting three chapters into two weeks which was hard, as well as the rest of the quarter moved very fast. Also, the TAs would answer questions in the chat, so every class I just read the chat because the TAs were the only ones who explained the material. The professor just read the slides and didn't really answer questions.

This course was rough. Prof. Weitz seems really nice but the lectures were so hard to follow. Half the time the slides would not even be correct or there would be slides missing from the presentations that we were going over in class. He would also do polls right at the beginning of class so if you were a few minutes late for any reason you would miss them. The TA's were literally my saviors because they did such a good job explaining things during office hours and review sessions. This class was just so hard and I did understand it a little more than 110 because I relied a lot on youtube but they were both challenging. If you have to take this class it's just something you gotta push through, which is hard and discouraging especially when the grades are always so low before curves. Make sure to pay attention to the chat in zoom because the TA's answer questions that are helpful.

This course was very challenging especially in a remote setting. What made it more difficult was Professor Weitz's lecture slides which did not always match up with the book and sometimes contained irrelevant information that got confusing.

I would recommend reading the textbook before every class, printing out the lecture slides the professor posts, and taking notes on

6/9 those based on the readings and class discussions.

The course was styled in a way that you had to learn via the book and the lectures were review of the book. If you want to learn, I would recommend going over the slides from lectures, and using outside sources such as youtube. This quarter was a week shorter than usual, so content was extremely rushed near the end (we had a whole chapter assigned to be completed in a few days).

Lectures were helpful especially since we could ask TA's questions while the professor was lecturing if we were confused. Homework was helpful but often contained overly difficult questions that were unlikely to be on quizzes/exams. Exams were difficult since they were less than 20 questions testing on multiple chapters, difficult but managable.

This class was extremely challenging and time–consuming for me. I highly recommend joining a peer–guided study group and going to office hours consistently for this class. These external resources are where I learned a majority of the material because Professor Weitz's lectures are extremely convoluted and difficult to follow. He treats them as supplementary to book reading on your own time, making it feel like no actual learning happens in class; everything must be learned on your own time. He goes on long tangents that amount to nothing, his slides are confusing to understand, and he isn't able to effectively answer questions that students have. Despite all this, if you take the time, I think the material is doable on your own, it just makes for a miserable experience. I was able to perform well in this class, not because of the instructor, but despite him.
hard material, but stick with it and get help. join a study group.

I knew Chemistry was going to be the most challenging sequence at my time at Northwestern and it has proven me right. Do the readings, do practice problems, and expect the unexpected. This is the way to make this class less stressful.

7/9

source_file: Student Report for Frederick Northrup (CHEM_110-0_02  Quant Problem Solving in Chem)_f4747ebc-d195-4568-a81d-40db6756a1fben-US.pdf

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: This course was very interesting but also challenging
The course did a great job of focusing on topics I will need in the future, as an engineering major.

Was an overall very stressful experience. The coursework has heavy especially given the fact that it is meant to be an intro class. I am sure that this is a common standard across future STEM courses, but as an introduction to university, I found it to be too challenging.
The course material was not that difficult but applying the course material to exams was the difficult part of the course.
There was a lot of stress. Yes, I learned a lot, but I could barely keep up, even though I was putting a lot of effort and have done my very best.
Prof Northrup did a good job of explaining things in class and as long as you follow along and pay attention you'll do ok. It is a lot of memorization despite what other people say.

If this is your first introduction to chemistry or you've taken a basic chemistry class in high school, listen to this. This class is challenging. The first two weeks are NOT a representation of how the rest of the course is. During those first couple of weeks, it will be easy, and it will seem like the entire course isn't hard, but do NOT be fooled. At a time when you go over basic math concepts and very introductory chemistry concepts, make sure you STAY on top of your work, understand everything, and study ahead. This class builds on itself, and it builds quickly. And so if I had one piece of advice, it would be to use those two weeks wisely. You'll quickly realize when you take the first couple of quizzes and the first midterm that you are tested on concepts that are barely touched on in the lecture and the assessments will feel like a curveball. You will have to use the textbook, your TA, and any resource you can to even begin to do decently in this class. Also, the practice midterms are not at all the same difficulty as the real midterm so keep that in mind while studying. Good luck.

DO NOT TAKE THIS CLASS WITH NORTHRUP. IF POSSIBLE TAKE NEMR’s CLASS. They don’t say this at the beginning of the class, but chem 110 is a weed out class. Why is the beginner chem class a weed out class?—idk, but it is. Chem 110 is notorious for being difficult for no reason. I’m writing all this knowing you will be forced to take this class and I want to give some forewarning and advice before you plunge into the hell that is chem 110. I’m going to split the up into sections: 1. Why Northrup sucks 2. Issues with Homework 3. Issues with Tests.

Section #1: Northrup

Northrup has been at this school for more than 30 years. He’s tenured out the wazzoo. He has no intent of changing his teaching style or anything to do with the way he does chem 110. He’s also the head of the chem department. Whatever he wants goes and no one can say anything to him. His lectures are often filled with information that you will not be tested on and aren’t related to the material at all. Sometimes he spends more time on the material that’s not tested or relevant at all than the actual stuff we need to know. During his lectures, he often does “iclickers” (the attendance) at the beginning of class instead of in the middle (god forbid your 5 mins late). He doesn’t listen to TAs, (teaching assistants) and is not someone to confide in for help (he will just tell you to do more practice problems and rarely offers any real advice). Northrup doesn’t work through problems well like Nemr does. Nemr will go through problems related to the ones on homework step by step like a regular person. Northrup doesn’t do that, he clicks through slides, barely explaining the processes needed to complete a problem (the slides aren’t even made by him, he steals them from the textbook). Northrup will tell you that you won’t ever need to memorize anything. That is the biggest lie ever. You will have to memorize countless rules, tables and other garbage throughout this course. Northrup is also just not approachable in general, he is very intimidating and even if you did go up to him, like I said earlier, you will often not get the help you need. Northrup also says at the beginning of the year that it will benefit you if Northrup gets to know you by name. That is another lie, his office hours are completely packed so you won’t even get the help you really need and also the benefits of him not knowing you by name are far greater than if he knew your name (if he doesn’t know your name, he can’t call you out on the middle of the class for looking tired or not paying attention). The last thing I’ll say is that Northrup often doesn’t tell you what material is going to be on tests or quizzes (even though he makes them). There have been multiple instances where he lies about what’s on a quiz or test.

Northrup is by far the worst teacher I’ve ever had up until this point in my academic career.

Section #2: Homework Homework is weird in this class. It’s assigned at the beginning of the week and is due at 9 am on Friday—which may seem great; however, you learn the material on the homework on a day by day basis. So in many cases, you can’t actually start on the homework whenever you want. For example, the homework has problems on it that require you to know material in the lecture on Thursday. The homework is made by the TAs and contains problems taken from the textbook. But some of the problems have been copied incorrectly from the textbook (or don’t contain he full context of the question), often making them unsolvable (I wouldn’t write this if this didn’t happen on multiple occasions) Also, when the TAs make these mistakes, they don’t correct them. No email or canvas message. Even worse, sometimes the TAs don’t know how to do the problems themselves. (Why are freshmen being given problems that the grad students can’t even do?—idk) Answer keys for homework and recitations are also often just wrong. Like the answer key can literally be wrong. I would highly recommend doing chem homework with a group or at least having help from ChatGPT (or go to a TA office hours)

Section #3 Issues with Tests: This class has more than 75% of your grade tied into assessments. The average on the first midterm was a 54% and the second midterm average was a 57%. The free response questions are by far the hardest to deal and guess what?—Northrup makes them. Tests are also usually a time crunch. The MC are usually not as bad cause they are picked by Nemr. I could go on forever why tests are incredibly hard for no reason but I’ll cut to the chase and give you some advice. 1. Multiple choice are the most important imo since they are 60% of the test and you’re guaranteed to get an answer. Try to lock in mainly on the multiple choice if possible. If you get all the multiple choice right, you’re automatically above the avg. 2. Do all the recitation and homework problems for practice (they base lots of the questions off of the recitation and hw problems 3. Don’t panic during the tests, you will run put of time, just move on 4. Try to get partial for the free response, if you don’t know what you need to do, start converting stuff and writing numbers from the question 5. MOST IMPORTANT: the practice tests they give you will be WAYYYYY easier than the actual thing, so don’t get cocky 6. If you run out of time on a free response, write a paragraph of the steps you would take to complete a problem in words 7. Rest before the exam if possible. They are usually at 7 and go till 9pm so take a nap before and get some food in your stomach

Conclusion This class sucks, but don’t beat yourself up over it. If you get a bad grade on a quiz, you have to move on. This class has taught me two things: 1. you have not time to be sad or tired abt a class, 2. I have terrible anxiety (this class was so bad that I now have a therapist). Don’t let ppl belittle you for being in 110. They don’t understand how bad you have it. Overall, you’re probably required to take this class, so please take Nemr if possible. Half the stuff in Section 1 can be avoided if you take Nemr’s class. I would know; midway through the semester I stopped going to my lecture time and went to Nemr’s instead and the difference in the classes is night and day.

This course is discouraging. Going from being a straight As, 4.0 student to getting 60s on the midterms is an adjustment to say the least. But fear not, this class is curved like crazy. It's just frustrating because every week there are lectures and then a quiz which no there is really no way to prepare for. It's only when you go to recitation after the quiz that the topics you are confused on get explained by your TA. Also, the homework assignments are hard and cannot be done in one night in my opinion. Make sure to start them early in the week or you will hate yourself (speaking from experience.)

This course was very challenging for an introductory class. This class was fast–paced and often required me to teach myself the material outside of lectures and discussions. The expectation is that you will spend all of your free time practicing chemistry and doing review problems to receive a 60% or below on each of the quizzes and exams. Good luck!

This course was very badly paced. The very three weeks of lectures were going over the syllabus, reviewing geometry, and reviewing unit conversions. After that, the course speeds up very fast which a lot of people did not do well with. The midterm free response questions are much harder and combine more concepts than any practice before, so try to find the most challenging questions to practice with. I think professor Northrup is genuinely a good person, but he sometimes comes off as arrogant when talking to students. Overall, I did alright in the class but I think some changes would help everyone do better, like balancing the pace of the course.

This course is exactly what the title says: quantitative problem solving. If you like math, the course is not terrible. If you don't like math, the course is pretty difficult. The homework covers content before it is covered in lecture.
It was very challenging and required a lot of wor outside of class. But as long as you catch up with the content on your own it should be good.

I found this course to be quite challenging. Even though I had done Chemistry in the past, I'd found it to be very rusty. The individual concepts of this course weren't that difficult to understand, but when multiple concepts were combined into one problem, that's when it became challenging. For this class, I found that understanding the basics was essential for the later chapters, as they are building upon what you already know. Finally, the most important thing I learned was the value of practice, the more I practiced the more confident I felt about the problems I was solving.
This class is pretty difficult but will pay off if you study and do the recitations and homeworks. Definitely a lot harder than I thought it would be though.

I think this course is extremely helpful for anyone starting out in college. I feel like I can apply what I have learned to other courses, not only further chemistry courses. This course adequately prepares students from all backgrounds, and gives them an idea on what to expect from Northwestern. It gave me an opportunity to build the confidence to ask for help when needed, which is an essential skill to have in higher level education. Overall, I am very pleased that I took this course, and I am happy with the knowledge that I gained from it.

The course was much more difficult than I thought it would be. It definitely requires you to combine the information you have learned throughout the quarter, and the quiz/exam questions are good examples of that. Northup is a good teacher and relatively helpful, but he explains things very technically.

A lot more difficult than I expected it to be. The structure of the class is a little weird since we don't usually learn the concepts on the homework for that week until the end of the week. There is definitely a bit of a disconnect between what's being taught and what's being assigned, especially when a lot of class is talking about the background behind something which we are not being tested on at all.

This course started off very slow, which is deceiving because that's not what you should be expecting for the rest of the quarter so enjoy it and prepare yourself.

I think the time spent at the beginning of the quarter reviewing "math–skills" would have been better spent taking our time explaining content throughout the quarter. Northrup is kind, but because he's been teaching for so long some things are intuitive to him and not so–intuitive to someone who hasn't taken AP Chem or any chemistry since their sophomore or junior year.

To do well in this class you MUST take your first midterm seriously and do the practice problems in the textbook and go over the recitation and homework questions again!! It's way better to be overprepared than underprepared, especially since the 1st midterm was not what I was expecting and is incredibly math–heavy. I wish I had done so earlier so I had a better chance of getting an A in the course. However, I was able to pick it back up after the first midterm by having a significant increase afterwards.

The weekly quizzes are typically far easier than the type you will see on the exams, and same goes for the content on the practice exams. Overall, this course is one that takes a lot of time out of your schedule in order to regularly practice, and in order to best prepare you need to work with a variety of problem sets and be okay with being asked concepts in an alternative manner. This is definitely meant to be a weed–out class.

Good luck!

I found it difficult to follow and understand how the Professor goes about creating his quizzes and exams. It caused a lot of stress especially since it's the first chemistry course of the sequence. I did learn a lot though not only about chemistry but how college STEM courses test their students in general. It was a much needed experience and I wish everyone goodluck!

This course is very difficult and requires a lot of time and effort in order to perform well. Even though it is difficult, I think the chemistry department provides a good amount of opportunities such as recitations, office hours and other helpful resources to help students. The most important way to prepare for exams is by doing practice questions, which are available in the homeworks, recitation work sheets, and textbook. I have also found office hours to be highly helpful if you are struggling with a certain concept.

This course was very frustrating. They care more about small aspects rather than you actually understanding content. Almost a "out to get you" mentality. The things learned in this class are almost useless as higher level chemistry does not require you to remember every small little detail. The teaching was mediocre and especially since my class was at 10am, it was difficult to get to class when all Northrup would do is read off the slides. Would not recommend taking unless you absolutely must.
A lot of math, with pretty hard exams, but it's a really good intro to chemistry.

Not the most challenging course in terms of the content, rather the quantity of material is what could get to you. I found it difficult to learn everything from simply class periods and recitation session, so I would definitely make an effort to attend office hours when possible to cover anything that was missed as we as get an idea of what quiz and exam question will be like. Overall not a bad class, instructors provided ample resources, assessments were fair, and homework was helpful.

This course is math–heavy which can be good or bad depending on you're strengths. Professor Northrup is very kind and caring, though he sometimes skims over concepts. The exam problems are a lot more complex than the practice. Having a strong foundation in stoichiometry, dimensional analysis, conversions, and general problem solving was very beneficial.
The recitations sections were helpful because they forced you to practice. I only think that the lractice tests are nothing like the actual tests.

I'm a sophomore, and I hadn't taken chemistry since my sophomore year of high school (and that was on zoom). I was nervous at first, but this course did a great job of really starting from the ground up and not expecting students to have crazy knowledge going in. Was it hard? Yes. Did I have to invest a lot of time into studying, staying up to date with the textbook, and doing homework? Yes. Was this sometimes exhausting? Yes. But also, I learned so much. TA office hours were super helpful, and so was working through problems with classmates. Sometimes it seemed like Prof. Northrup would judge people for being confused (even when the people asking questions weren't the only ones confused). This course is definitely something else, and there is a lot of content covered, but I'm really glad I took it––I learned a lot, I had fun sometimes, and honestly, it's just super cool to know more about the chemical compositions etc. of stuff around me.
one step towards my degree
CHEM 110 is brutal but it definitely teaches you basic chemistry.

This course was fair. Some topics were not always easy to understand right away in class, but the study materials provided were very effective in helping me be able to fully grasp the concepts. There were lots of notes, challenge questions, and practice tests at our disposal that I would definitely recommend making full use of. Professor Northrup truly wants you to succeed and does his best to help you.

Overall, this course was very interesting, yet fast–paced. The midterms and final exam were more difficult than the questions we learned in class, but were similar to the questions in the homework and textbook problems. I recommend studying by doing practice problems and going to office hours to walk through problems you are struggling with. The TAs for this course are very helpful, so utilize them as much as possible!

For a course that is this hard, the way the information was formatted and conveyed was pretty hard to understand. This, or it could just be the fact that this much intense chemistry is shoved into one quarter of an intro level class.

This course was extremely challenging to me and I found many of the content very hard to understand especially when they come up on quizzes or exams. But success in this course depends on how much work you're willing to put in and although it is a lot of work it is doable

What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
