comments: This class was a good way to get back into chemistry and set me up for success with the rest of the chemistry sequence. While it was difficult content, the pace was manageable.
it was hard, not very flexible to meet everyone needs in order to perform their best.

Even though this is a basic chem class IT IS HARD! The exams also seem way harder than any practice test or quiz you take and you have to have a super good understanding. Lectures felt super boring and most of the time confusing. You definitely have to dedicate a lot of time to this class. Also the homework is due at 8 pm every monday instead of 11:59 which was annoying and weird. This is totally a weed out class so be prepared and study A LOT

Don't take this class if you don't have to. There are weekly homeworks, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and a final. The midterms were hard but doable, with 50–60% averages. The final, on the other hand, was actually impossible. This class will also take up a lot of your time, it meets 5 days a week for 50 mins on–top of a two hour discussion section. You also will not know your grade until the end of the class. The only time you'll confidently know your letter grade during the quarter is if your failing, in which Prof Nemr or Northrup will reach out to you post–midterms. I had Prof Northrup but went to Prof Nemr's office hours, which were much more helpful than Northrup's. Honestly, the key to this class is practice problems. Before midterms and finals, do every practice problem in the textbook, redo PEPs, finish HW and recitation problems. Though the exact questions won't end up on the exam, those questions are the closest you'll get to practice for the exam. This class was difficult, you'll make it through!!!! It is a weed–out class, and you will see peers drop out at various points at the quarter, but honestly just push through, you def can!
It takes up a lot of time and they want you to practice a lot

Look at this point, it's not even who teaches it. The content is heavy at first, but you find a rhythm to it and think you know it. Then the professor will quite literally give you everything possible: lecture notes, multiple HW problems, Exam problems, and previous exams that are supposed to "help" you. Until the exam comes around and they write it so you can't understand anything, and ask it in ways you have never seen before, so it's almost impossible to even understand what they are asking. TAs also vary a lot, and some can be very helpful and want you to succeed, while others will just be there and have no input or give actual advice to do better. Overall, the course is only hard because of what is expected from you. There is no set way of how things are done, and you can spend hours on a topic just for them not to ask it all. Don't take it if you don't need it.

The course is as good as you can expect for a college Chemistry course. You definitely have to spend a lot of time outside of class to teach yourself the materials because Professor Northrup just reads off of the slides and does not do many example problems even though all of the homeworks and exams are just math based problems. The exams were difficult but manageable. I realized that I had to memorize all of the rules for everything even though my professor told me not to. The course moves very fast so you can not fall behind.

Just get through it honestly. Don't be burned out by first midterm scores espif your around the mean your doing fine. The book actually helps but don't touch it the night before an exam its a waste of time. If you are like me and are damn near new to chem practice problems and repetition will get you through.

It did help me learn, they were very thorough in class, going over the concepts and how to practice them. The concepts don't see too hard when learning them, but applying multiple of them in one question can be hard to recognize and tackle.

If you are taking this class, I strongly encourage you to make it your priority. There is so much content covered throughout the quarter, and there are a lot of resources but you must take the time to thoroughly practice. It’s not horrible if you stay on top of your assignments which is why you should be working on chemistry every day, even if it’s just 30 minutes. I recommend reading ahead in the textbook and doing the non required homework problems along with the entire recitation packets. The quizzes are annoying and can be difficult to do well on but I wouldn’t let that discourage you. The exams are very difficult and I wish more practice exams were provided. However, if you do above average you should be fine and the averages aren’t very low. Going to office hours is also something I encourage because you can get your face known to the professors and they are always willing to help.

This class was a lot to take in first quarter, and despite having taken chem in soph/senior yr of high school, it was still difficult. Lectures weren't bad, but they do pick up in speed after the second week so be prepared. The exams are very hard, with averages in the 50–60's. The whole class gets curved at the end, so as long as you lock in you should be somewhat fine. GO TO OFFICE HRS!! I wish I did more of that so maybe I wouldn't be as cooked as I was. Also read the textbook, its super helpful. Overall good luck, you'll need it!!

This is genuinely the worst class offered at Northwestern. The chemistry department should be ashamed of itself and its lack of help offered to students. They’ll do anything but admit the struggling students is due to them and not our lack of effort. The averages ranged from 40,50,60% on exams and quizzes and it’s way too normalized that we were all failing through the entire quarter. Prof Northrup is such a nice and endearing professor and is very nice with great knowledge and experience, but the instruction was extremely poor and I often walked out of class feeling like I knew the same amount of information as before I walked into class. Homework genuinely takes so long (like 5–8+ hours) and quizzes are impossible. Don’t expect to succeed in this class. It ruined my GPA.

The course overall was alright, if you didn’t have any prior knowledge of chemistry it can prove to be challenging, if you have northrup he mentions some important things in class that are not on the slides so make sure to go
More difficult than it needs to be, not enough time given on exams

This class is so hard. Like so hard. I spent hoursssss studying for quizzes or midterms just to fail them. One thing is the chemistry instructors are very well–versed in the how people struggle, so they will tell you whether you should drop the course. It is clearly a weed–out class and I promise you , you will be studying until 2am for this class.

Honestly, where do I even begin. This class is unnecessarily difficult for an intro chem class. The concepts are not even that hard to understand, but Northrup's slides suck and lectures were confusing/hard to follow. It is made to be a weed–out class. You really have to study for midterms and the final if you want to do well. But still, they are hard and MUCH HARDER than the practice tests. The professors will say that the practice exams are the same difficulty as the exams, but they are not. The practice exams are so much easier than the actual exams. Also, doing poorly on one exam will kind of screw you. I did well on the first midterm and okay on the second midterm, but doing just okay on the second midterm will probably screw my grade. The final is also pretty difficult. Time is also super limited. The people who get extra time on the exams have a significant advantage because the exams are just too long and too hard to finish in two hours. Going to office hours and doing a ton of practice problems is super helpful. Also going to drop–in peer tutoring :)

That being said, don't let me scare you lol. You will get through this and still have a life. At the end of the day, it's just chemistry, and your worth isn't defined by how you are doing in a class. If you're reading these CTECs, you probably have to take this class. You'll get through it!! I believe in you!
Don't take this class if not required.
Basic foundational chemistry course––good if you've never ever done chemistry before, otherwise skip it.

The exams were a lot more difficult than the practices done in class, especially the final. I would say to really take advantage of the extra problems given in the homework to practice more challenging problems as well as the bonus questions given in the recitation sessions.

The class isn't overly hard. Reading the ctecs last from last year had me a little worried but as long as you do the homework problems you'll be caught up fine. Other people may have felt differently, but I thought both midterms were pretty fair. However the final was outrageous and didn't feel like it was on the same level as the rest of the assessments. The midterm averages were 68 for the first and 58 for the second, so you don't need to strive for perfection.

I think that this course starts off simple and then builds on itself as the professors start to combine new concepts. I will say that for me, the acid–base part of the class was the hardest, and that Midterm 2 was a lot to study for. Besides those notes, I think the class is a nice environment to start learning chemistry as a new student.
Very tricky course

First, don't let the CTECs scare you. If you have to take Chem 110 for your major or premed, you will be fine and get through it, as literally everyone does. It requires you to put in the effort and extra time to study and learn the material, because what is covered in lecture is just the absolute tip of the iceberg in what you are expected to understand. It is clear that both Northrup and Nemr are passionate about the material, and they are both nice and helpful in answering any questions you have.

That being said, the class is otherwise pretty poorly run and the majority of your success is up to you putting in the extra time and taking initiative to teach yourself or go to office hours and ask the necessary questions. The recitations and homework assignments are all just copied from the textbook (which I found to be annoying and kind of lazy– I would be doing those problems anyway to study, so why can't we be given a small number of new problems??) and almost always have a number of errors in them... I made a habit of going through the textbook problem section when I was confused on a HW question because the problem was almost always just missing some critical detail or measurement that was included in the original problem but forgotten when pasted into the book.

Overall, put in the time and effort, and you'll be fine. Do as many practice problems as possible, and prepare for the fact that the exam averages are in the 50s and 60s.

This course goes by really fast, which is expected because we are on the quarter system, but the professors are more interested on pushing the information into your brain then actually making sure you understand it.
i didnt like it
This course adequately introduced a lot of topics to me, and was a good refresher for a course that I took three years ago in high school.
If you're an engineer, just cheat on the placement exam and do chem 131. You can pretty easily self–learn this material and it will save you an entire quarter of miserable practice problems.
The lectures were unhelpful at times. Use the textbook.

This course is very challenging and can feel demoralizing at times, but it is totally possible to do well if you are willing to put in a ton of extra work on your own. Recitation practice problems are super helpful and so are office hours. The final exam was brutal, much more difficult than the two midterms so prep extra hard for that.
It was disorganized

This class is very difficult. Each quiz and exam feels like a wicked game in which you don't know the rules and are entering in blindly. The materials provided to prepare for exams are rarely beneficial and do not resemble the difficulty of questions seen on exams. To try to succeed, office hours are essential (Professor and TA) in addition to practicing questions that are more difficult than the types of questions provided.

I found chem 110 to be very difficult, especially when getting used to the quarter system for the first time. Professor Northrup would fly through the material, so it is up to you to deepen your understanding outside of class. I highly recommend doing the practice in the Dahm & Nelson workbook first before the Petrucci problems. Dahm & Nelson gave me a stronger foundation of the basics and prepared me to have the skills I needed to solve Petrucci problems, which feel like riddles and will be impossible to solve if you don’t know the basics already. Please have a set routine just for this class and stick to it, this class took up the majority of my time in this quarter. The quizzes also never end, so make sure you’re on top of understanding what the quiz will be testing for that week. The multiple choice questions most resembled problems I would see in the Dahm & Nelson workbook while the free responses were like an extra complicated Petrucci problem. It is so important that you do as many practice problems as you can so you can get used to the phrasing and determining what the question wants you to solve. However, before doing several practice problems, make sure you are looking through questions you have solved incorrectly and ask for help right away if you don’t understand, because all concepts that you learn are cumulative, even if professor Northrup doesn’t say so. Always make sure you understand your errors and why they are wrong.
This course was pretty standard, but not nearly as scary as its reputation. Teachers want to help you succeed, and there are plenty of resources if you are struggling.

Many people say that chem 110 is one of the hardest courses at NU, but I found that it's manageable if you stay on top of coursework by reading ahead in the textbook and doing lots of practice problems. The fearmongering is definitely overexaggerated.

Class is reasonable difficult and requires a good amount of studying. Recitations are extremely useful and those are where you learn the most. All the tests are significantly harder than the practice tests Northrup posts which is a joke.

This course will be a lot, but not unmanageable. If you're struggling, go to office hours, the course TA's, or any academic help. Basically, as long as you are keeping up with the class, you will be managing it. It's when you fall behind you need to put in a lot more time and effort. Do practice problems often, obviously including assigned ones but also optional practices or examples from the textbook. The class sometimes is very 1:1 with the textbook, which is good for having an additional resource but bad if this teaching style does not mesh with you. All that said, the quizzes and exams are closely matched with the practice problems from class. There will be trick questions, but they are tricks that if you do the problems and review the material, you will know how to see.
Overall, its a general science course, not the best, but not too bad.

This has been a really rough class for me if I’m being completely honest. I’ve struggled a lot and I know of a lot of people who have felt the same. I do however believe that there is a lot of resources for you to take advantage of and I cannot stress enough how important it is for you to do so. whether it’s attending office hours or forming study groups, it is really important to do what you can to stay on top of this class. There is a lot of help around you and it is in your best interest to take advantage.
this course was well designed to be a good introduction to chem. As long as you follow along and try to keep up, you can succeed pretty well.

source_file: Student Report for Ilya Mikhelson (GEN_ENG_205-1_21  Engineering Analysis I)_e3e4bb74-af55-49be-ba08-dfb86ba18765en-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: Iyla is the goat. Take this class and learn a lot from him and his amazing lectures.

This course kind of just throws everything at you starting with Matlab, which honestly is quite confusing to learn and for me required me to read through the documentation myself rather than anything else. The linear algebra section is just incredibly conceptual, too much so for only a couple week's affair. While Prof. Ilya is indeed a good lecturer, I think the class is just doing too much at once. Also, the due dates are placed pretty poorly – Friday at 6AM sometimes tricked me into leaving my code unfinished on Thursday night.

Its definitely not an easy class, the first half up until the first midterm with MATLAB is not than bad but the homework code gets exponentially more difficult every week. Midway through you pivot to linear algebra, but you still have to write weekly MATLAB code which is graded with basically no guidance, and it gets really complex and challenging, like just the concepts the code should solve are foreign. The linear algebra is moving at a really fast pace, basically all the discussions are treated as normal lectures that cover more concepts and it's all over the place.
Programs take a lot of time, but the content is fairly easy. Going to the office hours really helps.

If you have no experience coding or with lin alg, this course can be a bit much. It's perfectly manageable, but you'll have to put in the work to actually retain what you're doing as the pace is extraordinarily fast.
The class is interesting, but covers all content in an incredibly tedious way. The homework was far too long and completely unrelated to anything on the exams.

Ilya is a very skilled lecturer, and his office hours are incredibly helpful. The course begins by focusing on MATLAB, which can be tricky if you do not have any programming experience. After the first midterm, it shifted focus to linear algebra, although homework assignments still involved MATLAB programming. Although some homework programs/quizzes are difficult, office hours are easily available and very very helpful.

The final was so far from what we practiced in class. All of the practice materials were way simpler than the test. There were many questions that didn't resemble anything we had done before. I did not expect the difficulty level of the homework programs to be so high.

Ilya is a good guy, it's obvious to anyone that he's rooting for us and wants us to learn. The coordination for the course was kind of a mess, though. Important dates on the Canvas page were wrong and the discussion teachers all went at different paces and hit different topics. Just because you paid attention and studied your notes from your discussion section doesn't mean it is everything that's supposed to be covered. Also, the midterm grading was bombastic. Just 3 wrong on very small questions cost me 12 points, while the big questions were less than that. Maybe it was supposed to be easy points, but... Overall, I really enjoy learning linear algebra, it is really cool and the content is not awful. The programs can get a little sick, so go to office hours on Wednesday. The TA/peer help sessions weren't good for anything for me, they also didn't know what was going on. Overall the grading sucked but I liked linear algebra. I don't really care about my grade, so don't take my words too seriously.

Hated this class because how unfair everything was. There isn't a way to fix this but if you have never coded before, it is such a disadvantage since most people prob took ap comp sci in high school making all the test averages so high. The home work programs were also crazy time consuming and confusing so deff go to ilya's office hours. The linear algebra part was not too bad and I could understand it very well conceptually but the test itself was very very unfair timing wise and space wise. It was not even at all reasonable since you would flip the page and you had to take 10 orthogonal projections with 1 inch of working space. Making the final more conceptual was simple and understatement. They straight tup just made it impossible.

Definitely a hard class!!! Personally found it really challenging, Ilya is a great professor though, he explains the stuff quite well. BUT my discussion professor was horrible, I was so confused and had to watch all of Ilya's discussions afterwards, which was really time consuming, so just take Ilya's discussion.

I thought I would have more fun in this class than I ended up having. I felt like I understood a lot of parts of the class, then exams came and I didn't really know what I was doing. Focus on concepts and actually know what to do on a variety of situations and you should be okay. The final was nothing like what they gave us for practice. The content was actually kind of cool and I think if I figured out what I didn't know sooner, I would've done better in this class. Ilya is a solid lecturer, but he's pretty quick with how he gets through content, so look out for that.

The course is a little annoying and oddly structured, but mostly okay on a week–to–week basis. The weekly programs were sometimes frustrating, but honestly doable as someone who has never coded before, and the quizzes are fine. I think that lectures can be a bit fast–paced or you could end up not getting to certain topics, depending on how many people ask questions, so the textbook is a nice supplement.

EA1 is a course that is effective at taking you from the ground up to being at the levels of competence it expects you to be in regarding both MATLAB and Linear Algebra. However, the Linear Algebra section (post Midterm 1) moves incredibly quickly at around the halfway point, which can make it very difficult to stay afloat. The MATLAB portion can also give you a rough start if you do not have any prior knowledge in programming, but even an hour or so watching a tutorial in a simple language (even watching a Python tutorial on YouTube) would give you the fundamentals that you need to not get caught up in all of the syntax. There is also a tremendous amount of resources available from lecture notes, YouTube videos made by Prof. Mikhelson, the textbook, large blocks of office hours, and the Ed discussion board.
Very solid class, good teachers. Go to office hours!

The first few weeks of the class dealing with MATLAB are easy. However the 2nd half dealing with linear algebra is definitely challenging, and it doesn't help that the lectures move fast, Make sure to attend classes and study the content right after the class if you want to pass

EA 1 is a pretty challenging course. While homework assignments and quizzes are usually applications, the exams are very theoretical, requiring a deep understanding of the concepts. For prospective students, my best advice would be to NOT fall behind. The content is very vast and new material is introduced up until the very last lecture. Catching up is not easy. Nevertheless, with the ED platform, TAs and professors will answer to your questions in no time. The class offers all the resources you could possibly need to do well in the class.
Very engaging, but easy to get lost as the course builds on its concepts.

This course was very interesting to me and I liked the format of the large lectures and smaller discussions. However, the different discussion teachers were a little uncoordinated and I found myself having to watch all of Ilya's discussions after my discussion section. The weekly HW quizzes and programs were pretty time consuming and didn't really teach you much or show up a great deal on the exams. I would say it is pretty easy to fall behind in this class because there is only one midterm and one final for grades, so make sure to stay on top of the material even when you aren't being tested because it will pay off at the end of the quarter. Ilya was a very good teacher but went VERY fast during lecture. Overall, you will probably have to teach yourself a lot of material and rewatch a lot of lectures because the pace is pretty fast.

What you give this course is what you will get out of it. I stopped going to class around week 3 and would just watch the lectures online which helped me slow the content down as Ilya speaks very fast. If you do this though, don't get very behind on lectures. I did this and am now cramming for the final.

In the grand scheme of things, this course seems really hard because it is one of the first classes you will take at Northwestern. I dropped this course last fall and decided to take it again this fall to see if I could prove to myself I could do it. This definitely made a difference as fall quarter of your first–year is just extremely tough! So even though this course seems super awful from all the other CTECs you are reading, and you ultimately may not get the A you hope for, try to give yourself grace and focus on learning how to learn.
Start those programs and quizzes early in the week because your Thursday night self will thank you. And go to office hours!!!

This course is quite challenging and can move fast at some points. Really make sure you are staying on top of the work, and if you don't understand somethin,g try to figure it out right away instead of waiting for the Midterm/Final.
it was kind of confusing to have matlab and linear algebra in one class and i didn't really understand how everything fit together

The first part of the class's difficulty varies wildly based on your coding experience, but the linear algebra portion means most people are on fair ground. It isn't an insanely difficult class, but it'll probably be your second hardest class freshman year (behind Chem).
I would recommend staying locked in and attending lectures. It can be easily understood if you put the effort in. Conceptually, it is an interesting class, and taught well.

Ilya was a good professor and was very considerate. Matlab portion of this course was enjoyable. However, that final on linear algebra was insanely challenging since it was a curveball from what the practices looked like.
The midterms and the finals were easier if you did the homework program and quiz yourself.

This class was pretty good. The matlab portion was kind of boring, but the linear algebra was very interesting. A lot of this course is very vocabulary heavy. The discussions were pretty useful and some of the homework programs were kind of fun. Overall, I enjoyed this course.

You have to go to discussion because it is always new material. Try to be in Ilya's discussion. Lock in in lecture also. I didn't know how to code or do linear algebra so this was super difficult but there was help available and I would suggest doing a PGSG!
I thought it was good.

EA1 is on MATLAB and linear algebra. The first half is on MATLAB and the second half on linear algebra. There are weekly homework programs and homework quizzes. The second half of the course moves at a pretty fast pace, so make sure you keep yourself on track every week. Professor Ilya is always really patient if you have any questions. You can always go up to him during office hours or after class to get your questions answered. Overall this course is manageable. Just put in some extra time if you need to.

Freshmen, this will likely be your worst nightmare, especially if you do not code and have no experience in linear algebra. Let me explain why: This course has two parts, the first one is MATLAB and from week 4 or 5 onward you will only do linear algebra, but still weekly MATLAB hw. first, the matlab hw i absolutely horrendous. it was so hard that I just gave up on doing them and went to office hours (which btw ilya solves the whole thing there). linear algebra was alright but I just could not understand Ilya no matter how hard I tried. he is a very nice guy and he cares about teaching but his style of teaching is kind of all over the place and he does not go through the details and makes a lot of assumptions on what you know already. I gave up going to class and just wacthed MIT lin alg lectures like 2 weeks before the final, which worked pretty well for me. MATLAB, on the other hand, is a lost case an I still cant solve half a hw till this day.
Go to Ilya's office hours!!! He walks you through the homework program and it is very helpful. The lectures are fine but it's very easy to get lost or disinterested.
the linear algebra doenst go into depth enough

Ilya is amazing, I hope he will continue to teach EA 1. The course is very structured and well thought out. The homeworks are very challenging and take up IMMENSE amounts of time. The homework quizzes are a great tool to apply knowledge. Overall, nothing but amazing lectures by Ilya.
I impressed by the usefulness of linear algebra to engineering
This course was pretty straightforward, matlab for the first half and linear algebra for the second half. Ilya is a nice lecturer, go to his office hours!
Chill class if you know linear algebra. Otherwise, still not too difficult, just pay attention and ask for help when needed. Go to office hours.

I deeply enjoyed this course. The course was partitioned into two: a linear algebra section and a MATLAB section. Each section had its challenges and a wealth of resources. Both the midterm and final were discussed at length in the course.

The first half of the course is MATLAB, and the second half (after the midterm) is linear algebra. EA1 is easy if you have previous experience with coding and linear algebra. If not, you will have to put in more time and effort as the difficulty of the class increases quickly, especially for MATLAB.
I didn't really attend that much class after the second midterm, but the MATLAB part as someone who barely coded was difficult. This was a hard class.
They run it well, and there are plenty of resources. I believe most kids that felt it was too hard didn't take advantage of the resources.
Very easy class to fall behind. Labs are kinda useless. Homework programs are long but interesting.

Ilya is a great professor, very willing to answer questions. The course is difficult if you don't keep up to date with all of the content. Especially the linear algebra section of the course. The MATLAB portion is not too bad. At the very least, try to do all of the programs from the first half of the quarter without too much help. It will help a lot.

This class is difficult and takes a lot of your time! I highly recommend going to office hours for homework programs (especially Ilya's). The way both Ilya and Michael explain the material in a confusing manner but the textbook explained it more simply. Go to EVERY lecture, as you can get behind very quick. Don't take the class if you don't need to.
This course is taught in a way that makes it difficult to master either Matlab or lin alg. Go to office hours.

Since the class only had one midterm and then a final, it was bound to get put on the back–burner for that week or two of the second set of midterms. In order to do well on the final, make sure to recover from this quickly or do not let it occur. The final covered a lot of unexpected material as well. Hopefully, this issue will be resolved going forward since this was the first quarter running the new curriculum.
Don't slack off after the first midterm. You'll end up a month behind, skipping every class, and cramming for the final.

This class is very time–consuming and can be a bit frustrating at if you have no coding experience or haven't done any linear algebra. However, if you do all the quizzes, homework programs, go to class, and make an effort to actually understand the content, you an expect at least a B. Iliyad usually gives the homework answers during office hours, so make sure to go to those. Overall, he's a fun instructor and is always willing to help in any capacity. Sometimes, his lecture can be a bit confusing since he goes quite fast and the content itself is hard. So, again, I definitely recommend going to office hours, meeting with him after class, and rewatching the lecture. The averages for the exams tend to be in the C–B range, so the course instructor does usually curve towards the average.
Got difficult after MatLab

This course combines linear algebra and MATLAB in a way that makes it easier to learn either –– the MATLAB is easier to learn with concrete examples, and the linear algebra is more completely understood by translating math language into MATLAB language.
Course is meh. Matlab instruction is mid. Linear algebra instruction is ok and rushed.

This class was okay. In terms of content, the lectures did okay at explaining new concepts. I utilized a lot of outside resources to better my understanding of these concepts. One such source is "STEM Support" with their Linear Algebra series. I highly recommend this source if you are going to take this class, as it covers like 90% of what is gone over in class, in videos that are typically 10 minutes or less. Like no joke, the guy behind that channel is amazing. Regarding the MATLAB stuff of the course, I feel like the extent to which we covered MATLAB did not compare to the MATLAB programming required for the homework assignments. I think that I am speaking for like 95% of the people who went to EA 1 office hours, but most of us went there for homework help, which says a lot about the homework already. Like no joke, the lecture room that the office hours took place in were always full. They were so full that there were literally people sitting on the floor, leaning against the wall, and just sitting in places you weren't supposed to sit. So yeah, MATLAB homework assignments were no joke.

EA1 is a hard course if you have never programmed before, but there are plenty of resources to help. I came into this course with zero coding skills but managed to do decently. Always attend Prof. Mikhelson's office hours if you have any questions. Professor Mikhelson is very enthusiastic during lecture and always takes student questions. He seems very interested in making sure we understand each topic before we move on.

EA1 is a challenging but good course. The first part of the course focuses on MATLAB programming, which isn't too bad, and the second part of the course focuses on linear algebra which most people find to be a lot more challenging. I found the textbook to be a great resource in getting linear algebra practice and clarifying things. Make sure you know your theorems (Invertible Matrix Theorem, properties like det(AB)=det(A)det(B)). Professor Ilya is a great lecturer and super helpful during office hours. He's very passionate about the subject but talks super fast. The way that HW assignments are worded gets really confusing after the first assignment. Use EdStem to ask questions. You also don't get a lot of practice problems for the midterm and final, so you need to have a really good conceptual understanding of the material. It's also really easy to fall behind, so GO TO EVERY LECTURE! For this quarter, the average on the final was a 70%, and there's usually not a curve in this class, so take that as you will. Overall, keep up with the work and utilize resources like PGSGs and peer tutoring. It's a weeder class but it's doable.

EA1 is a class that is difficult conceptually. It will be half MATLAB coding and half linear algebra. It is easy to forget to study for, since you don't have weekly tests and only a single midterm. However, once you break it down, you'll realize it goes rather slowly. Go to Ilya's Office Hours for help with the programs, since those are the most difficult. For the linear algebra portion, read the textbook. It explains things very well and will help you conceptually. Join a PGSG for practice problems.

This class was pretty much split into two separate 5–week courses, where the first focused on MatLab coding and the second focused on Linear Algebra, with practically the only thing that ties the two being a singular coding assignment regarding matrices. I thought that the first section of this course to be a little challenging, but could be understood with enough effort. However, the second half of the course was much worse. The topics are more conceptual, the disjunct between the course instructors becomes obvious, and the coding becomes less relevant. As the course progressed this only got worse. The final exam, from the exam itself to the prep, was handled poorly. I did find that office hours were useful.

It's a fine class, but most people can't really avoid taking it. The first half where you focus all on MATLAB is definitely a lot easier if you have any programming experience (even in different languages). The second half is just linear algebra which I'm sure is also easier if you have taken it (I hadn't and it was fine). The weekly homework programs and quizzes can take some time but they're not horrible. The exams were pretty fair from what was covered in lecture/discussion. I'd recommend taking your discussion section with Professor Mikhelson because he's the main lecturer for the course.

Lot of matlab which I didn't like – most of the first half is solely matlab and homework programs due every week. The linear Algebra section is more in depth than if you took it in high school, but shouldn't be too difficult. Homeworks are super annoying to do and can be obnoxiously confusing some weeks
There's a lot of support given, only bad thing is that the homework take a lot of time

Ilya is amazing! He cares about everyone and is so patient when instructing and answering questions. HE LITERALLY ANSWERS QUESTIONS FOR MORE THAN 20 MINS AFTER EVERY LECTURES. Both linear algebra and MATLAB can be hard, personally I feel like linear algebra is harder since it has a lot of things intertwined together.

This is a challenging course however if you spend time on this course throughout the quarter there will be much benefit. It is helpful to review the topics covered in class before class becaouse the pase of classes is very fast. I personally didn't like how the coding and the linear algebra were combined. I don't think enough time was spent on linear algebra.
great intro course for getting linear alg done while also getting cs homework and introduction through matlab

Ilya teaches really well and you can tell he's very passionate about it. That said, they changed the structure of the course for the first time this quarter, and instead of 2 midterms and a final, we got only 1 midterm and a final–– that means that you have very few chances to improve your grades. The final exam was also a remarkably hard exam with the mean decreasing 10% from last year.

And don't get me started on the SYNTHESIS SHEETS!!!! They are so freaking annoying and don't help at all. Everyone has different ways of taking notes when learning concepts, and it just felt like unnecessary unhelpful additional work.
It's a difficult class but one that's fairly reasonable with enough studying.
love the prof smzing guy but i did not understand in the lectures i had to stop snd rewatch them

This course is very very challenging, especially if you don't have a background with coding or vectors, but its very rewarding. Ilya is very supportive but goes SUPER fast in lectures, so be prepared to self study alot. Knowledge from each lectures often builds on top of each other, so it does take alot of effort to keep up with the pace of the course.

this course is very challenging. If time and effort arent put i wouldnt have been able to pass it. However Ilya is one of the best professors. He is funny and engaging even though the class is really hard. He will take time to answer your questions and talk to you about anything you need.
This course was extremely challenging, especially the first midterm that consisted of MATLAB.

If you already know another programming language, Matlab will not be too bad. The programming is the part of the course that most people find the hardest, but with prior experience it is quite manageable. Personally, given how much code is given to you directly in the homework program pdfs and you often just follow steps of code to write, I didn't ever spend more than 5–10 minutes thinking about the algorithms used in the homework programs until the last 2 or 3 programs.

In terms of the linear algebra part of the course, I find that it is quite weird. I had an issue for the first 4 or 5 weeks of the course where I struggled to explain what EA1 was to my non–Northwestern (and even Weinberg) friends because while it is technically a linear algebra course, you barely learn any linear algebra until after the first midterm. But the actual content isn't too hard, with the exception of the vocabulary that has to be learned—you will quickly find that almost every lecture is just learning new words to describe things that you already know about intuitively.

In terms of instruction, Ilya is amazing, no notes. The discussion sections can vary, and it is important that you have a decent instructor (later on in the course, important topics such as the determinant are only covered during discussion section). I had Prof Honig who can be a bit boring and slow. I would sometimes zone out for 10–15 minutes, look up at the board, and still see the content he was covering the last time I was paying attention at the top of the screen (he hadn't filled a whole page). But his section fit best into my schedule and I barely had to walk from my last class to go to his section, so I continued going, even if it was a struggle to pay attention.

I swear to god GO TO CLASS, never skip. Make sure you understand the lecture material and hope not to get a final like we did. Illya is the best instructor for the course try to get him for discussion and class

Overall, the homework quizzes and programs every week were manageable, and the first midterm was extremely fair as it was significantly related to lecture/discussion/homework/practice exams. On the other hand, the latter portion of the course completely failed to set the expectations for what we were expected to know, and the final felt completely unrelated to how we had been applying the content beforehand.
Ilya is a very good teacher.
This class is though

EA 1 is extremely interesting and requires a heavy time commitment, and although I wasn't too interested in coding, Prof. Mikhelson is extremely passionate and won't hesitate to answer any question you have.

I really dreaded this course. I often stayed up late into the AMs trying to finish code, as although you'd have access to it for a week, it was usually almost impossible to do with no prior coding knowledge, forcing me to wait for office hours. That paired with the lectures that constantly left me in the dark, it honestly felt like I was wasting about 5 hours a week on class when I should've just been watching YouTube tutorials.

EA1 is quite challenging, especially for those without coding experience. Professor Ilya is absolutely amazing, though, and the way he explains things makes the challenging content much more manageable. Overall, it is fairly feasible to do well in the course as long as you use the resources available to you –– go to Ilya's office hours and review sessions, actually understand MATLAB instead of using AI / copying other people's programs, etc.

I found this course challenging but interesting and useful. I had no prior experience with programming but found learning MATLAB to be ok. Ilya always brings lots of energy to the lectures. Sometimes he speaks too fast, but his lecture videos are all on YouTube. In addition to the lecture videos, his old tutorials for MATLAB and linear algebra are extremely helpful if you are looking to review a specific topic. Office hours are helpful to resolve individual questions or work on the homework program.

However the course is barely curved, so your grade in the class is fully determined by your performance on the homework programs, homework quizzes, and exams.

Everything was fine until the final. The first midterm wasn't that bad. The quizzes and programs weren't that bad if you went to office hours. And then. The final.

When the passing grade for a class has to be a FIFTY, you know something has gone horribly wrong. The final was 15 pages, two hours––60 percent obscure theory, 20 percent long computations we didn't have the time to do, and 20 percent stuff we had actually encountered. Practically NONE of the lectures or assignments so much as mention theory. The exam was almost entirely just that, while every single practice material we were given for the final was practical applications (which is what we were taught). When most of the class cannot fathom what is on the exam and doesn't even know where to START, then that is fully the fault of the program. We clearly were not taught what they tested us on. The curve might have clutched up in the end, but I'm genuinely disappointed in the instructional content and the expectation that we would somehow absorb this out of thin air. Absolutely diabolical.

This course is a ton of content with 2 lecture and 3 discussion sections a week that are all new content. IIya is a tricky teacher with his pace of instruction but honestly a joy to have for lecture given his energy and passion. The exams are hard but not impossible if you have a deep conceptual understanding which involves taking time outside of lecture to wrestle with the content. The homework programs are super challenging and time consuming, but professor and TA office hours are a huge help.

I find it important to show up to the lectures, even if there are recordings. For your homework, the best way is to try it by your own first and then show up to one of Ilya's office hours, he usually goes through most of the code (or show up to both if you want to have a higher chance of getting most code). If you can get Ilya's discussion sessions, do so. I was in Professor Honig's discussion, he is always pretty on topic but every discussion is literally just going through a bunch of stuff and quite boring. I've heard Ilya is better. Also, Professor Honig doesn't gives hints on the homework, I've heard some other professors do. For the midterm, I found that some questions are exactly the same as the practice exam or very similar, so make sure you totally understand all problems on the practice exams. They actually do help you. The practice exams for the final were pretty easy but that actual final was super long and included types of problem that didn't show up in practices, like drawing.

As someone that didn't have much experience with math, I found the linear algebra portion of the course quite challenging. This course goes at an extremely fast pace, in both teaching programming and linear algebra. While Ilya is a good teacher, it is really easy to fall behind especially if you miss any classes.

It was a good introduction to linear algebra course, but I wish that we could have gone at a slightly slower pace at times so that the material would better sink in as a first time learner of linear algebra. I also wish that there wasn't as big a disconnect between the lecture's material and that of the discussion.

Ilya is a really great professor. The course itself is pretty fast paced, so definitely go to lectures, because Ilya does a great job explaining and answering any questions. Ilya's youtube videos are incredibly helpful for review. As someone who had absolutely zero coding background, I was able to pick up MATLAB, but it definitely is a bit of a learning curve. The homework programs can take a lot of time, but Ilya's office hours are super helpful if you're stuck on anything.

The class is pretty good, it wasn't my absolute favorite but it covered a lot of content in a really organized and well thought through way. I would recommend staying on top of it as the linear algebra section has a lot of vocab and content in general that isn't too bad, but taken all together can be overwhelming. The first half is entirely focused on Matlab, which was pretty easy for me but I did have previous coding experience. I heard it was a lot harder for some of my friends that were new to computer science, so be prepared to spend a little extra time on it for the first few weeks if that's you.
The resources provided by the instructor were really good. Despite the huge class, he was good at teaching and communicating with us all.

The course is a combination of linear algebra and coding that really helped me learn a lot. Although the midterm and the final especially were very challenging, I do feel like I learned a lot through this course.
I found the class pretty hard, having not taken lin alg or programming courses previously. Definitely a class I had to put lots of time and effort into, though I know some others find it easier.

My main complaint is that this course was way too fast paced. The discussions were meant to be review of the lectures, however they almost always taught new content, which defeats the purpose of a discussion. It felt like a constant race with no break to absorb the difficult concepts. However, once I began to understand linear algebra, I started to appreciate and enjoy it. I would recommend to anyone taking this course to watch Ilya's COVID videos, because not only are they shorted by almost half of how long lectures are, Ilya also goes slower and you will understand the concepts better. Most of what he teaches is covered in this videos, but just to make sure I would has a basic understanding of what he teaches that day.

this is a terrible course I hated it a lot. The quizzes and programs have nothing to do with the final, we get no lin alg practice. if it was structured like a lin alg course or a Matlab course it would have been fine, but together they're horrendous. Ilya's a nice professor but even if you go to office hours and stuff it legitimately sucks. Considering you take it usually fall of freshman year when you're adjusting, meeting people, etc the class is set up terribly. People who succeed probably spend 10 hours a week outside of class on textbook readings, really understanding the programs, office hours, etc.
I think the MATLAB in tandem with the quizzes really helped but having due dates at 6 AM was an odd experience.

Tough class, but required. First few weeks were okay, but suddenly the homeworks and quizzes become very hard to complete on your own. The lectures fly over new material and its basically impossible to understand the concepts in class unless you put in a lot of extra effort to study after each day. Generally, the matlab portion of the course was easier to follow than the linear algebra portion, but the coding becomes very hard in the last few weeks. Discussions were either so complicated it was hard to understand or so straightforward that it was not worth it.

Don't let this class lull you into a false sense of security. Finals week will hit, and you will discover that you didn't know what was going on the whole time. Ilya, as a teacher, is hard to follow, especially in lecture. If you can, take his discussion section. You'll end up watching his discussion videos anyway.
Dr. Mikhelson was an excellent instructor, and the course itself is well–paced and taught very accessibly.

definietly the hardest class i took this quarter. the assignments themselves aren't that bad, but once you get to the linear half the concepts are hard to grasp so quickly. ilya always lectured with a lot of passion so you can definitely tell he loves and cares about the class. however, he goes so incredibly fast during lecture i found it hard to keep up with taking notes and comprehending what he said. lecture recordings are posted, though, which helps a lot. definitely go to ilya's office hours if you are struggling with the weekly homework programs

Ilya is a great professor. He explains things well and if you go to office hours he is super helpful. However, the homework programs in the second half of the quarter were a mess. The instructions were unclear and the only way to complete them was to go to office hours. There was only one midterm this year and it was very easy. Final was difficult and very conceptual.

There is a good amount of content in this course. It is somewhat challenging, and it can be hard to keep up. Both discussions and lectures cover novel topics, and therefore, simply being distracted in either one of these will lead to a student falling slightly behind. In class, while Professor Ilya is absolutely well–versed in the topics, he may tend to get more in–depth, and in turn, make something feel more complex than it is. Pace during lectures is also somewhat fast. I do, nonetheless, think this course gives a fair taste of what pursuing engineering and being a part of McCormick is like.

The course consists of a lot of information within a short amount of time. It is important that students focus on understanding material straight away because the professors will move on to new content every single lecture. Other than that I found the linear algebra portion to be academically challenging but also extremely interesting! Overall the class can be challenging but also filled with content that is unique from other first–year classes, making it exciting.

Professor Mikhelson is a great professor. He's very nice, encouraging, and wants to help you succeed. His interest in teaching and the subject make it easier to focus on the class, even when the topics are hard. He does occasionally go through topics fast, but all the lectures are recorded, and rewatching them helps a lot for comprehension and studying. While the course was difficult, especially in the conceptual understanding aspects, going to office hours definitely helped a lot, especially with the homework programs. There are a lot of practice resources provided too, especially for exams, so take advantage of them. Edstem is also very helpful, since the instructors respond very quickly.

I thought that EA was a wonderful class. I really enjoyed the instruction from Ilya, and I think they did a great job sequencing the course. My one main issue with EA this quarter was that they made the final as if we were taking purely linear algebra, which was not the case, so a majority of the final did not relate to practice problems we had done. If you can look past the fact that they made the final worth 30% of your grade ridiculously hard because they gave us no practice for it, I really enjoyed the assignments in the course and felt that my scores accurately reflected what I did and did not know.

Prior to this course I had no coding background, and after I feel that I have a solid understanding of the basics of matlab and writing scripts. I also learned a ton about linear algebra and have come to find it incredibly interesting.

I was pretty excited to learn what MATLAB was used for, but over time ,the writing of the syntax of the language became repetitive (I should have stopped with the homework programs earlier in the quarter if not relevant to the final exam). I also enjoyed understanding the applications of linear algebra to machine learning, like linear regression.
Difficult course and hard to understand conceptually, tests were hard as well.

Professor Ilya is a great professor and really cares about what he is teaching. He does speak incredibly fast, so be braced for that. Also, go to office hours. They go over the homework, and if you are confused or need a nudge in the right direction it is extremely helpful.

This class is a mixed bag. I really enjoyed the linear algebra part of the course and felt I learned a ton through that. The MATLAB section felt a little monotonous and pointless. Case studies were interesting and engaging. The actual lecture of the class, however, was completely useless. Tech AUD is extremely uncomfortable and hard to learn in. There are also WAY too many people per lecture. The discussion sections were much much more helpful and Prem Kumar was great. I don't understand why the class cannot be taught completely through more discussions instead of the horrible lectures. Ilya was kind and memorable, however his pace and slides were not the easiest to follow. Also, the course coordinator, often was passive aggressive in communications which detracted.

Some people entered the course being very adept in Matlab and Linear Algebra concepts, while some students had never been introduced to them before. This benefits some students but really harms others and so placements tests should be given for this subject as well and students should placed accordingly.

lowk had to divert mid quarter to self study, and felt much better coming back. the course is fine if you already have a basis in lin alg or coding or both, but it's not the best at helping build up knowledge from the ground.

Class homeworks and lessons were very good and informative, midterm one was a fair assessment of what we had been learning so far throughout the year. However there was no second midterm unlike previous years and the final was the biggest joke of the professors’ lives and most of it was on problems we had no previous practice or preparation on.
Ilya is an amazing, amazing professor. A lot of material is also covered in the discussion sections, so you need to go to them (or at least watch the recording)

The first half of the course was all coding which was easy but the second linear algebra half was much harder. The final was nothing like what I expected and really hard. Since there was one midterm on coding and then the final on linear algebra I didn't know what to expect from it so I recommend studying a lot for the final and putting the effort in. Overall, I'd recommend taking this course but really locking in for the final and expecting it to be way harder than what we learned in class. Also for hws go to office hours it will make your life so much easier

It is a challenging class but it sets you up well for what is to come. If you have Ilya (sorry if I misspelled) he will talk very fast but he is awesome just try keeping up with what he is saying. If you tell him to explain something again or to show it again he will and is very nice about it. He always answers any questions you have before, during, or after lecture. I really recommend taking it with him as your professor.
The class itself is kind of confusing and unpleasant but there's no way to avoid it. Ilya is great though

This course is the hardest class I've taken in my life so far. Once you fall behind slightly, you'll be lost for all new topics introduced. There are so many technical terms to know that they become jumbled together and unclear as to what we're actually trying to solve or learn.

Ilya is an excellent lecturer, and he also posts videos on Canvas to explain every topic. The only major work outside of class is the weekly homework program, but it doesn't usually take more than a couple hours.

I love this course. Granted, I've had a big head start in linear algebra so there wasn't too much struggle, but I felt like there was minimal busy–work and that everything was adequately challenging and engaging.

The course material is much easier to understand if you're in MATH 228 by the time you take it. Otherwise, it will be a challenge to understand the point behind ideas. The first half of the course is only learning to program in MATLAB, so if you don't have programming experience it is advisable to begin self–learning the language online before arriving as you will have to learn it in about 3 weeks (the MATLAB website has a free "on–ramp" course that can help with this once you activate your account from NU). Once the linear algebra side kicks in, it will be more difficult to keep up as the content is not presented in the most friendliest or simplest manner so you'll have to decipher it yourself (it's also recommended to watch 3blue1brown and Professor Ilya Mikhelson's own explanations on YouTube). If possible, enrolling in the corresponding peer guided study group would be a wise decision to help with learning.

This class is quite challenging. I though the linear algebra portion was quite difficult so never ever fall behind or your thanksgiving break and finals week will be hell. Make sure you do the homework cause theyre on the exams
It was super good and Ilya is a great proffesor.

This course helped me learn a lot, but I don’t think it was structured in the best way. The first half of the quarter is all MATLAB, and then the second half switches to linear algebra. After the first midterm, the pace sped up a lot. This year we only had one midterm and one final, plus the homework programs and quizzes. Once we got to the linear algebra part, it felt a bit unorganized because different discussion professors were covering different things at different paces, so it didn’t really feel like everyone was on the same page. My biggest tip is to go to Professor Ilya’s office hours, they were super helpful.

The course was very disorganized with topics not connected to each other from the different lectures to the point where I was confused for 80% of the year on what we were doing. There seems to be no real course plan and how the ideas were connected to each other was not evident. It was incredibly difficult and fast paced while the actual content was not too difficult. Reading the textbook in a week is honestly more helpful than 2 months of lectures.
Good course, MATLAB is an easy enough subject but you have to stay on top of the linear algebra portion. Once you start to fall behind, it can be pretty hard to catch up.
definitely utilize office hours, ilya is an amazing instructor, and genuinely wants his students to do well

It's confusing if you start with zero linear algebra knowledge. Start revising early and make sure to prepare before lectures/discussions, because the instructors use complicated language and rely on advanced understanding of pretty abstract concepts. The homework programs are very difficult, but the office hours with Ilya were a lifesaver. Quizzes are hard too, but when you do them carefully, they help you learn a lot. Overall, the content is very messy – I didn't like how the case studies were randomly squeezed in between the basic LA topics. I just didn't really understand what was going on. MSP was amazing.

Ilya is a wonderful instructor who loved answering students' questions. For the MATLAB part in the first half of quarter, I would say it's not hard if you have learned AP CSA or coding stuff. For the linear algebra part, compared to linear algebra course in my high school, he broke down concepts into small details and drew images to build connections between related topics. HOWEVER, it also led to a super difficult final (we do not have midterm2 this year, so final really matters) that contained huge amounts of content and challenged us in a crazy way. I'm not sure about the curve policy, but for this required course in Mccormick, be prepared to spend time on homework programming and exams.

The exams in this class were some of the most difficult ones I have taken at Northwestern. Most of the time I had no idea what was going on during lectures. The material isn't actually that difficult (especially the linear algebra), but it is presented in a very daunting and unintuitive way that makes the course more challenging than it should have been. The homework quizzes were extremely helpful, but the homework programs really didn't help me once we got past the Matlab unit.

This class is a baptism by fire into McCormick. Please please please learn how to take good notes. And do practice problems in the textbook. And go to office hours and drop–in tutoring. If you have minimal programming experience (like me), you will be in for a nasty shock when you see your midterm grade. Don't be afraid to ask questions!!!

The class is kind of a mess. The first half being taught in matlab includes many theoretical problems with hand waved algorithms and are deeply confusing without a background in linear algebra. Switching from coding to conceptual linear algebra was deeply confusing, and I felt like I had to play catch up all quarter. The final was nothing like the practice and felt intentionally unfair.

The first half of the course is all Matlab, so the last half is very fast–paced and crams a bunch of linear algebra. Definitely study the linear algebra outside of lectures and discussions (with Ilya's videos).

The content in this class isn't too difficult and the workload isn't bad either but the final exam was really difficult compared to what was taught in class. Following the textbook and doing textbook problems can really help you succeed for the linear algebra portion of the class. Actually doing the homework will also help you keep up with the content every week and prevent you from falling behind.
All the lin alg is in one final. so this is a bright lights merchant moment. be prepared to LOCK IN

The homework quizzes were usually good ways to learn, but the associated programs were sometimes not as useful. The last 3, which involved creating an alternative to rref, finding the least squares solution of a system, and finding a basis for the null and column space, were not as helpful because those are things we'd typically be doing by hand on the final. The programs themselves were enjoyable to attempt, and going to office hours/drop in peer tutoring to complete them helped acquaint us with these resources. I'm very glad there aren't 2 midterms anymore, that would definitely be excessive for this class. The lab assignments that we had to do on Fridays were not necessary as assignments in my opinion. All they did was serve to distract you from the HW quiz and program with just another small test that you have to take. Those discussion Fridays could be better used as actual discussion sections, like they are after the Lab assignments are complete, where you have a TA you can talk to while working on that week's quiz or program.

Most everything (lectures / discussions) are online, but I would definitely recommend going to class and being present to make sure you engage. Ilya focuses on concepts and linear algebra, while discussions focus on MATLAB in the first half and other LA concepts in the second half. Ilya is a very enthusiastic lecturer and he is clearly knowledgeable, but he does go very fast. He is also great at answering questions and very open to them. However, due to his pace of lecturing, you may need to spend time rewatching lectures or using the textbook. Ilya will always go back to something or slow down if you ask him to, so please do that if you missed something, and definitely ask questions if confused. It will save you so much understanding in the long run.

The course used MATLAB which is a very basic coding structure and good if you've never coded before, but I found it annoying at points and I'd have rather used something more relevant like Python. The homework programs can take a sneaky long amount of time, so use the resources available to you whether it be your peers or office hours. Also check the ed (it;s the courses open discussion) for any questions you may have. Even if you don't have a question you should check to see if something got clarified there that may be relevant.

The linear algebra topics can for sure be intellectually challenging, so you should use all the available resources to understand the concepts. There is also a good amount of overlapping terminology, so really focus on what everything means. The quizzes will focus more on these topics and provide good practice for exams.

All in all, it's a solid course that definitely requires a good bit of effort, but it isn't overwhelmingly difficult.

EA 1 is an interesting course. Ilya is a good professor who gives good explanations, but if you don't study the material as it's being taught in parallel with the class, you will get lost. Also, in the lectures, Ilya kind of talks like he's about to explode–– that is, he talks very fast. He clearly cares about his students' success. I recommend keeping up with the material while taking this class, or you will pay for it come the final.
This class is hard, but Ilya is the goat.

The course covered the basics of linear algebra and MATLAB, both of which were new to me. The content was divided into two segments: the first 4 weeks before the first midterm were only MATLAB, and the rest was linear algebra. The content is not extremely difficult, but you need to keep track of the course to make sure the material doesn't pile up, because you won't have much time to cover it.

Its not that bad, just do the homework programs early and actually learn to do the lin alg earlier as you go along because its hard to learn it right before the final. Matlab portion is not hard if you've programmed before, but the midterm is like 70% syntax from what it felt, and the rest was actual code and how it worked. Linalg is just learning that one equation is everything in lin alg and that every class you learn somthing new that means the same thing as you learned last class but just with a different name or something. If you skip lecture just watch the videos on canvas. Those are cramming lifesavers.

Ilya was a great professor for this course, his enthusiasm for the content of the class was great to witness. The actual course content is quite difficult, but attainable to complete if you collaborate with peers and discuss with TAs and go to office hours.

I was pleased by how in depth and applicable the EA homework programs were. I enjoyed that they went real in depth on topics like markov chains and transformations, and taught us matlab skills that could be easily applied to real engineering problems.

EA–1 is definitely not an easy course. You will have to put in a lot of time outside of class to keep up with the fast pace Ilya sets and rewatch lectures and discussion sections often. The MATLAB portion is really like learning a new language, so if you haven’t ever coded before, which I hadn’t, then this course will be super challenging. Ilya is a really good teacher, though, and gives you a lot of resources and opportunities to learn the material before exam time.
If you are a McCormick, you have to take it. It is not as bad as people say. Ilya is a great professor. You have to make enough time commitment for this class.

This is just a general engineering introduction course that is required so there is not much interesting about it. It is hard and you need to put a lot of work into it, but it is manageable as long as you keep up with the work and do well on the homeworks. It is definitely a weed–out course and is challenging so be prepared. I felt the concepts mightve not applied to some majors but we still had to take it. The professors are great and know the topics very well.
Linear algebra was interesting

I didn't like the EA 1. Didn't feel like I learned a lot. The lecture was bigger than it should be; the exercises that they gave were not very helpful in teaching and understanding material. The course being divided into three sections was not helpful. The course felt like I shouldn't go to class and should study by myself. Because whenever I was in the lecture, I didn't understand anything. I feel like the case studies were too detailed, and I didn't feel like I learned stuff. I wouldn't recommend taking this course if you are not in McCormick least favorite class of this quarter.

The course content did not seem overly difficult, however, the assignments sometimes took way more time and were due at a strange time being 6am on friday. The final also seemed to be very difficult compared to the midterm and practice exams given.

I actually really liked this class. If you have had prior computer science experience, the first part of the course (matlab) is pretty easy. If you haven't it's definitely more challenging. I would really recommend taking a Peer Guided Study Group for this class. Mine helped by reviewing the material taught each week with some practice problems. I liked this class, Ilya is great.

This is a class where you absolutely have to understand concepts from the beginning, or you get lost on the way. You cannot just memorize things, you actually have to understand things conceptually. I would not recommend as the homeworks were very hard, along with the midterms and final and it was overall a very rigorous and tough class. You have to go to office hours to understand things more.

This course is rigorous and requires active participation for success. Office hours are a godsend, but no alternative to a strong understanding of the material. The material can be difficult to grasp if you don't have a background in MATLAB or Linalg, but there are ample opportunities to seek help, and to apply concepts as a means of learning. A conceptual grasp of ideas, more than a merely practical grasp, is helpful.
Ilya is a great instructor. However, I felt the content could have been presented in a more organized manner.

This class was very interesting to me. I entered not having any knowledge of any of the topics, so I learned it completely from scratch. Also, all the topics we learn are very basic linear algebra topics, where there are a lot of videos on youtube that you can watch. I found that Ilya is completely willing to answer any questions, and it's clear that he loves teaching. Also, all engineers are taking this class, so don't be afraid to lean on them and ask them questions because it can help all of you.

The teaching team does minimal, most of which I would pin on Henry Abrahamson, the course coordinator, because he seemed highly disorganized. Ilya's lectures were boring and slow, but you needed to know the material, so I just read the textbook. Everyone gets free answers in office hours so it's more difficult if you actually try to learn the stuff yourself, like I did, and do the homeworks independently. Not crazy though, just get through it.
I didn't like many of the professors' teaching styles; they weren't very enthusiastic or detailed, apart from Ilya, and even then, he speaks very fast, so sometimes I have a hard time processing.
Just a bunch of memorization for the different ways to describe a matrix in lin alg, and the case studies are often rushed through except for the first one.

This course is a hard and a pain. The homework programs and quizzes are stressful because its basically do it yourself with very little instruction as the matlab programs aren't reflected in discussion or class. Go to Ilya's office hours!!! He gives homework program answers. The matlab section of the class wasn't bad, but linear algebra is hard and you will fall behind. You have to spend a lot of time outside of class understanding as the class moves super fast, but when you understand all of it, its not too bad. He assumes you know a lot more than you do and his teaching style didn't work for me, but he really is great and willing to help. My discussion class didn't help at all, so I just watched Ilya's videos in slow–mo to help understand.

Go to office hours. In general, the class is kinda just hard. Its definitely not actually the hardest material, but its fast paced. Also, to me, Matlab isn't as intuitive as other coding languages for me, and linear algebra felt like we were rushing as it only took up like five or six weeks.

source_file: Student Report for Arantzazu Alarcon-Fleming (GEN_ENG_205-2_23  Engineering Analysis  II)_bdc2ff7b-056d-450b-9322-e5a32fa1fbf2en-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
