comments: I went into EA2 after not liking physics in high school, and have come out with a much more positive view of physics. It is a somewhat challenging class, but if you put in work the concepts do make sense.
Great class, very interesting. Homeworks and projects take a lot of time. Can be very difficult depending on the professor. Easier than EA1
I thought this class was great for applying high school physics concepts.

This class challenge me intellectually, and I feel like the professor was well–equipped to teach the students well. I think this course is a rite of passage for engineers as even if the class isn't relevant to the McCormick major, it develops the skills that each McCormick student should have going forward.

The course is challenging but it is definitely doable if you put in the time. The vast majority of your grade is based on the exams so it is important to study for them. There is plenty of time to take care of homework and it is helpful for studying concepts.

This course with Alacron was great. She spoke with a lot of passion and is just a great person overall. This course requires a lot of work outside of the psets, and you can do just fine if you put in the effort.

The content is fairly boring and not too interesting to me. The idea of doing something like this class for my major made me switch out of civil engineering. That being said, the application of the content in the exams and problem sets was very challenging. This would have been very enjoyable if I had a lighter load this quarter or better time management skills.
Interesting subject and easily broken down. Did need some studying outside of class to keep up. Helpful to have AP Physics C credit.
Content is not hard, but going through problems takes time and you need to do many problems to succeed. In other words, It takes a great deal of good time management to do well.
Please just go to class the content is not bad at all if you just attend class.

Overall: There is just so much to say about this class. I'm sure you have heard people say “EA 2 is WAY better than EA 1” or that it's much easier. I too heard this and it raised my hopes too much. If you haven't taken a physics class, this class is insanely difficult. Even if you have taken physics; if it isn't your calling this class may suck for you. It's taught in a way that assumes you understand the basics of physics, so if you don't it's really hard to keep up. The quarter system and the grading scale also add another level of stress to this class. The first midterm is worth 15%, second is 20%, and the final is

45%. Lectures are not recorded unless they are hosted online due to weather. Professor: Putting my hatred of physics aside, this class would have been 1000 times worse if i did not have Professor Alarcon. She is an AMAZING professor and very understanding. If you come to her for help, she will help you. She will point you in the right direction and go over concepts as many times as you need it as long as you show that you are putting an effort in. From what I have heard her midterms are typically harder than other professors, but since everyone gets the same final the final feels easier. She also has videos for some of the later topics which was helpful. TAs and Office Hours: The office hours truly saved me. It was the best way to get the homework done and have my answers checked. Like every class, some of the TAs were really unhelpful, so watch out for them. Tips: Look through the textbook, find weird problems and do those. GO TO RECITATION and understand what's going on. One of our midterms had an exact problem from recitation AND one of the book's example problems so make of that what you will. Make time to study. This is all to say that this class will be really fun for some and absolute torture for others but none of that is Alarcon's fault so take it with her because she's the best!

This course is not easy. Physics is generally a hard topic to study, but Professor Alarcon is an incredible professor. She is so kind and approachable on top of being a good lecturer, as well. She provided us with many resources, including notes, practice problems, and practice tests. It is so clear that she wants her students to succeed and is so willing to help, so I'd recommend taking this class with her. Her tests are also fair, and she is willing to meet with you one–on–one if you need clarification on any of the topics from class.

For people who took AP physics mechanics in highschool, this course isn't the exact same but will feel extremely repetitive. Alarcon is a great teacher but is extremely slow with her teaching. I would suggest if you have taken physics before to take it with Huang or another faster professor as Alarcon takes her time very slow to go through every detail. If you don't have prior physics experience then Alarcon is an awesome teacher.
It is easier and than ea1

Professor Alacron is super nice and so approachable. My friends and I would even eat lunch with her in Sarge on some days. She definitely knows what she is doing and is very knowledgeable about statics. A bit disorganized at some points and teaches some concepts in convoluted ways, but despite that, I would recommend taking it with her because she is willing to help you understand concepts that you are confused about.

EA 2 is not as difficult as EA 1, but one must be professional in submitting homework, projects, and exams. It is imperative that they are submitted on time and in the correct format otherwise, you will be dropping a lot of marks.
This class was not too bad, the topics were understandable

Not too hard, not that easy, the course was manageable. Just do lots of practice and you'll get used to it. The professor goes really slow in class, step–by–step. Definitely just do your own practice with past year papers, textbook, recitations, or homeworks and you'll be fine.

it's alright, it's physics. alarcon is a good teacher when it comes to explaining questions, loved being in her class and she's wonderful. i found the textbook helpful because class time was largely just walking through practice problems, which i'm not a huge fan of when it comes to being able to do the material myself—not a fault of the teacher though, that's just how the class is. every class average is curved to a B, so that's something to keep in mind.

good class. i liked it (i'm a civi major). class curved to a B. lots of cee tutors and ta office hours which is super helpful and accessible. prof. is like a mom, she's nice but ive heard other people said she's strict with grading so make sure to follow the instructions for how she wants you to solve the problems (draw fbd etc). if you have ap physics experience, it will be better for you.

The class is mostly a review of physics mechanics with a few engineering topics as well. The homeworks can be long and are very tedious. Professor Alarcon doesn't post her notes and bans photography in her class, so if you miss a lecture or are slightly behind you get punished. This policy seems to be punitive towards students who skip class and does not actually promote student learning. Overall a pretty poor class but not complicated stuff.

Professor Alarcon is amazing. She is very engaging and passionate about the concepts. Also, I had to have surgery in the middle of the quarter, and she was very understanding and even checked in on me after my midterm (the week after my surgery). She truly cares about her students.

Class wise, I enjoyed the class. It was very focused on equilibrium (sum of moments = 0 and sum of forces = 0). Do lots of practice problems in advance of exams (i.e. redo homeworks, recitations, or just do problems in textbooks). The questions are very systematic, so you need lots of practice to get the process down right. Timing on the midterms can be tricky; once again, doing lots of practice makes sure you can finish in time.

This class is basically AP Physics C with 3D systems and some other topics. You learn a new topics every day, so it's important to keep up. I recommend not leaving the homework until the day before, beecause they take 5+ hours to complete in one sitting. The final is 45% of your grade though, so it really makes or breaks it, Alarcon doesn't post the answers to the examples she does in class, and she doesn't record lectures. You also aren't allowed to take photos of the board, which makes it harder to retain information and wrote notes at the same time.

I love Professor Alarcon! She explains everything in a concise, easy–to–understand way. Plus, she's always so open to questions and never makes you feel dumb for asking them. She also takes the time to get to know everyone on a personal level, despite having so many students!
I thought that all around this was a decent course

Professor Alarcon is a very passionate teacher but that passion, unfortunately, overshadows her ability to effectively communicate complex ideas. It often feels as though she jumps from topic to topic without fully outlining the methods or definitions we need to solve a problem. You will find yourself relying heavily on the textbook. Also, I recommend going to office hours early in the week to get the help you need on the homeworks –– very helpful.

If anyone tells you EA2 is the easiest one, they are 100% lying. It is the worst class I have ever taken. Alarcon is kind of condescending and teaches like a high school teacher, if you even try to take a picture of the board she will say no (she doesn't record her lectures so it is such a drag to go every day). It is 10% homework, 10% project, 15% midterm 1, 20% midterm 2, 45% final, and she doesn't even write the final. It is also curved so the average is a 3.0. Class is tedious and goes over the same thing every time, we relearn things we learned in ea1, projects are matlab, and annenberg is depressing.
EA 2 is a good class as long as you attend the lectures and do a little bit of the weekly homework everyday.
This is a good course(better than EA). Proffesor Alcaron is nice and makes sure she explains everything. She also gives mother vibes which is good

I don't think this class should be required for all engineering students. The first few chapters on forces and equilibrium are already taught in physics, which I think is a more useful requirement if it isn't already for all engineering majors. Trusses and frames aren't really relevant to engineering besides civil and environmental engineering and probably architecture.

EA 2 is the bane of my existence. The way she teaches the concepts is so round–a–bout and needlessly confusing. Also this class is so stupid and unnecessary for my major. Like when will I ever need to know how much force is in a truss member or pulley as a matsci major like??? Waste of my time and happiness and I want it back.

Alacron is Fantastic. Her exams are definitely the hardest among EA –2 Professors, but her teaching methods and enthusiasm are without doubt one of the best I've seen so far here at Northwestern, she is really dedicated to helping students and offering extra support or answering far–fetched questions. If you are taking EA–2, Alacron should definitely be a professor you should consider taking with.
Quite similar to high school physics. Gets a bit tedious to do endless cross–products and systems of equations. nothing to complain about tho.
Go to class

I was expecting to participate in a challenging class, but Alarcon definitely made my experience more enjoyable. The class was still difficult, but her energy definitely uplifted my mood during lecture.

Prof. Fleming is a good professor and cares about her students. Course is not too bad. The final being worth 45% of your final grade is a little crazy and the midterms and material covered is slightly different across the sections. I found the class not too challenging. The homeworks are a time drain, especially the earlier assignments and being only 10% of the final grade is a little crazy. Okay course in hindsight.
it's hard. do pratcie problems. she's a good teacher. idk what else there is to sqy.
Lots of thinking but the math itself is not hard
ALACON IS AMAZING please take this class with her

This course is definitely on the easier side however it still provides its challenges. I felt like the material was not very hard to learn and much of the class was about applying this knowledge to the questions. I feel that this class is well structured and feels like you have to actually think about approaching problems rather than trying to catch up on material.

Alarcon can be a bit confusing at times when she's explaining content; she's a very enthusiastic teacher and I can tell that she's passionate about the material, but sometimes she doesn't finish example problems in class to leave up to us to figure out on our own time or doesn't write critical information on how to solve the problem down on the blackboard so I ended up confused. She also writes ridiculously light, so good luck trying to see anything if you're sitting anything further back than the first row. Some of my peers dropped this class because of how they found the content difficult to understand despite going to lecture and using the study materials.
If I did not know that everything would go into equilibrium, I would have gone crazy
I learned a lot from this course. This course was very challenging

I don't know if this is a hot take, but this was harder than EA1 for me. Actually, I wouldn't necessarily say that the content was harder, it was just harder to get through.

First of all, Alarcon is great! If you're taking EA2, I would definitely recommend you take it with her. She's a very engaging lecturer, super fun and she teaches the content well. She also posts good notes and resources on Canvas.

Overall, though, EA2 was just kind of a drag for me. The content is absolutely not necessary at all for my major, and I wasn't really interested in physics coming in either. It feels like doing the same few things over and over, until they throw a curveball your way and suddenly you don't know how to do it anymore. Even though I know statics is probably useful, everything was taught kind of in a vacuum.
Alarcon is very nice and cares about her students. I had a hard time with some of the concepts but they started to click after doing lots of practice questions.
The course not only focused on mechanics but also on display of work. How you display your homework and exam answers had a heavy emphasis and was an integral part of the course.

TAKE THIS CLASS WITH ALARCON. She is a great professor and will explain things well. If you've taken AP Physics C in HS before, some of the early concepts and friction especially should be very easy. However, trusses/frames are absolutely the hardest part of the course, and you should definitely go to lectures and recitations to understand these.
Calculation–wise, it is easy, but this course was harder than expected to understand if you don't have a physics background. Alarcon is nice and has high expectations for her students.

I don’t know about others here, but I feel this course was easier than EA1 – at least in terms of the material being taught. Physics I feel is naturally intuitive and far less abstract than linear algebra, so if that’s your style, that will be a plus. This class focuses on statics, so you’ll learn basic things like forces and vectors, then later on moments, trusses, and friction.

What I think makes people say this class is harder than EA1 is that you expected to learn this material and do it FAST – the exams feel practically like IQ tests. The first couple of midterms are 50 minutes long, and the final is two hours. Since these problems frequently get you bogged down in calculations, this really isn’t a lot of time and if you don’t know what to do in the moment, you’re kind of out of options. This makes your grade in the class pretty volatile – homeworks are only 10% of your grade, the rest of being almost entirely exams (15% for midterm 1, 20% for midterm 2, and 45% for the final exam). I don’t think the weighing of this makes much sense at all – the final being 45% is especially frustrating and stressful. Thankfully the problems aren’t typically super hard, but again you just have to move fast. The grades for these exams also very a lot from section to section – the midterms are all different from each other of course, and there have been times where one section has a 61% average and another a 90%. You just have to hope you get lucky – either way things theoretically equalize in that the average score is curved to a B.

In typical EA fashion, you aren’t really given the resources necessary to prepare (only one practice test is given out ahead of time) – thankfully the problems very closely mirror or are even taking directly off of textbook problems – simply going and practicing text problems is the best way to study.

In regards to Professor Alarcón–Fleming, I found my experience with her a mixed bag. Yes, she is very kind – even makes an effort to memorize every single student’s name. Her teaching style though was hit or miss for students – she’d typically begin explaining concepts, but often time to spend most of the class going through example problems. Some of us thought she should’ve explained concepts more deeply first. She is also not good at answering questions – I don’t know if she just doesn’t know what we’re talking about or what, but many times she just fails to give us clear answers– too often you’d just get back something kinda unrelated/indirect, or she’d even flat–out tell you to try/figure it out yourself. This has happened even with simple questions. I still think you could very well. Enjoy your experience with her – I certainly wouldn’t call her a bad teacher, I guess her teaching style just doesn’t match with everyone.

Prof Alarcon is amazing. You can tell she cared from the fact that she memorized almost everyone’s name in that huge class. Putting this class in Annenberg and not providing Panoptos were a big all time low from the engineering department imo. But the professor and the class is great!

I thought this course was harder than EA1 simply because the content was much much simpler and therefore much harder to test on. This made the exams much more difficult and a terrible measurement of genuine knowledge of the topics.

THIS CLASS SUCKS. No matter which professors you take it with, its just so meh. With that being said you should take it with the professor that has the easiest midterm exam: aka do NOT take it with Huang. However, the final is all the same for all classes and its made by Huang, so take that into account. I had Alarcon, she's clearly passionate about the subject, but that does not translate into a good professor. She teaches so slowly and makes all the concepts more confusing than needed. All she does in class is just do examples, and the answers to those examples are already online. She is also very high school teacher vibes: very patronizing, unecessary strict about the smallest details, and does not actually answers your confusion/questions. I suggest just don't go to class and watch youtube on topics.

If you are taking this class, then it's probably required for you. If not, WHY. So I can't really recommend you to not take this class, but here are some tips and tricks. There is allegedly an answer key to all hw questions somwhere online, FIND IT, IT WILL HELP YOU SO MUCH. The discussion section is pretty useless. Do as much practice problems as possible. Good luck!

This course has a relatively low time commitment. Homeworks aren't too long (less than one hour generally), projects aren't too bad either (maybe three hours each if you are efficient). The lectures weren't very informative but the professor was nice and somewhat engaging.
This first midterm is much easier than the second. The material really picks up both in speed and difficulty after the first midterm.
MATLAB projects don't help you understand any of the material in the slightest, they're nothing but busy work. Class is overall fairly challenging.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments:
It's quite fun I would say, imo the content is way more interesting than EA2, and I had a lot of fun with diffeqs. For the last part of the course we learned how to map electrical systems to mechanical systems and vice versa, but apparently there is little real–world application involved in here and quite useless. I had fun tho. Professor Sun is a very good instructor, and the learning experience in his class is very enjoyable. Exams aren't difficult and are, mostly, quite reasonable, though I do have some opinions on them.

I did decent on the first exam and really good on the second one, but the third exam was very, VERY long and the practice exams provided were quite misleading. I would say I know the content of this class very well, maybe even better than the average EA3 student, but I unfortunately got nerfed from an A to potentially a solid B because I wasn't able to finish the last exam.

Aside from that it's a decent class.
I found this course quite interesting—honestly, much more so than EA2. I just wish there was more practice material available.

Not a particularly interesting class, not easy to understand at times, but the tests were much easier than the homework, so there's that? I feel like the relative ease of the tests didn't incentivize me to work to understand what I was confused on, especially because the class isn't terribly relevant to my major.
The content is very easy in hindsight, but with the course moving quickly and the course being so disorganized, it can be hard to know what is going on.

EA3 is a strange course. The course is weighted on 3 exams (75% total, 25% each) and homework assignments (25%). I felt that this course's content was quite tricky, but going to office hours made things clearer. Prof. Sun was not a bad instructor, I just think the content is conceptually difficult to follow. Also, exams and concepts taught vary by class. My best recommendation is that you go to office hours for homework and try to do the past quizzes from your professor.
na

I would take this class with Professor Sun if possible. His exams are ok if you know the content, and his teaching is much more intuitive and helpful than other professors (granted, this is based on things I've heard, since I only had him as my professor). This class also has a ton of practice exams, which is nice, but do understand that some of the practices are probably from trailer EA which is way easier, so the actual exams are much harder.

I had Professor Sun; he was passionate in teaching and provided clear explanations for the concepts through analogies. Combine his instruction with review from the textbook and it's a solid learning experience. The last few weeks of the class focused on circuits, so if you have prior AP Physics C E&M background experience, it makes it much easier. Otherwise the differential equations were kind of thrown at you, and because this is not a diff eq class, they were limited in difficulty and scope.

There is no formal textbook and no practice problems offered outside of homework and some past exams so it is difficult to practice and study. Prof. Sun is pretty good at explaining topics, but the class may still be difficult for those with less prior physics experience due to lack of resources. Weekly homeworks are pretty manageable although MATLAB sections can be long and repetitive.

This course is very challenging, but make sure to look through the wiki and read all of the course material prior to class, because in class more of what is happening is practice of the content in the wiki, they dont teach the material as in depth in class, but practice is very useful for this course. i find the most effective way to study is go through all of the practice quizzes.

Sun was a great professor. Did a great job explaining the content and assessments were more reasonable compared to what I've heard about other sections. EA3 is weird since you need to use DiffEq's but you don't learn them until EA4, but Sun was very clear in explaining the differential equations concepts which are important for this course

I found the class a little difficult because not much content is usually covered in class. The textbook that we have to read before each class is also not easy to follow. In class, we went over the same examples for multiple days, which were not very helpful. You have to be responsible for doing more practice problems to prepare for exams, however the professor was very willing to help when you ask.

I knew I did not like physics going into this class and I did not like it any more leaving this class. Prof. Sun isn't exactly the best lecturer, but his tests were generally very fair (sounded better than most of the other professors at least). The web textbook is actually pretty well written and helpful for this class.

Some people said this was easier than EA3. That actually ended up being true in my case, but more so than the other EAs, your experience depends a lot on who your instructor is. Best to do some research before picking a section.

The class itself is all about dynamic systems – it's taught intuitively by taking about mechanical systems first (which involve the movement and behavior of real life objects like springs, masses, and dampers), and then transitioning to differential equations and lastly electrical system (which, while pretty different at first glance, has a lot in common with the behavior of mechanical systems). If your like me and didn't learn about dynamic systems going in, this class can be genuinely intriguing at points – it's not at all like the static systems of EA2 and AP Physics Mechanics. The tough part of this course is just in trying to understand this world of dynamic systems – it sits in this weird middle ground between being abstract and naturally intuitive. EA1 was far more abstract than this, but in that class you could just throw aside all your natural logic/intuition and focus on the rules of linear algebra to get a sense of how that realm works. EA2 was on the other end of the spectrum; real life logic/intuition could carry you to a fair degree, and you didn't have to rely as much on all the rules. EA3 has some aspects where it's best to just look at the rules taught to you to solve, but other aspects where natural intuition is kinda necessary to figure things out. That's what can make it tough at first, but you end up figuring things out in the end though.

Prof. Sun I think was definitely the easiest, at least compared to Lynch and Keys. He certainly wasn't the best teacher though – he's nice, not intimidating, happily answers any questions you've got, but he's not that good at explaining things. The TAs in our section also weren't that great at explaining either. Lots of class lectures weren't quite as organized and weren't really possible to follow and internalize fully – as much as it sucks, looping back to the Panopto recordings might be a good idea. But if you really wanna get the most out of lectures and stay on top of things, make an effort to do the webtext readings (AT A SLOW, COMFORTABLE PACE) as well as the Reading Quizzes before class.

Homeworks definetly can take time. It's the same for all sections, but it's due at the wonky time of 5PM Thursday. The assignments are best tackled with a group of friends. And if possible, it's best to have things mostly wrapped up by Wednesday, so that Thursday you can just focus on cleaning things up before you submit.

Prof. Sun's tests were not bad – if you take the time to prepare and have a comfortable grasp on how things work, you can do great. The first quiz in class had an 82% average, then the second quiz had a super high average – almost a 92%. He did make the last quiz harder as a result (75% average), so if people do really well on a quiz be extra prepared for the next. Even with the last quiz (which was easily the hardest), nothing was borderline impossible nor malignant – the entire time the assessments were fair and were able to be adequately prepared for. Not sure how strong the curve will be after everything though...
Very positive, it was very interesting
Sun was a great professor. Material was interesting
Instructor is very enthusiastic

Professor Sun is a chill guy who is usually very direct with his lectures, but I was confused on a lot of the homeworks and it felt like the people I went to for help would explain the same things in completely different ways.
More difficult than EA2. You should attend lectures regularly and office hours as needed, although the homework and content are not too bad. Decent amount of algebra.
Not terrible overall, just pay attention in lecture and do the HW and you'll be fine

The course was very similar to a basic physics mechanics, and physics electricity and magnetism class. This class was very straightforward and the web–text was very useful. In order to get a good grade you have to do well on all of the three quizzes.

I found EA3 to be better than EA2 in terms of the content, it was a little more interesting. It was more challenging, all while having easier tests. Sun is pretty good, he isn't the most engaging, but he covers all the important material, and all lectures all really well recorded. Homework can be a little challenging though. Discussions and the TAs were the worst part of this course. All grading and communication was very slow.
Take it with Prof Sun! Great professor, great lectures, and fair exams.

I liked the content in this class, but the sturutre was very difficult for me. They use an online textbook that is very difficult to work with and understand, and has very few practice problems. Some of the concepts touched upon in the 'textbook' aren't covered or covered differently in class, which is confusing. Also, the homework seems to be the same for every section, while the quizzes are different for every section, which means completing homework problems are very challenging because they include things not covered in the textbook or in the lectures sometimes, and the questions in the homework aren't even the same kind of questions asked in the quizzes. Overall, I feel like the lectures, homework, and textbook are all very different sources of information, which makes it difficult to be able to do well in the class.
Do not sleep on the web text, it will teach you way more than the professor

The course's difficulty and instruction were great. I think our professor did a great job with pacing and explanation of the concepts. I think the course definitely feels inconsistent, especially with what is taught in other professors' sections. The organization of the course itself also feels loose and somewhat arbitrary, and I think it feels incomplete to a certain extent. I wish we could have learned about differential equations sooner, and I think only learning some electronics was confusing. Studying for exams was also somewhat tricky, but luckily there are lots of practice in our professor's lectures and the released practice quizzes.

Sun is a jolly guy. He likes to ramble, but his slides and work make sense. His tests are very fair. The averages for my class were between 70–90. I think out all the EA3 profs, his is the best combo of lectures and exams. Also, go to office hours and get those HW points.

The course emphasized the homework which took a decent bit of time. It varied, but I spent anywhere from 1.5 to 7 hours on homework. I thought that while it was tedious it helped a lot in learning the ideas.
Professor Sun was okay, he went over concepts a lot which I liked, and the exams weren't bad unless you reviewed extensively.

The class was initially frustrating. However, taking it with prof Sun was beneficial since his exams were fair. Sometimes, he went fast through some concepts, but every professor has their teaching style, and they examine different things.
It's a lot of differential equations and professors are very different.

This class is not too difficult. I had professor Sun and he did a good job on informing us the topics we need to know for the exam. To follow his lectures, you need to read the textbook. His lectures are mainly for deepening your understanding of the material with examples. His midterm exams were relatively easy, but the final was a lot more difficult. I strongly recommend going to TA office hours for help on the homework.

Professor Sun was a very nice instructor, I would highly recommend taking it with him. His quizzes were on the easier side compared to other teachers (perhaps not the easiest thought). Sometimes he would have redundant lectures, but overall I believe he prepared us well for the quizzes. Sometimes I would not have to look at the textbook because he covers the content relatively well. Make sure to take the practice quizzes! Although some of the instructors covered content that Sun did not.

EA3 is a jump from 1 and 2. It definitely gets harder so be prepared to be a little more confused. Notes:

1) Pick a professor that suits you! Some professors give more confusing lectures but easier exams 2) The curve is important 3) Practice even though there are not that many problems

This is a standard EA course. The content is pretty specific with spring/ damper/ mass systems and differential equations which then led to electrical systems. Each professor has a different style of teaching and the quizzes are different for each section. We had 3 quizzes and weekly homework that made up our grade. Half to most assignments had MATLAB questions which I didn't enjoy very much but were generally manageable. Professor Sun is a solid professor and I think our quizzes were pretty standard and fair.

Respectfully, Sun was a confusing prof–– he tended to talk through lots of the example problems without writing anything down which was not easy to follow. BUT his tests were pretty easy except for the last one (I think he needed to level the curve)–– the trick is to hopefully be smarter than your classmates so unfortunately its highly based on curve.

This class was exactly what everyone else says –– pretty tricky and really all–over–the–place –– but it is more annoying than it is impossible. The specific course content isn't really explained in the broader context of engineering, so it can feel like you are just memorizing steps rather than truly understanding. Prof. Sun covers content in lecture that is not really discussed in the webtext, and vice versa, so you have to read the textbook AND attend every lecture in order to be prepared for his exams. Homeworks are time consuming, and you will probably need to go to office hours to get them done. Grading is 25% HW, and 25% 3 exams. This course definitely was frustrating and time consuming at times, but it's nothing you won't be able to handle! Go to class and office hours and you should be fine.

It sucks that the course is just comprised purely of three quizzes that take up 75% of your grade and homework. The last quiz was very difficult compared to the first two and I felt unprepared. It’s unclear what the curve looks like.
Pretty alright class, not that bad. Don't use intuition. Cheng Sun is pretty good, but going to lecture is not necessary.
You'll be fine if you do the homeworks and read the readings.
Sun is not exactly a bad teacher persay, I also never went to the other lectures, so I won't compare them. Exams were pretty easy compared to other sections.

Alright course. Professor Sun is a really caring professor, and he's always smiling while lecturing. At times, though, he'd go on some tangentially related rambles. On one occasion, we only did one problem in class together, and he did not finish it because he was talking about other things a lot. I would say Prof Sun is the middle option compared to the other professors.
Best EA I have taken so far. Prof. Cheng was amazing!
Midterms weren't too difficult, practice midterms made preparation easier.

I think that the homeworks were pretty fair; however, I was not able to understand why there were MATLAB portions in the homeworks. The way in which electrical systems was crammed into the last few weeks also let only a little bit of time to fully understand it.

EA3 was honestly not bad for me. I think doing physics in high school gave me a little bit of a head start at the beginning of the quarter. The material isn't too hard, it's just kind of hard to visualize.

This class was split into mechanics and electricity, with mechanics being the focus of two tests and electricity the focus of one. The grading system is 25% homeworks, and then 25% for each of three tests. The homeworks always took me a long time. Professor Sun was really good, though. His tests were extremely fair, and he was really kind –– you could tell he wanted us to do well.

EA 3 is a completely different experience, solely based on your lecture professor. You will have DRASTICALLY different content covered on quizzes and content learned in class. Cheng Sun is a great professor, knows what he's talking about, and teaches you a lot about dynamic systems; HOWEVER, there is a very big and clear difference between lectures from Lynch or Keys. It is night and day for lecture content and quizzes between professors, and if you want to be slightly challenged but have a light workload and easy quizzes, take this course with Cheng Sun. You are guaranteed a good grade if you put in the work for this section because the quizzes cover content from the lecture, and that content from the lecture is much easier than in other sections. If you would like to be challenged, this is not the section for you.
it was overall good, nothing special

source_file: Student Report for Gayle Ratliff (PHYSICS_135-1_01  General Physics)_b5786dac-572a-4b58-9d7f-ed48a63a0248en-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time

Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?

comments: Great course. Very easy to get an A, very self paced, group exams.
My favorite class so far, professor makes it worth it.
Take 135 with Ratliff. She is awesome.
This course is very similar to AP physics. Whether you took the algebra based or the calc based, it doesn't matter. Mechanics is mechanics.
This course is great for an intro into physics, especially after an algebra–based sequence in high school and other daunting intro science sequences here at Northwestern.
Class is easy. Go to class. DO TAKE NOTES. otherwise you wont learn. just do everything.
Professor Ratliff is wonderful and really works to make sure that every student is learning. I'd highly recommend taking this class with her
Prof is great and the class is super easy.
Prof Ratliff is amazing! Take this.
Take this class as your science foundation discipline!!!!!!!!!!!!! It is so easy and Dr Ratcliff is amazing. I am genuinely horrible at physics and passed with flying colors. Take it.
Love Professor Ratliff. She makes physics enjoyable. This class is very low stress. Most definitely take it.
No complaints. Ratliff the best

I didn't learn anything in this class. it is set up in a way where you don't have to attend and you dont actually have to pay attention or learn anything to get a 100%. it was honestly a waste of time and tuition in my opinion. super easy A but at what cost?
Great intro to physics with challenging homework assignments to solidify your understanding.
Love the teacher fr
I love this class. Love the professor. She’s the best
n/a
Lovely course. I learned a ton of new things, and the course sparked my curiosity to learn about physics. 10/10 would take it again.
The class is very manageable with group exams and homework.

This was an easy A, and all my friends and I finished with a 100%. Ratliff is a really sweet professor and unbelievably accommodating. There are three group midterms that you have 12 hours to complete remotely, and they're incredibly straightforward. You would really have to try your absolute hardest to not do well in this class in order to not get an A.

This course is great! You get out of it what you want. Professor Ratliff wants to see everyone succeed and it's not hard to get an A. However, for the more ambitious students, she also goes more in–depth during lecture for those who truly want to learn more about physics.
Take home group exams. Need I say more?

Professor Ratliffe is the kindest professor I've ever had and that makes taking this class a lot more enjoyable , especially as someone who doesn't have a very strong physics background. It was also the lowest stress STEM class I've taken at Northwestern because the 3 exams are take–home group exams. Despite this I learned a lot because we had to do a whiteboard assignment at the end of every class on top of weekly Achieve homeworks and pre–lectures, so I was regularly thinking about and working through the material without any anxiety–inducing cramming.

Overall this class was great. Professor Ratliff is very passionate about teaching physics, and genuinely wants to make this an enjoyable experience where you are able to develop a strong understanding of the material. I really enjoyed the group exam aspect of the course, because I felt like I learned just as much as I did in other classes with more traditional exams, but with a lot less pressure. As a pre–med, it was nice to feel like I was actually able to focus on learning the content as opposed to worrying about a grade the entire quarter, as this is not always the case in other pre–reqs.

Take this course with Ratliff! She is an amazing professor and the class is very collaborative with regular practice built in. Exams are group exams, so it is a low–stress course that still facilitates learning.
good class

easy class in the sense that if u go to class, do the homework and keep up, it's not bad. the exams are group take home exams so not super stressful, but you still have to keep up in order to know what you're doing.
10/10 amazing physics class. This is probally the most enjoyable stem class I have taken at this school because you can really tell she cares about the students more than anything else
Great Class, love Professor Ratliff.
This class was set in a way that was really manageable and I enjoyed learning from Prof. Ratliff

I really thought this class would be the bane of my existence since I've never taken physics before and don't like math, but I actually really enjoyed it. There is a prelecture to watch before each class which doesn't take long, a white board problem due at the end of every class day to practice what you learned (which you get 100% on just for genuinely trying), and weekly problem sets which can be a little bit tricky but attempts are unlimited. Exam questions are released in the morning on exam days and due by 9pm, and you take them in groups which makes it easier to catch small mistakes and overall score better. I really enjoyed Professor Ratliff's lectures because she made physics seem interesting just by genuinely having so much passion for it. Beyond loving what she teaches, it also seems like Professor Ratliff really loves teaching. She really cares about her students and their learning which was really cool.

Ratliff is a great professor exited to teach and help you learn. It is possible to slide through this course doing the bare minimum but I recommend putting in the effort to do the hw and whiteboards with some thought (should not take too long) because her problems are great for learning. She is eager to help if you have any questions and I would recommend going to class because she can be pretty engaging.
This course was so awesome. Ratlfif is so passionate about what she teaches and really structures the class so her students can succeed.

This is a well–designed course. Professor Ratliff does a great job of helping students grasp the content without making the course overly stressful, which is why this course is so popular. Professor Ratliff also provides an abundance of easily accessible resources which make all of the concepts understandable. Workload is reasonable – assignments are pretty constant so you will need to stay on track, but none of these assignments are exceedingly difficult or long. Overall really solid course, highly recommend.
Great course, Gayle is a saint, highly recommend for anyone who needs a science
I enjoyed this course. The prof is so nice and really wants you to learn. Not stressful at all too because of the group exams.
Prof Ratliffe is super kind and materials were great

Professor Ratliff is a wonderful person and very engaging professor, it is very evident that she loves physics and is incredibly enthusiastic about teaching. I really appreciate the emphasis on the learning process in the way the course is structured –– the whiteboard assignments and group exams keep us accountable but encourage us to both collaborate and reflect on our learning more actively.
This class was definitely easy as long as you kept up with the material. The group exams helped me learn the most because we all brainstormed how to solve the problems.
I really appreciate the structure of this course and I found it really helpful for succeeding in the course.
Professor Ratliff is amazing
Loved this class. I feel like I could learn without crazy pressure to compete with my peers.
The homework was good practice and had unlimited attempts, encouraging work to be done without any guidance or help first. The recorded lectures made it easy to learn and review material.

you should take this class. It's a great introduction to physics and multivariable calculus is not required. There are group exams but you are given ample time, including outside of class to complete them.
Prof. Ratliff is awesome and truly cares about us learning the material. The format of the class made it very easy to succeed, as long as you keep up with the work each week.

Reverse lecture style almost, there are prelectures which are very relevant to the topics being taught and then online assignments with the textbook weekly and whiteboard assignments due by midnight of each lecture. Group, take–home tests are used for this course and it really helps with understanding the content.
Professor Ratliff is amazing, highly recommend. She makes the course and difficult content manageable
Professor Ratliff is really nice and the structure of the course made things very stress–free. :)
Great class, and Professor Ratliff is amazing. She actually cares for her students and that is so rare, Northwestern truly does not deserve her. Could not recommend this class enough!

This class was great, especially for students who are pre–med looking to study abroad in the fall of their junior year. Professor Ratliff is so nice, and the course structure balances well with tougher classes like Biochemistry. I definitely recommend taking this class instead of waiting to take 130–1 in the fall!
ratliff is awesome– group tests take the stress out of it and make it much easier to learn! def recommend over the algebra sequence
By far the fairest class at Northwestern. If you work hard you will get an A.

This course was a great intro physics course. Professor Ratliff is very thorough and explains concepts thoroughly. The flipped classroom method helps reinforce topics. Though there are lots of assignments to keep track of and submit daily, this course is doable, and you are very well supported during it by Professor Ratliff, the TAs, and your exam group.

Fantastic class! I already took mechanics in high school, so I was just taking the class as a refresher. I thought the professor did an amazing job of explaining concepts and really helped solidify my understanding. The class was also set up in a way that really promoted learning.

Professor Ratliff is THE BEST professor in the stem department. She is not only kind and considerate, but also explains concepts exceptionally well. I learned ALOT from this class and I found the formatting of this class extremely conducive to learning. The exams were challenging but rewarding. This physics class made me increase my confidence in this topic and I really appreciate Prof Ratliff for making this environment. It is amazing and I wish more classes adopted her mindset: challenging yet rewarding that is conducive to learning .
Loved this class! Prof Ratliff is awesome and so kind
easy and helpful. learned a lot.
Prof. Ratliff is such a great teacher, she’s such a fun lecturer and you can tell she really loves the material. The class wasn’t very difficult, but I still felt it was interesting and fun.
I enjoyed the structure of this course and I liked working with others.

TAKE THIS CLASS WITH RATLIFF!!! She is such a great teacher, and I learned so much from her. There were 3 exams – all of them were group. The TA was so helpful in teaching any gaps in knowledge that you had. The homework really reinforced what we learned in class, and if there were any problems that were really hard, we went over the math in class so that you could do it on your own as well. The discussion sessions prepared me well for the exams.
really good class I recommend! easy A if you put in the work and time

Overall a great course. Prof. Ratliff is amazing. I will say that since there isn't that intrinsic pressure of doing well in the course since the tests are group take–home, you need to have the self motivation/discipline to stay on top of everything.
This is an extremely easy class, lectures are not required to attend and all exams are online and open note. Homework is all online and is easy to complete.
Highly, highly recommend this class to anyone who has to take the physics sequence. Prof. is awesome and so sweet and understanding. Learned tons
its pretty boring

source_file: Student Report for Jennifer Brace (BIOL_SCI_201-0_2  Molecular Biology)_99e294e1-4ae5-466a-b7d5-da895db0d421en-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: Cool material but this class is not for the week. You gotta have your study habits locked down. Take good notes and consider active recall.

Bio 201 is a very interesting class! However, you do have to make sure you review content after every class since there are midterms every other week. Make sure you understand important terms and techniques – those can be troubling to understand. Professors were good but sometimes content would be stated in class but not be in slides, so maybe rewatch the lecture video after every class. Make sure to do a lot of practice problems from material given. Graded only on attendance, four problem sets and four midterms, however there is an optional final that replaces your lowest midterm score.

This class wasn't too bad. A lot of people will convince you it's easy which isn't entirely false. But it's also easy to mess up your grade. Getting tripped up by the wording of questions on exams can be rough especially if you are confident in your knowledge and understanding. Other than that it's a pretty fair course and the exams every other week aren't bad unless you have other classes stacked ontop of them

The test question wording was annoying, but other than that its not too bad if you really review the slides and make sure you understand everything. Pretty manageable work load. Only outside of class work is studying and I liked the 4 midterm format rather than 2 heavier weighted ones, which is most other courses.

The material was interesting, the professors and TAs were good at giving explanations during office hours that helped with understanding some of the more difficult material in class and there are plenty of practice problems and videos on TopHat to do. Overall, this course took to getting used to especially with the recommended textbook readings, but create a group and study with friends to go over material and you will be good.

The course content itself wasn’t particularly difficult, but the way we were tested made the class unnecessarily frustrating. We had two professors who were knowledgeable, but their lectures often lacked clarity and consistency. Key information was sometimes glossed over or left out entirely, which made studying more difficult than it needed to be.

The exams relied heavily on tricky multiple–choice questions, which seemed designed more to create grade distribution than to assess genuine understanding. I understand that multiple choice is necessary in a large lecture hall setting, but in this case, it felt like the format prioritized test–taking strategy over actual comprehension of the material. I didn’t perform as well as I expected (not because I didn’t know the material, but because the questions often tested logic games rather than content recall or application).

As a biology major, I was disappointed by how the course was structured. It didn’t include a lab component, yet we were still expected to understand applications that would’ve made more sense through hands–on experience. A lot of the instruction felt focused on memorizing isolated facts without offering the broader context needed for true understanding.

If you keep up with the material and study consistently, it’s still possible to do well. But I do think the course needs better organization and more explicit instruction. Also, please don’t buy the textbook because it goes into far more depth than what’s tested and sometimes even contradicts the lecture content.

This course is required for many students, so if you’re taking it, hang in there. After the first exam, you’ll have a better idea of what to expect and how to prepare.

What you would expect from an intro to bio class. Some of the questions feel intentionally tricky but for the most part you can figure them out. Definitely not an easy A... or B, but if you study the slides then you should be fine.

As a sophomore, I was scared to take this class after hearing how my friends struggled last year, and also since I didn't take AP/IB bio. To my surprise, this was one of my easier and more enjoyable classes this quarter. This class is four exams, an optional final (replace your lowest exam grade), four problem sets, attendance in lecture, and extra credit with two in–class surveys for an overall additional 2%. I did well on the first exam and just repeated my study habits on every exam which got me a solid A without doing the extra credit or taking the final. Here are some tips: go to undergrad TA OHs the weeks you don't have exams to solidify new materials and ask questions while it's not busy. Go to prof OHs the week of the exam. Register for a PGSG for some extra practice questions and to resolidify the material; I recommend doing one on Tuesday or Monday so you have it right before exams which are on Wednesdays. Ask as many questions as you can in OHs and PGSG till you feel like you fully understand. Start by studying the slides. Cover the important info and try to recollect it without looking. If you have goodnotes, use the tape option. Do this starting the week before your exam so it's not overwhelming. Make notes in class (don't try to write everything down bc theres too much) just to stay locked in and then review them later that day. Do the pset the weekend before so that when you get to class it's not your first time of seeing it. Do all the practice questions/exams available to you and practice reasoning out loud. Treat all questions like select all that apply so you're ready when they show up. READ CAMPUSWIRE. Go through as many questions as you can and the responses. Take a stab at responding to one if you feel confident about your answer. I would skim like 200~250 questions per exam which doesn't take as much time as it sounds hehe. I felt like I knew the thought process of how to think through questions and exactly what the profs wanted us to take away from each topic. Exams have group portions, so chose partners wisely. High chance one or two people will just sit there and let you answer all the questions or come with the wrong answers, so the best thing is to go in to each exam expecting/studying enough to carry the group (even if that doesn't happen). All in all, you'll be fine. Not an easy A, but very much within reach for not too much effort.

This class is not that bad as long as you dedicate a few hours to it. Both professors are super helpful and approachable. I'd definitely recommend attend office hours and joining a PGSG as the multiple choice questions closely aligning to the midterms. Having a midterm every other week can get annoying but the resources they provide us with are pretty helpful. Active recall will be your best friend.

While this class made material easy to grasp, the exams (especially exams #3 and #4) often did not reflect pure material knowledge, but rather knowledge of how to do the problems that the professors would think up.

Exams were often confusing and worded so poorly that semantics would cost you a significant portion off of each exam. Also, the lack of the ability to check your answers at any time (instead, having to go to a TA–led review session) was a huge inconvenience and took away from students' abilities to improve after each exam

The class is really well structured; for each of the 4 sections in the quarter, there are about 4 lecture classes, a homework assignment, and an exam. The grading in the class depends heavily on the exams, and the class isn't curved. An A requires a 91% overall (2% extra credit given at the end), so there's really only a fixed number of questions you can miss on an exam to get an A. At the end of the course there's also an optional final that replaces the lowest exam score. This class takes attendance, but there's a number of classes you're allowed to miss and still get full points in attendance. All the exam content is drawn from the lecture slides, so being familiar with them is really important.

I felt like I understood the material of this course pretty well, but the exams seemed like they were designed to make us fail. I didn't score higher than a 70% on any of them, despite putting in hours of work and feeling mostly confident going in every time. The exams were really poorly written in the sense that some of the questions were SO tricky, to the point where my interpretation of a single word could change the way I would answer the question (which normally would be okay, except that we weren't allowed to ask any questions during the exam, even to clarify the phrasing of the exam). As someone pursuing a biology–related degree, that was frustrating, because I didn't feel like my final grade reflected my actual understanding of the content at all.

I loved this course, but I definitely know of people who struggled. For me, it was a breeze after Chem 171/172, and really reinforced my desire to major in Biological Sciences. Both professors were so kind and supportive, and the tests were very fair.
If you like memorization, take this class, although you probably have to take it anyway. It kinda sucks.
N/A

This class is pretty difficult and time consuming. Both professors Truchan and Brace seem pretty nice, but the lectures are very fast–paced, pack in a lot of information, and to me can be pretty hard to follow. I found that for every single lecture I had to rewatch the recording and spend hours just taking notes and trying to understand the material. The four midterms are every other week which basically means you have to be constantly staying on top of the material if you want to get a decent grade. The midterms themselves are also pretty evil; the questions will try to trick you in literally every possible way and by the end of the course they get pretty wordy. In the end, I definitely learned a lot about biology which is pretty cool but be warned that this is a hard and fast–paced class. If you took AP bio this might be easier for you but I only took freshman year bio in high school so I had basically no background.

Prof Truchan and Brace are both great lecturers, but the very large classes are an impediment to this. This course will be very interesting if you genuinely like the course material, but otherwise it requires a lot of work to score well as there are midterms every two weeks and it is not curved.
ummmm i really like biology but the exams were pretty dumb because i felt a lot of the questions weren't reflective of what you learned ans your understanding some just felt like a guessing game
This course felt very fair. Having 4 midterms was daunting at first, but in hindsight, I'm glad for the opportunity to not have to take the final having done well on the midterms.

I think the switch of professors really messed up my studying and understanding of the content. Prof Brace and Truchan teach very differently and it was difficult to adjust my prep for classes and exams.
Great course, great professors. Study hard for the exams and make sure you read all questions fully.

This course was really enjoyable! It is very manageable if you have taken AP Biology beforehand and the concepts are not too difficult to understand. I would say the best way to study is through reteaching the content to your friends.

This class is more a test of reading comprehension than biology. The exams have a lot of trick questions, and the "select all that apply" questions are especially difficult if you haven't memorized all of the information off of the slides. The professors are very nice but I would recommend going to office hours and asking questions because I don't feel like lecture goes that into detail.
This class was very challenging for sure but at the end of it I learned a lot that will help me in my later classes at Northwestern.
The amount of information required to memorize was a lot.

Overall, good class. Tests are straightforward (sometimes wording is weird, but not to the point where its unfair), extra credit opportunities, good study materials, lectures and slides are helpful. Would recommend signing up for PGSG or having a friend who is in PGSG because they get a LOT of great study materials for tests.

Memorize everything on the slides and you should be set for the exams. Never cram and take every exam seriously if you want to avoid taking the final. Pretty generous extra credit (2%) and if you do end up taking the final, it's no biggie because it's really not as bad as people say it is. If you work hard it should be very manageable to get an A/A– in the class.

The class has a very big lecture and no discussions, so I think a lot of the times I had to figure things out myself or with a classmate. The materials are pretty dense and you have a spend time every week to study, because there is a midterm every other week. You have to stay organized and keep up with the lectures.
biweekly exams not the most fun but it's calm

This course covers a lot of the same stuff as AP Biology with a few new examples, so it's mostly review. So, the content isn't too challenging, but they put a lot of trick questions on the exams. They more test your test taking ability rather than understanding of the material.

If you're taking this course it's probably because you have to. Take good notes and do the practice problems before each of the four midterms, but don't expect anything mindblowing. There's no real homework, which can definitely be a plus, but there's also no hands–on learning. The professors are thorough with their instruction and there's a lot of good information in the slides, so if you pay attention you will definitely learn what you need to get by. I didn't really see the point of Tophat beyond taking attendance in a giant lecture, and I definitely don't think it was worth paying for when most of the same functionality is available on Canvas.

As a non–bio major who is no longer pursuing the pre–health track, this course was hard. It took me a long time to understand concepts and information, but the way the class is set up into 4 exams made it a little bit easier to organize. I found it helpful that every few lectures were an exam, because that meant there was less material to go through to prepare. However, the exam questions are pretty difficult and often trick you with small details, which is not impossible but a little unfair.
Truchan teaches the first half very well, Brace falters on the second part: study more for exam #3 and #4. PSETs with Truchan are harder

I actually really appreciated the structure of this course. The exams are broken up into two sections:You take the test normally but then after you take the same test again with a group of your choosing. The final grade is then weighted 85% individual score and 15% group score. People tend to do well on the group exam so it serves as a curve on the total score which is really nice.

People told me that this was the worst class in the bio sequence, and now I see why. The exams were terrible. I came into them well prepared and ultimately left feeling confused because the wording of the questions were meant to trick us and not test our knowledge of the material. And don't even get me started on the "select all that apply" questions. I also hated that the professor switched halfway through the quarter because each professor was better in a certain area? All it did was make us have to adjust to this change halfway through the quarter, which was honestly difficult when we got used to a certain style of teaching. The group exams were actually pretty helpful, and I appreciated the pre and post survey for the class that boosted your grade.
Good course! Learn the slides and you will be fine on the tests. The multi–select questions are annoying, but overall the exams are pretty fair.

This course is mostly just memorization, and if you memorize the concepts and why they work you should be fine. A lot of the exam questions are either things you have already discussed or concepts you know with one minor change to throw you off. The exam questions can be worded really badly too, but the group exam process is nice for getting some extra points. Also make sure to do the 2% extra credit surveys at the beginning and end of the quarter.

This course seems designed not to help you learn but to trick you on tests with questions that are rarely useful outside of a class setting. Professors were impersonal and difficult to work with, and despite not learning much and studying a lot, my grade does not reflect this effort. This course also requires significant night availability, which is very difficult for students with jobs— I almost lost mine and the professors made the accommodation process very cumbersome. Do not take as an elective for fun, and try to test out if you can— it’s a real pain.

Memorize the slides. Intro biology is not a complicated class, so don't dive into it. Memorize EVERYTHING they mention in lecture, do all the practice, and you'll be fine. Just note that while taking the tests, they try on purpose to trip you up and make you make silly mistakes. Read everything carefully and go slow

I overall liked the lectures. The TopHat questions included in the lectures were helpful to my learning of the course materials. I found the tests more frustrating. I did well on all of the tests, but I often felt like my scores on the exams reflected my ability to take a test and only partially displayed my knowledge of the material itself.

This class felt pretty standard for a bio pre–req. It covered mostly AP bio material with a few new things. The instructors emphasized biological laboratory techniques a lot alongside the standard molecular bio processes. Although the material wasn't too difficult, this class is not an easy A. You will definitely need to put in the hours to know the material inside and out. The exams (MC including select all that apply questions) will include many trick questions that will trip you up if you don't read super carefully. It is really easy to get a lot of points off on small technical details that they will try and trick you with. That being said, there isn't too much homework, so I would recommend studying a lot for each midterm since they are most of your grade. There is also leeway since they take the best 4 out of 5 exam scores (4 midterms and 1 final). The lectures were pretty fast paced but they are also recorded, so I recommend rewatching the lectures to study for the exams since the exams are more lecture focused than textbook focused.
If you've already taken AP biology, this class is mostly review. The tests every two weeks can feel like a lot of work, but if you keep up with the lectures it's very manageable.
I struggled in this course simply because I couldn't find proper study strategies that helped me succeed. Take advantage of your resources, like professors and TA office hours, campuswire, etc.
I enjoyed the course and thought it focused on a number of subjects that were both important and interesting to me.
This is a great course for anyone interested in biology and both professors are great it’s also useful as MCAT prep as the style of questions is very similar

i have heard very mixed reactions to this course. the goal of the format is to encourage deeper understanding instead of memorization, although adding a lot of "trick" questions on tests, in my opinion, is a bad way to do this. i know so many people (including myself––many times) who got a question wrong because they misread one word on a diagram or because something is switched from the way we did it in class (written 3' to 5' instead of 5' to 3' for example). i personally think this is an unfair system and doesn't really represent how much you learned. there is a lot of memorization for this class, but i found it doable as long as you put in the work (i made flashcards of EVERYthing on the slides and i feel like i learned what was necessary). i really liked how there was no homework (and the pre–test assignments we got a class period to work on) i do love the profs and TAs and i found office hours really helpful. i would pick this class 45x over than chem. overall i did enjoy this class and i dont have to take the final so! it all worked out
Exams are hard, its not just about memorization wording is very tricky on exams.

This course was a decent intro course to biology. I took AP bio in highschool so I did not find it too challenging to learn the material and understand the terminology. If you don't have any bio background, I think this course would be quite hard, you would have to spend a lot more time. The exam questions were written in very confusing ways and even if you understood the concepts, you might not be able to get the questions right. Only take this course if you need it for a requirement.

This course is equal parts memorization and problem solving, and don't let anyone tell you otherwise. If you do not study and familiarize yourself with all content taught, you will get burnt. However, with due caution and effort, this class can almost be trivialized. Also the problem sets end up being worth a lot more than you would think, so make sure to collaborate and get as close to perfect as possible.
The exams are all multiple choice which sucks because the professors throw in a decent amount of "trick" type questions but the class isn't too hard.
The lectures were very informative. Felt like the exams did not reflect the difficulty of the material provided, often being much more difficult by way of trick questions.
I enjoyed deepening my knowledge of molecular biology. The PSETS also encouraged me to work with others.
One of the worst classes ive taken, the professors were nice but the tests were reading comprehension not content understanding.
The class focuses on applying knowledge rather than memorizing facts, so it's very important to understand each concept to do well.

I really appreciated the course going into important and common lab techniques since it prepares us to work and gain experience in a research setting. I just wished the way they taught us it was less info–dump and more elaborative on the important steps.

What stood out most to me in this course was the stark contrast between the quality of instruction across disciplines. While the engineering side of the course was poorly organized and often unprofessional, what I valued most was the opportunity to work with a strong instructor in the biology domain. The biology professor brought clarity, structure, and a deep understanding of the subject matter. Her lectures were well–organized, expectations were clear, and she made an active effort to connect biological concepts to our design goals in a way that was both relevant and inspiring. Her approach made the learning feel worthwhile and showed how good teaching can elevate even a challenging course.

This class is pretty much the first half of AP Bio, so if you have taken that class, you should be fine. The annoying thing about this class is that the tests are all multiple choice, which could work out in your favor. The class is pretty much memorization with little application, so just memorize whatever is on the slides, and you'll be fine. I tried reading the textbook in the beginning, but then I realized that I didn't need to about halfway through the quarter. The nice thing is that the final is optional and if you do end up needing to take it, it can't hurt your grade. I would recommend joining a PGSG for this class, since they give a lot of good extra practice MC that is very similar to the midterms.
The exam style was annoying but other then that it was fine
didn't like it

Very based on memorization more than learning I think. The test questions were sneaky and made it seem like memorizing small tidbits of information was more important than understanding the content. The exams are really annoying, but if you study extensively and just read the wording you should be fine. The exams are really sneakhy in wording.

This class tested more on how to take a test than it did the material. Even if you know everything, you're likely to get tricked by a singular word or diagram and miss the whole question so take the tests carefully

Definitely a challenging but manageable class. Almost all of the points in the course come from multiple choice questions (from problem sets to exams). If you're good at multiple choice tests –– especially tricky "select all that apply" questions –– you will be set for this course. Otherwise, the course is very memorization heavy and rewards being able to closely recall all the moving parts of a process in detail. Overall, the class really isn't that time consuming (there's essentially no homework), but you will end up studying a lot because there are midterms every other week (final is optional though!).
not well structured.. way too specific.. not rlly testing what u know, just your ability to memorize

This class is easy for some and very difficult for others. Prof Brace and Truchan are good at teaching the course content, but the exams are very difficult, and they are meant to trick you and question yourself. Do not take this class as a distractor or if you don't need it for your major. The exam averages are in the high 70s, there is no curve, and 80% of your grade consists of exams. There are 4 midterms and 1 optional Final Exam. The exams are very difficult, and you need to understand the content extremely well. This class is a weed–out.

I enjoyed the four midterm structure of the course, although it felt pretty fast; however, it was nice that there wasn't too much information to memorize on each midterm. I felt like I learned a lot in this course, but I had to do the majority of the learning outside the classroom by rewatching lectures and searching for additional videos to aid in my understanding of the concepts. In lecture there was a lot of material covered very fast and I had a hard time grasping the concepts in lecture thus a lot of review had to be done the weekend before the exam. I found the exams to be of decent difficulty, but somewhat annoying in that I would study hard for the material and grasp the concepts, only to find that the exams felt like they were written to trick me. It felt more like they were assessing my reading comprehension skills than biology. There are four multiple–choice exams with multiple questions that require a "select all of the above" selection, which can be easy to miss points on, as no partial credit is granted. I also felt like there was a lack of questions similar to those on the exam to help me prepare adequately for multiple–choice questions.

This course was fine. The content itself was not too challenging as long as I kept up with notes/lectures and didn't skip class. However, some of the test questions were absolutely heinous. Many questions were "select all that apply" but there was no partial credit, meaning if you get 4/5 correct, you still get ZERO points overall for that question. The profs both LOVE their trick questions so you have to be so so careful when reading each question or else your grade will suffer. If a question seems straightforward, it's probably not and they are trying to trick you. Such a bummer because the topics covered were interesting, but each midterm was actual hell to take.
Homework was only once every two weeks and did not take too long. The tests weren't too bad after listening to the lectures and reading the textbook.

i just didnt like this class. you learn a lot and its not too time consuming, and they go over pretty much everything. however, they truly make the questions as applicable as possible. Even if u know the content extremely thoroughly, youre going to overthink questions that can be really ambigious or touchy on wording. Review sessions defintely help cause they talk about certain things that appear on the exams. Overall, they make the tests unecessarily tricky that really prepare ur focus and reading comprehension as much as your biological knowledge honestly. it's not horrible, but its pretty easy to lose a bunch of points if u tend to overthink or don't do well in these type of tests. Definitely come in wary on test questions

I learned a lot on my own from this class. The material is genuinely quite interesting but the professors are terrible. The lecture slides are poorly organized which doesn't even matter because the professors talk so fast it is impossible to take a singular note before they switch slides. Not a single person I know paid attention in class because the style of teaching is genuinely horrible, you have to learn the material on your own. Also the exams are meant to trick you which is quite unfortunate, it can be very demoralizing because you can walk in with every concept fully memorized, every practice question answered correctly, and afterwards still feel abused by the exam.
