I came into them well prepared and ultimately left feeling confused because the wording of the questions were meant to trick us and not test our knowledge of the material. And don't even get me started on the "select all that apply" questions. I also hated that the professor switched halfway through the quarter because each professor was better in a certain area? All it did was make us have to adjust to this change halfway through the quarter, which was honestly difficult when we got used to a certain style of teaching. The group exams were actually pretty helpful, and I appreciated the pre and post survey for the class that boosted your grade.
Good course! Learn the slides and you will be fine on the tests. The multi–select questions are annoying, but overall the exams are pretty fair.

This course seems designed not to help you learn but to trick you on tests with questions that are rarely useful outside of a class setting. Professors were impersonal and difficult to work with, and despite not learning much and studying a lot, my grade does not reflect this effort. This course also requires significant night availability, which is very difficult for students with jobs— I almost lost mine and the professors made the accommodation process very cumbersome. Do not take as an elective for fun, and try to test out if you can— it’s a real pain.

This course is mostly just memorization, and if you memorize the concepts and why they work you should be fine. A lot of the exam questions are either things you have already discussed or concepts you know with one minor change to throw you off. The exam questions can be worded really badly too, but the group exam process is nice for getting some extra points. Also make sure to do the 2% extra credit surveys at the beginning and end of the quarter.

Memorize the slides. Intro biology is not a complicated class, so don't dive into it. Memorize EVERYTHING they mention in lecture, do all the practice, and you'll be fine. Just note that while taking the tests, they try on purpose to trip you up and make you make silly mistakes. Read everything carefully and go slow

I overall liked the lectures. The TopHat questions included in the lectures were helpful to my learning of the course materials. I found the tests more frustrating. I did well on all of the tests, but I often felt like my scores on the exams reflected my ability to take a test and only partially displayed my knowledge of the material itself.

This class felt pretty standard for a bio pre–req. It covered mostly AP bio material with a few new things. The instructors emphasized biological laboratory techniques a lot alongside the standard molecular bio processes. Although the material wasn't too difficult, this class is not an easy A. You will definitely need to put in the hours to know the material inside and out. The exams (MC including select all that apply questions) will include many trick questions that will trip you up if you don't read super carefully. It is really easy to get a lot of points off on small technical details that they will try and trick you with. That being said, there isn't too much homework, so I would recommend studying a lot for each midterm since they are most of your grade. There is also leeway since they take the best 4 out of 5 exam scores (4 midterms and 1 final). The lectures were pretty fast paced but they are also recorded, so I recommend rewatching the lectures to study for the exams since the exams are more lecture focused than textbook focused.
If you've already taken AP biology, this class is mostly review. The tests every two weeks can feel like a lot of work, but if you keep up with the lectures it's very manageable.
I struggled in this course simply because I couldn't find proper study strategies that helped me succeed. Take advantage of your resources, like professors and TA office hours, campuswire, etc.
I enjoyed the course and thought it focused on a number of subjects that were both important and interesting to me.
This is a great course for anyone interested in biology and both professors are great it’s also useful as MCAT prep as the style of questions is very similar

i have heard very mixed reactions to this course. the goal of the format is to encourage deeper understanding instead of memorization, although adding a lot of "trick" questions on tests, in my opinion, is a bad way to do this. i know so many people (including myself––many times) who got a question wrong because they misread one word on a diagram or because something is switched from the way we did it in class (written 3' to 5' instead of 5' to 3' for example). i personally think this is an unfair system and doesn't really represent how much you learned. there is a lot of memorization for this class, but i found it doable as long as you put in the work (i made flashcards of EVERYthing on the slides and i feel like i learned what was necessary). i really liked how there was no homework (and the pre–test assignments we got a class period to work on) i do love the profs and TAs and i found office hours really helpful. i would pick this class 45x over than chem. overall i did enjoy this class and i dont have to take the final so! it all worked out
Exams are hard, its not just about memorization wording is very tricky on exams.

This course was a decent intro course to biology. I took AP bio in highschool so I did not find it too challenging to learn the material and understand the terminology. If you don't have any bio background, I think this course would be quite hard, you would have to spend a lot more time. The exam questions were written in very confusing ways and even if you understood the concepts, you might not be able to get the questions right. Only take this course if you need it for a requirement.

This course is equal parts memorization and problem solving, and don't let anyone tell you otherwise. If you do not study and familiarize yourself with all content taught, you will get burnt. However, with due caution and effort, this class can almost be trivialized. Also the problem sets end up being worth a lot more than you would think, so make sure to collaborate and get as close to perfect as possible.
The exams are all multiple choice which sucks because the professors throw in a decent amount of "trick" type questions but the class isn't too hard.
The lectures were very informative. Felt like the exams did not reflect the difficulty of the material provided, often being much more difficult by way of trick questions.
I enjoyed deepening my knowledge of molecular biology. The PSETS also encouraged me to work with others.
One of the worst classes ive taken, the professors were nice but the tests were reading comprehension not content understanding.
The class focuses on applying knowledge rather than memorizing facts, so it's very important to understand each concept to do well.

I really appreciated the course going into important and common lab techniques since it prepares us to work and gain experience in a research setting. I just wished the way they taught us it was less info–dump and more elaborative on the important steps.

What stood out most to me in this course was the stark contrast between the quality of instruction across disciplines. While the engineering side of the course was poorly organized and often unprofessional, what I valued most was the opportunity to work with a strong instructor in the biology domain. The biology professor brought clarity, structure, and a deep understanding of the subject matter. Her lectures were well–organized, expectations were clear, and she made an active effort to connect biological concepts to our design goals in a way that was both relevant and inspiring. Her approach made the learning feel worthwhile and showed how good teaching can elevate even a challenging course.

This class is pretty much the first half of AP Bio, so if you have taken that class, you should be fine. The annoying thing about this class is that the tests are all multiple choice, which could work out in your favor. The class is pretty much memorization with little application, so just memorize whatever is on the slides, and you'll be fine. I tried reading the textbook in the beginning, but then I realized that I didn't need to about halfway through the quarter. The nice thing is that the final is optional and if you do end up needing to take it, it can't hurt your grade. I would recommend joining a PGSG for this class, since they give a lot of good extra practice MC that is very similar to the midterms.
The exam style was annoying but other then that it was fine

Definitely a challenging but manageable class. Almost all of the points in the course come from multiple choice questions (from problem sets to exams). If you're good at multiple choice tests –– especially tricky "select all that apply" questions –– you will be set for this course. Otherwise, the course is very memorization heavy and rewards being able to closely recall all the moving parts of a process in detail. Overall, the class really isn't that time consuming (there's essentially no homework), but you will end up studying a lot because there are midterms every other week (final is optional though!).
didn't like it

Very based on memorization more than learning I think. The test questions were sneaky and made it seem like memorizing small tidbits of information was more important than understanding the content. The exams are really annoying, but if you study extensively and just read the wording you should be fine. The exams are really sneakhy in wording.

This class tested more on how to take a test than it did the material. Even if you know everything, you're likely to get tricked by a singular word or diagram and miss the whole question so take the tests carefully

This class is easy for some and very difficult for others. Prof Brace and Truchan are good at teaching the course content, but the exams are very difficult, and they are meant to trick you and question yourself. Do not take this class as a distractor or if you don't need it for your major. The exam averages are in the high 70s, there is no curve, and 80% of your grade consists of exams. There are 4 midterms and 1 optional Final Exam. The exams are very difficult, and you need to understand the content extremely well. This class is a weed–out.
not well structured.. way too specific.. not rlly testing what u know, just your ability to memorize

I enjoyed the four midterm structure of the course, although it felt pretty fast; however, it was nice that there wasn't too much information to memorize on each midterm. I felt like I learned a lot in this course, but I had to do the majority of the learning outside the classroom by rewatching lectures and searching for additional videos to aid in my understanding of the concepts. In lecture there was a lot of material covered very fast and I had a hard time grasping the concepts in lecture thus a lot of review had to be done the weekend before the exam. I found the exams to be of decent difficulty, but somewhat annoying in that I would study hard for the material and grasp the concepts, only to find that the exams felt like they were written to trick me. It felt more like they were assessing my reading comprehension skills than biology. There are four multiple–choice exams with multiple questions that require a "select all of the above" selection, which can be easy to miss points on, as no partial credit is granted. I also felt like there was a lack of questions similar to those on the exam to help me prepare adequately for multiple–choice questions.

This course was fine. The content itself was not too challenging as long as I kept up with notes/lectures and didn't skip class. However, some of the test questions were absolutely heinous. Many questions were "select all that apply" but there was no partial credit, meaning if you get 4/5 correct, you still get ZERO points overall for that question. The profs both LOVE their trick questions so you have to be so so careful when reading each question or else your grade will suffer. If a question seems straightforward, it's probably not and they are trying to trick you. Such a bummer because the topics covered were interesting, but each midterm was actual hell to take.
Homework was only once every two weeks and did not take too long. The tests weren't too bad after listening to the lectures and reading the textbook.

i just didnt like this class. you learn a lot and its not too time consuming, and they go over pretty much everything. however, they truly make the questions as applicable as possible. Even if u know the content extremely thoroughly, youre going to overthink questions that can be really ambigious or touchy on wording. Review sessions defintely help cause they talk about certain things that appear on the exams. Overall, they make the tests unecessarily tricky that really prepare ur focus and reading comprehension as much as your biological knowledge honestly. it's not horrible, but its pretty easy to lose a bunch of points if u tend to overthink or don't do well in these type of tests. Definitely come in wary on test questions

I learned a lot on my own from this class. The material is genuinely quite interesting but the professors are terrible. The lecture slides are poorly organized which doesn't even matter because the professors talk so fast it is impossible to take a singular note before they switch slides. Not a single person I know paid attention in class because the style of teaching is genuinely horrible, you have to learn the material on your own. Also the exams are meant to trick you which is quite unfortunate, it can be very demoralizing because you can walk in with every concept fully memorized, every practice question answered correctly, and afterwards still feel abused by the exam. If you don't have to take this class, don't! Other bio classes are much better.

The four midterms were painful to say the least and I did not like the depth that we went into each of the topics: sometimes it felt not deep enough and I was not able to rationalize some concepts that we were learning about. You get more used to the exams as they go on but they are not easy by any means.

To get an A you need an average of about 29/33 in each exam which is definitely doable, but you cannot go in and just hope for the best – you will be punished.

The textbook was useless so my advice is do not even bother finding/buying it.

The Professors are fine, but content is covered so quickly that you have to be alert when at class otherwise you will not retain anything. by the end of the quarter I would show up to class, but was too exhausted to listen to them so I just rewatched the lectures on Panopto and learned the content from there.

I was honestly quite disappointed with the class. I love biology it was my favourite class in high school, but the way this class was structured was awful. Exams every 2 weeks, constantly cramming material and once you take the exam you never go back to that material. Exams were pure memorisation and all material was very surface level. After this class do not want to take another biology class at northwestern
The content was bland, and the switch of professors created a lack of continuity. The problem sets are manageable. Some of the questions just try to purposely trick you.

Pretty intriguing course that covers a lot of material. As someone who had not taken a single biology class since middle school, it was a little challenging trying to understand these concepts, but they were manageable. The exams were fair, although some of the questions on them were a little frustrating as they could be interpreted in different ways. The problem sets are representative of what is done in class, so they are good at testing your knowledge. Overall, I thought the class was good and pretty engaging.
Although the exams were not bad, some of the exam questions were purposefully made to be tricky; if you missed a certain detail you would get the question wrong.

Professor Truchan's part of the ourse is organized much better. For me, professor Brace sometimes says the wrong words during lecture, which makes it a lot harder to understand the material, also, there are not many words on her slideshows, so there is a bit of ambiguity with the learning material. Overall this was a OK class.

This class isn't particularly challenging but also isn't an easy A. There is a ton of material covered at a fast pace, so making sure you space out your studying and using your resources is really important in this class. Even though I didn't go to office hours, I would highly recommend attending (especially the professors) as they can give you insight into what they are looking for on exams. It is pretty clear that students who utilize professor office hours do much better in the class than students who don't.

As someone who hasn't taken biology for a few years, this class was a bit tricky but not impossible. There was a lot of information but as long as you put in the time to read and study, take notes on the lecture, and understand how all the concepts connect to each other, it wasn't too bad. The exams are fine as long as you study, and the group exams are helpful for bumping your grade.

Biology major; this class was not too much work, just a lot of memorization needed. Lectures are quite fast and not really that great for learning material. By the end of the class I was not taking notes at all during lecture, just listening and answering required TopHat questions. I would have to revisit concepts in order to learn them. Tests were fair. Recommend answering all Learning Objectives to study. Also, take course survey, as it will boost your final grade by 2%!!!

There are exams every other week, so it is very important to stay on top of the content. There are many opportunities to ask questions, but they are unable to release the exams, which makes it difficult to learn from your mistakes.

This course is an excellent introduction into microbiology! It is a very informative course that provides a broad yet equally in–depth overview of the topic in a friendly manner. Although there is quite a lot of information to learn for each midterm, it is very doable if you pay attention in class and know the slides very well.

As a non–STEM major, this class is rigorous but manageable with proper studying habits and keeping up with material. Falling behind during the quarter hurts, especially with biweekly exams. Definitely reach out to classmates, TAs, professors, and go to OHs for explanation and better understanding of the material. The group exams definitely helped with understanding the content and discussing right and wrong answers.

This felt like a course designed to trick you. The tests are all multiple choice, but there were many problems that were just trick questions and designed to mislead you. The scores that you recieve do not reflect what you actually learn, but more so your ability to spot trick questions. However, it was nice that the tests didn't contain too much content and were pretty easy to study for. Only take this class if you need to, shouldn't be as hard as it is.
I learned a lot about the application of molecular biology

The course was decent for an introductory bio class. I was not sure what to expect with the large lecture sizes and entirely multiple choice exams, but I found it pretty easy to do well. There were only 4–5 lectures before each midterm, none of which were cumulative except for the final, so there was not an absurd amount of material to memorize. The questions were designed to be tricky, however, so know the material well before each exam since the cutoffs are not the most lenient. I really enjoyed Truchan's lectures but found Brace's to be a bit less engaging.

This class was conceptually very understandable. The part that hurt most people was the exams, as they made up 80% of our grade, and the questions were intentionally worded to be very tricky. The select all that apply questions were HORRIBLE, no partial credit given, you had to get all of the answers or you would get zero points. The class was interesting in content but not fully enjoyable because of how unnecessarily difficult the questions were so there can be certain grade quotas kept in place. Just know that you need to memorize and diagram and STUDY ACTIVELY a lot for this class. Not as bad as general chemistry, though.

I think this class was overall okay. The midterms focus more on applied knowledge rather than just straight memorizing content. The instruction for the first two midterms was better than the instruction for the last two midterms.

This course was quite easy overall. There is an exam every other week which can seem like a lot, but the exams were not overly difficult. I felt as if I had already learned a lot of the material in my high school AP Biology course; however, this course did teach me more about research methods related to the molecular biology material.

It's not exactly an easy A because the questions on exams are sometimes worded weirdly, but all content comes from the slides and is usually fairly easy to understand. However, the group exam portion (worth 15% of your exam grade) helped a lot, and they have a survey that adds 2% to your grade (so the cutoff for an A is a 91%).

I greatly enjoyed this course. Switching the professors halfway through the course was not a positive for me because I felt as though I was just getting acquainted with one professor and their teaching methods and then having to get to know another professor. They were both great though.
This course was easy if you memorized everything on the slides

This was an interesting class, with a fair amount of interesting material to go along with it. However, there are strict cutoffs for the grades within the class, and the tests are not easy unless you spend a lot of time studying per exam.

201 is just a class you have to get through. Don't underestimate the difficulty of the course, even if you took AP or any college level bio in high school. It starts off slow, but then by the time exams come around, you are expected to understand everything said in lecture in great detail. The problem sets aren't too bad if you start on them before and ask for help during the problem set lecture. Top Hat as a platform is not convenient; would have preferred if Canvas and Iclickers were used like in gen chem. Truchan is good, but her lectures can be a bit dry at times. Brace has a better talking cadence, but does not lecture as well as Truchan in my opinion. But the professors are not what makes the course. A lot of time is necessary to completely grasp all the info they throw at you. The exams are more reading comprehension then actual content, which is really annoying. If felt like I wasn't able to show my mastery of the content in these exams. Some questions were worded very weirdly, and you are not allowed to ask questions during the exam. The group exams don't boost your grade up by much unless you get a 100 on it, but it's a boost nonetheless. Overall, the course will take a lot of study time, but its doable.

Honestly was a pretty easy class. As long as you study and understand all the points on the slide you will be fine. The class does get harder in the second half, but I did about the same on all of the exams. I will say that they try to trick you a lot with wording, pictures, etc. on the tests so read carefully!

I really liked the course it wasn't overwhelming and there was a lot of material covered. However, I did not like the tests since they didn't really test knowledge on the course but your test–taking abilities.
Pretty classic bio class, tests were as expected based on the practice and the material given in class.

Too frequent quizzing, too infrequent smaller assignments, felt like the quiz content didn't really encourage understanding. Full multiple choice in a subject about explaining intricacies of things is not great, and there are too many clearly trick questions, the lecturing is fine, a lot of overlap with AP Bio

This course was a nice introduction to molecular biology. Gradewise and testwise, the class was unnecessarily harder than it needed to be, and exams were definitely written in a way that tested reading comprehension and semantics just as much—if not more—than actual understanding of the material. I used ANKI to study and found flashcards helpful, and I often rewatched lectures to reinforce concepts I struggled with.

The most challenging part of the course was usually the research technique lectures, which tend to be dense. However, working through the problem sets carefully helped me identify weak spots and prepare more effectively. For our year, both Truchan and Brace were strong lecturers overall and taught the material effectively. That said, the section on plasmids was confusing for many students, and the instruction during that part of the unit lacked the clarity seen in the rest of the course. This could have been improved with clearer slides, more concrete examples, or some sort of review worksheet.
Overall, the course is doable with consistent effort, but expect to work harder than you'd assume for an intro–level class—especially when it comes to interpreting exam questions.

This course is great! The professors are really helpful and I strongly encourage going to professor office hours, asking a ton of questions during the in class problem sets, and keeping up on the content during the weeks with no exams. This course is really doable and is somewhat hard to adjust to the pace to at first, but once you understand that the class becomes much easier.

This class isn't very hard. The topics taught are essentially some of what was covered in AP Bio. The tests thought were meant to trick/mentally challenge you. TAKE YOUR TIME reading every question carefully, it could save your grade.

This class is filler meant to punish and weed people out, plain and simple –– the actual content it covers is really easy, but the way the tests are structured makes for a really frustrating test environment. The class doesn't reward understanding, but instead reward memorization of minute details on slideshows. Read every question carefully, because they love asking trick questions. Overall though not extremely hard, just annoying.

To be straightforward: This class is a lot of memorization, the exams are doable but they have a lot of trick questions. You really have to read every single word. The professors are not really helpful one on one and the slides can be confusing. Sometimes the slides just have photos and the professors don't get into them, yet questions about them appear on the test. However, really know each slide and make sure you do and you will be good for exams. Finally, do not waste your time going to exam review sessions. They are not helpful at ALL. Only specific TAs (aka 1) were helpful during the year. A TA used AI during office hours which I did not like. Good luck with this class if you need to take it.

ATTENTION TO DETAIL IS ESSENTIAL IN THIS COURSE. yes, this class is very doable, but the exams are a bit rough (a lot of all– or–nothing select all that apply questions), even if you study the material thoroughly. additionally, the instruction is poorly paced, but the information on the slides is really all you need to study to get a good grade and have a good understanding. pay attention, take good notes, and DO WELL ON THE PROBLEM SETS!!
Content is pretty interesting but the slides are super dense and the exams are really annoying

I have nothing against the professors that taught this course. I am sure they are perfectly nice people, but I seriously hated the way the material was taught. No explanations were ever given during lecture, they just sped right through everything without taking a pause between anything half the time. It also felt like we were being babied through the class. I took this class as a sophomore, so I understand that the freshman haven't been in undergrad as long, but people at least have common sense. I find it completely unnecessary that we had to practice taking an exam when it was literally just multiple choice. I would prefer to do things how I have learned how to do school without professors holding my hand and talking to me like I'm a child. The focus almost went entirely away from biology sometimes to the point that I thought they were more concerned about our ability to do exactly what they want. When I am taking a class, I would prefer for time to be spent on teaching me the material, not telling me how to sit down and take a multiple choice test.
Great profs, engaging lectures (although paced a bit too quick in lecture, course pacing is fine). Not too much work outside of class, really self managed (you can do as much work as you want to).

The course was pretty meh all around, honestly. The content itself was very interesting, but many of the exams felt like they tested your ability to decipher challenging questions instead of your actual understanding of the course material. Also, the professors switch half way through the quarter, so right as you figure out the style of the first professor you have to get used to the second. Personally, I enjoyed the second half of the course much more and found the exams easier, although I do not believe that this is a common opinion.

The class is a lot. While biweekly exams sound like too close apart, there is a lot of content covered in each lecture. It's easy to fall behind and the pace is quick. What helped me was looking at the slides before lecture then annotating them during it. The psets due before each exam are quite hard so make sure to take them seriously cause they matter in your grade. The optional final could seem like a good way to lighten the loaded during the quarter, since you'll be able to drop ur lowest exam grade, but it's quite tough since it covers content from the whole quarter. If I had to estimate I think a third of the class attempted it, and it wasn't too difficult but don't keep it in mind as a safety net but as a last resort
truchan is great

This class was so much better than gen chem. Although you need to memorize every detail on the slides in order to do well on the multiple choice. Memorize the graphs they show you, example, and the little details. Do that and you will do well in the class. And if you do well = no final! yay!

This is a really standard introduction to biology that can be pretty challenging but it also very understandable with effort. There is an exam every two weeks which I really liked because all 4 exams were weighted equally which means you don't have to learn to much at a time and if you are happy with your grades you don't need to take the final. Overall, study a lot for the tests and make sure you know everything mentioned in class, do all practice material, and you should do well.
The class is reasonable and interesting, but the exams can be tricky (they do build critical thinking skills though). The average exam grade is 80 and the average final class grade is 87

This course covers macromolecules and the central dogma which doesn't seem like a lot but honestly covered a LOT of content. Some parts of lectures felt rushed like the professors didn't have enough time to fully explain all the concepts in order to get through the slides on time. There's a problem set and a test for every unit (biweekly), and you take the exams twice, once alone and once right after with a group. Your final exam score is a mix of your individual and group score (unless the individual was a better score) which was really nice because it allowed you to work through what you didn't understand, boost your grade a little, and leave with a general idea of how well you did. The exams were kind of annoying because of "select all" questions where you have to select all the right answers to get credit (no partial credit). Also watch out for trick questions like switching 5' and 3' ends and expect ambiguous wording which you are not allowed to ask for clarification on which is really frustrating. Overall the class is fine just a bit rushed and test scores depend a bit on luck and semantics.

I was not a fan of how this course was taught and tested. We had exams every other week, so I feel the class was very exam– centered, rather than encouraging us to learn. Even though it was heavily exam–focused, we did not have much material to practice other than a practice set before the midterms (heavily technique–based) and an additional practice exam. I would highly recommend joining a PGSG for this class, for the weekly review sessions and extra practice questions were extremely helpful. I do not recommend this course as a distribution requirement: only take it if your major/minor/track requires it.
The course moves quickly because of the every 2 weeks exams, but overall it's a reasonable courseload.

All of the material on the quizzes is covered in the slides (so you don't necessarily need to look at the textbook), but sometimes the wording of questions is weird and it's easy to get caught up on semantics

If you took AP bio and study a bunch before the tests an A is pretty obtainable. The hardest part would probably be techniques and applications be there again just make sure you understand them and you’ll be fine

As someone very interested in bio as a career/major...its' fine. As expected in terms of content and difficulty. Profs Truchan and Brace don't seem particularly interested in the material but they do a good job and they're nice people. However, the group exams ARE HORRIBLE. Thanks to the genius idea of taking the same exam twice in a row, I can now say I have wasted exactly 4 hours of my life, one from each of the FOUR F*CKING MIDTERMS THEY HAVE IN THIS CLASS. The exams are all MCQs, and have a bunch of "select all that apply", "more than one of the above", "none of the above" etc. It is, as bio often is, all about your ability to (1, fortunately) understand the models, i.e. how they look and function, and (2, less fortunately) memorize some random pieces of material. Overall, solid class. I would venture far enough to say it's an easy A...as long as you know how to study properly. My advice if you want an A without taking the final: – ​​Be caught up on all lectures 24+ hours before exam – ​​Look at the "Learning Objectives" and do active recall to put everything you know for each of them. Then, use Chat or Gemeni to see what you missed (both great for this). – ​​Next, do the practice exam. They say 30 mins, try 20–25 to mimic exam pressure. – ​​Correct yourself and fully understand everything you may have gotten wrong – MOST IMPORTANTLY, as close to exam as works with your schedule, rewatch all 4 lectures for that midterm on 2x speed, writing down all things you didn't know/not committed to memory. This is how you get in those facts you may need for the exam but not for real understanding. If you're reading bio ctecs you likely have to take the class anyway. You'll be fine, it's not too bad.

I came into this course thinking that the CTECs from past years were overexaggerating or false, but I soon found myself agreeing with them. The first exam was fine and made me feel that I understood what I was doing, but as exams kept occurring, I felt that I was more so being tested on my ability to test take and to read during tests rather than the material which was on the test itself at times. This got significantly worse after the class switched halfway through from Professor Truchan to Professor Brace and even the lectures themselves almost became unwatchable just because of how much information was in the class. I understand that everyone says Organic Chemistry is the weed out class for many, but in my opinion it was this course as this course just seemed plain unfair at times and I often felt that my hours of studying would always lead to the same subpar outcome regardless. I also have to say that the faculty involved can be somewhat not helpful at answering questions at time, and students do not receive any feedback whatsoever unless they directly go to Office Hours to view their tests. Even then, I felt that these reviews were not very helpful except for making me see all the times I was tricked. TA Caroline Bond is also rude to students. Just overall a class which left me with a sour taste in my mouth (my brain).

this class is kind of free if you've taken AP Bio or any bio class that has covered the central dogma, enzymes, and other such things. otherwise, it's very fast–paced and it's a lot of content. regular tests mean you have to be up–to–speed on the material. however (this year at least), all the tests are multiple choice, which means the difficulty comes partially from remembering the little things you're taught in class but mostly from trick questions that are just kind of questionable. who am i kidding the ctecs don't really matter for this course it's offered once a year with the same profs and if you're taking it it's because you have to. they're not bad professors; they offer a lot of help. it's a very light class work–wise, but if you don't know the material it can be a lot to memorize for tests.

I think that the lectures are generally pretty good. However, some of the topics are pretty boring so it can be hard to pay attention. I really dislike TopHat as a platform and its really unfortunate that they use it. Logging in sucks and you have to pay to use it. I would really prefer if they used Canvas. Also, it is very annoying that they take attendance. For students who have taken similar classes to molecular bio before, coming to class may not be the best use of time especially since lectures are recorded. I don't think forcing students to come to class is beneficial because if I'm forced to be there when I don't want to be then I probably won't be paying attention anyways. Overall though, material isn't very hard and the professors are nice.

Not easy, but definitely not impossible to do well. Lecture slides alone can be kinda confusing, but they are very reflective of exam content. Both profs were good, TAs were also very helpful (Campuswire was a good resource for me)! Should note that i know of many people who got weeded out of this class/their major..... but as long as you go to lecture, keep up with content, ask questions, etc. you'll be good
The exams are hard because they try to trick you a lot with the wording instead of testing your recollection of class material
The grand majority of this class is raw memorization. Just study the slides and you'll do fine.

Overall, this class was a bit of a pain, due to the grading system being 4 midterms, and your problem sets counting for the remaining percent. Professor Truchan taught the content very well and her slides were very easy to navigate and understand. Professor Brace also taught the content well, but her slides were a bit more difficult to understand since content was a bit scattered. It was a great refresher to content taught in AP Bio, but the tests were tricky at some points and a bit challenging if you didn't study the material well enough.

A pretty standard general biology course. It felt like a breath of fresh air after the gen chem sequence. If you study consistently (like an hour a day) and get used to the types of tricks they use on exams, an A or A– is definitely within reach. For context, I rarely went to lecture and crammed the night before and day of exams, and I’m currently sitting at a B or B+ going into the final.

This course is manageable. 4 midterms and 1 optional final. The exams test attention to detail more than memorization and application (i.e. trick questions). There were two instructors for this course. The instructor for the first half had great slides that were easy to review and covered all content. The second instructor's slides were not as comprehensive, so rewatching lectures was more helpful for studying.

The course is basically a really hard memorization exercise, but there’s nothing else to it to make it super stressful. There’s no homework, so basically all your grade in the class is dependent on your exam performance. For those, you have to memorize everything on her slides, so don’t underestimate how much you have to study (and try to do good on the first exam). However, you do not have to read the textbook – going through and memorizing the slides is enough to get an A in the class. I also found the videos posted in the additional resources section in TopHat to be really helpful.
This is a challenging course that does require one to study a good amount of time. If you don't watch the lectures, it may be really difficult.
Overall the material was very interesting and near easy to pick up, I just got a bit bored with the teaching style

4 exams every other wednesday night (noncumulative) and optional cumulative final during exam week in June if you want to try to get your grades up. You have 1 hour for 32 MC Q's and then 30 mins with a group of four to retake the test. Each exam score is 0.85*original score + 0.15*group score. Your total grade is 80% from exams and then like 12% or 8 % is from 4 problem sets throughout the quarter that are not super hard and the TAs help you and the remaining 8 or 12% is from attendance. They geolocate you for attendance which is OD but just show up. If you study a few hours for a few days leading up to each exam you should be fine. Just read slides, do the practice problems, catch any helpful hinting words.
Great course. Group exams (for 15% of exam grade). Grading is VERY forgiving.
good class, enjoyed it, not too difficult but the content is mostly difficult, you do have to put in the effort and content.

This class was dissapointing. For an introductory course it was not welcoming to students. The teaching of the material was okay but sometimes lacking in organization. Time spent studying and practicing often did not reflect in grades.

I think overall this course was really good. Both professors were great (loved Truchan especially) and they were really concise and straightforward with their lectures. As someone who loves bio, this course was a great intro course in terms of just the content being fun and interesting to learn about. One LARGE issue I had with the course was the grading and exams. I loved the content, and honestly loved going to class and learning. However, I think the grading system is pretty harsh. Four exams, 32Q each, worth 80% of your class grade? If you missed a few questions on each exam, it was basically impossible to get an A because the exams are less questions and worth so much. And for the exams themselves, the material you need to know is completely straightforward (basically just learn everything on the slides), but the style of the exam jsut tests with tricky wording of questions over actual content. Even if I completely understood the content, a word or two that is slightly changed in an answer choice can lead to the wrong answer, so be careful! It can be the difference between an A or A–. And with the average overall class grade at the end of the quarter being a B, I am surprised that they never do any grade alterations or curves throughout the course even after lower test averages.

What should’ve been an easily digestible course was turned into a battle of words and tricks when it came to exams. The profs were decent lecturers and the material, though dense, was approachable and relatively easy to understand. However, the exams in no way reflected the content the students mastered. Many of my classmates and I knew the content thoroughly before entering an exam but would leave feeling diminished and awful because the exam questions would incorporate tricks and riddles. I am not trying to solve a mystery puzzle, I’m trying to learn biology. Additionally, the multiple select questions with zero partial credit really admonishes students and doesn’t recognize any form of growth mindset or stepwise learning. This course stressed me so much more than orgo 1 lecture and lab combined, not because of the material but because of the tests and my grade. When I tried to figure out ways to improve my scores, the answer came down to completing as many practice problems as possible to be prepared for the question format. However, this was difficult since the profs only release one, short practice midterm that barely scrapes the content we’ve covered. I relied on my PGSG for questions, but many of those were student–written and didn’t quite match the style of the exam questions. I came into the class with a passion for biology (it’s also my major). But I’m leaving this class considering switching to chem.

I found this course pretty reasonable, especially after going through the gen chem sequences. Both Brace and Truchan have passion for biology, and although four exams felt like a lot during the course of the quarter, I got much better at taking exams and knowing how I study. I enjoyed the group exams, as they gave me a better understanding about how other students think through the material.

This class was much harder than it needed to be for the material it covered. While I feel like I have a good understanding of all the class material covered, assessments unfortunately aren't designed to test your understanding, and are full of "gotcha" or trick questions that assess your ability to read closely much more than your ability to recall the content covered in class. Although assessments could definitely be designed more fairly, I do feel like I learned a lot in this class, even if the grade I receive may not reflect that learning.

The content in this course is relatively straightforward, but the exams are definitely made harder than they need to be. There are a lot of trick questions and all–or–nothing "select all that apply" questions. The exam averages were consistently in the 70s (the exams make up the majority of your grade) and there is no curve. While the professors were nice and helpful enough, I just wish that the exams were more fair.

As long as you watch the lectures and memorize your notes you can do well in the class. It's a lot less about learning than memorizing specific words. The tests are DESIGNED to trick you. A– is fairly doable but you might need to grind for an A.

The teaching and structure of the course are very well done because there aren't many assignments outside of class (if any, because the problem sets are in–class), and the exams make up 80% of your grade. However, the exams try to trick you, so you have to be very careful in what they say and how you pace yourself. If anything, this class taught me how to be a better test–taker than actually learning how to apply biology, which may or may not be helpful in the long run.

This class is basically a weed–out class. I have seen people switch their entire path because of how the class was. First off you have to pay to see all the slides and do the problem sets which is completely ridiculous. It's like 35 dollars and you don't even use it for the full term. The midterms were alright, but what really costed me majority of the "select all that apply" questions. If you select one too many or less, it's all wrong. There should be some partial credit because it's not reflective of what the student knows.

I did really enjoy this class, especially after taking gen chem. The professors and most of the TAs are super helpful and seem to want everyone to succeed. It is a lot of information and there is an exam every two weeks for a total of four. Also an optional final that will only be applied to your final grade if it improves it. You have to keep up with it, but if you do its pretty straightforward! However, the exam questions, specifically prof brace's, are confusing on purpose, and seem like she wants us to get it wrong. Other than that I would recommend this class!

I had already taken AP Biology in high school (and helped teach it as a TA when I was a senior in high school too) so I was familiar with many of the concepts however we went further in–depth on quite a few things, so I still learned quite a lot of new material as well. The exams are kind of difficult, and I don't know how helpful the optional final is since I don't know my final grade yet. However, the professors are great. I would highly recommend rewatching lectures to make sure you don't miss any material too. Practice problems from a PGSG (get them from a friend!) are super helpful for preparing for exams.

The course is relatively easy: it averages around an 87. But the material is taught very boring,ly and all the exams are multiple choice. Studying is a must, but even then, the exams are worded hard and not great tests of knowledge. I think 32 questions per exam is not sufficient to differentiate between kids. Still, learned a lot.

I think that the course overall is pretty good. There isn’t a ton of busy work, which is botha blessing and a curse because that means that so much of your grade is dependent on your exams. The exams are relatively fair, but the select all questions can really tank your grade, since you need to get them all correct in order to get points, otherwise you get a zero.

The slides were really organized and the I lockers were helpful to understand in–class topics. Professors were always open to questions and were really honest about the content that would be seen on the exams.
All the information that is needed are in the slides. Make sure you understand all the images and diagrams and can explain them.

This course is very frustrating because you could know all the content very well but still not do well in the class. The optimal final is a lot harder than the other exams and does not cover the big ideas of the class. This class is all about details to the point of just trying to make it hard for people to do well. It feels like the profs are more interested in tricking us on exams than testing what we know.
I liked the fact that we had 2 different professors and I enjoyed the group exams but at times the testing process seemed a little chaotic

This course is not super difficult, but you will need to put in work outside of class to make sure that you understand all of the material. The tests are difficult because they are completely multiple choice, and there is no curve. But, there is a group portion of every exam that accounts for 15% or the exam grade. The problem sets can be difficult.

I thought that the class was informative and important to the Biology major. The tests are fair once you understand the select all. Choose a good group to do the group portion with as that can really help your grade.

This was not a good class. The slides, textbook, and overall class content were especially detached from the problem sets and exams, and so I often did not know what to study for. The exams felt more like English reading comprehension rather than accurate representations of our understand of the class content. We were also given very little materials to prepare with. Lectures were very boring; TAs did not help during office hours and appeared equally as confused as we were.

This course was challenging of course but doable. You have to make sure you listen and memorize everything that the professors say in lecture and understand the material fully because they will test on the subtle details that you may overlook. Professors Truchan and Brace are really great and are experts — you will learn a lot about biology and this will be a great base. The wording on the questions are tough and the select all of the apply can get you, but understanding the material well enough will get you through it. Believe in yourself!

The content learned in this class is very interesting however you will learn to dislike the exams. The exams are given every 2 weeks so you will take 4 exams. The optional final can replace your lowest score. The exams are 32 multiple choice questions each worth 3.1 points, so the exam is out of 99.2 points. The exams are difficult as they are made to trick you, the way questions are worded can confuse people. The select all questions is where you will probably lose points, if the answer is A, B, C and you only say A and C you get a zero, there is no partial credit.

Exams every other week are tough, if you fall behind you are just kind of screwed for the next test. The exams are also really tricky and it seems like the professors word questions weirdly in order to trick you. At some points they seemed like picture/reading comprehension questions. The notes are also impossible to take in class, you either have to do them beforehand or rewatch the lecture. But the final is optional! It’s definitely hard to get an A in this class but a B is definitely doable.

This class could have been so much better… I found it to be extremely unengaging, as the course is extremely uninteractive in a huge lecture setting. Similarly, I don’t think there were enough grade assignments (only a problem set once every 2 weeks) that it was hard to gauge progress. Profs were distanced and somewhat bland in lecture style; lectures are all essentially just reading from slides that it almost seemed useless to attend, but attendance is also taken and graded which is absolutely crazy for such a huge class! You are forced to buy tophat (an attendance software), which is 33 American Dollars (?!?!?!?!?!) and isn’t used in any other course (!??!?!?!!!). Honestly, I would try to avoid this class if possible
If you take the time to just learn what's on the slides you'll be totally fine
I do not like tophat :(
Heavy memorization

Your knowledge is tested through 4 non–cumulative exams, which I enjoyed, as the information was distributed fairly well. Each lecture is packed with a ton of content, so it is important to stay on top of your notes and review regularly so that the information is integrated into your memory. This is the primary workload, as there isn't much homework outside of problem sets.
I enjoyed the content taught in the course, however, instruction moved rather quickly and did not focus on making sure students gained an understanding of the content.

Bio is a challenging class since it is straight memorization. If you have the time though to study a bit every day you will be fine. The wording on questions can bad but it is not to hard if you know the material

If you took AP Bio, most of the content will be review, but NOT ALL OF IT. This course is very heavy on memorization of content, but Prof. Brace included a few more questions on her exams that encouraged application of content (still not many, like 3–5 total). The slides will be your best friend for this class, including some of the trivia–like facts on them. However, connecting your understanding of the content as a whole—asking questions that may be "beyond the scope of the course"—is often helpful to contextualize what you learn. Ask those questions!

This course is difficult if you have issues with multiple choice questions that are phrased a bit strangely. Besides that, the assignments are few and pretty easy to get help with. The exam questions can be a bit tricky, but if you've taken AP or IB Bio it should be easier than if you haven't. If you haven't, I recommend reviewing after every lecture, which can seem a bit daunting, but it was what helped me digest the material as someone who had not taken AP or IB Bio. Don't be afraid to ask for help and go to office hours, they are very helpful.
The tests were annoying. Study practice questions a bunch.
Memorizing slides was vital toward doing well in the class even if one didn’t know the actual content.

Bio 201 is challenging, but it is manageable with effective studying and putting in the work. Going to office hours is very helpful, and you should take advantage of the TAs and professors who want to help you succeed.
The worst class i have ever taken at NU

Not too bad. Topics and lectures are really interesting and the slides/materials are great (although I never really used the textbook). Exams are difficult for no reason, with badly worded multiple choice questions. Also there's no curve. But if you study before each exam (there are 4 midterms!), you should be good.

I thought the course was okay overall. There are four midterms and an optional final, which I prefer to two midterms and a final, since all of the midterms are non–cumulative. In addition to that, the midterms are all multiple choice, but as long as you study the material from class, they should be fine. There are several tricky questions, which is why I always check my work afterwards. I thought the last two modules were taught at a very fast pace, and they also covered more material, so I had to start studying earlier for those ones. I also liked that we got one lesson for the problem set and one lesson for review every two weeks, but I thought the Canvas page could be made clearer, and I thought using TopHat wasn't very necessary, especially since students have to pay for the service.

Pay very close attention to the slides, and actually study for the 4 exams. Since 80% of your grades are exams, you shouldn't skimp on paying attention in class and understanding how the content can be applied. There's always the optional final. Good luck. love
The course was overall well structured and organized. The class isn't too hard if you pay attention during lectures and go over the slides.
Bio is generally hard but Prof Truchan and Brace tries to make the materials easier to understand with the extra resources. But the tests were difficult in the way that they try to trick you.
THE WORKLOAD IS LOWKEY A LOT. I was surprised by how much work I had to do because the exams made it more difficult.
This course is a solid start to Biology. Its pretty straightforward, and as long as you stay on top of things, the exams should not be too difficult and information in class will become more clear.
Lot of memorization and a lot of exams. Exam every 2 weeks and you have to cram for it. The rest of the course is chill, basically no assignments.
I went to office hours, stayed on top of course work and still did bad. I think the mcq isnt the best tester.

This course taught me a lot about the foundation of biology and lab techniques. The group exam policy was okay, but I did not like that we did not get our tests back to review. It was nice that the homeworks were collaborative.

There are 4 midterms and an optional final. While the exams seem very frequent, but the content is less so it’s broken up better. The exams only have MCQs but they’re quite tricky and meant to trap you. Know the content well before the exams, even small details could be relevant.

This coursed challenged me intellectually. The material itself was relatively straightforward but the exam questions were truly a test of how much you really understood the material. The lectures were helpful but not critical to attend to succeed on the exam. Strategies like active recall and explaining to yourself the learning objectives are necessary to succeed on exams. However, some exam questions felt like they were designed to trick you, as sometimes the whole question is predicated on a small technicality that will cost you full credit for the question.

source_file: Student Report for Vinzenz Unger (BIOL_SCI_101-6_05  First-Year Seminar)_76f7736d-6d6f-4edd-ae6e-69e970212be5en-US.pdf
