comments: It was a nice complement to 201 and prof unger is chill and offers an engaging perspective on biology

This course was interesting. It was at 8am so people were not always very engaged but the material was interesting. No work on a daily basis just a few writing assignments. My main complaint would be that the expectations for the writing assignments were not very clear so it was hard to know how to improve. All of the assignments were very broad which was nice but also made them sort of difficult

The majority of the class is the Prof lecturing about biology. If REALLY like biology, then please take the class. It's worth participating (he grades pretty hard for participation). It can be boring, it can be interesting––What you put in is what you get. I'll note that the 8am class time makes it difficult, so if you're not an early bird then I'd suggest to look for another seminar.

This class was a LOT of work! I chose this class because I thought it would be an easy seminar based on previous CTECs about this course, but my experience with the class was the complete opposite. We only had one reading and the rest were essays along with a big project presentation at the end of the quarter. These essays took me a long time to write, and they're really difficult because the instructions were so unclear. Moreover, the prompts were very weird. The class average on every assignment was also not so good and that illustrates how strict this class was. You also have to do a lot of outside research to write your essays because in–class lectures did not help. Overall, do not be tricked by last year's CTECs on this class.
I thought it was going to be a lot more interesting and engaging.

This class was very nice and I learned a lot of information. The in class activities were fun and interesting. The writing assignments were slightly complicated since they were so open ended and the prof can grade pretty hard. Overall the class was not too time consuming and interesting at times.

This class was not what I was expecting and I would overall not recommend taking this seminar. Though there are only 3 writing assignments along with a final project (10–minute presentation + essay), I thought the assignments were poorly distributed. The first two assignments were due in the first couple weeks of class, while the last few were all due in the last week of the quarter. This meant some weeks had no work outside of class which was nice, but then there were other weeks where I would spend a full day or multiple days working on homework just for seminar. Additionally, the professor is an extremely harsh grader on papers! The instructions per assignment are extremely vague and unclear, yet the professor seems to expect a very specific submission. You have to be very careful on everything you submit, from grammar to flow to word choice. The class average on these assignments did not look good and I'm sure everyone was nervous about their grades. On the bright side, the grade cutoffs for an A were generous and the lectures did often present valuable and interesting information on wonders of biology.

I did not really like this course as a first year seminar, which was a little disappointing because I was excited about taking it. Lectures were hard to keep up with, and I didn't really learn much from them. However, the writing assignments and projects were interesting and I enjoyed doing them because we had a lot of freedom in what we wanted to write about in the realm of biology.

5/7

source_file: Student Report for Wendi Gardner (PSYCH_110-0_20  Introduction to Psychology)_69340fc4-278b-4b3b-9c19-8643fb42ec96en-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: Take this course!! Everyone is probably saying it's incredible, and it's because it is. That is primarily because of Prof. Gardner, who is one of the best lecturers I've experienced thus far. She is incredibly passionate about psychology and very sweet. In terms of the content, it's a lot of stuff that was covered in AP Psych, so if you took that, it might be helpful to look back at those notes. Definitely go to class — attendance isn't required, but Prof. Gardner gives really helpful examples and extra information during lectures that isn't covered in the slides. That being said, she posts all the slides after the lecture, and they are very detailed, so if you have to miss one, you'll be okay. Your grade is made up of weekly discussion posts, a weekly open note quiz, and three non–cumulative "midterms" that are really just unit exams, worth 80 points each, all MCQ. The highest grade in the class is curved to be 100, and the questions are very fair for what we've been learning. If you ever have questions, the TAs and Prof. Gardner are always willing to clarify what they think you need to know or what is fair game for an exam. All that being said, I did end up typing up over 35k words worth of notes over the period of this course, so it is definitely a lot of content per exam. You can't learn everything in a night, but if you pay attention during class and start revising a few days before the exam, an A is almost guaranteed. This was a super interesting psych class, and I had the best time!
I loved the parts about the therapy techniques and clinical and behavioral side, since that is what I am interested in pursuing for a career in the future
Fun class.
The class wasn't bad at all. There is not a lot of work to be done other than studying for the midterms and finals.

This class is easy but can be overwhelming with the amount of information given to you (an A is at least a 94, and I missed the cutoff closely since I didn't do as well on the first midterm). There are three midterms that are noncumulative, and they make up 75% of the available points (so pretty much your grade depends on them). I highly recommend making flashcards (like Anki) to prepare since there is so much information. The questions on the exams can be tricky sometimes as they often give a stiuation in which you apply a psychological concept, but at least they are all MCQs (no free response). The other 25% of the grade is made up of weekly discussion boards with a response (write like 2 sentences for both) and mini–quizzes that are online and open note. Professor Gardner is perhaps one of the best professors with her engaging instruction, though her slides may get wordy at times. I highly recommend you take this course, especially if you're premed for the MCAT.
I think that the course taught a lot of areas of psychology at an intro level and could provide students with an overview.

This class was lowkey really boring and slow but very easy compared to engineering classes. Wendy was a good and enthusiastic teacher who you could tell wanted the students to do well and loved the content but I personally hated this class because of her teaching style. The lectures were and hour and 30 min long but the entire time was spent moving very slowly from topic to topic. We would spend like 20 mins on a single topic just going through repetitive examples which meant we covered less content in lecture and had to make up for it by reading the text book for tests. Overall the tests were relatively straight forwards but full of the multiple choice questions where it was like "A, B, C = answer a and b, D= all of the above, E= none of the above" which lowkey made the tests hard. Overall pretty easy class that wasn't too time consuming just boring and made me frustrated during lecture.

The course was very insightful to me as someone who'd never taken a psych class before. I found a lot of the material interesting, which made going to lecture for eighty minutes and studying before the exams quite manageable. Professor Gardner is an enthusiastic lecturer so while they are long, they aren't particularly boring. There are three non–cumulative 60 question multiple choice midterms for 80% of your grade, so they are very important, and if you aren't a great test–taker, you may struggle with the course for that reason. I would highly recommend going to every lecture and engaging with the material because it will pay off so much when it comes time to study. Material also comes from the textbook (20% of exam questions), so it is good to get ahead on that when she tells you which sections to read. Studying for the midterms is really the only large time commitment of the course. If you are confident with the material, you can probably get away with starting a few days before the exam, but it is likely best to get ahead on the material and make sure you know everything as you go, as the exams cover a solid amount of material. This class is not really a free A, but if you do a decent bit of studying, it is very possible.

Professor Gardner is clearly very passionate about the class and is a phenomenal lecturer. Unfortunately, most of your time will be spent on textbook readings. As annoying as they are, reading them and going to class will help solidify the information in your mind and make exams significantly more manageable.

It's an interesting class covering a lot of information I never learned before, and Professor Gardner is really passionate with teaching her class. However, the amount of materials that we have to know for each exam is quite demanding. I will say it's not an easy class which is possible to bring down your GPA if you don't work hard to study the material. Unless you are going into psych major or fields that require this course, taking it just for fun is not recommended.

Definitely take this class with Wendi Gardner!! The exams are fairly straightforward and there are plenty of extra credit opportunities to make up for lost points throughout the class. She teaches with a lot of enthusiasm, which makes it easy to stay engaged in class, and there are easy discussion posts and mini quizzes each week to provide some cushion in your grade. The exams are straight memorization, making them manageable. Otherwise, not much is required of you outside of class.

Good class. Prof Gardner is really nice, and her examples really help explain concepts learned in class. There is a lot of memorization, so maybe try to space it out before the exams. Other than memorizing for the exams, there's not much work outside of class.
This class covers a lot of simple topics within psych. We talk about a lot of interesting things and I feel like I learned a lot.
Not an easy course. This course takes a lot of effort to do well.
Professor gardener is amazing!! 7/10 because I think the mcqs are tricky
It was an easy class as long as you attended lectures, but not the most interesting.

All of the content was super interesting, however, I would say that there is a lot of content per exam despite being non–cumulative. The exams sometimes tries to trip you up as it would be options like A,B, and C etc... Gardener was a good professor and engaging but her slides are very pact and it's usually the stuff that is on the exams
Information that was provided was very clear. Examples were provided that made course material easy to follow. The homework was weekly discussion posts which were actually very fun to write!

I thoroughly enjoyed this course. Professor Gardner is really warm and approachable and her lectures were engaging and straightforward. I found the workload very light, and before every test you can use the provided study guides to spend your time studying topics in the textbook that will actually be on the test. The weekly online open–note quizzes were simple and a good way to review the content before next class. There were 3, 60 question multiple choice tests throughout the quarter. These weren't too hard and aligned exactly with lecture notes/textbook content.

Psych 110 is a great class for anyone unfamiliar with psychology as it covers so many different aspects of psychology in a way that is intuitive and easy to understand. Tests are all multiple choice and is pure memorization, and with the right amount of work put in it is a very easy course.
Amazing teacher, amazing content, well paced, and manageable work load
Love Professor Gardner! She was so good at lecturing and keeping the class engaged. Paying attention in class will get you really far into the class and help you succeed.

I took an IB Psych class in high school that covered all this material. Still found it somewhat challenging. Extra credit is nice, but the exams are pretty difficult. They do get easier, however, and if you go to lecture you'll be fine.
Professor was great, lectures could get a bit boring at times but overall I learned a lot in the class. Not much homework, but the tests could sneak up on you if you fall behind.

Wendi Gardner is a fantastic and engaging professor, and definitely will pique your interest in this class, even if you are not intending to be a psych major. The homework keeps you consistently engaged in class, although I feel that there can be a little more consistency in the expectations for the weekly reflections, as it was quite evident that some people were not putting in effort for the assignment.

The exams were quite fair, albeit the overall grading system (>=94% for an A and similar for other grades) feels a little challenging especially with the small margins of mistakes allowed for the exams – although I think the system of exam reviews is fantastic as it gives you a chance to argue for your own case!

We started by learning different psychological schools of thought, research methods, and parts/functions of the brain. Then we transitioned to more social behavior and by the end of the quarter we were talking about personality and therapies.

Did a pretty good job as an overview of intro psychology, and covered most concepts I was interested in to an appropriate depth, even incorporating recent research into the course material. Additionally, all outside–class assignments were pretty light and somewhat flexible.

I enjoyed learning everything covered in this class because it was cool learning about the things that makes humans who they are and what motivates us to do those things. It was particularly interesting to learn more about social psychology

I believe that lecture was by far the most important part – going to lecture, paying attention to what my instructor emphasized, and taking notes during lecture propelled me to do well in class the most, and as for review, lecture slides had all the content that was necessary for examinations.
The exams were very fair and covered a wide range of topics. The extra office hours were helpful, and I learn a lot about psychology from the class.
Overall, I liked the course, but as it is a lecture, it is hard to create a close relationship with the professor and TA's.

I took psych in high school and still found this class hard mainly because the final grade is calculated based on 3 midterms that are 60 multiple choice questions. the lectures and prof are great and amazing but if you can't memorize/apply you're stuck
I loved the class.

This is a good introduction class! Pacing is not too fast and the tests are reasonable. You will need to study for the tests and you should go to lectures. Lectures are engaging and help with understanding the content. The professor is very passionate and seems to have a true love for teaching.

This was a great course! It was not too difficult but I still feel that I learned a lot. The weekly assignments did not take long and were helpful in reviewing the material. The assignments are very clear and all of the instructions are easy to follow. Even though the content is relatively straightforward, it is necessary to study well for exams, because the questions can sometimes be tricky. Prof. Gardner is such a great lecturer and makes the content really interesting.

I LOVED THIS COURSE! It was challenging enough to be interesting but easy enough not to be stressful. I feel like I should have put more effort in throughout the course instead of cramming, but oh well. I think you can get through this course and get a lot out of it as long as you do what is expected of you.

Wendi Gardner is a great teacher who presents the material in a way that makes it a lot easier to learn and super interesting at the same time. The workload is super manageable, and I was able to study for tests just by making a Quizlet. I would really recommend this class.

Wendi Gardner is an excellent lecturer and covers the course content very well. The readings are short enough that you could leave them until the end, but I personally think the course is best enjoyed alongside the readings. This course definitely made me more interested in different areas of psychology and it left me excited to continue exploring the field

Professor Gardner is a great lecturer and really made learning basic psychology concepts really interesting, especially since her enthusiasm is contagious. I think that this is a great class to take if your looking for a lighter course and also have an interest in psychology.

This course does not require too much outside of class. It can be hard to study for exams especially if you don't find the topics too interesting. I really enjoyed the content discussed at the end of the quarter.
I highly recommend this course. Not only is it an easy A, it is also very interesting and generally fun to learn.
It is an intro psychology class. I wish a larger proportion of test questions were from the slides than the textbook.

This was a very fun and interesting course! The lecture were stimulating and Gardner was a really good professor. She was very passionate about psychology and taught the material very well. The tests were very representative of what we learned in class, and very manageable if you memorized the lectures and book chapters (but you really should know EVERYTHING from the lectures and the required sections of the book). Overall, it was a very easy work load, but a heavier study load before exams.

For those who are interested in psychology, this is a very well–taught introductory course, and the material is very engaging. Although at times it moved rather quickly with a lot of coursework, I felt that it was on the whole pretty manageable.

Wendi is incredible, one of the best profs here. The course is interesting and engaging, but exams are 80% of points which kind of sucks. While I wouldnt say its an easy A (i studied quite the fair amount for exams), its fairly straight forwards. Just memorizing the slides and book. I highly recommend this course for anyone though, youll learn alot about yourself and people + open doors for other cool psych courses
I enjoyed this course and found it very engaging. Psychology 110 was a great introduction to the science and has taught me many cool things about humans I didn't know before.

A great course overall. Would definitely advise taking it. The professor again is so passionate about psychology and makes the lectures as engaging as they can be (at the end of the day, they are still lectures so they can be boring at times).
I have nothing bad to say about this class. The lectures were so interesting, the content was relevant and memorable, the instruction style was engaging, and the exams were challenging but fair.

I thought the class was interesting since I like psychology. The only homework that takes any time at all is weekly textbook readings, which you can skip if you don't care about getting an A. Professor Gardner is great and kept the class time very interesting. If you want an A, go to class always and do all the readings. If you're fine with a B, take good notes and pay attention in class, and you'll be fine without doing the readings. Just make sure to study what is in the textbook study guide before every test. I enjoyed the class and definitely recommend it.

This course has an amazing professor her style of lecture is both engaging and informative. Likewise her exams are designed to be forgiving for first time psychology students and reflect what is taught in class well. If you go to all lectures and read you’ll do well in the course

Professor Gardner is really nice and she is passionate about what she's teaching. She makes the class interesting, and you spend maybe 10 minutes each week outside of class to complete that week's assignments. There are 3 midterms and no finals, and the week before the midterm you get a list of readings to complete in the textbook, but they also don't take that long to complete. You can do well in the class as long as you study by going over the slideshow and taking notes on the reading.
It was very helpful and interesting

Professor Gardner is such a sweet soul who makes a class full of first–years and 300 people so engaging. She shows videos, does polls in class to demonstrate how the concepts we talk about are applied to real life. I really liked the content and instruction, but I wasn't the biggest fan of the grading, given that an A boundary was 94 instead of a typical 93. I feel like this course was definitely not an intro–level course. The exams were long and were weighted very heavily. I can say that this course is memorization–heavy with a lot of information to internalize and apply to foreign scenarios. But in general, everything was so interesting and engaging!

I really enjoyed this course. In spite of having taken AP Psych just a year ago, more interesting material was introduced. Additionally, exams were straightforward as long as you didn't wait till the last minute to study.

Professor Gardner is the best lecturer!! There were 3 non–cumulative midterms which were very fair based on what we learned in class, and she posts the slides after every class (doesn't record lectures). There are super easy take–home quizzes each week which can boost your grade, and there are also extra–credit opportunities before each midterm. I never took a psych class before, and the topics here were super interesting and I learned so much! I totally recommend this class if you're interested in learning the basics of psych, it's been my favorite class at NU so far!!

Professor Gardner is a fantastic teacher who is very passionate about teaching in every lecture and uses many relatable and applicable scenarios to help us digest the material. The class is not hard, but certainly not the easiest A. Most students probably end with an A–. A great way to study for this class is to use flashcards and review notes. The class also includes discussions and mini–quizzes, which are great ways to review the lecture content. If you are at all interested in Psychology, this class is a great introduction to human behavior and the mind.

Professor Garnder posts lecture slides after class. The slides are super helpful but make sure to memorize ALL the concept mentioned on both the lectures slides and book chapter she assigns. The weekly quizzes are also helpful to access your understanding of the material every week. There are also plenty EC to bump up your grade.

Can be a very fun and engaging class, teaches you a lot of valuable things about psychology that you can apply to your real life. The class can be very long and there can be a lot of words on the slides and lectures it could be easy to lose focus on what the instructor explains so make sure to not get side tracked. Knowing what's in the textbook is important as well but it is not talked a lot about in class. We are given certain sections to know for the exams, so make sure to read it thoroughly and consistently, and try to make connections to all the topics we learn during our lectures. For the exams, it is important to try to contextualize and make connections and examples of all the content. The lectures are very large but it can help to know your T.A's and discussion groups. Exam viewing hours can be helpful to learn from all your mistakes on the exams, it is very important to know the broader significance of all the topics as well as the real world examples.

I LOVED THIS CLASS! I'm not even a psych major or minor, but I really think everyone should take this class (with Gardner!) to get a baseline understanding of psychology before they graduate. A lot of people seemed to struggle in this class, but it's also a large lecture and I'd guess at least half of people either don't come or just play games on their laptops. I managed to do pretty well (sitting at a high A right now) by just listening and taking handwritten notes in class and then figuring out a study method for the midterms that actually works. I depended a lot on Quizlet and then whiteboarding all the material to visually see how it all connects to each other. There is a LOT of information to learn but it's not hard to understand conceptually. You just need to have good time management before each midterm!! Every other assignment just helps to boost your grade (there are 4 2–point extra credit assignments and some other things). The weekly discussion posts were sort of annoying sometimes because you had to respond between the end of lecture on Thursday and midnight on Thursday, so it can be easy to forget sometimes, but that's very minor. Overall, an A is very achievable and I would highly recommend this class to anyone!

Intro to Psychology is a great class and the instructor does a great job of engaging with the class through humor and in–class demos. It is not too difficult to get an A in the class, however it would require at least bi–daily content review.

This course was a great introduction to psychology. We covered a lot of topics in not that much time, but it never felt rushed or like we were skipping over anything. Overall, I really enjoyed this class and it is really great if you are looking for something that isn't too hard, but still is engaging.
The topics we learned about were interesting, but I think the application and evaluation of those concepts could have been done in a more stimulating way.

This course was really interesting, and the workload was not terrible. As long as you studied continuously as you learned the material, the exams were very manageable, even with having a ton of concepts to understand. Also, the course was not just focused on memorizing keywords, but on understanding and applying concepts, which made it much more educative.
The course did a very good job in covering a lot of important baseline material while still having engaging and exciting lectures. I really appreciated the Prof's lecture style and personality.

I found this class super interesting and not too challenging. Although a lot of information is covered, it is all pretty connected across the quarter and it was clear what we were expected to know on the 3 exams. The exams were not too challenging and felt like they were designed for students to succeed, not to be tricked. Ultimately, I felt like it was a class designed for student success as long as you put in the time to study and pay attention in lecture.

I enjoyed the course. I think the material was interesting. The exams had a couple of trick questions, and it was hard sitting through an 80 minute lecture every week (although Prof. Gardner tried her best to keep it engaging), but I found the material interesting.

I think the psych studies we were required to do were way too many credits and took up too much time. I’d also say this is a difficult class for a 100 level ontroductory class, would not recommend to freshmen who are not used to rigorous work at northwestern

Prof Gardner is great!! This was a really interesting course, and did not take much time outside of the lecture times. I will say, there is a LOT of content, which makes the midterm prep a lot (at least compared to what the course requires outside of exams). I recommend flashcards and getting started on studying earlier. Professor Gardner is clearly really interested in the material and comes to lecture with energy that makes the 80 minutes a bit easier to sit through. My hand would start to cramp during lecture because of how much there was to write down, though. But, Prof Gardner would often do little activities or surveys in the lecture hall to demonstrate concepts, which kept students engaged. I would say great class, especially if its also a requirement for your major!

This class wasn’t too bad, and Wendi Gardner is an amazing professor. Her lectures are genuinely entertaining, which makes the material enjoyable to learn. Each week, you complete a discussion board post, respond to a classmate, and take an open–note, untimed quiz. The challenging part of the course is the exams. There are three exams with 60 questions each. About 80 percent of the material comes directly from the lecture slides, while the remaining 20 percent is from the textbook. It’s important to know all of the vocabulary well because the exams are entirely example based—you have to recognize what concept each example is demonstrating. The answer choices often include combinations like “A and B,” “B and C,” “none of the above,” or “all of the above,” which can make the questions tricky, especially given how much vocabulary is covered on each test. There isn’t much of a curve either, since she curves to the highest score, and in a large lecture like this class, someone almost always gets a 100. That said, the class is very interesting, and the weekly workload outside of lectures is pretty light. The only major time commitment is the focused studying needed to prepare for the exams.

Great class and prof Gardner is amazing. The content is interesting, but the exams are challenging, not because of the content but because the multiple–choice style is confusing. I felt like I could have answered every question she asked on the exams in a short– answer format, but the way the questions were phrased was difficult, which I did not really understand, and I don't see how it facilitates learning.
I found this course extremely interesting and enjoyable to attend. I also found the instructor very nice and welcoming to questions.
Amazing class. Professor is the goat.

This is not too difficult of a class if you are consistent with your studying. Definitely use flashcards because most of it is memorization, and do the extra credit because it doesn't take too long either. There are not too many assignments, and even then they don't take too long either. It's mostly just studying that takes a while. The exams are all multiple choice, but the questions can be really hard sometimes and can be application based. Reading the textbook on your own time is essential as well since questions based on the textbook content often show up on exams as well. Professor Gardner is a very good instructor though, and is definitely quite engaging during class.

A really interesting course. You can really learn a lot about psychology during this course. There are three non–cumulative exams. Also, you need to participate in studies assigned to you outside of class time.

Professor Gardner is really cool, even though the lectures were really long and Fisk is the ultimate sleepy hall, I still found many topics to be really interesting and she explained it very well and in a way that made sense. She is very encouraging and the tests are not difficult. I would say that the textbook kind of sucks and the slides are worded in a way that can be hard to decipher if you haven't taken psych before, but it is not that big a deal. Memorizing from the slides for the exams was time consuming, so I would advise you to start early.

I really enjoyed this class. The class is lecture style, so at times the work can feel tedious, but that was expected. It is also not an easy course if you are not genuinely interested. There are a lot of concepts and material to memorize, which can make the exams more difficult than expected. I do think Professor Gardner is a great professor and did a good job of making the class as fun as it could be.

The lectures aren't boring at all, and I found myself excited to come to class every day(though I'm a psych minor FYI). That being said, the first exam is quite difficult in comparison to the other two(three exams a quarter), because I had no idea how to study for the class. Having a SYSTEM is the most important thing. The three extra credit papers also saved my grade, and are with doing! They help you understand the material without being overwhelming.
I really enjoyed the psychologically taught in this class

This was a great course. I'm in McCormick, so it was a big switch–up from what I'm used to, but I loved it. Professor Gardner is great. She is so nice and friendly and really passionate, and she genuinely wants all of her students to learn and appreciate psychology as well. The class was a great intro, covering a bunch of different topics to sort of get you familiar with the field. The weekly quizzes were not at all daunting as they were unlimited time and open–note, so they were really more helpful for studying than anything. The tests were very fair, and Professor Gardner and the TAs each provided a review session in the days leading up to the test which I recommend taking advantage of. I will say I did have to put in a lot of time with studying, but I enjoyed it because I find the material so interesting. I would definitely recommend this class and Professor Gardner!

I loved Wendi Gardner as a professor, she was so fun, and really made the course exciting. My only bone to pick with this class is that the exams were all multiple choice, and I feel like I endd up getting worse grades than I normally would have on a shrt answer style test, where I could explain my thinking, instead of getting tripped up on wording for the multiple choice quetstuins

I really enjoyed this class. I found that the content was very engaging and Professor Gardener to be spectacular in lectures. My one complaint is the lack of exam–style practice problems provided to us. I found that after the first exam, the next two felt substantially easier once I was able to get a sense of what can be asked, how it is asked, and just a better feeling of how the instructors of this class formulate the exams. With more practice, studying would be easier and more beneficial. For many classes, especially this one, exam–style questions help concisely test if one can apply concepts from class. Without them, I felt that my studying efforts were ineffective. All in all, however, this class was very interesting and fun.

The class was very difficult, but I felt as though what was expected of the class was communicated quite effectively. Study materials often reflected exams, and there was never a time when there would be random influxes of assignments dumped on to us.
Good lecturer and relatively easy tests, just memorize flashcards
This was a very interesting course and you truly learn so much. This was a class with low amounts of work, and I genuinely looked forward to going to each lecture because they were fun.

Highly recommend this course, especially if you can take it with Gardner. She is the reason i'm now pursuing a psych minor, and she is one of the best human beings I've ever met. I know that literally everyone shares this opinion, but I found myself paying attention in class every day even though I already had learned most of the content in AP Psych.

This is definitely an interesting class. The averages on the exams are mid 80s, but you NEED to go to lecture and takes notes to do well on the exams. There are no recordings and she will say things that are not on the slide. Do a ltitle bit each day since the content can get overwheling (150ish flashcards per exam). Otherwise she is a great lecturer.
This course doesn’t have many highlights, but it’s a decent/OK introductory course to psychology. The Professor Gardner's teaching style is enthusiastic, which makes contents interesting.

This course was interesting because it shows how individuals learn, remember, and recall, and it teaches you about certain schools of psychology, as well as how some people either do really well or struggle with certain tasks because of how different our brains are.

I thought this course was great as an introductory course, especially for pre–meds. Of course, Prof Gardner made sure you learned the content on a deep level by giving fun and interesting examples to all of the topics, so that by the end of the unit the content was pretty much ingrained in my mind.

I really enjoyed this class and thought it was easier than I expected it to be. As long as you attend class, take good notes, do your work, and read the sections of the textbook that Professor Gardner points out, you can do well! Gardner is a great lecturer and I always found class insightful + fun.

LOVE professor gardner, she's genuinely the sweetest person and always willing to meet during office hours to go over concepts or exam concerns. the content itself is not difficult, and the weekly discussion posts are very easy to get done. there are 3 midterms and no final. midterms are all 60 multiple choice questions. i struggled a little with this, because i could study and walk out of the test confident i got an 100% but end with a much lower grade than anticipated. definitely figure out your study method earlier rather than later. definitely recommend studying with friends.

I love this class so much. Prof. Gardner is such an engaging lecturer! It made me want to switch my major to psychology. Questions on midterms can be tricky sometimes, so make sure you understand all the concepts. Studying with others is helpful.

This is a very well–taught course. Prof. Gardner is amazing and very engaging and funny. The lectures run a bit long, but the concepts are explained very well. It is not a free A, but it is not difficult to get a good grade in the class as long as you submit assignments on time and study well for tests.

It was a good class. You defiantly need to study to get an A. If you don't do the readings and memorize all the vocab, you won't get an A. Gardner is a great teacher so you are in great hands. She makes it a fun environment to learn.

If you took AP Psych in high school, this class is incredibly similar to that (I just didn't score high enough on the AP exam for credit at Northwestern so I took this class). Because I had learned it all before, this class was pretty easy. The slides aren't the most clear because of how much content they contain (but if you miss class, it is very helpful bc literally everything is on there). Professor Gardner is also a great lecturer, and the exams aren't bad (just make sure you actually put in ~2 hours or so bc the questions can get pretty specific and there's a lot of facts to know).

This class is amazing for anyone who is even slightly interested in psychology. It provides a solid foundation for psychology that is very similar to AP Psychology, with just a little bit more depth and variety in topics. There are three midterms that consist of only multiple choice questions, and most students will be able to earn an A if they put in the time and effort, which is very small compared to other classes. Understanding the content is very easy but there is lots of memorization required. The class doesn't require too much reading. Prof. Gardner is the most sweetest professor I have ever met, and she teaches with great passion for the subject. She was absolutely amazing and very helpful; there is no need to worry about having a bad professor in this class.
It was fairly easy. It was a lot of blunt memorization (which can be good depending on the person).
Dr. Gardner was a very good teacher and explained the subject very well. The tests weren't very challenging but definitely still take time to study for.
I liked this course. Professor Gardner was very enthusiastic and fun to listen to.

As an engineer, I really enjoyed this class. It was so different from everything else I was doing and I found myself talking about things I'd learned outside of class. Professor Gardner has structured the class really well.

Wendi Gardner is the BEST! 3 exams, not too bad, next one gets easier. Extra Credit opportunities and weekly open note quizzes are amazing. If you're taking 110, please take with her. Best lecturer as well.
Great psych course, just the psych study participation credits are a bit of a pain

I took this class thinking it would be an easy way to fulfill a distribution requirement. If that is why you are looking at this class, stop and go do something else. It was not easy and I ended up going P/N. Wendi is great and sweet and clearly cares a lot abt the class so thats wonderful. But exams were difficult and highly weighted and I really strugled. Even after studying intensively for the exams and using the textbook pratice quizzes, I could not break 65/80 on the midterms. I'm annoyed I spent money on the textbook and wish I had not registered for this class. That said, if you are already good at psych and know you like it, the prof is great and the weekly workload is not intense.
I liked how this course covered a lot including learning about the brain, about personality disorders, about substance use, and a lot of other topics.
Taught very clearly, good introduction to the subject, covers a good variety of topics
Great lectures.

Professor Gardner is incredible. Course material is light, lectures are pretty interesting (she makes what would be really dry content into something engaging), but the exams kind of got me. I didn't realize it would be so difficult. I tried adapting my study methods, but still the hefty amount of memorization just did not work for my brain, and I got consistent B's. She gives 3 extra credit papers, and open–note mini quizzes every weekend which inflate your grade. Most people did really well though.

Very pleasant course; the material is interesting, prof. Gardner and TAs are really nice and helpful, and everything can be done on time as long as you follow the lessons and assignments on weekly basis.
TAKE THIS CLASS! Professor Gardener is the best Professor ever! She is amazing and teaches everything so well! This class is by far the most interesting class I've ever taken.

Prof Gardner is super adorable and passionate about psychology! Her enthusiasm makes you learn super easily in lectures so you don't need to study much. Content was a very classic Psych 101 type of thing so if you know a bit about psych you probably know most of this course. However, still learnt some cool facts.

I absolutely loved this class! If you have the chance to take this class with Professor Gardner, I highly highly recommend. She is the absolute best professor; she is incredible at answering questions, so so sweet to her students, and an incredibly engaging lecturer. I really loved learning more about psychology in this class. All the topics were really interesting, and it was a great overview of the intro subjects with no prior experience. There is not much work for this class either––a weekly open note quiz and discussion post/reply. There are also three extra credit papers that can help boost your grade a bit. There is reading for this class, but I recommend waiting until Professor Gardner posts the reading guide, which lists exactly which sections of the textbook you will need for the exams. There are three noncumulative midterms that definitely require a few days of studying beforehand, but I did not think that any exam was unfair. If you put in a bit of effort, study hard before each exam, and read the required sections, getting an A will not drive you crazy.

The class itself is fairly simple if you go to lectures. The class consists of two weekly lectures and textbook readings, and then a discussion board about the lecture/textbook content for the week. There are also quizzes each week and three noncumulative exams. Overall, I would highly recommend taking this course with Prof. Gardner, as she does a really good teaching and is very engaging.
I learned so much about myself in this class! I highly recommend taking it, regardless of your major.

I absolutely loved this course. Professor Gardner is without a doubt one of the best professors I have ever had. Her enthusiasm is apparent, her grading is fair, her lectures are engaging, and this course was planned very clearly and very well which helped me to stay on top of everything.

I really enjoyed Introduction to Psychology and felt very intrigued during lectures. Dr. Gardner does a fantastic job presenting information and explaining it in a comprehensive way. She really captivates the students in her lecture. Her passion for psychology is clear. I also appreciated her assistance and her TA's assistance with unexpected absences. Professor Gardner and her TAs provide all the necessary recourses to prepare for the exams. The exams are very challenging and require complete understanding of almost every aspect of what we went over in class as well as the textbook sections she recommended we read. I loved taking this class and hope to take more courses in this topic with Professor Gardner.

This class was very similar to AP Psych or any other intro level psych course. We touched on pretty much every part of psychology, but I never felt that the amount of content was overwhelming. Lecture is not required and slides were always posted, but I suggest actually going to class because that is how I learned most of the material for the exams. There were 3 non cumulative exams which made studying a lot less stressful. Overall I really suggest this course if you are at all interested in exploring psychology, and I learned a lot!

Overall a great choice for the THEME requirement in McCormick if you have even a little bit of interest in the subject. The work is manageable with the rest of the freshman fall quarter engineering curriculum. The exams aren’t super difficult as long as you attend lectures and study the material well. I came into this class with a lot of interest in the subject and was eager to learn more and was not disappointed in the slightest. I highly recommend it to anybody.

Take this course! Definitely not too challenging, weekly homework was quick and tests are all multiple choice but focus on applying the concepts in different scenarios, in a fair way. There’s a required lab component, where you participate in studies, but breaking those up throughout the quarter makes it much more manageable.

This course was mentally stimulating and fun to learn, definitely not a walk in the park but once you learn how you are tested and link with real life examples and don't focus on memorization you will be golden. The professor is very good at getting info to stick and going to lecture really helps as she builds off of powerpoints and sometimes everything she says isn't in the slides.

This was by far my favorite lecture class. It was so interesting. Definitely challenging but rewarding! As long as you keep up with the material and review consistently, you will be fine, and there's lots of extra credit opportunities.

The material is interesting, and Prof Gardner is great at teaching it and very passionate. The tests are not easy, but nothing unreasonable, and there are opportunities for extra credit which is nice. I would definitely recommend this class. It's honestly interesting to learn about, and the prof is very nice and willing to answer questions.
It was an interesting class, Gardner is a great lecturer. Solid start to my psych major.

This course was a little disappointing. The instruction was good and the workload was light, but it definitely favored breadth of content over depth. Additionally, you don't have to really understand the content to do well in the course—the exams are mostly key terms and very basic questions covering various topics.

TAKE THIS CLASS WITH WENDI GARDNER!!!! This class was my FAVORITE that I took this quarter. It was so interesting and engaging, she is genuinely the sweetest person and a great lecturer. I learned SO MUCH and still think about the content all the time. Highly recommend!!
I thought that the instruction was by far better for learning than any of the homework or reading.

I was only mildly interested in Psychology before taking this course, but now am fascinated with the science. The lectures were very well made and super engaging, but there is a lot of content that must be memorized. There are many extra credit opportunities to buffer your grades from content–heavy exams.

I loved this course. I thought that the material was very entertaining/interesting and the professor was amazing. She was so passionate about the class and made it so much better/caused me to look forward to it! I would definitely recommend this course to anyone who is looking at it.
Very fun and easy.

It was very engaging, professor is great and wants your to succeed and learn, slides are informative, activities are fun, easy to get an A, multiple opportunities for extra credit, research lab portion is easy to cram if you're too busy during the quarter
This was such a fun class to take, you get to learn a lot of interesting things about people and psychology that you can apply in the real world.

This is a fantastic introductory class to psychology and really gets you thinking about different psychology concepts around you every day with many relatable examples. Professor Gardner's lectures are very detailed and engaging, and if you put in the time to study, you will do well.
Professor Gardner is THE BEST lecturer I've ever had. This course is so so interesting because of her. She is AMAZING! Take this class!
Engaging and very understanding professor. She is a great resource. Very important to read the textbook in addition to attending lectures!
Great!

This course is very easy if you go to lectures/take notes. Wendi Gardner is the best and so nice — definitely take it with her. There are three straightforward exams and none are cumulative as well as a weekly open note quiz that's take–home. Also weekly discussion post.

Great class. WG is an amazing teacher and lecturer. Despite what a lot of people say I don’t think it’s the easiest distro. Exams aren’t cumulative, but you have to memorize A LOT of information for each test. Test averages were around 85 and make up the majority of the grade, so definitely not an easy A (unless you’re good at memorization). I learned a lot and would definitely recommend to anyone interested in pych.

This is not a blow–off class. It is not hard, either but I think a lot of people go into it thinking it will be easy but it requires a deep understanding of the material. The three tests throughout the quarter pretty much make or break your grade and the discussion posts do very little to help average it. The prof was AMAZING tho she was the cutest ever and super passionate.
The course was great at connecting to current topics, and it was constantly engaging.

I have nothing negative to say about the course or Dr. Gardner. The course material is presented in a way that is engaging and easy to remember, the tests are exactly what you'd expect and not cumulative, and it feels like Dr. Gardner truly wants everyone to do well. You cover a ton of material without the course being too difficult and Dr. Gardner is an amazing lecturer.

I highly recommend Psych–110 as a distro or If you have ever had an interest in psychology. Prof Gardener is a great lecturer and I am genuinely considering a psych minor because of this course. You should get an A if you try. The class is out of 300 points – 60 free points for homework and weekly open note (asynchronous) quizzes, 6 free extra credit points, and 240 points from 3 equally– weighted midterms (80 points each). You have to get 282/300 to get an A (6 points of error on each midterm – or factoring in extra credit…8 points), but its honestly not that hard if you go to class and start studying a day or two before. The tests are super fair and they get curved to the highest score. There isn’t any homework, but given the low margin of error to get an A, it is worth the time to study a good few hours the weekend before (depending on how fast you learn things) and take good notes. Overall, I thought it was a good class, not as easy of an A as I thought, but if you put in the time (which really isn’t a lot), you should do well and have a good understanding of psych. I actually reference what I learned in this class all the time, kind of fun.

This class is fantastic. As a freshman who had never taken a psych class before, this was the best introduction to the subject I could've asked for. Prof. Gardner is a great lecturer. She brings energy every day, and there's always at least one thing (usually multiple) that she says during lecture which genuinely surprises you and makes you reflect about your own life/mind. There isn't much homework outside of the textbook readings, but she provides a study guide that tells you all the readings you need to do for the test (so you can skip everything else). If you do all of those readings and take notes, it takes a while to get through but you only have to do that three times in the quarter for the three exams. In general, the exams are very fair. If you take good notes during lecture (very important) and do the reading, you will probably do very well in this class.
Normal intro to psych cousre, very engaging.

Professor Gardner is the best! She is so nice and enjoyable to listen to. However, the slideshows are the only instructional pieces you receive and the tests are soooo much harder than the information you learn in class as the tests expect you to apply the terms and concepts, which isn't taught in lecture. While there's not a lot of work week–to–week, succeeding on the tests requires A LOT of preparation and practice.
If you don't like memorizing, don't take this class.
I really enjoyed learning about all the interesting studies in psychology history. Professor Gardner explained them all very clearly and helped us understand the concepts better.
There is a lot of memorizing specific ideas. Make sure to know the content well, especially from the textbook. The class itself is very interesting and you learn a lot of cool concepts.

This was an amazing course! The concepts were very interesting and Professor Gardner was so supportive and kind! Though the class has 3 difficult exams, you get better with each test, and you start to learn how to study, so I would still highly recommend this course!

Hmmmm... I took this as class as a sophomore and definitely felt like I was one of like 10 non–freshmen (Gardner also I think kind of assumed everyone would be a freshman haha). I thought Gardner's lectures were quite good, but a lot of her lecture slides sometimes just felt likes walls of text. Exams are 60 multiple choice questions. They were okay. The wording of questions I felt sometimes could be better. Just memorize the slides, and you should do fine. Very low commitment on non–exam weeks, which is nice if you have other time–consuming courses.

For me, the most important thing was having a professor and TAs who truly support you. The exams can be a bit challenging, but there’s no shortage of opportunities to improve your grade. Whether through weekly quizzes or additional papers that offer extra credit.

I took AP Psychology in high school and decided to take this class, thinking it would be an easy A and cover the same content as I learned before. In reality, obtaining an A in this class is much harder than high school, as out of 300 points, you can only afford to lose 18ish and still have an A. That being said, all of the non–exam grades are very easy and straightforward, with the weekly quizzes being take–home. If I could take this class again, I would've studied more for the exams and gone to office hours, but with minimal studying, I got an A–, so I'm not disappointed. An A is very achievable, just put in the work, even if it seems like it's not necessary. Gardner is really passionate about psychology and makes every lecture as interesting as it can be, even though some of the content itself is quite bland.
I thought this was a great intro course! It encouraged me to be a psych minor. The instruction and assessments are clear and fair.
The lecture slides and explanations were really good and informative.
I loved this class and Professor Gardner was a super good lecturer.

This class has no final and is instead graded on open–book quizzes, weekly discussions, and three equally weighted exams which was pretty nice. If you can make sure to do the extra credit as well because it's fairly simple and quite a big grade booster.
Professor is super sweet and is definitely passionate about psychology. A lot of memorization in this class, not a super easy A.

Class content was fascinating to me personally. Dr. Gardner is a great lecturer. Frustrating thing though is the only thing that contributes to your grade is the weekly discussion board post (super easy A), and the exams. That's literally it (unless you consider the optional extra credit papers). Also the exams were always unnecessarily difficult in comparison to the actual material taught in class.

Also, the lab requirement was super irritating. The studies are assigned to you and you have no choice but to go, unless you want to write a 10–15 page research paper. Almost all of mine this quarter were in person (in fact, some of my online ones were switched to in–person), even in locations off–campus, which can be an annoying trek, especially in the cold. Not to mention the studies (at least the ones I was assigned) were all very boring and tedious.

This course is interesting and the extra credit assignments and the best way I was able to demonstrate my knowledge of the topics. However, the exams had many mistakes and even after the TA's acknowledged there were mistakes on the exams, they would take note of them, but points were not distributed back. The exams are weighted SO HEAVILY and the curve provided little to no benefit for the vast majority of students. This class may be difficult for individuals who struggle with test performance.
Professor Gardner is the best! Her lectures were so engaging and her tests were very fair.

As a theater major, I don't particulalrly like big lecture classes, so my opinion of the class is sort of tainted! The professor was very lovely and gave very very detailed lectures that made understanding the material very easy! If for whatever reason you have to miss, I would recomend setting a meeting as the slides didn't always have all of the information talked about. The professor gave a few extra credit assignments and tests are curved (hope for no one who gets 100% every time). If you don't like lectures classes, I wouldn't try it. Tests get easier in order, so don't despair if test 1 has you scrambling and panicking.
The workload for this class is very light, as long as you attend the lectures and pay attention it's easy to do well. The lectures are really interesting and engaging as well
Very fun class. Got me interested in psychology. Easy A. Studied for all the exams the night before. Just make sure to do all your homework, and if u have some time left, do the extra credits!

I really enjoyed this class because the material was interesting to me, and prof. Gardner was amazing. She went through lectures at a reasonable pace and always gave examples to help us understand concepts. Workload was super manageable; I found it pretty easy. There were 3 tests throughout the quarter, and most of the tested material was straight from lectures. There were recommended textbook readings which usually went a little more in–depth about stuff but its content really only came up on 3–4 questions on the test. I definitely recommend this class if you're interested in psych or just want an easy distro.
Good intro psych class! Lots of memorizing for tests, but I thought this helped me really learn the content (there was a lot).

I loved this course, so much so that I am now planning to major in Psych. Professor Gardner is amazing. She is kind, adorable, and so enthusiastic. She made learning so interesting and fun. The slides she posts are very helpful when studying for all the midterms. The extra credit opportunities really boosted my grade so make sure to do them. Overall amazing course that I would 1000 percent reccomend especially with professor Gardener.

This class is not that bad if you just put in the work. Go to class even though it might be tempting to skip. Pay attention and take notes. I wrote my notes down which probably helped me more when studying for exams. When studying, make flashcards and make sure you know all of the info on the slides and in the textbook. Gardner is an amazing lecturer and she is always answering everyone's questions. The exams are not that bad.

Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments:
This course was broken down into the following components:

4 Exams: 80% Participation: 8% Problem Sets 12%

With an exam every Friday, every day was spent in the library preparing for the exams. This was quite literally a full–time job. Professors Brace and Truchan were wonderful and want you to succeed, answering diligently on the discussion board and staying after class to answer any extra questions. The summer version of Bio 201 is the exact same as the spring course (content wise), but the class size is much smaller. This helped me in the course as it felt more personable and you were able to ask questions in class anytime.

This course went FAST and it is extremely important to keep on top of the studying.

This course was intriguing and interesting but it did require lots of studying and practicing and working with the content outside of class. This biology class has a lot of content to learn within a few weeks and it’s a lot of memorization.

Taking this class in the summer is great. With the smaller class size, the professors are much more accessible. Although it’s definitely a challenging course, it’s not like THAT HARD HARD — it’s doable if you put in the work.

Taking Bio 201 over the summer was both easier and harder than taking it in the Spring. The smaller class allowed me to ask questions that I would not have been able to do otherwise, and the active participation during lecture allowed me to stay focused. With that being said, the course was extremely challenging with a lot of material to learn. We were told it would be like a full–time job, and it truly was. As someone with other activities and jobs outside of this course, that made it difficult to keep up. The exams required students to know every detail, and were as much of a reading comprehension exam as a biology one. However, the professors were always as helpful as possible, even with the amount of material we needed to learn. It was definitely fast–paced, so it really depends on what each student prioritizes in terms of how they learn.
I learnt a lot.

source_file: Student Report for Hilary Truchan (BIOL_SCI_201-0_1  Molecular Biology)_eecffa9f-18e3-40f9-a949-4a28ca18db3fen-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: This course was very engaging and useful.
The class is manageable only if you stay on top of lectures and studying. There are exams every other week which is not bad because the tests aren't cumulative.

To succeed in this class you don't have to put in that much effort all the time but when it comes to the tests you will need to study significantly to do well, besides that if you just put in the work I think you will get the results you want.
The lectures were quite engaging and the group exam encouraged collaboration which is important to me

If you haven't taken AP Bio before this class will be pretty hard. I was not able to keep up with my friends (or the curve for that matter) as most people in the class already knew the information being taught. The instructors are honestly terrible at explaining concepts, and the tests are mostly based on your ability to READ a question and figure out the semantics rather than actually knowing the content.

This class was very difficult in terms of the exam, the homework assignments are not even close to what the exam is like. I thought I had understood the material well but you can never study enough for the exams, they are very unpredictable. I didn't like how there were 2 teachers for it.
This course established a strong foundation and was very manageable. I took AP biology in high school and this course reflected many of the things I learned there.

Super super easy intro biology class. Way easier than anything I took in high school. The only complaint is the teachers. I hated that they switched in the middle of the term. I really liked Truchan, she is very nice, talks slowly, and has very visually appealing and informative slides. Brace is kinda the opposite. Her slides were confusing, uninformative, she talks at the speed of light, and everything was disorganized. The exams are individual, all multiple choice, and fairly easy. There isa. group portion that you take with 3 other people, but it counts for like nothing of your grade so don't worry about it. Uncurved, but you should not need a curve if you study. Learn and memorize the learning objectives that they publish.
Ok. Make sure to read every question and answer word for word on the tests
Grades didn't reflect understanding of material.

For someone who has never taken bio before this class was a lot of information, especially in the second half. It got to be pretty fast

4/15 paced but outside of class there is like no work. There is a 15q problem set due sundays before the exams. there were 4 midterms one eveyr other week that were 30ish mcqs (w select all that applies) and we would have an hour to take it individually and then an additional 30 to take it in groups of which our final grade was weighted 85/15. The final was optional to replace our lowest midterm.

The content of the course was overall interesting. However, the tests were difficult not because of the biology content but because of the ambiguous wordings, multiple answer selections, and trick questions.

Prof Truchan did a better job at explaining content, so it was easier to study for the first two exams (bc she taught the content for those). Prof Brace moves very fast at times, and you miss a lot of what she says in lecture. She goes fast and its more difficult to absorb the content as class goes along, you kinda have to go back after class and review yourself to really digest it. I also felt like she included small details on the exams that she maybe mentioned once during lecture. With that being said, Brace is still a good teacher (at times she would emphasize certain things to study and how to study them, and these topics were always heavily emphasized during exams), but you have to do more outside review with Brace's part of the class. That is also went class material started getting harder. Overall, good first bio class and both profs are very very nice and easy to talk to if you have questions
this class was slightly difficult at times but it was a good class because the lectures and the assignments were very useful in helping us learn.

This was a good course overall. 4 exams every other week, plus an optional final that can replace your lowest exam grade, plus an extra 2 points to your final grade if you complete the surveys (which is a must). First half is taught by Prof. Truchan and the second half is Prof. Brace, both of which were very good lecturers. Depending on your learning style, you may favor one over the other, but what really saved me personally was rewatching and taking notes on the lectures. I found it very helpful to make flashcards for the important points and concepts.

HOWEVER, I will say that it is possible to over–study for the exams. The exams are 1 hour for ~32 questions, and a handful of questions are "select all that apply," which for the record does NOT mean that there is more than 1 right answer. Most of the battle on the tests is reading the question correctly, as they can be tricky. It is very easy to overthink a question, so I would recommend knowing the fundamental points that are stressed in lecture and in the learning objectives instead of getting bogged down in insignificant details, which actually can hurt you if you overthink a question too much. Piazza was also very helpful to ask clarifying questions.

My small complaints are about the problem sets and the group exams. First, the 15–question problem sets are supposed to "prepare you for the exam" but I found them to be confusingly worded (which seemed almost intentional at times) and they did not reflect what exam material looked like. This was annoying since a lot of my time was spent worrying over something that really wasn't relevant for the exam. Second is the group exams, which is the same exam from the individual portion in your chosen groups of 4. It only counts for 15% of your "final" exam grade (and does not count against you if the group does worse than your individual score). Unless you do significantly worse than your group exam, the 15% really does not do much for your grade. But the main thing is that it's the *exact* same exam as in the individual portion, which seems not very helpful in my opinion... I feel like taking another similar shorter exam would reflect "group work/collaboration" better than just people saying what they put for the individual portion. On top of that, maybe making the group exam weighted more would also help boost grades and reward group collaboration more.

The course gives out good information of macromolecules but the exams are quite challenging. This is because the instructors do not give out any practice materials and only one problem set which you cannot even access answers to after you have taken it. The one mini practice exam before the actual exam is the closest thing to giving help and even then, that is not nearly enough. Basically, you have to watch through the lectures again and memorize every specific case that they may ask on the exams. So in short, good material but could be a better way to get the information through to students.

This class was interesting but definitely requires you to put in work to understand concepts. Professor Truchan is very straightforward, and all the information necessary for exams are on her slides. Professor Brace wasn't as clear and her slides were also a bit confusing at times.
This felt like a weed out class.

This class is terribly taught. Both professors are bad, but Brace is terrible at teaching. They both go so fast and give you power points that barely have any explanation on them because they are copied and pasted from the textbook, which also does not explain more. Truchan is completely unsympathetic. The test only tests memorization, they say otherwise, but everyone who has an A just says they memorize the powerpoint. The tests are also written to trick you, which is weird because is this not a bio class, so why are doing trickery! It becomes how much time do you have for this class.

This course is very manageable, but can be fast–paced due to the testing schedule. But really all you have to do is be attentive during class and takes notes on the lecture recordings before an exam and you should be fine.

This course should be only taken if you have to. I took it because of a degree requirement. There is a lot of information shoved in to short periods of time. There are four midterm exams, one every two weeks. Before each exam, there is a problem set, which are nothing like exams and don't really help all that much for learning the material. It's tangentially related. There is the option to take the final exam to replace the grade for one of the midterms. It's not too difficult, but you definitely have to study. Professor Brace moves even faster than Truchan so make sure to keep on top of everything.

Solid bio class. Nothing too crazy. 4 midterms but no sweat. Just make sure you are reviewing lecture slides along the way so

5/15 you're not stressing out too much before each midterm. Aim to not have to take the final.
This course is challenging in its testing methods.

I was a bit disappointed with this course. I find biology fascinating and was super engaged with the material, but the frequent testing was exhausting and the exams did not always feel fair. They were all multiple choice and no partial credit was given for "Select All" questions. The two professors also test pretty differently, which I wasn't prepared for.

This class has 4 midterms and 1 optional final. There is a problem set due before each midterm and a practice quiz and review session the classes before the midterms. Make sure to stay organized and on top of what you're learning because its especially hard to cram for this class since its so much memorization – paying attention in class goes a long way.
This class is not very difficult if you prepare well enough for exams. Homework is minimal and classes can be somewhat boring.

This course was very challenging but there is a lot of help you can receive. The professors were confused and the pace was very fast at times, where teachers couldn't maintain the pace, but overall it was an okay course.

I liked this course, but it is one of those courses that people take because it is a requirement. I was not a huge fan of the teaching; it felt very rushed. Having four exams, one every two weeks, can be very brutal, but there is a LOT of content covered on each. It was interesting having one professor teach it in the first half and another teach in the second half.

This course is a rodeo. You need to be on top of it from the beginning of the quarter. With that being said, it is not impossible to do well. It is a little hard to adjust to the difference in teaching styles once the switch with professors occurs mid–quarter.

As someone who really did enjoy biology previously, I think this class made me like it a lot less. It constantly felt like the exams were purposefully trying to trick us and that they were testing us on arbitrary little things that were barely mentioned. That said, it's not super hard or a lot of work, so just try your best and hope for the best – and be a very good test taker!

This course was pretty bad to be honest... mostly having midterms every other week. The lectures went crazy fast so it was hard to catch everything, without going back over the slides/recordings. It was also weird having professors switch halfway through the quarter. Tests progressively got more difficult in my opinion.

I was really interested in Biology when I first came into the course. But this course really made me rethink a lot of things. Although the material itself isn't entirely difficult, the professors structure the class and the exams in a way that makes it really hard to do well which is unfair despite the amount of time you spend studying. On top of that, it is around a 400–person class so you would expect averages to be fairly ranged but for some reason, they were always extremely high. I do appreciate the group exam portion, however, since you get a few points back. But the questions on exams themselves were designed to really trick you, which isn't always necessary given that you have understood the content fully.

This course is a great introduction to the biology sequence– group exams after the individual portion, great extra credit, optional final– but I wasn't a big fan of how the class was taught. After the first 2 exams and talking with other people, I found that the only effective way to study is to rewatch lectures and rewrite notes over and over again. I would memorize the material for a couple days, and then immediately forget it after the exams. Truchan's notes and teaching style was a lot easier to follow than Brace too. Brace's notes were often unorganized, and she would bring up unnecessary information up during lecture as well.
Class did not help me learn or appreciate biology more. Class is focused on trick questions on exams rather than actual understanding and application of biological concepts.

Professor Brace and Truchan are pretty good profs, and they make their expectations pretty clear. The Sunday Zooms were super helpful, but sometimes really confusing in terms of format and stuff. Sort of odd why you can't review exams any time after the the actual exam? A little hard to get ahold of the profs outside of class, though.
Learned a lot about the subjects that were provided which I need to know for my major.

the homework and class does not prepare you well for the exams, which are majority of the grade. The exams are all multiple choice with no option for partial credit. they also include trick questions to confuse you. molecular biology is an interesting topic but the course is unnecessarily difficult and does not test your knowledge well.
I really enjoyed this course! I thought Professor Truchan and Brace taught the subject very well and at a good speed. All exams were mostly fair, but many of the questions try to trick you.

This class was really interesting and gave a great introduction to molecular biology. I personally enjoyed the material, and found it VERY helpful to rewatch the lectures and take notes on the lectures again in order to cement my understanding of the material. It was kind of an abrupt transition to switch professors in the middle of the quarter because you have to get used to a completely new teaching style, but after a few lectures, you are able to adjust. Further, the four midterms can be daunting because you are taking an exam every other week, but the nice part is that there is only four lectures for each midterm, which makes it nice because there is less content for each midterm, which helps when trying to study. It also helps that they aren't cumulative, and I think it overall helps cement the material earlier on as opposed to two midterms.

The class is weirdly paced, in addition to there being 2 different professors. There isn’t a really good handoff because their styles of teaching is very different. The professors seem to care about students, so attend office hours when necessary. I don’t like the idea that the class isn’t curved.

It was hard but was to be expected. Make sure to really understand the slides. You don’t have to read the textbook so don’t bother.

6/15
