It's probably a required course for you so just take it. You just have to know all the content on the slides and from class. The tests are weird and they try to trick you so it's kinda more of a comprehension test rather than a bio test sometimes

4 midterms every other week. 1 optional final that is part cumulative part choose which midterm you want to retake. The midterm test select all that apply questions are annoying but the actual content on the exam is very reasonable and okay if you study. If you study a bit every day this class should be light. That being said, I still think that an intro level bio class should not be this annoying with exam questions.
Good Biology class with a lot of AP content review. Exams are multiple choice and not too hard, but you will be expected to understand how to apply concepts. The professors are very nice.

As a bio major who did extensive bio in high school, BIO 201 was MUCH harder than I thought it would be. A very big class with a heavy premed concentration, so don't expect the class average to be low. There was a lot of material and I feel like there were times when things weren't explained very well. Find a good group of people to work on the PSETs together. Exams every other week was extremely draining but ensured that you don't fall behind.

Overall, I enjoyed this course despite its few flaws. The wording on some of the test questions was pretty ambiguous and could lead to incorrect answers despite knowledge of the content. However, I do think that studying effectively will still pay off and lead to success regardless. This class is also helpful for those who want to improve their study strategies, since it requires pretty effective studying to do super well.
Overall a fine class. Need to do very well on the exams since every exam is heavily weighted.

For a lot of people, this class is just the start of a long sequence of stem classes. It's a bit unfortunate that there's no curve, but the post–course survey is basically the same – 2 points is a lot, make sure you don't miss it. If you want an A– or A expect to study a bit, but most of the challenge comes from understanding the test questions rather than the basics of the material. Psets were the worst part imo, make sure to check your work with friends and ask around in the TA session if able. Not all TAs are created equal, so find one who works well with you and then stick with them. Get feedback on your exam score if you're confused. The 1 optional replacement test is a nice safety net of sorts, but don't slack off on the last test because many people seem to agree that it was exceptionally challenging.

This is a really disappointing class for people actually interested in biology because it takes the fun out of lectures. You just have to get through it. The slides were not great aids and the pacing of the class is fast. But, if can come to class well rested and pay attention it makes exams a lot easier. By far the worst part of the class are the exams, there is no partial credit given on the select all questions. However, the 2% grade boost through extra credit, problem sets, and participation grade give opportunities to get your grade up. Just be ready to commit a lot of time to studying!

This was a very solid foundational course giving students an introduction into biology. The tests are very fair and taken mostly from the lecture slides, and the group exams allow you to go over the exam while it is still fresh in your brain.
I don't think the exams were an accurate representation of the material. Many of the questions were made to trick you. The actual material isn't difficult, but the exams were unnecessarily hard.
I think the material and instruction were okay. The lecture slides were not great, but the instructions engaged us with polling questions.

The class is mostly about your test–taking ability, and how good you are at interpreting questions, instead of actually knowing the biology. Your grade relies heavily on the 4 exams you take, and you need to remember every sentence on the slides to get an A. There is a decent amount of extra credit to help.

Lectures are efficiently planned; not a lot of outside class time needs to be spent reading the textbook. The 4 problem sets and 4 midterms make the class manageable, especially since the final is optional.

After coming from Gen Chem, I found this class to be much easier/more manageable. There is definitely a lot of content that must be learned in a short period of time, but if you stay on top of readings and go to office hours often (even if it's just once a week), then that makes a world of a difference. The instruction is also very good, but is sometimes almost impossible to fully understand the first time, so I recommend rewatching the lectures.
This course is very interesting. The exams are written to trick you so definitely read the questions thoroughly.

Overall, this course was solid. Interesting material and professors are engaging. Professor Truchan's slides and lectures are very clear and easy to follow. Professor Brace could sometimes rush through material but similarly had a good command of material. Structure is 4 midterms and a problem set before each. There is an in–class practice activity and review session before each exam which is helpful. Make sure you find a good group in the class because exam grades consist of 85% individual score and 15% group score (you take the exam again right after in a group of 4).

The course is nothing special. As an introductory course with 300 students, you get what you sign up for. The professors are fine because teaching to a 300 person class in a captivating manner is challenging. The content is pretty much just pulled from a textbook and not much work is done to relate the coursework to ideas outside of class.

This was a solid biology course and I found it interesting. The slides were very helpful and covered almost everything on the tests. You have to know the material very well to get an A, especially since the second two tests focused more on applying concepts, but it is definitely possible. Taking AP bio definitely helped but is not necessary to do well in this course.

7/15

Bruchan and Trace are great professors! I think this class is SIGNIFICANTLY better and organized than any of the chem classes so this was a nice change of pace if you're pre–med. I think this class was fine, there is just a lot of content so the material was difficult more than the class itself. I wish there were more questions on the exams (30ish questions) because if you missed 3 then you were already at an A– so it was difficult to get a good grade on the exams. The select all that apply questions were really annoying because you didn't get partial credit for them and they were usually very tricky. I didn't love this class but if you're good at bio and poorly worded questions then you'll be fine. I am just accepting my A–.
It was a standard intro to bio class. The first half was really enjoyable for me, not so much the same for the second.
I wanted to learn a lot of the foundational information because I was to take higher level biology classes

If you have to take this class, you have to take this class. It's not so bad difficulty–wise as some of the other science courses I've had to take here, but man is it dull. I had a really hard time staying focused in class. The pace is super fast and there is a ton of content per lecture. If I had any advice for this class it would be to watch the recorded lectures over again in your own time and make sure to really pay attention to every detail.

Initially, this course with Truchan was significantly better than with Brace. Truchan's slides are much more clear cut and show all the details that we are expected to know for the exam. There aren't any practice exams and the questions are tricky, so be wary of that. Overall, it was a very doable class as long as you made sure to put in the time to clearly memorize the content and learn how to apply it.

This course was overall a positive experience. My one struggle was that some of the exam content would focus on very minor details which made it hard to focus my time studying on understanding the content conceptually as I would fixate on these details. Besides that the professors explained everything very clearly in lecture and were always available and helpful in answering questions outside of class.
This course is designed for students to learn and to succeed, overall it's a very effective course that I recommend.

This class is weird. The concepts are not hard. You go into the tests very confident. Then, you get like a B on every test and get an A– in the course. I swear the people who write the questions 1) don't know english and 2) don't care about learning. Sometimes, you will get a question wrong because you noticed a nuance in a question, that the question writers didn't think about. YES – instructors and TAs will make mistakes. But they won't admit it, and will find some way to defend themselves, and its even harder for you because you don't have access to the test questions. Coming from a guy who got A's in chem with ease, the way this class is structured, taught, and tested is garbage. This is not just my opinion, but the opinion of my many peers as well.
Made me hate biology.

This class was a breath of fresh air from gen chem. The midterms are pretty fair (except for a few select all that apply questions), but definitely got harder as the quarter went on. I highly recommend rewatching lectures on panopto in preparation for them. It's easier to pay attention to Professor Truchan, because she is a much, much better lecturer and her slides are way more effective. There was no point in even going to Brace's lectures except for the participation points, since she is very hard to follow. The problem sets did little to nothing to help us prepare for the midterms. I liked the group componenet of each exam (you take the same exam in a group of 3–4 people after you take it individually, and it's worth 15% of your final midterm grade ONLY if it boosts your grade). Overall, not as bad as I though it would be
I really enjoyed this course. I liked the way it was taught and found the content super interesting. The professors were also very helpful as well.

I liked the teachers, but professor truchan was more organized than Brace. The exams can be tricky with small details. The problem sets were not super easy to solve without woirking with freidns and comparing answers.

This class was definitely manageable and you get out what you put into it. However you have to put a lot into it. There is a test every other week, and a problem set due. I often found myself rewatching each lecture so I could take notes on everything the professor said, because sometimes there are test questions on a random sentence in the lecture, which is very fast paced. Also the professors switch halfway through the quarter which means you have like 2 learning curves with the different professors styles of teaching and testing.

I took this class because I find biology interesting. I'm a Medill freshman. The class wasn't very work intensive besides the problem sets, which were difficult and they consumed the weekends. There are four tests, the select all that apply questions are very annoying. It's pretty much recalling information from the slides though.
The course is difficult with the quick pace of the class. There's 4 midterms and the optional final with no curve.

I took this as a neuro major requirement, and it was much less of a breeze from general chemistry (CHEM 171/2) than I thought it would be. By the fourth exam material, the course content does get pretty redundant. However, the class seems like it does set you up to get a bad grade—not that much weight from homework, and the no partial credit policy on the "select all" questions despite the exams being entirely multiple choice does throw you off the hook. The exams ask for very small details, and the class doesn't really prepare you to apply the lecture knowledge to real–life examples, plus the homework didn't really help me as much to prepare for the exams.

One of the aspects of the course that I think might not be fair to students is the grading policy. Although fair in the sense that it holds each students to the same standards, the policy of not rounding up grades for people that are so close to achieving another letter grade with 0.10% or less is extremely harsh.

8/15

I found this course very interesting especially since I had an interest in biology coming into it. The course switches instructors halfway through the quarter which is a difficult adjustment. But overall I found the lectures very helpful for learning content and using the slides to study for exams.

Prof. Brace and Prof. Truchan are great instructors for this course. They give great lecture slides and also provide helpful videos and additional links to help you learn the course content more. The course itself is challenging but the instructor office hours are great at answering any questions.

There is a lot of content covered in this class, which makes it really nice that you have exams every 4 lectures (otherwise it would require you to remember a lot more material). I personally don't think the professors are very good at conveying the content because everything seems disconnected until you go back and relearn it yourself. I read the textbook for the first half of the class but realized for the second half that it's better for you to just know what's on the slides for the exam, even though it's hard to understand what's being explained in lecture and on the slides a lot of the time. Overall not a terribly difficult class though.

The class is lecture–based but all slides and videos are posted on Canvas so you can review later. Molecular biology is a lot of memorizing facts about biological processes and more applications of biology (lab methods) than I expected. You have to stay on top of notes since there is an exam every 2 weeks but there is also a group portion and a chance to remake one as the final.

This was a pretty good course, not super hard as long as you stay on top of the material. I didn't really like that it was split up between the two professors because they have different teaching and testing styles so as soon as you get used to one style of testing and questions being asked, it changes, which is annoying. Overall, majority of what I learned was about the experimental techniques because the rest is really similar to AP and IB biology. Exams are frequent, which means you have to stay on top of the material, but it's also nice because there isn't as much content to have to learn for each exam. Also the optional final and extra credit was a really great aspect to the class that made the grading more forgiving.

I'm afraid this is one of those classes you just have to get through. The professors are unfortunately not a lot of help and often left me with more questions than answers. The TAs are good in directing/ clearing up confusion but it depends which ones you go to, start off the quarter by visiting all their OH and figuring out which works best for you.

FIND A STUDY GROUP. For this class, definitely need to read (not thoroughly) the textbook before lectures, otherwise, you'll just be lost. In lecture, don't try to copy notes down but rather write down points of confusion to ask after class or in OH. After class, since all lectures are recorded, go back through and take notes, making sure to answer your questions/ points of confusions in the notes. Studying for the exams is easier with groups, where you can explain concepts to each other and have them fact check you. Go through the terms in the slides and make flashcards for them. Take time to draw diagrams out on the flashcards as well.
It was difficult but I have to take it for my major. Some units were pretty interesting.
Not an awful required class but a lot of the times exams and problem sets are designed to trick students rather than truly test them on their knowledge.

The frequency of the exams does make the course feel more rigorous but I did like the overall structure of the course and how the final is optional. The professor switch in the middle of the quarter took a little bit of time to adjust to. The lectures are pretty fast– paced. Rewatching the lectures on my own time helped me out a lot in my understanding of the material and it was my main way of studying for the exams.

This class is genuinely irritating. Use all the resources for problem sets because you should not trust your instincts. Also, the tests are extremely finicky so do not rush through anything, these questions are meant to trick you. Also, go to TA office hours because they may not know the tests, but they know the content. There are also no practice tests, so rely on the in–class activities and problem sets to understand content. I was not a big fan of this class, but that is also likely a product of the fact that I was doing orgo 1 at the same time. It is not a difficult class but you have to put some effort in.

4 exams + an optional final are the assessments (total 400 pts). 60 pts come from hw and 40 pts come from attendance. Know the slides like the back of your hand – exams should be straight forward then. Exams 3 and 4 are harder than exams 1 and 2. talk to 2– 3 high scoring ppl to compare answers on the hw (should get 100 on hw then). 85% of your exam score comes from you taking the exam the first time. You then retake it in a group to see your mistakes – this counts for 15% of your exam grade. If you do worse on the group portion, your individual portion grade is your final grade. Again, take the group portion with smart ppl.

I would say 2/3 of the class is what you learned in high school, basically just slightly more in depth. It was annoying that there were questions on the test that were deliberately meant to trick you rather than test your knowledge. Truchan’s slides were definitely much better than Brace’s, so it was a hard adjustment half way through the quarter as you couldn’t really use Brace’s notes to study. Overall, it wasn’t a terrible course.

This course had a lot of useful resources but I hated how the test questions made the exams so hard for no reason. Like you could know the content front and back but get tricked by one of the questions and then get a bad grade on the exam. The exams make up most of the grade so that was frustrating

I think that Professor Truchan is definitely better at lecturing than Professor Brace. Truchan's slides are always very organized and almost every question on the exams come directly from the slides. For Brace, I feel like she jumps around in her lectures a lot and it's hard to really get the main ideas of her lectures. I think best way to study for exams is definitely reviewing the slides and studying with friends. The exams are pretty fair in my opinion, I don't think they every tested on anything that was never covered.

9/15

This class is honestly a scam. I loved bio in highschool and was excited to learn in the course, but it makes you feel very stupid. The content is not hard AT ALL, but the way the questions on the exam are written set you up to fail. It is honestly not fair, I spent a lot of time studying and doing all the extra practice only to receive a poor grade that doesn't reflect the time I invested. At the end of the day, it is a weed out class.

This class was very straightforward and simple to pass if you put in the effort. Professor Truchan was precise and to–the–point during her lectures, and as long as you listened to small details she would say in class, you would do fine on the exams. On the other hand, Professor Brace didn't have as good of lectures compared to Truchan, and I had to rely more on the textbook during the units she covered because her lectures got kinda confusing as the class got more complex. I would definitely recommend rewatching lectures to study for the exams.

If you have to take this class then you have to take it. It isn't especially brutal. but it is a bit frustrating when it comes to the exams. It is a lot of information from AP Bio but it is just communicated worse. The exams are worded in insanely twisted ways that make it very hard to actually succeed based on what you know. Even if you have a great grasp on the material, the wording of the questions makes it so you basically have no control over your grade.

The first half of the class with Professor Truchan was easier to follow and the slides felt more cohesive. I struggled a lot more in the second half but overall the class isn't too bad, you just have to learn what's on the slides for the most part.

The course itself isn't super difficult. Prof. Truchan teaches for the first four weeks, and Prof. Brace teaches for the last four. Prepare yourself for an increase in difficulty when Prof. Brace begins teaching because the material becomes harder, and Prof. Truchan's teaching style is a little clearer than Prof. Brace's. There is NO curve in the class, so study as much as you can for exams. The final exam does provide a useful back–up option, so take advantage of that if need be.

This is a phenomenal 'introductory' Biology course (even though it's not quite introductory). The material on the exams is entirely from lectures, so studying those extensively is key to earning a good grade. Understanding the material isn't difficult either, because the lectures are relatively easy to follow and include several diagrams. The professors are phenomenal, but at times, they can be a little quick. Office hours are helpful, and there are 4 exams. Pace yourself, study the lectures, read your notes after every lecture before bed, and this class is straightforward. Great extension of AP Biology and feeder into Cell Biology.

This course is challenging but definitely doable. I enjoyed Professor Truchan a lot more than Brace. Brace's class felt a lot more rushed but I think that has to do with the more difficult material that is covered. In order to be successful in this course you have to stay on top of the material, I dropped the ball on reading the book during Brace and I think it was more necessary during Brace because of the complexity of material.

This was maybe the greatest class I have taken at Northwestern. The previous CTECs paint a terrible and false picture. The content is so relevant and super interesting. The professors work so hard and are wonderful at explaining things. Don't be discouraged by "Professor Brace doesn't explain things well", because the material is simply just harder it's not the fault of the professor. You will have to work harder during the second half since the first half of the class is basically laying the groundwork. Don't be scared about this class...so easy to do well if you just work hard!
We had to do a lot of content

I thought this class was great! The readings are mainly supplementary but certainly helpful to go over before or after class. Lecture had well–organized slides and relatively straightforward explanations of the concepts we went over. I'd say the most confusing aspect of the course were the problem sets as they were more so adjacent to the material in the current unit, but it does help develop critical thinking skills. In general, prepare for the course to become more difficult as you progress throughout the quarter.
The lectures were incredibly boring. Lectures were recorded, but attendance was taken and a part of your grade. Your grade relies solely on tests.
This class wasn't hard material–wise, but the grading system made it tough to get an A, but easy to get a B or B+.
The teachers will go over content very quickly, so it would be better to prepare before class and be ready to spend time outside of class to go over topics again.

This class was somewhat challenging but very doable for me personally. I imagine it would be quite difficult if you have no background in biology due to the pace of the course (an exam every other week, 4 total exams during the quarter and an optional final to replace an exam grade), but if you took AP/advanced biology in high school, it may be mostly review for you. The most challenging part of this class is the frequent exams and problem sets, as I felt that the questions were sometimes worded in a confusing/ambiguous way. No partial credit is offered on exams (all multiple choice), which is a little frustrating because there are "select all that apply" multiple choice questions. I found reading the textbook and answering learning objective questions after lectures to be VERY helpful. Making use of Piazza is also crucial if you are still confused. I found Truchan's teaching style to be more organized than Brace's, but both are quite nice and overall good professors.

It seems incredibly challenging to do well in this class. The tests are all multiple choice and pretty short so getting one question wrong really tanks you. They try to trip you up with wording too. Would not recommend taking this class unless you have to for a major. Super interesting content, just not really taught in a way that connected for me. I essentially had to reteach myself everything for the tests.

This class is basically just AP bio content with exam questions literally written to try to trip you up. The content in this course is simple, or at least there is not too much content in the course, but since they want the class to be a weed out, they had to make

10/15 difficulty somewhere and so they did that by making trick questions on exams, and weekly problem sets that cover content not learned in class and irrelevant to exams. In order to do the problem sets going to office hours is basically essential since otherwise you will have no clue what is going on, and will lose points intended to be completion points. Painful experience.

4 midterm exams taken and its pretty route memorization based exams, the final replaces an exam that you want but includes some culumative questions as well. I felt like overall Professor Brace was a disorganized instructor and had a lot of trouble verbalizing clearly some of the ideas. Truchan was fine. I enjoyed biology overall.
It was very informational and interesting!

This class definitely isn't the greatest. A lot of premed students + bio majors will have to take this, but just try to grin and bear it. The instruction feels juvenile and I felt like I was being talked to like a child for much of the class, and the exams are less about the content and more about whether your reading comprehension skills are up to par. The questions are designed to trick you and the instructors want you to get them wrong, so read carefully and honestly, just learn the content as best you can.

This class is unpleasant. Unless you have to, i wouldn't take it. The class is designed for middle schoolers with attendance checkin and participation questions throughout the classes. The exam questions are designed to trick you instead of seeing if you learned anything. If you have to take the class prepare to memorize a bunch of crap

Gotta say this was way better than chem. The professors knew what they were teaching and had clearly taught before. The exams can be a bit annoying with some questions being designed to trick you. Honestly, if you just memorize everything on the slides you'll do well.

The class is well taught overall, and is only really time consuming in the day before an exam. Exams usually cover what was taught but can occasionally include really minor details mentioned once. Problem sets and practice activities are really good in preparing you for exams

This class is very straightforward. 4 exams that you take every other week and an optional final at the end to replace an exam score. Read the slides to understand then front and back, and the questions on exams are made to trick you.
Office hours are really helpful. the friday assignment before the test was also extremely helpful. Wish we had practice exams though
This course is pretty challenging. Some tests are pure memorization and others force you to think very broadly about the information taught in class.

Typical Northwestern pre–med weed out class. The concepts themselves in general were not challenging, I just feel there weren't any opportunities to practice test–like questions outside of the activities and problem sets.

This course helped me learn a lot of information in a short amount of time because of the fast–paced nature of the course. The lectures were designed to teach a lot of material, so it sometimes felt fast. The 4 midterms also left little turn–around time between sets of content, so it was important not to fall behind.

The class was very big (in tech AUD) and switches professors half way through so there is a big change in information and how it is taught. The second half is definitely harder. The class was very similar to AP Biology and didn't go in–depth. Exams were pretty fair, just know the slides and you will be fine.
professors don't care if you learn. sucks but it is not your fault

4 midterms that are every other week is a lot and you can't cram memorize. It is a lot of work but if you actually take good notes during lectures and get a good understanding, an A is def achievable. Get a good group for the group exams and go to office hours for the problem sets. Just overall a lot of work and time for this class. The exams also purposely try to trick you and some of the content we hadn't learned yet but can't do anything about that.

This course is like a godsend compared to general chemistry. Things just make sense and the course is built in a way that is fair. The teaching is nothing to write home about, but that doesn't matter you are given the tools to do well in this course and the exams are good tests of how well you prepare. A very good class that is tough but fair. Highly recommend
It was more fast paced than I expected although I did gain a very good understanding of the material and context of the class.
NA

I’m not a fan of switching the professors halfway though the course when we’re just getting used to the first professor’s teaching style and exam questions. It’s not very helpful to the students. I’m also not a big fan of the point system. I think my grade doesn’t really reflect what I know

This class is very information–packed, but the 4 midterms actually help digest the information every 2 weeks. If you study at a reasonable pace for the days leading up to the exams, this class isn't that bad. The transition between professors is a little awkward but not too bad.

I think this class was okay. It was 10x better than general chemistry but it was still hard. Having exams every week meant constant studying for this class. Which could be a good things because it keeps you on your toes but it was also stressful for me. Both of the professors were really nice and it was clear they cared about their students. But I feel like their slides could have been better. They could of included a lot more information on them instead of just including diagrams and explaining them out loud. Also, the exams were just really tricky. But I think you get the hang of it as you take more of them. At first I was thrown of guard on how specific they were to the slides. But once I started spending hours memorizing each slide from each lecture I started to do better. I also liked the

11/15 group portion of the exam. I liked discussing questions with my peers and it also improved your individual test grade (not by too much though).
I am not sure if the class was particularly effective. It was just so fast, so I needed to learn everything on my own.

The exams feel more like a reading comprehension test than a biology test, there are so many small semantics tricks that you have to catch if you want to get good scores on the tests. If you take the tests carefully, the class is somewhat straightforward. The professors are not that engaging, and it was odd that they swap halfway through the quarter.

The course is introductory biology. Each test is basically an accumulation of just 4 lectures which is not too hard to study for and is generally straightforward material. The material is very easy to understand. The problem is for some reason the tests don't test your ability to apply this material, but rather your ability to catch tricks. Most if not all test questions are designed to trick you, and this makes a really easy class harder to get an A in. Honestly I didn't learn much about biology that I didn't already know, what I did learn was that I had to re–read each exam question multiple times to make sure I wasn't being lowballed despite knowing the material. SUMMARY: Easy class, but tests trick you for no reason

This course was definitely more challenging than I thought it would be. There isn’t much homework, but you have to spend all of your time studying since there are exams every other week. The exams made it feel like you never had a break, which was frustrating but it was still manageable. The professors were very knowledgeable and good lecturers, but the slideshows were not always great. I know many people (myself included) who had to rewatch most lectures or teach themselves concepts because the lecture slides were occasionally unclear. Overall, this class is manageable but requires a lot of independent work.

This course is challenging, but very fair. I would say the material isn't the most interesting thing in the world unless you love molecular biology, but the professors are very passionate, which makes the class more fun. There are plenty of opportunities to go to office hours with TA's and professors if you have questions, which is a great resource. Overall this class is hard, but worth it, especially if you're majoring in a science/ pre–med because it provides a solid biological foundation

Molecular bio is alright. It moves super quickly and the two professor thing is kind of weird. The first two midterms were easy to do well on if you knew the material, and the slides felt more straight forward. After the professor switch the midterms suddenly had a lot of trick questions on them and the slides felt harder to understand. Overall not bad though. The extra credit points really help, and make sure you really know your stuff before each exam!
This class was surprisingly enjoyable–exams aren’t easy but there’s a ton of in class review and the profs structure the class so you can succeed.
The tests are meant to be tricky and push grades down. They really do not test whether or not you know biology rather how well you can actually read a question.

While I enjoyed this course for the content I learned, it was difficult in that the wording of exam questions were such that despite knowing the material it was easy to miss something in a time crunch.

This course has 4 midterms and an optional final that can replace your lowest midterm grade. I would say that some of the questions on the midterm can be confusing but it is manageable to do well in the class if you keep up with the studying and know everything presented on the slides. The exam questions can be tricky with the select all that apply questions and no partial credit but you should be fine if you study and do not fall behind. There are plenty of office hours and resources for this class so I definitely think it was helpful.

This course was weird, as it focused well on the content it was specifying. However, the content felt rushed, and the different teaching styles of the two professors did not make the class easier to understand.
This course was very interesting. The topics we learned about are very applicable to the real world.

I really like biology, so I was pretty excited for this course. And I would say that I had an overall good experience. The only bad thing was that it was annoying to have exams every two weeks, and the exams themselves were poorly worded sometimes.

This class is definitely harder than I thought it would be, and requires a decent amount of work to do well. The exams do not test you on how well you know the content but rather how well you can interpret their questions. Truchan was easier to understand but harder to approach, and Brace was a little scattered in lecture but honestly the sweetest person alive.
No, this course did not help me learn. The professors provide very little practice problems which makes the exams very difficult. You literally have to pillage the slides.

I really liked prof Truchan's teaching style and found her classes to be easier than prof Brace's lectures. I felt prof Brace went over material faster than Truchan which made it harder for me to learn. Overall, I learned a lot from this course and actually enjoyed it. You should definitely read the recommended textbook readings before classes since that really helped me.
Reading the book really helps to understand the information

The slides provided for class aren't the most helpful and lectures are typically just the professors reading off of the slides exactly. The textbook is truly your best friend for this class as it helps you understand the topics the most. The exam questions are usually pretty tricky and involve at least 5 or 6 select all that apply questions, which they don't do partial credit for. The exams are only multiple choice, however, which is somewhat helpful. I wouldn't take this class unless you absolutely have to for a major requirement. The content doesn't really stick once you move on from each topic.

Using the slides and the textbook in junction is extremely helpful

12/15
I enjoyed the class and learned a lot, but did not like the testing format and feel as though the testing format was not reflective of my understanding
I am terrible with multiple–choice tests so this was difficult. You need to be confident and put in the time to study. The bi–weekly exams are rough but help to break down the content.

The exams were pretty fair but the wording for some of the questions was confusing. Make sure you work in groups for the problem set and attend the Zoom session so that you can get a good grade on the homework assignments!

I feel generally disappointed with how the class was structured and how the test questions were presented. The class was extremely memorization heavy and it was occasionally difficult to apply the material if a very specific detail was forgotten. I feel like I did not learn much biology and as a pre–med, I feel stressed.

This course was pretty challenging, but manageable if you do the work. Once you start to slip behind, you're pretty much screwed because there is so much information to learn. My one critique besides the rushed pace was that there was little to no practice questions to prepare for exams.

Overall I enjoyed definitely enjoyed this class. It wasn't necessarily the most exciting or engaging class, but the content was interesting and the workload wasn't too bad. One thing I would definitely suggest in order to do well in this class is copy the lecture notes before class, and then mostly just listen during lecture. There's a lot of information on the slides and they talk pretty quick, so if you're trying to copy down the slides and also listen to the professor talk you wont absorb much of it. Having tests every other week was a little strange at first, but I actually think it made you rly learn the material. Instead of trying to study a lot of information in a short period of time for 2 or 3 bigger tests, you have multiple opportunities to study less at once. If you do well on the problem sets, keep up with lecture notes, and start studying a at least a few days before each test you should do well in the class

This course was pretty good overall. Prof. Truchan had really nice slides and explained every very well. Prof. Brace's slides were much more disorganized and her half of the class was definitely harder but still doable. Reading the textbook isn't really necessary and the PGSG was really helpful.

This class was ok. The first half had good slides and the material was organized and easy to follow. The second half had less words on the slides and was a little harder to follow, but maybe that is due to the material being harder. It was tough but I cannot complain that it was not fair.

i think the avg for this class was around a B. if you are not earning around 85s every exam, you probably won't get an A in the class. there is a 2% extra credit opportunity. the switch from truchan to brace was a little rough– truchan has all the info. you need to learn laid out on slides but brace's slides were sparse/ you needed to listen more. quizlet and re–watching lectures helped me most for the class
I loved learning about Genes and what makes our body tick. It's fascinating
This class is one you need to set a good amount of time aside and early in order to succeed in. it.

The course had great material. I learned a lot about molecular biology. The teachers were overall pretty good with some bumps here and there. The transition from Professor Truchan to Brace was a little weird because Brace was slightly less engaging at first and rushed through the slide deck. After her first couple of lectures, she also became engaging. The TopHat platform was an excellent way to check our knowledge intermittently. The grading format was very predictable and transparent. Great course.
This course teaches a lot of useful information. Make sure to learn what learning strategies work best for you and utilize them every day with the class slides.
This class was difficult purely due to the test format. The MC was very unrewarding and the smallest mistakes can move you down letter grades. You have to master the content to earn an A.

I liked the collaborative opportunities that helped us raise our grades. Aside from exam grades, everything else felt essentially like a completion grade. This course is a lot more lenient/straightforward in grading than chemistry. There are four midterms and an optional final that is concentrated on your lowest midterm grade and is aimed to help you replace that low grade. You take the exam individually and this score will be 85% of your grade. Then, you take the test again in your chosen group of students and this score is 15% of the grade. It's a good way for you to boost your overall grade. I really liked the Piazza, which is basically a way to ask questions online without the need to go to office hours. I really liked the first half of the course when Professor Truchan was teaching, but it begins to speed up when the Professors switch after the second midterm.

Bio 201 was decent, it is structured as 4 midterms plus an optional final replacing a score on a midterm. Exams are difficult, so be prepared to study a lot. There is a large emphasis on small details in the class that show up on the exams.

13/15

source_file: Student Report for Sara Broaders (PSYCH_110-0_21  Introduction to Psychology)_18e74eed-4f9c-4ad0-b993-0502a9bc9b14en-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: I think that, as someone only moderately interested in the subject, the course did a good job in maintaining my interest. The exams, however, felt heavily weighted towards memorization of terms rather than deep understanding. To some extent that is probably inevitable, but in this course it seemed harmful.

I took AP Psychology in high school so this class was a lot easier for me than a lot of people. However, I would still say to not underestimate the class since it covers so much material in such a short period of time. I thought the class would be really light so for the first midterm I got a 81 and had to lock in for the next 2 exams so I didn't have to take the final. The exam questions are basically 90% of the practice quizzes so just do them 15–20 times until you have them completely memorized. I would say to not take PSYCH 110 with this professor just because the exams are longer than the other professors but you are able to drop an exam and just take the final instead so there's plus sides to it. The lab requirements were kind of annoying to fulfill but it really wasn't that hard. Make sure you go to all the lectures because there's no recordings and there's an attendance form which gives 0.5 of extra credit for exams.

The class was pretty interesting and the class was engaging. Professor Broaders is very passionate and a great teacher. Exams are not bad, but it is a lot of material to cover. Studying is essential to do well in this class, which involves looking at the textbook. So, it is straightforward, but time–consuming. Overall, I enjoyed this class and learned a lot. I would recommend taking this class.

This class is a great introduction to psychology. The content is very digestible, the exams are easy, fair, and based mostly on lectures, and the assignments all focus on material you will be tested on and are designed to help you learn and/or memorize information. On that note, this class is mostly memorization, as in, the content does not go very deep, but it is still interesting for sure. You are shown many in–class demonstrations, examples, and applications about concepts you learn about. You must also take part in several psychology studies yourself, but this is not difficult or stressful. Many are online, and the ones I had in person tested things like depth perception and analogical thinking. One thing is that Professor Broaders sometimes goes through the slides pretty fast, so you should try to be quick at note taking, but even then the slides are posted online. Also make sure to go to class because you get some extra credit opportunities if you do.

I think this course is a great experience and a great class to take. Note that the tests are multiple choice, but are sometimes a little tricky and do ask you to apply not just regurgitate information. There is also A LOT of information that is jam–packed into every module and it's fair–game to be tested on any aspect of this information so it definitely requires you to be prepared. However, with that in mind, a lot of questions on the test you can kind of just answer based on common sense reasoning.
This course was easy to grasp and fun to learn. I recommend highly.

I didn't know what to expect going into this class, but it has definitely turned out to be one of the best classes that I have taken. Broaders is amazing and knows the material super well. She has lots of fun demos and stories that connect to the material and make class fun. Definitely recommend taking the class with Broaders.

If you took AP Psych, a lot of this will be review but there is new content too. It is also described interestingly so you won't be bored if you are interested in psychology. Know that you will have to do a lot of learning on your own to cover all topics of the course (including those that will be on midterms)– didn't love that. Had Dr. Broaders– apparently other psych professors gave exact chapters to read in the book for the tests, we did not get that (would've been helpful). But Dr. Broaders is a very engaging and kind professor with lots of examples, including personal ones, which helped us feel connected in the large class. Fun demonstrations and fun class overall, just was difficult to succeed on the midterms and final (I didn't put nearly enough time in, know that if you want anything in the A range you will need to start studying days before and the tests are quite frequent, every 2 weeks or so).

Broaders does a great job of teaching this class. The lectures were engaging, informative, and well–structured. These lectures, along with the practice quizzes, prepare you well for the exams. Overall, great course.
I loved this class and talked with everyone I knew about it way too much
It covers some of the topics that could entice our interest in specific areas of psychological knowledge.

This class was mainly just something I wanted to take because I thought it'd be relatively easy, but I think that it really does teach you a good amount, especially if you like psychology more than I do.

Take this course. Broaders is a great professor, and the tests are 80% practice questions that are on canvas. It is an easy A if you do all of the readings and watch all of the videos before each class, and there are so many extra credit points even if you mess up on the tests. Highly recommend
The homework took some time but it helped to prepare for the exams

It wasn't an easy A, but it is definitely still worth taking. Broaders is a great lecturer and was truly interested in what she was teaching, which made it even more interesting to me. This is a class where you need to do the readings and watch the supplementary videos, because it is also tested on the exams. The practice quizzes are golden and pull questions from the same question bank as the actual exams, so if you do a bunch of the practice quizzes, you're likely to see most of those questions on the exam too. I think people go into this class thinking it won't be a lot of work, but if you treat it as a class that will require some time and attention from the beginning, I think a good grade is very achievable. Also she offers extra credit to everyone who attends lectures almost every time!

I think the class was decent. The lecturing seemed unnecessary, though. Professor Broaders says the tests are mostly skewed towards the lecture slides, but I never looked at them once and barely took notes. Just read the textbook and made flashcards, still got a comfortable A. Go to class for the extra credit.

Professor Broaders is a good lecturer, make sure to take good notes. Her exams were around 80 MC questions during class time and one extra credit with a brain teaser/riddle. I enjoyed taking the class because some of the topics were very relevant to college/general life. I find myself thinking about how psych terms apply to my everyday life. You would be surprised by how many phenomena actually has psych definitions.

This class is great! Professor Broaders is very engaging and helpful with in–class examples. Would definitely go to class because you get 0.5 extra credit points that go toward your exam grades which are 84% of your grade so very important to get those. Made me want to major in psych!

This course was very informative. The textbook–based learning makes it a little tedious, but you can definitely pass and do well in the class without the textbook. Professor Broaders is a good lecturer.
Very easy class. Just do the homework and practice quizzes and you'll be perfectly fine on the exams.
Basic psych class. Broaders made it unecessarily complicated and difficult to complete.

This class is primarily memorization, and fortunately most of the terms are rather intuitive. Not difficult by any measure, and not too much of an impact as far as hours spent per week. Professor Broaders is fun and energetic.
It was entertaining and it was immersive. I appreciated how this class had in–class demos of psychological phenomena as well, which was cool to see.
Great course

This was a great course! I spent a lot of time doing the readings and studying, but I also know some people didn't do the readings. The tests are mostly swayed toward the lecture, but there are a couple questions from the readings. There are a lot of repeat questions from the practice tests, so do those!
This course provided me with a strong foundation in psychology, and it’s really piqued my interest in psychology as a whole, leading to me wanting to minor in psychology now.
Lovely intro! Learned a ton, the course was so much fun, and prof. Broaders is excellent! 10/10 would take again.

I loved this course. It is so interesting and interactive, because all the topics you learn about can be applied to everyday life. This course helped me be more observant and I was able to use what I learned to talk about things I didn't know before. I especially recommend taking this course with Professor Broaders as well. She is great at making every class fun and interesting, both by giving great examples and by making the class interactive with fun demonstrations. I think this is a great class to take for anyone – in terms of workload there is a fair amount of reading for the chapters (but it is very manageable) and doing the practice quizzes/reading the chapters were a great way to prepare for the exams.

Make sure you do all the homework in the days leading up to the midterms – they prepare you really well. And take the quizzes multiple times as some of those questions are directly recycled for the midterms even though they can be a pain to do over and over again.

Great class! Incredibly interesting lectures with lots of demonstrations. Not a difficult class, the expectations are very clear and many of the exam questions are pulled from the quizzes so those are a great study resource. I found flashcards to be super useful for the memorization portions. I definitely recommend this class to everyone, understanding psychology really lets you see the world in a different light.
Liked lecture, prof was good at teaching. Disliked homework and text, practice quizzes were cool

This is a great class. I think Broaders gets a bad rep around NU from students, however she is the most engaging professor I have ever had for a humanities or non–stem class. I really enjoyed class and liked how she emphasized a no phone policy, even in a 300 person lecture room to encourage us to really focus in lecture. She also gives out so so so many practice problems and ways to apply the content of the class. I thought the inquisitive were good, but the practice quizzes were the most beneficial tool. The quizzes had questions that would appear on the exams, where about 50% of the stuff we were asked were the same questions we had already seen in the quizzes, which could be taken an unlimited amount of times. If you're reading this, wait to take psych 110 with her, she is truly amazing and extremely knowledgable.

I LOVED this class. Professor Broaders is extremely approachable and is a great educator. The lectures could be fast–paced, but her slides and teaching style were very engaging. I especially liked how various case studies and experiments were taught along with specific concepts, as it made the content learned in class a lot easier to remember. It's also very easy to get an A in this class, as exams are only multiple choice questions and extra credit is offered every class. If you want to put in a bunch of extra time into this class by reading the textbook or attending office hours, you can. But, if you just want to get a good grade with minimal effort, you can do this too.
interesting course for sure, but was very surface level for each topic. doesn't take many hours and exams are rel. easy

This is NOT AN EASY A. I rolled into this class thinking it was gonna be a no brainer GPA booster and I was wrong. This is a TIME COMMITMENT. An A is not unachievable, but I simply did not have the time to get there. Studying takes forever because you have to memorize three chapters of the textbook in extreme depth and then spit it back out on the exam. It's whatever, you're going to have to take this class anyway if you're doing anything with psych, but I've heard other professors are better so honestly choose someone else.

It is very intense, not gonna lie. Better stay on top of everything because it can get very difficult if you fall behind. Having said that, I really enjoyed it and feel like its one of the classes where I've learned the most at NU.

For reference, Psych 110 was a course I chose for myself as a fun elective, as I would like to eventually take the 200 and 300–level Psych courses in the future. Now that I've properly finished this course, all I feel is disappointment and exhaustion. It's such a shame that I enjoy it as much as I thought I would.

Some highlights of this class:

–Prof. Broaders is an entertaining lecturer. She knows how to capture your attention and keep you engaged for 90 minutes, often tying in real–world experiences to the concepts in the curriculum. I was always impressed with the different mediums of media she incorporated in class, such as talk–show clips, documentary clips, TED Talks, podcasts, and pictures. –She and the TAs were on top of posting resources immediately on to Canvas and grading exams in a 24 hour window. –By coming to class, you would automatically be given extra credit. –Some questions on the practice quizzes can be found on the actual exam.

Some downsides of this class:

–The structure. For an introductory psych course, I don't think it needed to be so demanding. I understand that psychology is inherently a memory–based subject and that you are expected to learn many terms in a short period, but each midterm required us to memorize maybe 100+ terms from 3 drastically different fields of psychology. To make matters worse, a majority of the terms you needed to know and study for on the exam were not explicitly taught in class; they could only be found in the 20–30 page (each) textbook chapters online. It's unfair to expect that individuals with more demanding majors can put in an extra 10 hours a week to learn content beyond the content taught in class. I feel like the textbook should have only ever been a supplementary resource, not a substitution for learning. I would much rather have met for Psych 3x a week for 50 minutes a day, than learn the bulk of the content alone through the book.

–The ambiguous wording of exam questions. Before experiencing Psych 110 for myself, I was warned by friends that on the exams, sometimes it felt like any answer choice could have been the correct answer. I truly understood what they meant the moment I took the first midterm. You may be wondering... maybe this author just doesn't have any critical–thinking skills or understand how to take an exam.... and for that I say, in my defense, the TAs in office hours admitted to not really understanding what Prof. Broaders was going for when she wrote the question. I think the main problem is that Prof. Broaders needs to be more explicitly clear on the objective of her questions because she tries so hard to trick you that she blurs the lines between the answer choices. This is the one instance where studying so hard could backfire because you would be torn between the multitude of possibilities. It's never a good sign when you struggle more on the exam than the actual content taught in the course.

–Optional is never optional. Similar to her requirement of you reading the textbook, she provides optional links in the course schedule that you can check out while studying. However, these optional links aren't optional, as they appear as questions on the exams. Even if I caved in to completing the optional material, they were so time–consuming that I couldn't possibly review them. I'm sorry, but I cannot watch hours worth of TED Talks when I have to complete a 3–hour–long InQuizzitive assignment and read the textbook for 3–4 more hours.

If you don't have to take this class, don't take it. If you do or are interested in other psych classes that have this course as a prerequisite, then make sure you're mentally ready to tolerate this course for the long game. Good luck!
Very challenging course, but I learned a lot
Exams are easy in that I can use just the Canvas quizzes, Intuitive, and slides to get A on exams. Mass participation credits should go from 14 –> 10.
There is a lot of content to cover; you kind of have to know all of it because you never know what's going to be on the exams

This course is definitely very manageable. I would recommend taking lots of notes from the textbook since not all of the topics are always covered entirely in lecture. The textbook chapters are long and time consuming, but it isn't anything difficult. Doing the practice quizzes on Canvas are also very helpful in preparing for the exams since the exams usually contain a lot of those same questions. The lectures were very engaging, as they contained many interactive activities and polls, but didn't always cover all of the needed material.
Very interesting class.

This course was a very fun introduction to psychology if you don't have a background in psych. You cover a broad range of topics and the workload is not difficult. In class demonstrations and surveys keep the lectures from getting repetitive and boring.

This class is really interesting. As long as you read the textbook and attend class, it isn't hard to get an A. With Professor Broaders, there is sometimes extra credit available during class, which can help improve your exam grades, so I would recommend attending class every day. Also things that are mentioned in the textbook that we don't get to in class are still on the exams.
It's a ton of memorization, but if you study hard you'll do well
Attend lectures for extra credit and stay on top of the work assigned.
I think this class was straight forward and very fun!

I loved this course as a psych major! Fair warning though, there are supplemental lectures, textbook readings, and Ted Talks that are time consuming to complete on top of the homework already. Exams were also very lengthy which made them fatiguing to finish. Otherwise, this class was wonderful.

I wish the class involved more critical thinking and free response on the midterms. The all multiple choice format isn't my strongest method of testing and it made it very difficult for me to do well on exams.

The course wasn't too bad but there is a lot of work and lot of material. The midterms cover a wide range of material. There are four exams and you can drop one of them so you don't have to technically take the final. The inquisitives are kind of annoying and I didn't learn much from them. The practice quizzes are def more helpful.

I really enjoyed this class. I always crammed the night before the tests so it got a little stressful at times, but overall the material wasn't that hard if you did enough practice quizzes and I was able to get an A in the class anyways. Professor Broaders was great, and made all the lectures super interesting and interactive.

Its kinda what you would expect from an intro course. In terms of the workload, its kind of light. There are four exams, the lowest one gets dropped, and before each exam there is some reading with mandatory practice quizzes and some online assignment stuff. Its guaranteed you can get 100 on the practice stuff as long as you try a little, and that can really help you prepare for the exams. You also have to be a part of some psych lab studies as a test subject. While it definitely isn't hard, it is a little annoying to schedule the stuff and it can be a pain.
I really liked this course it wasn't super easy but I found it very interesting
It was interesting and I enjoyed it – not too stressful!
I think the exams were unfair. Psych doesn't really seem like a subject where 80 multiple choice is good for a midterm, but otherwise everything was great
Happy

This was a great intro class. Definitely people say it's a lot of work, and I'd agree there's a lot to learn and memorize, but if you find the right studying strategies, you can definitely succeed in this class. It's nice to have 3 exams and one final which is optional, so you can be done pretty early. The content is SO interesting though, and I think I learned so much more about myself as an individual and the way people are, think, and behave. It's also so nice to be able to extend psych knowledge beyond just psych itself and into other fields whether you are an engineer, journalist, musician, or truly, anything. I would definitely recommend if you are even a bit interested in this class to take it! I had Professor Broaders and she was great. I love her enthusiasm and the way she explains the material.
This course had a bunch of random inquisitive assignments that didn't really help you study and took a ridiculous amount of time. Not a fan of the heinous amount of random videos we have to watch too

This course was not too difficult (coming from someone that took AP Psychology), but there were a few topics that needed more attention on. The exams were fair and most of the material was presented in an engaging way. Additionally, the course was very informative and got me interested in several psychology topics.

The lectures were pretty well organized and engaging. Broaders cared a lot about students and ensuring everyone understands. She also gave extra credit if you go to class. Readings are long but necessary to understand. Everything outside of exams was graded on completion. Exams are all multiple choice and not bad.

This is a great class, make sure to take it if you're interested at all in psych or want a strong basis for the MCAT! Professor Broaders is an excellent lecturer––she is very engaging, supplements our learning through demonstrations, examples, extra credit questions, and more. I really enjoyed her style and explanation of the material. The class is also super straightforward, as Prof. Broaders is very clear about what exactly she wants you to know for the exams. Not a crazy amount of homework––only the InQuizitive assignments for each chapter as well as a practice quiz for each module. Listen to her when she tells you to do them early and often––the questions on the practice quizzes were super helpful in terms of preparing for the style and content of the exams. Also please just go to class––she gives an extra credit opportunity just about every lecture, and while they're not worth all that much individually, attending lecture consistently can go a long way to improving your exam grade, especially if you end up borderline between grades or something like that. I also prefer her quarter to winter (even though Prof. Engeln is excellent as well), as the opportunity to take the final and drop one of the midterms if you didn't do so well went a long way into easing some of the stress going into exams. Overall, super great class! I can't say I studied all that much outside of reviewing notes and redoing the practice quizzes, so also totally not that big of a time crush either.
Broaders is a great teacher and very amicable. This course takes you through all the basics of psych and is quite fast paced, but its overall pretty easy to get a good grade in.
Bootcamp course. Take it in the fall with Gardener for a MUCH easier time.

Professor Broaders was nice and open to questions from students. Attendance is required at lectures because she does not record them. She posts various readings, videos, and supplementary lectures to watch beforehand, which I recommend you do as some of this information will be tested on exams. I personally found exams to be fair; there was 80 multiple–choice questions that we had to complete in around 1 hour and 30 minutes. As long as you pay attention in class and take notes, you should be able to do well.
This course made me love psych even more. Easy as an introductory class but stimulating material and good demos in class.

Not a bad course. Quite heavy for an intro course and wouldn't recommend for a bistro but pretty interesting. The key for doing well on the exams is doing the Canvas quizzes. Sometimes the questions on the exam are exactly like the ones on the Canvas practice quizzes. Also the course material is very applicable to being a student in college which I found gave me more motivation to actually learn the material

This class was a good intro to psych class and introduced a general overview of psychology well. The tests weren't too bad and the inquizzitives/canvas quizzes are manageable, but make sure to read the textbook, as this will make them much easier.
The lectures were most important to me. Do not skip them they are essential to doing well on the exams!
Taking good notes during lecture and doing practice questions is really all you need to do well in this class.

This course was great. Even though the exams cover a lot of information, it is manageable so long as you make an effort to do the practice quizzes multiple times and carve out several days in advance of the midterms to study. The professor is fun and engaging and is generous with extra credit. She is personable and integrates eye–opening real–life examples of psychological concepts.

This course is an easy A and super informational. I didn't take AP psych in high school and had no idea what to expect from this course (took it for pre–med requirements), but I was thoroughly pleased. Broaders doesn't go in as much depth as the textbook does, but her practice quizzes were extremelyyy helpful in preparation for her exams, which are pretty long but fair and only multiple choice. Definitely made me interested in taking another psych course.
this is a great class! Make sure to not cram all the materials last min tho!
This class requires a lot of memorization of a lot of definitions, if you are fine with that, then this class will be easy and somewhat enjoyable

Pretty easy course. Exams are all multiple choice. Prof is a good lecturer. ~70% of questions on the exams are from the practice exams. 3 midterms and a final, top 3 exam scores count. Would recommend as an easy theme.
I enjoyed learning a lot about the mind.

As someone who did well in the course and missed many classes, I would definitely say just read the texbook and focus on the practice quiz questions. A lot of the practice quiz questions appear on the exam. The prof only covers like half of what will be on the exams during the lectures, so its important to know all the terms in bold in the textbook.

comments: Prof. Zu is an amazingly kind professor who is truly invested in his students' learning. This will be a difficult class as it is very fast– paced, but the professor helps as much as he can along the way to make it as smooth as possible. I was very surprised with how much I was able to learn and execute it in only 3 weeks.

I had always wanted to take physics in order to truly understand how the Universe works, and this introduction to kinematics is everything that I could have asked for. As far as the summer term is concerned, the course is well–paced and it is one of the few courses that are I would consider to truly cover fundamental concepts.

I was nervous about the 3 week summer course initially, but it was well worth it. The class moved very fast pace but grading expectations were reasonable and rewarded conceptual understanding with heavy partial credit. Open note/open computer exams, daily homework, and discussions. Would highly recommend taking this with Prof. Zu.
