comments: EA3 just seems very unorganized compared to EA2. First of all, the fact that the tests are worth 90% of your grade is way too much and becomes even worse when they are just in–class 50 minute exams. That means there's only 2–3 questions per test, so if you misread or don't understand one question it could make a huge difference in your grade. The class just also seems very unorganized as a whole, and it is often pretty confusing to find what we're actually supposed to do for homeworks and other questions in class.

Try not to take Goshal for this course, he is confusing at times; generally he's alright, but honestly try to take someone else, it's not worth the headache of walking out of class every day confused. The tests are either extremely easy or extremely hard, so just hope and pray that you get the easy one. It's a requirement so not much else to say, you just gotta get through it.

I would not recommend taking this class with Ghosal. Although the guy can crack an occasional joke or two, it was INCREDIBLY difficult to actually learn or understand anything going on in lecture. I would often zone out because Ghosal would kinda just ramble as he's explaining concepts, and there would never be enough time for students to simply digest what was going on or ask any questions. The tests Ghosal had were also incredibly different from what he covered in class, so I highly recommend doing lots of practice tests.
This course was a lot of fun, and was one of the major reasons why I switched to MechE. However, the professor was a really harsh exam–maker and grader. I hated that.

This class very much required that you rely on the textbook because in class itself the professor did not explain concepts well and TAs discussions were not easy to follow. Additionally, the grading of exams is harsh and no partial credit is given even if questions are worth 40 points.

This class was challenging. Ghosal is a hard professor, and the class is made harder because it relies so heavily on the textbook. He teaches sparingly in class so you have to put a lot of effort to understand the material outside of class.
the course overall was good in what was covered, but the structure was weird and the textbook was meh.

This course did not help me learn because my professor wouldn’t effectively teach the content in the way he descibed and the syllabus described leading to a severe lack of example problems and practice problems throughout the quarter.

EA3 is a fine class–– it's not terribly hard, if you put in enough effort to complete the homework and study a little extra before exams you'll be fine. Ghosal is a good teacher–– he answers questions well in class, goes slow enough to understand, and is funny too.

4/8

In comparison to EA1 and EA2, I felt that EA3 was less work but more stressful due to the fact that 90 percent of your grade relies on your top two out of three quiz scores. There's no final or projects only weekly hw assignments, and the webtext isn't terrible in my opinion. It's just important that you don't fall behind because the topics move at a very fast pace.

Ghosal was an unfair and unkind professor who made it clear he did not care about students' well–being. Exams did not test understanding and lectures were often tangential and did not align with course objectives. I would suggest taking EA3 with a different professor. He took a simple, enjoyable subject and made it confusing, painful, and stressful.

Meh. It's EA, enough said. The grading system in this class is very weird with 3 tests, 1 gets dropped, and the 2 you keep are 90% of your grade. I liked professor Ghosal although sometimes his lectures could be a little bland (by the halfway point of the class less than 1/3 of people were showing up to lecture). He was funny and I enjoyed his jokes though.
The course is hard. Professor Ghosal is funny and his classes are entertaining, but his tests are incredibly hard
quiz can be either very easy or hard

Don't take it with Ghosal, literally choose anyone else. Just trust me. Compared to the other professors your exams will be twice as hard and he doesnt curve. No one shows up to class because he cannot teach for the life of him.
Ghosal makes hard quizzes. Don’t go to levtures uf you’re not going to pay attention. Go to another section or watch the recordings.
I hated it even more than I thought I would.

It was poorly organized, with the online textbook not matching the language or content presented in class. Neither of these sources fully reflected the content on exams. The content itself was not incredibly difficult, but Professor Ghosal was not a good lecturer. I ended up having to study and learn a lot of the content by myself, which is the only reason I scored slightly above average on the exams. The other professors were supposedly much better lecturers. Ghosal also had much harder exams than the other professors, with his averages sometimes coming out at 20 percent lower than the other sections.

The partial credit was unfair and getting a question right except for one tiny mistake was treated as barely doing anything right which was not representative of how much you demonstrated your knowledge of the content on quizzes. It was probably my least EA so far and the Professor Ghosal was okay.

This class was definitely harder than EA1 and EA2, but it was still doable. The readings seem to help but aren't really required. Ghosal was a decent teacher, but always had trouble making the quizzes at a correct difficulty. The first one was much too hard, and the second and third were way too easy. There seemed to be a lot of discrepancies between the different teachers and their quiz difficulties, but overall, I think I did learn a lot.

This was definitely my least favorite class of the sequence. I felt that it was far too unforgiving for its difficulty, given how much tests matter. I also think people with strong physics backgrounds have a huge advantage, and has been my most challenging class by far. I wish it was structed more fairly and there wasn't such a discrepancy between professors. My professor really did not help me learn, and his tests were awful.

I would absolutely recommend taking this course with any professor other than Ghosal. While his lectures were bad, this was not even the main problem. The exams were extremely unfair (did not test us on what we had learned and were phrased very strangely), and so much more difficult than the other sections to the point where their averages were in the 90s while ours were in the 60s. Not only that, but he told us TWO DAYS before the last exam that it would be cumulative, which of course was not the case for the other sections. I spent so incredibly much time on this course just for it to not really pay off in the end. I do not actually think the EA 3 content is that bad, and I feel like I know it fairly well, mostly because I had to spend hours and hours studying. Despite this, the scores on my exams do not reflect this whatsoever. The grading scale is also extremely harsh, so even when I walked out of an exam feeling good, it wouldn't end up that way mostly because the partial credit awarded is very low. Overall I would say spare yourself the time and energy and take this class with a professor who will adequately teach you the material without trying to make you fail.
Personal EA ranking (until now): EA2 (<3) >>> EA3 > EA1. I think we should spend less time learning concepts and more doing practices as in EA2.

The class seems to be very disjointed. There is a noticeable inconsistency between classes in terms of the information that is learned. Additionally, the tests felt either too difficult or too easy, without ever feeling balanced. Reading the textbook will help you understand the information much better than just going to lecture.

In EA 3, we had to pretty much learn all the content by ourselves, while the professor only recapped some important concepts in class and demonstrated some examples. Basically, if you did not read the textbook beforehand, you wouldn't be able to keep up with this course or succeed in the assignments or exams. I did not find the professor's lecture useful either since the materials were already self–taught before class, and the lecture sometimes confused me more rather than clarifying the concepts in the textbook. Besides, the professors were not really prepared to answer our questions about the homework problems during office hours.

Not that there is a choice to take this class but if you do do not take it with Ghosal. The flipped classroom makes for a confusing and uniquely terrible experience for this course and the exams and exam policies make everything even more frustrating.

Painful content and instructor for someone doing CS. A lot of differential equations without explanation on what they are. Abhorrent pacing and explanations. Literally any other professor would have been passably bearable.

5/8

EA3 is the most painful EA classes out of all of them. Ghosal's papers are horrible and even though he is an entertaining guy, I would not take him again as a professor. However if you go to all the lectures and pay attention to all his weird content you might be okay.
Doesn't really make sense and kinda sucks in instruction

I would definitely try to take this course with a professor who is more generous with partial credit when grading exams, because Ghosal definitely wasn't for my class. His lectures were a little boring, and while he does try to make them interesting with real– world analogies, he often went on tangents that weren't related to the course content. The webtext was helpful, but the content you learn in the course definitely varies based on what professor you have. Although I felt that Ghosal's exams weren't that difficult, the wording of the questions was often convoluted and he is way too harsh with partial credit, often taking off almost all credit if you made one small careless mistake. It's also a little annoying that two exams make up 90% of your grade, but that's a course–wide thing.

6/8

comments: I think this course is very interesting but extremely fast. It's difficult to keep up and will take up all of your time. Make sure you know how to effectively study (prqctice problems) r you're just wasting time. The curve is very helpful, but that doesn't cancel out the fact you need to try hard and do at least average for a "good" grade.
Pace is insane, manageable to do good but it's one hell of a grind. You can't miss lectures and have to practice constantly
I honestly liked this class much more over the summer; I felt like it was very fast (Northrup even says it’s like a full time job), however if you put in the time and work I think it’s manageable.

Very very fast pace! About a day and half worth material in the summer is equivalent to a full week during the regular school year. Prof Northrup seems to want his students to succeed, though at times it feels that the exams are very hard. It is not a class that you're going to ace and just something that you have to grind and get through.

The pace of this course during summer was more intense than I ever could have expected. Lectures are long, then a short break into discussion sections, then home to study for hours. Be prepared for this to be like a full–time job if you take it during the summer. I will say that Professor Northrup was great, his lectures were engaging, and he was truly supportive; you could tell he really wanted us to succeed.
The course is brutal and the pacing is extreme.
it was very fast paced, but if you stay focused you can do it.

Northrup is great. You need to stay on top of studying and get ahead in order to do well. I think his teaching style works great with this course and he is always willing to answer questions. If you have other things going on like work or other classes, be warned, you will not have time for them. This class is like a full time job
This class is very hard, you have to study for hours. If you know nothing about chem you will have a hard time.

comments: Besides having homework about stuff we didn't learn in class because we were behind, I thought the instruction was good and the teacher wanted us to learn. My sections were not as bad as the other sections exam so hopefully they can sort that out. Otherwise, I think it was one of the easier EAs of the sequence.

Professor had dry unengaging lectures, never told us what type of content would be on the exams, exams were super different from homeworks and practice exams. Also professor had bad handwriting and panopto recordings didn't really work either. Overall a bad experience. I felt I couldn't prepare for the exams fully either.

Licter is a great person and one of the smartest professors in the department. With that said, his lectures are really hard to attend. I felt the class was slow–paced, and not much was covered in class.
do lots of practice problems, all it takes is practice
Great course if taken in the Fall

Exams were okay, the class was not hard just sometimes not knowing one specific element can become a nightmare for you in the future; so definetley invest some time into making sure you are able to go through all the class content.
Prof. Lichter is an alright professor. As an EE, his depth of instruction was fine but I can see how he didn't really go in depth for the circuitry part.

At first, the class seemed to move very slowly, but I appreciated the pace since it allowed us to ask necessary questions and go more into detail about each discipline. The professor was very kind, and they were gracious graders. We covered a lot of material, but it never felt overwhelming. The homework would sometimes take a while, however, because sometimes we wouldn't get through all the material until the day before.

Course was supposed to be trailer but midterms were still very difficult and challenging. Even outside of testing, the new instructor would often teach the content too slowly, so we would be required to teach ourselves topics on the homework that we had never went over in class. To make matters worse, there was not a single day of review before the midterms, as we seemingly were always learning something new or each day in class. I had Chloe Ho as my TA and she did a wonderful job of breaking down the concepts from class, so if you have her as a TA make sure to drop by her office hours! Overall, course was more difficult than it should have been, paced quite oddly at times, and the content covered in class often was not near the difficulty of what was covered on the homework or midterms / final!

Lichter was as pretty cool guy. Content is not too bad, actually pretty interesting compared to EA2. Sometimes it can get a little confusing but the webtext and working through problems with other people is very helpful.

Lichter was a great professor, but the exams felt wildly inconsistent – some were extremely easy, some felt impossible to do in the time allotted. Do a lot of problems and prepare differential equations.
None

Prof. Lichter is a nice dude, but his pacing of this class kind of sucked. It never really felt like we were coving much content in classes, and most kids just ended up watching the recordings. He also didn't do a great job telling us what would be on the quizzes, for quiz 1 and 2 there were questions on stuff not addressed in class and sort of addressed in the readings, which we then covered in the following class.
It's like everything you thought you knew about springs except completely forget about all of it

lecture is boring, a lot of ppl stop showing up after the first couple weeks, tests were graded very generously so don't worry about them too much, if u dropped ea3 last quarter/are retaking this is light work tbh
Taking the trailer classes, a lot easier.

Course was difficult and requires thorough understanding of the concepts. The online wiki page is helpful but only if you get practice with problems in lecture/practice exams as well. A lot of the problems require similar processes to solve them, so get a lot of practice with the procedure used in the course.
Good professor, really comprehensive and teaches content to be tested in exam properly.

Since you probably have to take this class, I would say don't take it with Professor Lichter unless you have to. The TAs were really helpful, but he was incredibly vague about every concept whenever someone asked a question in class, and he seems like he barely knows the content. His lectures were incredibly disorganized, and he would even repeat the same exact content in multiple lectures. If you didn't go to class, it was hard to catch up with the lecture notes because they made no sense. He was also very stingy with giving exam practice materials, and he would give you two practice exams but half the questions wouldn't be content covered on the midterm. Also, he didn't cover all the content EA 3 was supposed to cover in the end. His slow pace was sometimes good because it meant the midterms covered less material but the other material is probably important for other courses yet we didn't finish it. The structure of this class is three midterms (each 25%). There wasn't a final, but there is a midterm during reading week instead. The midterms were hard and often didn't cover content that was fundamental to the course. Every week (mostly), there is a problem set that is sometimes only three questions and took a couple of hours but sometimes the problem set could be seven very difficult questions and took multiple days to finish.
prof. lichter is pretty monotone which makes lecture really dry, but he's good at explaining concepts.

The course is fine, it covers mechanical spring, damper, mass systems and electrical circuit systems. As a CS student, that's not really relevant to my degree, but the information is somewhat interesting. I wouldn't recommend taking it with Prof Lichter, granted this was his first time teaching the course, but what he taught and what was on the homework/tests didn't always align which was frustrating.

Class was a lot of memorization and sometimes a lot of self teaching. Many homeworks were also on things that had not been done in class so the TA office hours were a must. We also had to scramble to learn everything before a test because Prof. Lichter hadnt taught it yet.
well structured course with a helpful e–text

This class was ok. I think I was better off when I just did the readings and went to office hours instead of going to lectures especially since the lectures were recorded. The web text is good for conceptual understanding but it doesn’t have a lot of practice problems. The TAs are extremely helpful if you need help with the hw. Sometimes there were inconsistencies between how the professor would teach something and how the TAs would explain/grade things. The quizzes aren’t too bad if you study. One thing I did like was the pace of the class. It definitely wasn’t as fast paced as other McCormick classes. I found it easier to keep with this class compared to the other EAs. This was the professors first time teaching the class so it will probably be better for future sections.
This class should be easier than it is. In actuality, the content is useless, and the hw, past exams, lectures, and exams have nothing to do with each other. It is hard. The teacher was so bad.

comments: Great professor. Very energetic, nice, and funny, though sometimes you feel a little concerned that he may be overexerting himself and getting a little too worked up over the diff eqs. The HW quiz system started awful but turned out just fine by the third one, probably because it's a new thing. Labs might ruin your friendships and tests might ruin your weeks, but it is definitely possible to succeed in this course.

The labs for this course are ridiculously long. It really isn't that much raw material that you go over, but the labs are just so long.The quizzes and homework for this course are also incredibly lengthy. Despite the homework not being graded for accuracy, it still requires a significant amount of time to complete. Additionally, the homework does not provide adequate preparation for the exams. The exams themselves are very dense, making it extremely difficult to finish within the allotted time.

STUART WICK THE GOAT. Take this class with Wick if you can, he's probably the best instructor I've had at this school so far. He's funny and communicated his ideas extremely well, everything is organized and often times we were able to finish few minutes early while covering more content than Bayliss's class. A good instructor will definitely make this class more enjoyable and it's safe to say I enjoyed EA4 solely because of Wick.

The worst EA in the sequence in my opinion, exams were super difficult and are so dense that most struggle to even finish them. The labs are a headache even when you are working in a really good group, so pick the people you work with wisely. On top of the long labs, we had pretty long homeworks too. Since most people that are taking this class have to take it, just know that if will definitely be a class that takes up a ton of your time. Prof Wick is a pretty fast talker, and lectures can go by really fast so it sometimes is hard to keep up with him. He is pretty much the opposite of Bayliss, who is a lot slower.
So many population models and data analysis. We definitely don’t cover half of actual differential equations but more on applications.
The class is hard but the homework and labs are very good practice for the exams.
Not a hard class if you spend a lot of time studying, labs are annoying and long

The labs are going to take a long time but shouldn’t be too bad if work is split evenly and is checked with the TAs. The exams are on the longer side for having to be done in 50 minutes, so work on speed and accuracy. Overall the most challenging and time consuming course in the EA sequence.

Professor Wick is such a good lecturer. He teaches content in a very entertaining (borderline goofy) and understandable manner, and he ends class early a good amount of times because of how efficiently he teaches. Take this class with him.

TA office hours are also really goated. All the TAs are chill and unbelievably helpful for the four labs you gotta do.

The labs themselves are a mixed bag. They are supposed to teach you additional diff eq concepts, but you’re kinda forced to split up tasks with your group, and as a result do not get the full learning experience. Do not procrastinate the labs, because the OH line gets very very long the last 1–2 days before the lab is due, and you can get stuck waiting a literal hour to get your questions answered.
Learn a lot from lectures. Prof. Wick is a great lecturer.

Yes this class is annoying to take for any engineering major because most of us won't be using what we learned in EA4 in any future classes. The most important part is to pick a good group for the lab because they are very time consuming and difficult. Prof Wicks is a very passionate lecturer, but his teaching style is a little rambling, so I would say u learn the most from just doing hw and do well on the hw quizzes.
Loved Wick. He was so energetic and open to interacting with students. He was also great at lecturing cohesively even though he'd sometimes go on tangents.

This course sucks but you have to take it. I found the labs horrible and tedious, and not even that relevant to what you learn in class. Stewart was a far better lecturer so I recommend you try to take it with him. I also the application of the equations to spring and masses to be very annoying and confusing.
wick is a tweak but good teacher

This course does drain a lot of your time away due to the labs. The hardest part of this course is the lab because they take a bunch of your time even with a good group and the labs feel a bit detached from the material in lecture. This wouldn't be as bad if you didn't have on top of the labs to do hw and quizzes, and studying for the midterm at the same time while also having your other classes taking your time. A lot of this class just is endurance rather than actual learning.

Probably the hardest of the EAs content wise. Not too horrible depending on how good your lab group is. I recommend Prof Wick, I think he has the best lectures. Unfortunately lectures are not recorded so you have to attend

Professor Wick has a lot of energy, and sometimes things can move pretty quickly in lecture. He's a fun guy though and overall lecture was good. Labs suck, make sure you have a good group or youll have a grim quarter.

The way that work is assigned in this class doesn't really make sense to me– there are a lot of weeks with nothing due, and then there are weeks with both a lab and a homework due– the amount of work you do week–to–week varies widely, so I would be prepared to not really know how busy you will be in advance.
A class in which lots of time is spent on labs. Easy to lose points on exams. Conceptually not difficult, just know how to solve differential equations quickly.

Wick is a great professor. He goes through lectures very quickly but in a digestable manner and answers questions thoroughly. He can sometimes be a stickler in class about people talking out loud and stuff like that and can get pretty frustrated but aside from that he's easy to talk to.

This is a weed–out class.

Regardless of what others may say, especially during the first third of the quarter, this class can be extremely brutal and even unfair. It's organized in such a way that it almost requires the student to focus solely on this class, as if the student doesn't have any other class outside of this one. Rather than try to provide a bit more flexibility to the students, the professors simply have an "oh well" attitude. It can be extremely frustrating to deal with a class that seems to ignore your well–being as a student, more interested in brutally testing your knowledge rather than fostering an environment in which a student can actually grow and learn.

To put it more succinctly, this class is not the type of class where you feel as if you genuinely learned something and took away an experience you feel you can build upon, but rather one in which you are forced to cram every single bit of knowledge you have before a test, only to immediately forget it right after because the instructors are more emphasis on pressuring students with abundant work rather than actually having them internalize the content.
I loved this class! It was very intellectually stimulating and application–focused.

This course is quite difficult. I spent a lot of time doing the homework but the homework was useful for the quizzes and the exams. The labs take a bunch of time so it is important to go to office hours and have a group that is willing to put in the work. The exams were difficult in terms of pacing because there is a lot of information on them. If you just do your work and pay attention in class, it should not be that bad.

prof. wick doesn't make the exams, bayliss did for all sections. I genuinely think Bayliss needs to retire. He acts like he is a nice sweet guy (not my professor, so I can't say much), but his exams are evil because you never get enough time to finish them. He doesn't give practice exams which also sucks. Wick never knows what is happening honestly, I wonder if he looked at the exams before giving them to us. Tbh the labs are not as bad as people say. My group was amazing and I'm glad I picked wisely. We all split up the work evenly and got it done. If you have a good group, you will survive. Yes, you will spend hours at office hours because the TAs don't know how to help or the answer key is just wrong. It sucks, but you have to deal with it.
Needed. There isn't much to say here. I say don't stress too much but the labs can be a do or die.

EA4 is a very hard course that I unfortunately let myself get behind in. There are a lot of different topics that, while interesting on their own, can seem pretty disjoint as a whole. MATLAB is also very frustrating to work with.

Wick is great, he is quirky but a much more passionate lecturer than Bayless (and a lot more clear, both in speech and handwriting). His signature comment was "I know its 4pm so I will finish up quick and let you guys go 5 minutes early" even though the first class he said he would always take up the full class time. The content itself isn't hard, but the exams are such a time crunch that it is tough. Even if I were to retake the test knowing all of the questions I would still be pressed for time because it is so much algebra. This isn't something you can avoid unfortunately as the exams are the same across sections (not sure if they are the same in trailer).

PICK GOOD LAB TEAMMATES. My god it sucks if one person does no work or starts really late and finishes like day of. They are intense, there are 4 labs which you need to write code and write a report for (think the reports in EA2 but more intense). You can check with TAs but I really recommend starting early especially because some parts are dependent on other parts. Our reports ended up being at least 30+ pages, if not 40+.

Other than this, the homeworks and quizzes are chill and not bad, and instructor doesn't *really* matter. Have fun!
Dr Wick is an amazing lecturer. However, EA4 is a terrible course.
I enjoyed this course. I went into it knowing it was going to be difficult and a lot of work but I think I got out of it what i put in.

Overall, the course is pretty interesting. Professor Wick definitely made the class more interesting, as he was very engaging and explained things pretty well. The homework isn't too bad, but the labs can be pretty brutal if pushed to the last minute. Definitely try to get a good group that can all pull their weight equally, so you don't have to worry as much.

The main mathematical concepts taught in this class are not too difficult to grasp, the difficulty in the course comes from remembering terminology and procedures while also dealing with the work load of labs, quizzes and homeworks. Luckily the homework is only for completion ,which was much appreciated

Probably the most interesting and hardest course of the EA sequence. The content was a bit hard, but I enjoyed it. The labs cover important practical applications, but are a bit tedious and require a lot of time. Prof. Wick was fantastic. He explained concepts well and made sure that the students weren't bored during his lectures.
This course didnt really teach anything important to me, so I was sad

I personally found this to be the most difficult EA course. Professor Wick is a good professor, as he went through lots of examples during class and answered student questions. However, I personally think that exams were too long, as sometimes I was unable to finish them within the allotted time period. The labs, which are done in groups of 5, took over 10 hours each. Start the labs well in advance. Also, make sure to go to office hours to check your answers with the TAs and get there early because the line was oftentimes out of the classroom.

I thought the lectures, homeworks, and quizzes were actually quite good. I tend to dislike math, but found myself actually taking a lot away from this course. The labs were the problem. I do not even think the topics of the labs were bad –– it was more so that they were so unbelievably long and tedious, that nobody could actually learn what was going on, simply because it was such a chore to churn the 50 pages of lab out. If the labs had been more reasonable, I could have seen myself enjoying this class more. The exams are completely unreasonable, but hopefully they are curved.
Wick is a great professor and he teaches very well and the exams are fair

This is an okay course, though in McCormick this is a requirement for nearly all majors. It is very difficult. Professor Wick's instruction was clear and we went through ample examples during class for solving differential equations and different applications of differential equations. The textbook is pretty good and the homework and quizzes do help with preparing you for the exams, along with the practice exam questions. There are 5 homeworks, 5 quizzes, and 4 labs. The labs are very lengthy and sometimes difficult. Definitely start them early, especially if your other group members rely on your task to be completed before they can complete their's. I spent over 10 hours on each. Definitely go to office hours to get help with and check the lab questions and MATLAB coding. I recommend going to office hours early because they get quite crowded. Labs are done in groups of 5. There are 3 exams, which are pretty difficult. Overall difficult class but the instruction was still pretty good.

source_file: Student Report for Laura Panko (BIOL_SCI_101-6_03  First-Year Seminar)_a5094b11-7148-4feb-8525-064877a11e86en-US.pdf

Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.
