What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: The course was well–organized and the Professor is very knowledgeable about dinosaurs. At various times, Professor Panko brought in in visual aids, and its clear she cares deeply about the progress of her students. The material of the course itself was not particularly difficult and the class reading was somewhat interesting. Grading was fair.

Dinosaur seminar is decent for a seminar, a bit boring at times but overall chill. It was nice to have a class with my PA group, where we're all just chill with each other. Not bad of a workload at all.
The professor is very approachable and understanding, workload is not too heavy, and course material is pretty interesting.
Not important for my life goals, but not too bad for a first year seminar.

This is an overall very good freshman seminar. There are several writing assignments that seem long and pointless, but they are not super difficult. There is also going to be a point where the professor will ask you to interpret a primary source that uses insane scientific language but it is only one of 2 difficult assignments. In class it’s not very hard to follow along with any of the material as long as you skimmed the first paragraph of the reading. The final essay is 2000 – 2500 words but you pick the topic so it’s not too bad.

Professor Panko is an amazing introduction to Northwestern and I’m extremely glad I took this class as my seminar. The work is not bad at all and I learned some very interesting things about dinosaurs.
This class is a very nice intro to college, definitely recommend taking it since there is not too much work and definitely not much of a courseload

Focused on writing skills and gathering information from primary sources

4/6

source_file: Student Report for Ilya Mikhelson (GEN_ENG_205-1_21  Engineering Analysis I)_7eb332a7-2982-4033-bfb0-f9576ae2e046en-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: I don't like this course. I think matlab could have been taught much more clearer and follow a much logical sequence, like math 228–1.

Ilya is a great professor, he is great at explaining concepts. Make sure to go to his office hours every week. Prof. Honig is also great for discussion sections – he gives lots of helpful examples. This is quite a difficult class – the Matlab portion is fine, but the class really ramps up once you get to linear algebra. Some of the homework programs are very difficult. The midterms and exams are also a bit challenging. Be prepared to study and review a lot.

This course is definitely 100% doable even if you have basically no coding experience like I did. Giving this course some serious effort and especially lots and lots of practice is definitely key to feeling confident. I found that the exams only focus a little on asking you to be able to do things like perform calculations and answer questions that you can practice, and lots of what the course asks you to do are answer conceptual questions that need a good understanding of some pretty abstract ideas. Even so, I was pleased to see that there were lots of resources to help me tackle these tricky ideas: Prof. Mikhelson's lectures were fast–paced, but full of lots of examples and intuition–building segments, and the discussions were great at reviewing what we were learning while also introducing new topics. Practice was definitely needed, but it paid off a lot!

Engineering Analysis is a decent introductory course to linear algebra, which is important in a lot of engineering fields. It is a combination of linear algebra itself and some MATLAB programming, which is a math–focused programming language. Since I'm a CS major, the programming part of the course was not challenging, but I know a lot of other people who didn't code before struggle with programming, so if you don't have prior coding knowledge, practice a lot of MATLAB and go to the office hours. Concepts of linear algebra that are covered in this course are not that advanced, and once you learn the basic ideas behind the material, every topic requires almost the same amount of information. The course is not impossible to get an A on, however especially if you are not interested in CS, you should put in some effort in the material and be familiar with the concepts.

When it comes to instruction, Prof. Mikhelson is one of the most enthusiastic professors I've ever seen, and he really wants his students to learn and succeed. The only down side of this was that sometimes, he glided through the topics, and didn't spend a lot of time on some of the important topics, leaving the student confused. However, overall, I think that he did a great job in teaching an intro course in linear algebra, which is generally not an easy concept for a lot of students.
Go to lectures. It is very important and you will learn lots!

This course was a fair amount of work, the homework programs could be challenging but altogether the workload was very manageable. Some of the concepts were difficult to understand from just one lecture or discussion, but reading the textbook or watching Ilya's videos were really helpful tools.
MATLAB was likely the most important part of the course and you learn linear algebra well when you're asked to code it and understand it in a logical, step by step manner
Ilya is an amazing professor! This class definitely challenged me and I learned a lot through it. He does go very fast in lecture, so you have to make sure you don't get distracted and miss anything.

Prof Mikhelson is an amazing guy, super sweet, cared about students, very very helpful during office hours. The way the course is structured is funky with a lot of important concepts being taught in discussion, which would lead to lecture/discussion mismatch as not all discussion sections were the same. MATLAB could be taught in a more structured way rather than just typing code into Matlab to demonstrate its usage.
The course isn't too difficult as long as enough time is put into it. The homework isn't too difficult and office hours are very helpful when it comes to doing the homework programs.

This course moves fast, especially for people with no experience in linear algebra. To be successful, you need to make sure you fully understand the content you are working with before moving to the next topic. It builds on itself, so misunderstanding in the early stages makes learning later in the case more difficult.
first day instructor talked at a million miles per hours and it was quite the shock but eventually you get used to it.
Interesting lin alg concepts paired with tricky/annoying matlab

Ilya is a great lecturer. He explains things well and multiple times. GO TO OFFICE HOURS. He will explain the entire homework code and give answers to the weekly homework quizzes. The discussion section with Kumar could be very boring and put me to sleep but he knows what he's talking about. The resources for this course on canvas are great. I highly recommend reviewing the notes they post or rewatching lectures. Also check out Ilya's youtube. Overall, this course is challenging but was completely new to me so it was really interesting.
Ilya is a dawg. If you don't like coding you may struggle a bit, and linear algebra can be tough at first but it all comes together at the end.

It pains me to have to write how disappointed I am by this course because of how much I was rooting for it in the beginning.

I admired Ilya's teaching style and the amount of care he displayed for his students during office hours and lecture. It was evident that among his YouTube videos, practice midterm questions, lecture notes, etc., Ilya filled the Canvas with a plethora of resources. We even had ED discussion, which was a platform dedicated to asking the EA faculty and your peers questions where you would get a swift response in return. Compared to a lot of other courses in McCormick, or even Weinberg for that matter, the structure of EA1 was organized in a manner that was designed to support the needs of its students. That being said, I feel like its main issues lie in a lack of communication between the discussion professors, pacing of the course material, and its management of extenuating circumstances.

I was part of a cohort of unlucky students who were placed in a discussion group with a professor (or in my case, post–graduate student) who didn't have experience teaching EA1 in years prior and didn't express any interest in teaching the content. For context, the thing about EA1 is that its discussion sections serve as extensions of the lectures with Ilya because of the inherent time constraint from the quarter system. My schedule didn't allow me to attend other discussion sections in person, so my only option aside from rewatching Panopto lecture videos online was to attend my own poorly taught discussion section lecture. I attended my own discussion section for about 2 weeks until I grew fed up with not being able to understand my discussion professor and his teaching style of having his back turned to the board while speaking at 2x the normal pace. I wasn't the only one: ever since the lecture room in Annenberg was at full capacity during the first meeting, more people progressively switched to another discussion section, and there were maybe 10–15 people consistently henceforth who attended. To add insult to injury, I discovered that my discussion section was significantly behind in material compared to other discussion sections. I don't understand why each discussion professor is allowed to significantly deviate from the curriculum's suggested pacing for the day. It only made me feel like I was given another systemic disadvantage to succeeding in this course on top of the knowledge gap of not knowing how to already code in MATLAB or how to solve linear algebra problems.

As for the management issue, I must preface with saying I was dealing with a horrible situation in the middle of my Fall quarter that occupied nearly all of my time, left me with severely deteriorated mental health, and interfered with my academic performance. If you ever happen to, heaven forbid, encounter your own unforeseen extenuating circumstance, do not expect the program coordinator behind EA1 to accommodate you with your exams. I genuinely feel failed by the management behind EA1. I had requested to take one of the midterms on the already scheduled makeup midterm exam date, and I was denied on the basis of not giving a warning months in advance—as if I precisely knew when tragedy would strike. I had the proper documentation and everything only to be dismissed and forced to take an exam when I was physically and emotionally unwell. I feel like the lack of support I received in this regard only tainted my performance in this course in the following weeks. It's so unfortunate that in the year 2024, individuals are still having to put up with inappropriate and insensitive responses to crises.
I wish you the best of luck with this course!
Tests and homework programs are hard, but are doable with some studying.
I find it really difficult as someone who's never done programming before. So prepare to be challenged. This class also makes me decide to not do engineering.

EA was a very challenging course. Ilya talks very fast and it's hard to keep up in lecture (I often have to rewatch them later). I had to go to my discussion professor's office hours and Ilya's office hours every week for extra help. The linear algebra concepts were hard to grasp and the programs were confusing. I would reccommend getting help right away if you are confused because it moves very fast. The practice exams were a lot easier than the real exams.

EA1 is a little annoying. Learning about linear algebra was interesting and MATLAB is a useful skill to have. It is frustrating that many interesting topics in Linear Algebra and are crucial are not covered (determinants, eigenvalues/eigenvectors) since the course focused so much of its time on MATLAB. Discussions are confusing since they do the same thing as the main lecture but have it separately two days of the week. Make sure you really understand the material since Ilya moves through material quite quickly. If you haven't internalized the material after each lecture, the next lecture will be very confusing since it builds on topics previously covered.

This course is very interesting and can be challenging. As a person who had zero coding experience before this course, I was able to (forced to) pick up MATLAB pretty quickly. The linear algebra portion would probably be the more challenging part of this course. It isn't very intuitive, but Ilya does a very good job of explaining the material. The exams are fair, but the grading is less so. It's challenging enough to be interesting but not too challenging.

This course covers a lot of material and it does so at a blistering pace. Every day we saw new material and we wouldn't spend that much time on a specific topic. As soon as we were done with it, we'd move on to the next one. For this course, I needed to review the material several times to understand it. The class went so quickly that my notes were often incomplete, thus I had to go over the lecture and discussion notes to complete my notes.
This was a good course. Not impossibly challenging, and very interesting. Ilya is a great professor. As long as you don't fall behind in understanding the concepts, you'll be fine.

The lectures were very fast–paced and I often found myself having to rely resources outside of class time such as professor office hours, TA office hours, textbook, all of which were extremely useful.

I really enjoyed this course. While this course is intellectually challenging and time–consuming, it’s certainly worth it in the end. The homework programs and case studies were great opportunities to apply the concepts we learned in class to real–world contexts. The concepts themselves were quite interesting as well. When I started out, I had no prior exposure to either linear algebra or MATLAB, and I’ve come a long way since then.

Professor Ilya's lectures are fast paced but informative. I believe that linear algebra is more difficult conceptually compared to calculus, but this course's lectures helped me understanding the content at a deep level.

Linear Algebra is SUPER practical and applicable in engineering. It serves as the foundation for a lot of things – machine learning, data/mathematical modelling, cryptography, search engine algorithms, epidemic simulations, etc. I would highly recommend taking this course because of how applicable and insightful everything is.

Overall this course is standard for both difficulty and expectations. I don't find that it was taught in a particularly helpful way. The things that this course excels at is giving more than enough resources to help you facilitate your own learning. Unfortunately, in my experience, I don't find that I have passion for the course content or work. I think the course lacks passion from the instructors and only finds its rhythm near the very end. Overall, the course is difficult but manageable.
EA1 is definitely a tough course but Ilya and Honig are pretty good teachers. Ilya tends to go fast so watching lectures over is necessary but he explains concepts well. Same with Honig.

It was generally hard and the teaching style of the professor didn't necessarily match my learning style. Generally it was fast paced and if you had no prior knowledge of coding or linear algebra, you would probably struggle quite much.
Everyone needs to take this course for McCormick but it's pretty fun. Ilya is a great teacher and makes things really care. GO TO HIS OFFICE HOURS IF YOU WANNA HAVE IT A LITTLE EASIER. No complaints.

The class was necessary for learning Matlab, and it was generally helpful in that regard. Lectures were fast–paced but helpful. Discussions were just another lecture which was weird. Homework programs were pretty quick but the documents were way too wordy. The case studies we looked at throughout the quarter were unnecessary. The lab assessments were easy, the lab activities were overly long and unhelpful.
glad everything was recorded, lots of resources, everything needed posted on canvas
You kinda have to like this class if you are taking this class, otherwise that just sucks but you will live through it.

I really enjoyed learning MATLAB programming and linear algebra, but combining the two on the exams was extremely difficult. Ilya was very helpful in his lectures, and his office hours were invaluable for completing the homework assignments.

This class was extremely difficult, especially as someone with zero prior coding experience. The MATLAB aspects were definitely the hardest part of the class. Linear algebra is certainly complicated but at least for me it felt more approachable than the coding. Definitely try to understand the fundamentals of MATLAB at the beginning because everything builds off of each other. good class overall, very interesting and some of the matlab assignments are highkey fascinating, ilya is goated

I mean you're going to have to take the course anyways if you're in Engineering so I don't see how the CTEC is actually that helpful. If you have Ilya when you take this class you are basically set. Just don't make it a habit to just copy his code during his office hours since he basically gives you the entire homework answers in his office hours and you just have to modify it a tiny bit. But the problem is if you do this you are basically screwed for the first exam since you didn't actually learn MATLAB. The first exam is entirely MATLAB, and the rest is all Linear Algebra. Great class.

The course can be quite difficult without any background in linear algebra. There is a lot of content covered, so getting behind can put you in a difficult spot. It is manageable, but just make sure to utilize office hours as much as possible and read the textbook whenever you get lost on a topic.

As someone who had no coding experience, the MATLAB parts of this class were a pretty good introduction to programing. The homework programs at times seemed to require an unreasonable amount of skill, but I went to office hours and did fine on all of them. The linear algebra part of the course is what I have a problem with: I have no idea why we learned any of it, what the point of it is, how I will use it, etc. That made it really hard to stay engaged in the subject, but also made it pretty hard to understand... like sure I know what a transformation is... but why?
Ilya is a great instructor and makes the class much more fun than it actually is. Assignments are difficult, but doable if you go to office hours/put the effort in.
I found the course to be useful mainly in learning linear algebra. The MATLAB was useful but often skimmed over.

Very challenging course, but if you pay attention in lectures, go to office hours, and go to discussion sections it is very manageable. The professor is great, and takes discussions with Honig if you already have some background knowledge, he goes faster. Else, take with Kumar, really patient and helpful with explaining.

Overall it wasn't as bad as I thought. I already had a coding background so Matlab wasn't too bad, but either way it's not very difficult. The exams are difficult but if you study it should be ok. I recommend using the textbook when studying. The reading material and the practice problems in the book are great, and it's something I wish I used sooner.

Ilya is a good lecturer, but he talks reallllyyy fast, which can be frustrating when he moves on quickly because each topic feels rushed. I personally liked the discussion sections better with Prof. Honig because he talked slower and explained concepts in a digestible way

Ilya was a phenomonal teacher who really knows his content and how to pass it down. However, he is very fast and it is easy to get lost in class sometimes. The material itself is not too hard, although the first half (which is MATLAB coding) really depends on if you have coded before or not. If you have, it's pretty straightforward.
It was a good way to start engineering. Mostly matlab and then linear algebra. Go to office hours

This course was very fast paced and if you missed a concept in discussion or lecture you would fall behind quickly. While the homework programs didn't always seem to relate to what we were learning in class, office hours were helpful for understanding what the assignment was asking and figuring out a good starting place.

Decent course. Ilya is a great teacher, who is really passionate about the subject and always willing to accommodate you with more information. Due to the nature of this being a 500 person class, it makes it far more difficult to get specific answers and in depth explanations. EDStem is decently useful, but you are better off joining a peer guided study group and getting examples worked out there.

EA 1 is tough. Especially coming from someone who went into it with no coding experience the first half learning MATLAB was a challenge. The course is structured into the first half learning matlab and how to perform basic function and the second half is primarily linear algebra with somewhat using the MATLAB skills you learned all ready. Office hours are very helpful in this course.
I did not enjoy this class a ton as coding is not my thing and I am not the best at it.

This course was super daunting for me at first due to my lack of coding experience, but I appreciate how the course encourages collaboration with other students to do the homework programs. I have one really good friend in this course, who notably also has no coding background, and we were able to figure out pretty much everything if we just spent enough time working on it together.
I enjoyed this course as it combined coding and linear algebra in a constructive way. It is fun to put linear algebra topics to the test using coding in practical applications.
Go to office hours whenever possible. For me, it was mandatory.
This course is pretty challenging, but the TAs and Ilya are extremely good at helping you as long as you ask. However, even with their help, I found the course very difficult.

EA 1 is a difficult course. If you have coding knowledge going into the class, it's pretty easy. The first midterm will likely be really easy if you have prior coding knowledge. I did not so it was a struggle for me. The linear algebra is definitely the more difficult portion of the class. Especially the last 2 or 3 weeks. With that being said, the final wasn't all that bad. Some people think that the final exam is super hard. The level of difficult of the questions is the same as all of the other midterms but the material is just more difficult. Office hours is really helpful and I would highly recommend going to them if you are struggling. The lin alg textbook is also really good and has tons of examples.

I had never taken linear algebra before this course. The pace of the lectures were far too fast for me to grasp the concepts as the quarter progressed (onto and one to one, least squares). Ilya has an irritating habit of starting with a practice problem, pasting the answers way too fast for you to think about how to get there, and then saying that the problem is trivial and quickly moving on. I had to self teach almost all of the linear algebra content of the course.
N/A
Good class, just go to lectures and try to understand whats going on.

This course was challenging but extremely enjoyable. I had no linear algebra experience coming into this course but was able to pick up the concepts relatively quickly. If there are concepts you don't understand, it is up to you to seek out the help you need/put in the time to study those topics in depth.

The course might be confusing at times, and it can be sometimes hard to understand MATLAB. The exams are not that complicated if you solve the provided past exams. The weekly homework programs are challenging and can take much time to complete. However, the office hours are beneficial.
I learned a lot because we had good professors.

This course is a great freshman first–quarter experience. It isn't too challenging, but for someone who doesn't know linear algebra or MATLAB, it pushes you to think and grasp new concepts in an instructive and creative way. It is a combination of a math class and a programming class, and you could argue that the linear algebra is somewhat surface level, but it is still a very engaging course to take.

Ilya is a pretty good lecturer, and there were lots of resources available if you don't immediately understand the content covered in lecture. For example, all the class notes and recordings are posted on Canvas. Discussion sections aren't exactly "discussions," they're basically extensions of the lectures and cover required content.
This course really did teach me a lot of all new things

EA is linear algebra and MATLAB combined. MATLAB syntax feels a little awkward to learn if you already have practice with other languages. If not, Ilya teaches coding in a very beginner friendly way. Discussions are literally where 50% of the course material is taught so they are very important to go to or at least to watch the recordings. Take discussions with Honig! If you are not a morning person, you will not get out of bed for an 8 am discussion, do not take it then. If you feel lost, read the textbook and do practice problems from there, it helps so much.
Course is overall helpful, Ilya does a good job teaching linear algebra and MATLAB in lectures. Discussions can vary, some are more helpful than others. Go to office hours.

I didn't hate EA but the class is structured a bit weirdly. Whereas other schools focus entirely on linear algebra we only talk about maybe 3 chapters and that is all that is required from engineering students. The other half is matlab which isn't that bad of a programming language. I'd advise you become proficient in matlab since you'll be using it for the rest of your time in the EA sequence.

The subjects of the course are difficult to grasp in the short amount of time given, unless you have been exposed to these topics in other courses. Often times, the discussion professors and the lecture professor would be unaligned, which can add confusion. Professor Mikhelson is a fantastic professor and does an amazing job with educating students on the intricate details of the course subjects.
EA I is definitely hard, but doable. Ilya teaches with passion and if you are really into linear algebra and understand the topics fully everything flows smoothly.

This is easiest the hardest course I've taken in my first quarter. You have to really pay attention and learn in this class more than others in order to really succeed and end up doing well in your classes. It proves extremely useful in terms of learning other coding languages but is very complex and dives very deep into the course materials. Ilya is a great teacher who knows what he is doing and teaches the class very well, but the class itself is no joke.

Ilya is great and explains things super accurately and thoroughly. He does sometimes move quick however, especially during the linear algebra portion, so make sure to thoroughly learn the material outside of class if you need to catch up. The exams or not bad as long as you understand the material well for linear algebra and some frequently used MATLAB functions.

Overall I feel like Ilya and Honig did a really good job teaching this course. Honig was really good for discussion he goes really slowly so it is easy to keep up, lectures with Ilya were a bit harder to follow because he taught really quickly, but his explanations are really good. I didn't go often, but for homework programs if you go to office hours Ilya basically gives you all the answers.

I actually really enjoyed this course. The combination of coding and linear algebra was pretty cool. The first 1/2 of the course is coding, which is pretty easy if you have had experience with coding in high school (I took CSP and CSA and the first part of this course was pretty easy). The linear algebra part is a bit harder, but still pretty manageable. I actually really enjoyed learning about the linear algebra part of the course and its application. Ilya is awesome, he goes pretty fast at parts but he is good at explaining concepts both during lecture and after if you go up to ask him a question. As long as you do your homework programs, complete the HW quizzes and understand the answers to those, you should be alright.
This course is very challenging, especially the homework programs. You need to go to office hours for the homework programs because Ilya will walk you through each step.
This course is kind of rushed, and to fully understand what is happening you have to learn parts on your own to fill in the blanks. Office hours can be very helpful for this course.
The recitation sections were not helpful and the lectures koved too quickly.
This course introduced me to Linear Algebra. Although it was fast paced, I enjoyed learning Linear Algebra.

EA1 is very difficult. If you have background in coding, MATLAB will not be as bad but it is a lot different than other coding languages. In addition, linear algebra is something that you will understand more at the end of the course. I recommend going to lecture with an idea about the topic Ilya is going to talk about. This will help you understand what is going on in lecture. In addition, it is important to have friends to talk about this with because linear algebra is a topic you have to be able to explain so by studying with others you can help the others by talking about it.
The aspect that was most important to me was learning how to learn difficult topics and use the resources I have available here at Northwestern.
Good professor, put in time to learn the material.
Will be hard to follow up if you don't already know how to program or use MATLAB.

Just in general I did not enjoy linear algebra or the use of matlab. It seemed to take concepts of matrices and vectors and shove them into many different contexts were many different terms were thrown in to explain the same concepts. The matlab weekly homework was also randomly complicated. They were thrown in confusing situations to help get you thinking, but just weren’t super helpful. Ilya was a solid teacher and displayed enthusiasm but also went super fast at times. This is a class that provides recordings of lectures and discussions however so classes could be watched back

Prof Ilya is THE BEST! Super passionate in what he teaches and you can tell he has been teaching it for a while. He will probably be the only option, but if not absolutely take this with him. For discussion I would recommend Honig. If you have coding experience, the course may seem easy at first because of the MATLAB, but it definitely ramps up in difficulty during the second half of the quarter (the linear algebra side). Suddenly it will become so important, so I would start supplementing your learning with textbook problems and YouTube videos (Ilya has some) which helped me so much in the end but I'd hate to cram it all next time, so spread it out! Also, GO TO OFFICE HOURS!!
Overall it is a good course. It's biggest flaw is that a lot of the examples (or case studies) go way beyond the scope of the course and introduce unnecessary confusion.
This course is fast paced and it really helps to go to office hours.
The content was pretty difficult conceptually in my opinion and the profs tried their best to help us understand. I had to still study outside of the class to fully understand the material, though.

Overall, the course was pretty good. However, I often felt like sometimes there was a disconnect between the programs we were assigned to write and the concepts we were learning in class (the concepts were the same, but the applications were expanded in directions that we were not prepared well for)
Taking this first semester was actually so humbling. The course is super challenging, making you really put in an effort.

This course is a requirement but I think it was overall a very engaging and interesting course. I enjoyed doing the homework programs and learning about linear algebra and Ilya is an amazing professor who is so passionate about the course. However, this course does move very fast, and it can be easy to miss a large part of the class if you miss discussions or miss lectures, but there are a lot of resources that can help, such as lecture videos and lecture notes. This class can also be quite difficult, given the speed and content, but again, with enough work, I found the course pretty fun and useful.

Way too fast, huge lecture hall, not friendly to beginners, literally did not learn anything because im just trying to stuff information into my brain to survive the class. Have a good coding and linear algebra base before taking this class or youll get cooked. This class and knowing I have to do 3 more ea sequence classes gives me 0 enthusiasm about this school. Idk whos teaching the course but if you have a choice take illya, his office hours saved my career

Generally the course is a good introductory course into matlab and linear algebra. I though the merging of the two is really smart because it allows me to apply the concepts I learned in class to a different context. Great idea. Prof. Mikhelson is clearly a passionate teacher. He shows the concepts well but his pacing is estranged sometimes, skipping over difficult concepts while spending a lot of time on others. However, he is generally good with it.
Overall, okay class. Have to try hard to make sure you pass.
It was not what I expected at all but in a good way; I ended up really enjoying learning how to code in Matlab even though the assignments often took a long time.

Strongly suggested to know beforehand what you will be facing in EA1, because it will be quite some shock going into the class without any knowledge on Linear Algebra (and MATLAB). It is not impossible to learn from scratch in this class but it will be a little challenging, especially if you're juggling this class with some other hard classes at the same time. Additionally, since a lot of the students takes this class first quarter of freshman year, it may be a little overwhelming with us still trying to figure everything else. Make sure to use the abundant resources this course provides if you're struggling in this class.

This course is very difficult and time consuming. I always felt as if I was fighting for my life due to the fast pace of the course, and we would go from basic topics to abstract complexities so fast.

Yes the course was very helpful in teaching a strong fundamental understanding of linear algebra and programming. I actually enjoyed MatLab and the linear algebra portion was conceptually challenging but doable. Ilya is the best professor but be prepared to spend some time on the homework and take ample class notes.
The homework programs are easy if you go to office hours and hard if you try to do it by yourself.

It's a course about MatLab and linear algebra, and that's the long and short of it. The instruction is good, but very fast–paced. All lectures are recorded and available on Canvas, which helps a lot in that regard. It's not an easy course, but if you work hard you will be rewarded.

I'm not entirely sure what the relation between this and engineering is, so that's that. The second half of the course is pretty much going over the same the concepts over and over again but giving it a different classification which was a lot to remember. I've also never coded before so I didn't have any skills to rely on going into this so this was a challenging experience.

I enjoyed this class. Some of the topics were hard at first, but when they click, it's the best feeling. The alternating schedule of discussions and lectures was helpful in making sure you understand the lessons fully. I cannot stress how great Ilya was. He brings energy and enthusiasm to every class and is really good at explaining the topics. He is so knowledgeable about MatLab and linear algebra, and you can tell he has fun doing it, which makes it more fun for the class.
EA was personally really hard for me as I had never coded before nor done work with linear algebra but the TAs and textbook were really helpful!

This course was very challenging toward the end, and I personally had to put in a lot of effort to understand the Matlab component toward the end. It is helpful to understand how tests are scored to see how to answer questions. Midterm I was a great representation, but for Midterm II there were some curveballs. Ilya is a great teacher but goes really fast. Office hours are helpful for understanding the code but are not really used for understanding linear algebra, which can make the course challenging.
This class is very hard, there's no two ways about it. Start the homework programs on time, maybe join a PGSG, and go to office hours.
I had a hard time on the tests, but I enjoyed the lectures and discussions.
I think that it was a little fast paced but manageable and the instructors were decent

The course was genuinely difficult, but felt internally rewarding. The way matrices are taught is pretty great and laid a sturdy foundation for my interest in engineering. I feel like this class kinda tells you how much you're gonna like McCormick, and for me, I'm super excited to continue.

The content of the class felt very disjointed at times. Like we would spend a while talking about some thing that I'm not even sure how we got to, and then immediately switch to something else. Yes, some of those excursions did help my understanding of linear algebra and MATLAB syntax and the like, but it was awkwardly executed. It would get frustrating often trying to figure out what to make of what we were learning, but after perservering through it, I'm happy with how the class turned out overall.

For a requirement it's a pretty interesting course. I turned the background of my matlab pink so it was more aesthetically pleasing and that was a game changer. The lectures are helpful and homework helps you conceptually understand the difficult stuff from lecture. Overall solid class.

Ilya D. Mikhelson. This man has the Will of D and is the best professor I had this quarter. In order to understand a concept, the professor has to have a deep, profound, and rich understanding of the concept to teach it, and Professor Mikhelson has that. I came into this class with limited programming knowledge and no linear algebra knowledge and I left the class with a mastery of both due to the instruction of Professor Mikhelson. If you are taking EA1, you are in good hands.

Ilya is a great professor! He makes the intangible topics of linear algebra make sense through spatial explanations, and his office hours help a ton with debugging MATLAB programs. I would recommend Honig for discussion section; he was consistently on or ahead of schedule, which is not true for all discussion sections. I have also heard that PGSGs for this class were very informative, so if you are nervous about the content, join one! This class was slightly humbling, but it is manageable.
Textbook is very helpful. Use it.

Lectures are a bit fast paced, so review your notes or rewatch them on canvas. Homework programs are basically given during Ilya office hours, so attend if you want answers. Exams are a bit more application and deeper thinking than the quizzes, so make sure to understand everything and how to use it in other problems.

The course itself wasn't too hard as long as you grasped important aspects of Linear Algebra. The Matlab portion was also easy to learn, especially if you don't already know anything about it. I just studied for exams, and the weekly programs, which wasn't too time consuming.
If you have no experience with programming or linear algebra, this course will be extremely difficult. I'd recommend at least getting a head start on linear algebra to make your life easier.
This class was my most challenging class

Ilya is obviously a very passionate and intelligent professor who is able to communicate his lessons relatively clearly. However, his office hours were a little bit chaotic, as many people would go just to get his answers for the homework and would leave very little time to actually discuss questions or provide help outside of just presenting answers.
Prof Ilya is very organized with his lectures. Using a combination of MATLAB demos, diagrams, and lecture notes, Prof Ilya sets a nice foundation for topic explored.
Ilya was the
Linear algebra is very interesting. This course explores a new topic that may be helpful to many majors.

The course content in it itself is not horrible, it's simply the way that questions are asked on tests that may confuse kids and ultimately make it difficult. Office hours are extremely helpful for this course, so I would 100% recommend attending (it helps with the homework programs). Discussions are helpful depending on who the instructor is. I had Prem Kumar at 8 am and it was not the best choice for so early in the morning considering it is a very slow paced discussion and thus motivated me to lose focus. Read the textbook....literally my life saver for this final. The instructor is very helpful, but at the same time very fast–paced during lectures. Taking notes during lectures did not work for me in the slightest because you are too focused about writing everything down instead of actually paying attention, so I stopped trying to write notes and instead simply paid attention. Later on my own time I would take notes from the lecture videos for full comprehension. The topics are not going to click in one sitting, this is a class that requires outside time and effort, especially if you have no experience in linear algebra (which was me).

I learned a ton about MATLAB and linear algebra. Discussions were really helpful... combined with the lectures, I learned MATLAB and linear algebra basically from scratch. You can tell Ilya and Honig are both very well–versed in both areas. However, a lot of the times the concepts were introduced way too quickly that things flew over my head and I had to rewatch the videos or other online lectures. HOWEVER, if you spend the time to understand the concepts, the midterms and final WILL reflect that!!

This class was super interesting. However, the pace of this class is breakneck. If you don't understand something the first time, good luck because you will just end up falling behind. My best piece of advice would be to make sure you follow up on things you don't understand the same day because the classes build on top of each other and not having a good foundation can be catastrophic. Also, make sure you go to office hours as they essentially become another lecture but also make sure you understand the things covered in the office hours.

EA1 is a very tough class, especially when taking it as a Freshman. It is very easy to get behind, and since what you are learning is completely new to most students, it isn't a class you can easily catch up to. The first week or two seem very trivial, but it get much tougher as the class goes on. Going to class, discussion, and most importantly finding peers to study with are essential to succeed in class.

Cool class, explores a lot of interesting case studies. First half is pretty straightforward if you have any programming experience. Linear gets a little complex. Go to class and don't fall behind. Go to office hours for help on the programs.

I enjoyed this course. All of the case studies were pretty interesting, and learning MATLAB was fun and felt very applicable to the real world. Plus, Ilya is a great lecturer and all around a great professor. Yes, its hard at times and the tests were pretty challenging, but if you take the time to understand the linear algebra concepts, it is totally doable.

Ilya is a good professor. This class throws you into engineering topics such as coding with MatLab and linear algebra. There is a lot of content but there are a lot of resources available for help. I recommend doing a PGSG if you are unfamiliar in the slightest with any of these course topics. I didn't do one and that is one thing I wish I did.
Lectures can go a bit fast and everything builds on top of itself so if you don't have a good understanding it will be hard to catch up.
The programs were very difficult. It seems imperative to go to office hours for help. Additionally, it is not recommended enough how important the textbook is.

Ilya's office hours, go!! It's so helpful for the homework program. Ilya is an amazing professor. He treats us like we are humans, little things like "go trick or treating" on Halloween and coming dressed as a unicorn. He's not like other stem professors that don't even say hi before starting class. He's always kind and willing to answer your questions when you are confused even if they feel like dumb questions (trust me, I've asked many haha). Please ask questions when you have them in class, it seems like everyone knows what is happening, but trust me, there are other people just as confused!! I thought I asked a dumb question and everyone around me goes "oh that makes sense now," so my question helped others out too. Ilya is the only professor so he has all the engineering freshmen for lecture, but its clear that despite that he really tries and wants you to know how to linear algebra. The class can be a lot. You are learning to code and do linear algebra in the same class. Many times you will be coding the linear algebra content you learned a few days before, and you will say to yourself that I can't do this linear algebra on paper and now I'm expected to be able to code a program for it too!? Yes, but it will all work out. The programs really test your understanding and make you work with the concepts. Just try to enjoy it. Try your best to continue and enjoy the learning process. you got it! I personally loved this class, it's making me want to explore coding even more (never thought I'd be saying this haha). The exams are fair too.

As someone who had no prior coding experience, this course was incredibly challenging. There isn’t any way around it in McCormick, so I encourage you to just do your best and not give up. Ilya is great, and his videos are super helpful. The linear algebra portion of the class was much easier for me (not easy). In my experience, the weekly programs were very time consuming and difficult, but Ilya was very open and helpful on them during office hours. The practice exams were quite indicative of what ended up being on the exams.

This class is definitely a class you will have to put a lot of your resources into. It is a tough class, but if you show up to class, ask questions when needed, and put in effort studying, you should be fine. Ilya's office hours are one of the most valuable resources, along with drop–in tutors and the TA office hours. Overall, the first half of the course in all about Matlab. If you have experience with coding, you should be fine. However, I will say there's a large disparity in the performance based on prior code knowledge. The second half is linear algebra. Do not underestimate this content. I took it in high school and still had to put in a lot of work to do well in it.

Personally, I think Ilya's lectures were not that helpful in allowing me to grasp the content. He talked really fast and would explain things in such an elaborate manner with complex vocabulary that it was difficult to comprehend a lot of what he said.

I highly highly recommend Professor Honig for the discussion sections!!! He was amazing, and his lectures really did help me understand the material we needed to. The discussion sections are basically additional lectures, so they are important and cover a ton of the content. Take it with him!!

This class was very tough but very useful. I would recommend trying to do all of the assignments yourself before giving up. Overall you should do pretty well as long as you pay attention and do the homework yourself
Hard at times, but fair, and I enjoyed how the topics that we learned were ultimately different ways of thinking about the same idea. By the end of the class I felt that I've learned a lot.
Lectures are amazing and prof is awesome. Homeworks can be absolutely brutal. Discussion sections are also hit and miss, can be super useless. Exams are not too bad and easy to study for.

This class is very interesting and gives a strong foundation in linear alegebra and coding. Since almost every concept introduced in lecture builds upon the previous lecture, it is important to make sure that you really understand the material even if that requires looking back at notes/rewatching lectures. Either way the coding projects are intresting and if you are struggling while coding, go to Ilya's office hours because he does a great job of explaining the concepts. Overall, if you pay attention it is not too hard of a class and definetly interesting content.

As someone with absolutely no coding experience, this course has been pretty hard. It definitely helps if you have coded before, or if you have taken a linear algebra class. My advice is to start each homework program early, don't wait for the last day to do all of it, or you won't learn as well. Reading the textbooks helped a lot for me in this class.
This class isn't that bad. As long as you study and do your work, you will do fine. Although there can be much to study at times, it isn't impossible. Ilya is a great professor.

EA 1 moves pretty quick as the course covers both MATLAB coding/syntax and linear algebra as well. Therefore, the course moves pretty fast and may require a decent amount of self study outside of class.

The structure of the course is designed to cover the fundamentals of linear algebra with matlab programming, not sure if it's the best combination but definitely a lot of intellectually stimulating challenges along the way. Ilya is a great instructor who communicates clearly, passionate about his subject and cares about his student. Read the textbooks and go the office hours you will most likely be fine.

comments: This class does a good job condensing a full quarter into 5 weeks. It is a very significant amount of work, with two tests a week, a ton of textbook readings, supplemental video lectures, and several other homework assignments per chapter. The homework is helpful for learning but takes a ton of time. Professor Broaders did a great job of keeping people engaged during lectures, although having no wiggle room for class attendance without significantly harming your grade was difficult to work with.
This course was very helpful. I enjoyed the combination of lectures on Zoom and asynchronous work, and the many opportunities to self–assess before formal quizzes and tests.
good summer course, Professor Broaders is great
I love the neuroscience
The course was interesting but there was just too much to memorize

I recommend taking this course if you're looking for a summer class. However, there is a lot of information condensed into a short time frame. Make sure you take full advantage of the practice quizzes and InQuizitives. Professor Broaders is knowledgable and I learned a lot from this course. However, you need to spend time reviewing the material on your own.

comments: Discussion–focused course.

Take this seminar!! Mordacq always made class very enjoyable and the workload is very manageable, with about 3 3–page readings per week and 3 2–3 page single–spaced papers throughout the class, along with a short presentation about each of your paper topics. You get to pick your topic on 2/3 of these papers and there are a wide range of options. Class usually consists of lecture and about 2–3 group discussions throughout the class time. The time flies by during class and it is enjoyable and not too complicated. The first third of the class focuses on a wide range of genetic disease, the second third is about gene editing, and the final third focuses on the social impact of different genetic diseases. Grading overall is generous and I highly recommend taking this class.

Professor Mordacq is an extremely nice professor who really knows the ins and outs of the school, so definitely a great a seminar course. Very up–to–date and relevant discussion topics. Good mix of lecturing, group discussions, papers, and presentations. I knew I could always email him with questions outside of class. A good amount of readings (2–3 short articles average per week?), especially during the beginning of the quarter, but it was fine. Gave us a lot of freedom with paper structure, which I thought was creative and educational

I really enjoyed this seminar. It was not an overbearing amount of work (short papers/writing assignment before each class and 3 presentations and 3 papers) and prof. Mordacq made the lectures very interesting.

This class taught me a ton about the pharmaceutical industry, politics, healthcare policy, and the most current cutting–edge research in synthetic biology and bioengineering. You write three literature reviews over the course of the quarter, and have weekly reading assignments which focus on very interesting technology that's being developed, ethical debates in science, etc. I highly recommend this class to anyone who has an interest in bioengineering or healthcare.

This course is a fun and interesting class. Professor Mordacq does a great job of promoting discussion and engagement. Do not fear taking this as a seminar even if you are not a pre–med or stem student the professor makes everything very easy to understand. You should definitely take this class! Stan Mordacq.

I liked this course because the professor was engaging during class and allowed flexibility in our paper topics. For each paper, I could pick a topic that I was genuinely interested in. Additionally, I liked the setup of in–class discussions. The professor would put us in groups of 3 and we would discuss with each other first before sharing with the class. As someone who doesn't usually enjoy discussions, this format made discussions easier as I could talk to peers first, and it also allowed everyone to voice their thoughts and opinions. The homework was very light, besides the three papers. Before each class, there would be a few short articles to read and summarize. The one thing I really didn't enjoy was the number of presentations we had to do. I don't mind doing presentations, but I think they took up a lot of time in class when we could have been learning or discussing other things.

this was my favorite class I have taken at Northwestern so far the teacher professor Mordacq was so nice and engaging during his lectures. He always discussed a new topic that peaked my interest, and I enjoyed every day of class. I highly recommend this seminar for anybody no matter their major I am a psychology major, and I thought the class would be intimidating, because there was a lot of stuff about biology professor mordacq made it possible for anybody to understand the concepts and apply them to their essays that we had to write he is very good at giving feedback on our essay. The classes are very chill and you do not do anything besides listen and talk very low stress and you get a lot out of the class. 10 out of 10 class please take!!!
Mordacq is the funniest teacher I've had so far, take this seminar it will be worth it trust

This course was really helpful to take. The class made me a more knowledgeable citizen after learning and discussing topics in gene editing, genetic disorders, pharmaceutical companies, and the healthcare system. Although this course seems to be heavily focused on biology, the professor is really good at simplifying concepts and I believe the class is beneficial for non–STEM majors as well.

5/7
