comments: EA 2 is sort of a physics class in that it focuses on vectors, forces, equilibrium, moments, etc. for the first half of class, however later on there are some concepts introduced such as trusses, frames, and supports that are not typically discussed in any AP class.

This course might seem difficult, but you will grasp the content as you progress through the class and continue to study. Professor Gomma is a really good professor but unfortunately won't teach future classes. However, the other professors are really good too. As long as you practice, ask questions when you have any, and continue on despite the struggles, you can do it.
It was super helpful & genuinely helped me understand what I want to do in the future.
It was difficult and not particularly fun

Prof. Gomaa (Shady) was amazing. I have already taken statics at a prior university as part of Physics 1, but has to take EA2 due to the distribution of my credits entering Northwester. Despite this, he encouraged lots of engagement and allowed students to be very involved in the class either by answering questions or going up to solve the problems themselves. He also explained all concepts to great detail to ensure that every student understood what was being taught and had the ability to solve problems given to them. I wish he was staying here at NU, but the University of Alabama is lucky to have him as a professor now. He was undoubtedly one of the best professors I have had.
Shady Gomma is the GOAT! It's unfortunate he is leaving. EA2 isn't bad. Just do a bunch of practice problems.

As someone who despises physics, Professor Gomaa was quite good about teaching the content and simplifying it which was great for an EA2 trailer class. The class is more of a math class with some physics concepts rather than. I recommend trailer EA2 for anyone considering it! The curve is significantly better, and the content is explained much simpler so it was very easy to understand and keep up with the class. This class had two midterms worth 15% and 20% respectively, two projects worth a total of 10%, weekly problem sets also worth a total of 10%, and a final worth 45%. It's nice that the midterms are worth less, but it also means you still need to do well on the final even if you did really well on both midterms.

Since you probably have to take this class for your major, I say trailer EA2 is definitely the way to go.

The tests were not difficult at all. Although the medians on the tests on this course sat around 70 for each one, the upper quartiles were very high at around 90% each time, so around a quarter of the class got above a 90%.

So basically, if you know what you're doing then you can easily get an A. If you don't know what you're doing then you're gonna do very, very poorly.

SHADY = GOAT. however very sad he will not be teaching at NU anymore :( best prof for EA 2 trailer as exams were very light and was lenient with due dates. whatever anyone else says either didn't make an effort to do work/go to class or just did not try LOL. class was very manageable as long as you put in some effort. way easier than EA 2.
Prof Gomaa is the goat. Made the class so easy and always let you know what type of problems to study for exams. 10X easier than EA1
Professor Gomaa was an amazing professor!
Material isn't terribly difficult if you put in the effort to learn. Good lecture notes and plenty of practice. Homework isn't too bad.

As a person who never taken physics, this course is good for building the foundational knowledge. The annoying part is you will have two Matlab physics projects that I found quite challenging. But office hours and TA are always your friend.

This course requires a lot of practice with questions. Understanding the concepts is not the most difficult part and will not get you far exams. Actually understanding how to do homework questions and practice tests will be the best way to be successful. Don't be scared to ask questions as well during lecture, and work with others to do challenging questions.
At first it was like oh flip I'm not doing so hot but then I locked in and got a 100 on the second midterm and the final so we are so up

Course gets harder at the frames/trusses section, the professor is pretty good at explaining that area, but it was definitely something i had to spend much more time on compared to the other content in the class
This course did feel better taught, I feel this instructor takes the information and does the problems that makes it a lot easier to understand and actually use for the midterms.

I tried taking EA2 during the normal sequence and I really struggled as a person with no previous experience with physics. If you haven't taken physics before, I recommend you take trailer. Professor Gomaa was a great professor but he's leaving after this quarter.
Easy A
Solid course. Material isn't fascinating but it's also not super difficult. Definitely not one of the harder EA's. Take the trailer sequence if you can, much better than the other one.

The class wasn't too bad (I did it trailer). I found it easier than I thought I would. The midterms were not too bad, and the content is pretty much the same throughout the whole quarter just adding a few things to it as you go along. Focus in class to understand and I think it'll be fine. Only annoying thing is that the final is 45% of the mark.

Shady was a decent teacher, but that's not super relevant since he won't be teaching this course anymore. Despite this being the trailer section, EA 2 was a challenging and time–consuming course, especially compared to EA 1. My biggest gripe is the grade distribution. Homework is only worth 10% of the grade, despite taking a minimum of 4 hours a week. The projects, while not too difficult, take a long time, are only worth another 10% of the grade, and are assigned at the same time as homework and near midterms. The average on the two midterms was about a 75, but the final is a whopping 45% of the grade, so you truly don't know how you're doing until you're done. To be successful in the class, start the homework and projects early and review all examples/topics given in lectures when reviewing for exams.

The class is not super hard. Homework takes a while but is only worth 10% of the grade. Exams are not super super hard but the final is worth like 45% of the grade. The class has two projects, which are unnecessarily long but not too hard. Shady is a great professor who knows what he's teaching.
Shady is the best he makes class really funny and interactive

I dropped EA2 during winter quarter with Alarcon and took it trailer spring quarter with Gomaa. Spring quarter went so much better than winter, I think the main difference was the teaching style. Gomaa explains things so much clearer and slowly, he is also super funny throughout all class. If you can, I would highly recommend taking trailer. The course itself goes more slowly, the exams are easier, and it's a smaller class size so there's more support for each individual student. EA2 is majority physics, I have never taken a physics course before, but trailer taught it in a way where beginners like me can truly learn it.

When I dropped EA 2, I was scared I had made the wrong decision, but after taking Trailer EA 2, I'm glad I did. The material in this course felt much more comprehensible. It felt like we were not rushing through it, and we were spending the appropriate amount of time on each section. Professor Shady was an awesome instructor, and his teaching helped break down the material for us. Additionally, he made the class enjoyable and engaging. The HWs and lecture notes, especially, were excellent practice for the exams. For the projects, I found going to office hours to be the most useful. For me, coming up with the code for the project was intimidating, but I decided to go to office hours and there I could practice writing the code and checking it with the TA. They were really good at guiding you through it.

The professor of this course, Shady Gomaa is one of the best professors I have had at Northwestern. I was pleasantly surprised by my successes in this class due to my lack of experience in physics. This professor made having no prior experience not matter at all. His teaching style is very understandable and easy to follow along at all times. If you do all the homeworks in this class and attend every lecture in my opinion success is guaranteed.
The professor has a very good way of teaching and he hold review sessions before every exams which helps a lot to learn new things and manage to get good grades at the same time.

I took this class with Professor Gomaa and while I liked him as a person and he was a very fun and interesting professor who kept students engaged, I did not like the structure of this course. I felt that all 3 exams were of differing difficulty levels, and the homeworks were not reflective of the material covered in class and were often harder than what we would see on an exam. The practice exams were also not reflective of the actual exams and were harder (Midterms 1 and 2) or easier (final) than the actual exams. A lot of people say this course is easier than EA1, but I thought EA1 was a lot easier in terms of understanding the material. In this class it felt like you could prepare a lot but there would be a random curveball on the exam.
problem sets and notes

EA2 is tough depending on what professor you have. If their teaching style isn't your thing, it can be really hard to grasp. I had to take it a second time, however, and Shady Gomaa's teaching style worked so well for me and things started to make so much more sense.
This class has a narrow scope of concepts and just builds on the applications. It can get tricky, but with consistent practice you'll find yourself catching tips tricks and patterns.
Shady is a great professor, would definitely recommend taking EA2 with him!

Professor Shady is a really good lecturer, and it's not hard to follow along in class and take notes. He doesn't make the class very hard, and I would say all of the assignments, projects, and exams were very reasonable. Just make sure to pay close attention to the deadlines of assignments and their times because late work is generally not accepted at all.

This course was so much better than the first time I took EA2. I took it in winter with Alarcon and nearly failed the class so I had to drop. I came into it this time hoping it would be better and Shady BLEW ME AWAY. He has a way of teaching that makes everything so understandable and I sat in class thinking to myself "how did I not understand this last time?". He breaks everything down so well and uses hand gestures to explain things instead of just talking at the class. This has easily been my best class at NU and possibly all time.

This spring section is much better than the winter one, and the professor is very patient and a good explainer. This course is simply something you are required to take, so no further comment.

This course was very engaging and taught at a comprehensive pace. Professor Gomaa was a great instructor who communicated the course material clearly and effectively. His passion for the subject was evident in every lecture, making the learning experience both engaging and inspiring.
I love math until I got to Northwestern but being in this course reignited that passion by making everything seem so applicable to how structures are built in real life.

Professor Gomaa is an amazing professor, one of the best at Northwestern, and I am so grateful to have been taught by him this quarter before he departs. As someone with no proper physics background, I found his method of teaching, his organized lecture notes, and his willingness to interact with the class and help us learn to be admirable and motivating.

Take the trailer class. My experience was the opposite of what I heard about the course from people who took the regular sequence. Shady Gomaa was an amazing teacher, who really started with the basics and taught even the most complicate subjects clearly. He also uploaded his notes online for review which was super helpful. The exams were challenging but fair and he tells you exactly what subject each question on the exam will be.

Pretty intuitive material, on the surface level it may seem intimidating but you really just end up following different procedures and using the fact that everything is in equilibrium to solve for systems of equations (light work)

Basically just AP Physics mechanics all over again. Prof. Gomaa is very nice, although lectures were kind of dull (although I am biased as this material is not relevant to my interests at all). Concepts not too difficult and exams weren't bad either, just make sure to get a lot of practice. TAs were a little mean with grading. You might get the same answer as in the textbook but get points off, and TAs will admit to being wrong but won't give points back. Overall you don't have to put too much effort in to succeed, just that the homework assignments and studying can be time consuming.

Prof. Shady was amazing and funny. The class is super easy if you take it as trailer, which I fully recommend. The only part that took time was some of the later homeworks and the first project, which i decdicated an entire weekend to. However, the tests are by shady were faitly easy and he gives you a good amount of heads up to the types of questions before the test. However, I guess none of this will be helpful since he's leaing to teach somehwere else for next year. Still, TRAILER EA!
Prof. Gomaa made the course super understandable and I loved it. The TA's helped a lot with the matlab projects as well.
Good reaction

For a course about statics, this class has a lot of moving parts. By that I mean that if you haven't taken a proper physics class before and often struggle with handling a great deal of information at once in problems, like me, you might struggle in this class. For this class, you're responsible for two midterms, a final, two "design projects" (basically just coding assignments that will take you forever if you have little coding experience), and a weekly homework problem set. I recommend doing practice problems from the textbook to help prepare for exams as the style of the problems is very similar. The weighting of exams this quarter was abysmal: 45% on the final. That being said, if you have to take this course, you'll likely do well with Shady, as he is a very good lecturer and a nice guy, but I unfortunately have a disability that made his teaching style unsuitable for me, and so I frequently found myself having to go to YouTube or other online resources to prepare for exams. Give yourself at least a few days to study for each exam, do practice problems, and you'll do fine.

Shady is an absolute goat and it sucks that he is leaving. I thought I was going to be terrible at physics but it lowkey was not that bad. Very test heavy but they are reasonable exams. You get cheat sheets on the final and everything is pretty reasonable. I was not a big fan of the lectures on the chalkboard in a big lecture room.

source_file: Student Report for David Schieber (SOCIOL_110-0_20  Introduction to Sociology)_358a4c82-6b16-43b6-8adb-e42a7a922dc5en-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: Very easy distro and easy high grade. You don't even need to pay attention in lecture to complete the five reading responses and ten short answer questions throughout the quarter. All that is required during class is answer the polleverywhere questions for attendance points. Very low stress and manageable course especially with a heavy courseload.

very chill class. the reading response papers are very easy and so are the short answer answer questions. I think they changed it this year so you have to show up to lecture, but besides that it's a good class I think some of the topics were definitely interesting for me.

This class is very chill and does not take too much time as an engineer. Assignments consist of some reading responses that you have to do before class, and short answer questions that you can do at any time. The professor is very good, I would recommend taking this class with him!

This is the easiest A I've taken at Northwestern. I don't say that in a lazy way, I say it because the assignments are really straightforward, the readings are so so interesting and actually leave you thinking, and having to go to lectures for points was a great incentive to keep us engaged. Prof. Schieber is a great lecturer, and the course structure is perfect for anyone who wants to learn more about sociology without the pressure of hard assignments or harsh grading.

This course was very well structured as an overview of different fields of sociology. I appreciated the amount of freedom I had in the writing prompts, and that there were no exams. This course was fun and thought–provoking.
Very easy course that you should take to fulfill a foundational discipline
super chill class but attendance is required now. if you do the work early you can be done with the class in a few weeks
I really enjoyed this course! It was a nice break from a lot of the really difficult sociology or psychology courses

The course is well paced and organized. Professor Schieber's class is clear and engaging (relatively, since not everyone is fond of tons of sociology readings). There is no exam, but five reading response questions (summaries and dive into the readings assigned) and ten short answer questions (based on readings and class) spaced throughout the quarter. They are not hard and you should get a good grade if you put in your effort.
Overall, it was a really chill course. Come to class since there's a participation grade through PollEV, and you need to be in the class to be checked in.

Really nice course to take for theme req. Only 15 assignments to do the entire quarter (10 short answer questions, 5 reading responses). Short answer questions are due at the end of the quarter, so you can either finish them really quickly and get them out of the way, or you can do the questions a bit after finals. You choose which reading responses to respond to, and you can redo assignments to get a higher grade. No midterms or finals.

This class is a really chill, easy distribution requirement. It's not a huge time commitment and the assignments are graded very leniently. If you want an A without having to work too hard, but you still want to learn something, I'd take this class. I feel I learned a lot just by attending lecture and doing the readings without having to spend a large amount of time each week. Especially being in McCormick, many students want an easy theme class. I'd recommend this one. It's interesting, chill, and you'll get an A. The professor is a nice guy.
The topical articles and news worked exceptionally well with student responses during lecture. I do think this course would be better with a smaller class size.
very easy A distro
I liked how I can go to class and just learn something interesting that is applicable to my everyday life.

Very easy. 5 response papers (half of which are just summarizing the readings) and 10 short answers. Attendance is required but you don't need to pay attention. Prof is a cool guy... only complaint is that my TA literally wouldn't grade anything unless i asked him to
Easy class but very interesting! This class made me want to pursue more sociology classes as the topics covered were really interesting to learn about.
interesting course, you get out whatever you put in

This course teaches you how to read dense studies and papers while also not being too strenuous. Although the readings may seem long, you're not required nor expected to read everything which is a good practice. Prof. Schieber is amazing and accommodative too!
Pretty simple course – lectures are required and interesting at times.
as long as you pace yourself with the longer assignments you'll be fine
This class is a chill, fun introduction to sociology. Schieber is an engaging lecturer. The course might help you read more efficiently, but it will not challenge you intellectually.
More work was added to this class to make it harder, but I would say it's busy work, and this class is still one of the easier ones I've taken.

Very easy A course and minimal effort distro, would recommend taking. There are 5 summary responses due before each class that each consist of a brief summary of each of the readings for that class and then personal responses to any of the readings. There are also 10 short response/test questions you need to complete by the end of the quarter that don't require much work and is just answering a test question based on class/readings. No midterms or final.

Great course for an easy distro. I personally found the readings to be more interesting than the lecture itself. All you need to do is attend class for the pollevs, write 5 reading responses, and 10 short discussion question responses.

This class is not as easy an A as it used to be. It's not a hard class by any means, but you do have to put in a little work now. Grading structure this quarter was attendance/participation (poll everywhere with location checking), 10 SAQs (250+ words), and 5 reading response papers (750+ words). In previous quarters the SAQs were optional with attendance/participation, but now they are all required. You have all quarter to do all of the work. It's not a lot of work, but it can catch up to you at the end.
Easy

Very easy class, not much else to say. I didn't get much out of it at all that I didn't know already. Yes some topics are interesting but the format means you don't have to engage with the material at all, so if you want to learn it's on you. Easy A but I would only take it if you really need an easy class to balance a schedule or if you're really interested in the sociology path.

This class suddenly shifted to attendance required which I feel like wasn't necessary. Most of what we write for homework is based on the readings and I feel that in class attendance is not required for us to do well in this class. It was annoying to having to come early for attendance that checks location and sit throughout class that I feel wasn't necessary. It should be open to come if you want to discuss material being shared that day.

I highly suggest this course to freshmen and sophomores. Taking it in my junior spring was a little underwhelming, which is somewhat predictable since it's an intro class. The assignments were pretty straightforward. I had written similar reading responses/short answer questions in classes before, so they might be more difficult to understand if you haven't done assignments like them before. I enjoyed most of the lectures, although I never really needed to take notes or pay too much attention. Definitely reach out to the professor or TAs if you have any questions, they were all very kind.

Pretty easy A. This quarter attendance was enforced with poll questions that would check your location, but you only had to answer 75% or more of the total questions to get full attendance points which were 20% of the grade. 40% of the grade was made up of 5 response essays (~750 words) that were each 8%, and the remaining 40% of the grade was made up of 10 short answer responses (~250–500 words) that were 4% each. Because there were readings and SAQs for every lecture, you got to choose which ones you answered which was nice.

Great distro easy–A class! recommended!! grading is 5 reading response, 10 short answer, and participation (pollev) the grading is good, if you can finish the essays in the first 3–4 weeks, you are basically done with this course also, the lecture is fun!
This course is super chill. Space out the assignments correctly and u will have no work for most of the year. Easy class as well and very interesting

this class used to be optional attendance and only 3 short answer responses. Now attendance is mandatory and has a geotracker, and there are 10 short answer responses (250–500 words) and 5 long responses (>750 words). Take a more interesting distro, because the short answer responses are a pain and so not worth it for how boring they were. Class is still easy but is a nuisance.

This course it an easier distro. No exams, you're just graded on 5 reading response papers, 10 short answer questions, and participation (answering poll everywhere questions in class). So definitely an easy class to do well in and not too time consuming. I did this class on top of 4 others, and didn't feel overwhelmed.
i was able to focus more on sections and assignments that interested me more than others
cool class, pretty chill A
Professor Schieber is incredibly kind, intelligent, and understanding. He makes the content fascinating and engaging. I learned a lot knowing nearly nothing and it was pretty interesting.
Good class and engaging lecturer

What an easy and fun class!! Please take Socio 110 with Prof Schieber. He is a great professor and lectures are very fun. You have to submit at least 5 response paper of at least 750 words each and you choose to work on your most interesting topic you want among many. There is so much flexibility to succeed in this course. Participation is 20% of your total score. You get full score if you show up to lecture or miss at least one lecture in the quarter.

Great distro! Super interesting class without being stressful at all. You have to do five reading response papers (you pick what days you want to submit them for), 10 short–answer questions, and get graded via PollEverywhere for participation. Take this class!

This class changed from previous years. A tad bit more work but overall not that time/effort consuming. Assignments are reasonable and lectures are very interesting. Make sure to do the assignments throughout the quarter or else they will pile up at the end.

I enjoyed the class and its low maintenance in work, the readings were interesting and manageable, and I would read the bulletin in terms of writing about the readings, since they do expect certain questions to be answered. The attendance was low, but everything is self–explanatory. Really good class, easy A
Good class.

Attendance does make up 20% of the grade, but you can still front–load your reading responses and short answer questions after lectures to get the majority of your points by halfway through the quarter. Lectures are interesting.
N/A
Very nicely paced course! The workload is fairly light and the texts and course content are quite interesting. They relate to important phenomena within our everyday lives.
The lectures were well prepared with informative information that helped me understand the readings better especially when writing the essays!
Very easy class–––the assignments were pretty cumbersome but Prof. Schieber is very passionate about what he teaches. Lectures were fine, just show up and do the assignments and you'll be good.
This course is great. The grading is extremely fair, you just have to make sure that you stay vigilant with the short answer questions.
Very simple format. Low stress which is great because of no midterm and final. Have to go to class because of the Poll questions.

I felt the structure of the course made sociology 110 very poor. Having assigned readings that are 30+ pages, just to "skim" in my opinion makes understanding sociology very difficult. The class' grading scheme also promotes putting in as little effort as possible. In the future, shorter readings with more intentioned assignments would promote more student participation.
Easy A and a really interesting class! I would recommend if you want to take an easy distro, or if you have a difficult schedule and you want an easy class.
It's a really easy class with a really engaging professor. Just show up, don't fall 8 weeks behind and you'll be ok.
This course was not hard, the timelines are very flexible in terms of due dates as long as you can space out and time out your work so you are not in a crunch.
Great class! Interesting topics and low amount of work. Easy A

This class is great! The professor replies to emails super fast and gives helpful feedback. Makes sure to post future assignments so students can manage homework on their schedule. Lectures are not boring and interactive. If you can take this class, take it!

There are 5 reading responses and then 10 short answer. You can do 5 of your choice at any time but do have to submit it before the start of class for the ones you pick, and then the 10 short at anytime before the end of the quarter
I love this class!! You get out what you put in– would recommend 100%. Professor is super sweet and flexible on top of built–in flexibility of the course.

This class was good. If you need it for a distro, definitely take it, or if you just need a class to fill for a busy quarter, then this is the class. You just have to attend lectures because participation(poll questions) is part of the grade, as well as 5 reading responses that you choose when to do them, and 10 short answer questions. Professor Schieber is good; he is very enthusiastic about class.

There are no finals or midterms for this class but attendance is mandatory and part of your grade. There is a check in for every class and it tracks your location to make sure you are actually in class. You have to complete 10 short answer and 5 response papers based on the readings that are due at the end of the quarter. I waited to do them all at the last week of the quarter which wasn't smart but it defiantly is possible to do.
prof is really chill
Not a bad class to take but if you're looking for an easy distro or elective this one does have a lot of homework that isn't always a guaranteed 100%
Good class
Very easy class, you do have to show up as attendance is taken but the coursework is both easy and very interesting!
This class can be very simple, if you want it to be. The lectures are interesting, so going to class isn’t hard. Just do the readings and get ahead on work instead of behind.
easy A! some contents are quite interesiting

Really appreciated how this class was formatted. 5 essays and 10 SAQs to complete more or less whenever you want throughout the quarter. My quarter was the first in which they implemented mandatory attendance through location based PollEverywhere, but lecture topics are interesting and David is clearly enthusiastic so it is completely manageable.
I loved this course !

I enjoyed this course! I only took it because the CTECs said it would be an easy A, and that basically was what it was. The class was pretty interesting for a field I knew truly nothing about going in, and Schieber is a good lecturer. The homework is not that hard and graded pretty leniently, but you do have to go to lecture because there's an attendance aspect to the grade. Don't procrastinate like I did on the assignments – get it done as soon as possible or at least pace yourself. Overall, this is a good class to take as a distro.

This class has a lot of content, but you aren't expected to learn all of it. You just need to do 5 readings, 10 short answers, and show up to class for a guaranteed 100%. Some topics are interesting, and it differs per person, so just engage in whatever interests you. Overall the class is pretty chill, and I probably out in more effort than was necessary to get an A.
I got to learn the fundamentals of sociology in an engaging manner

source_file: Student Report for Manohar Kulkarni (GEN_ENG_205-3_23  Engineering Analysis III)_41d0fcd3-fe27-4e4b-919f-54b48b1226e5en-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: EA3 is just EA3, it's not the most fun but also not the most easy. Just get over with it early and never think of it again. I doubt anything done in this course will be very useful in the future.

Prof. Kulkarni is not a great professor, and EA 3 is not a great course. Kulkarni is not very good at teaching, as he mostly just reads out slides and makes us do example problems. I, and many other students, didn't go to class and instead learned from the textbook. The textbook is okay, but every professor teaches the course differently, so there are some things on the exams that don't show up in the textbook. The homework assignments were pretty tedious and difficult, especially the MATLAB parts. Past CTECs said Kulkarni gives easy tests, but I didn't find them very easy, and the class averages were pretty low. The tests were a lot harder than the practice tests. Overall, a pretty boring, tedious, and not very useful course.
This was a requirement for me. The class itself is pretty good and the instruction is not too fast.

The class is pretty straightforward, 75% of the grade is based on 3 exams and 25% from homework. The exams were different for each section. Professor Kulkarni gave pretty easy exams but was not a great lecturer. The lectures were often confusing or had errors and frequently skipped concepts that were asked about on the homework. If you want to have a solid understanding of the material, I would recommend taking the class with a different professor.
Alright class, read the wiki and practice tests

I like the dynamic systems and the way they're presented in EA3, makes relatively complicated ideas more managable (professor didn't teach half of it, though, I went to every lecture and am certain I understand EA3 about a third as well as any student with a different professor)
This class is great and so was this professor, he went through clear examples in class to demonstrate what would be on the exams and was very helpful for regrades
I liked the lectures.

Professor Kulkarni does not teach this course well. I went to about 2 lectures and decided to go to a better lecturer, and learned significantly more. Additionally, previous CTECS made it seem like the quizzes were easy. They were not easy for us this year, unfortunately. Would recommend taking with a different professor to learn more because the things they teach you in EA–3 is pretty important.

Pretty easy exams, show up to class to understand the variables that he uses, but you can also go to other lectures if it is hard to understand. Kulkarni is very wise on the topic, but when having to simplify a topic to its roots, it is sometimes hard to explain, since at some point, these topics are just engraved in the brain. Weekly homework can get tough, but not the end of the world.

Wow. Where to begin. I am writing this on the friday of finals week and we took our final a week ago and still have not heard back about it. Kulkarni is all over the place and his class is really hard to learn from and study for. If you have the chance, just take it with Keys. The grading for this class was a mess, the TAs are incredibly inconsistnet with each other and would oftentimes add up the points wrong or flat out take off more points for no reason. I have no idea where my grade stands currently and I am scared to find out. Kulkarni has also said some very questionable things in class.
I hated this class, would have helped I actually went to lectures probably but the people around me usually seemed more confused from lectures than before. Join a pgsg if you want to pass.

it's a little bit impressive how little professor kulkarni's lectures enabled me to learn the material in this class. he makes his tests easy, especially if you go to literally any other lecturer and learn the material from them. what allowed me to succeed in this class was (1) the textbook (2) having my friends in keys' lecture teach me the content (3) keys' lectures. i learned more in EA2 in alarcon's class when i was asleep 40 minutes out of 50 (for comparison, i went to all of kulkarni's lectures and was awake in all of them until after quiz 2). i gained very little in the way of understanding from many of his lectures, as his examples were just showing answers to questions on a slideshow; this does not present concepts in a way that allow them to be internalized. to be fair, the difficulty of his tests are proportionate to the amount he teaches.
Kulkarni didn't really teach but he's a very nice guy and is very understanding if you talk to him. Just be prepared to teach yourself

This is the most professor dependent class I have taken, it is so unconsistent/unstandardized that you will learn different things in each class. Kulkarni is not a good lecturer but his tests were easy (so if you learn best by yourself take this for the tests). His tests felt like they were made last second since it was just all hand drawn. This was an awful experience honestly. I had to learn by myself most of the content, and the thing is that the textbook doesn't even cover everything you need to know (usually its the opposite way around where the textbook has too much detail .. nope). This class is just a mess and a horrifying frankstein monster consisting of EA4 with newtonian mechanics thrown in (which we learned in EA2 yet it was more dumbed down here somehow). Geniunely do not understand how this class was intended to be. Taking EA4 honors at the same time somehow made this quarter easier because it explained the why behind what we were learning. Dynamics and Differential Equations (EA4) are the same thing, so I don't see why they tried to seperate them, you can't. Oh and the HWs oml. Each MATLAB assignment was the same thing, it was using eulers approximation method. Its a copy paste each time with just more and more graphs it got annoying. You don't learn much from them at all, especially the true and false ones. T/F and multiple choice are terrible tools to learn actual material. The end got cool by making a comparision between mechanical and electrial systems, but it wasn't explored that much (we just learned the equivalence not like why its useful lol).

If you want to WASTE thousands of tuition money to teach yourself EA3, feel free to take this class with Kulkarni. I mean its general knowledge that he's a horrible professor but he has easy tests right? WRONG. Our class avg for tests were like a full 10 points below other professor's class, and he likes to add things that he didn't teach on the tests. After comparing kulkarni and keys tests, I feel like the difficulty is similar, but you gotta teach yourself everything in kulkarni. At our last exam, bro claims that everyone's going to get a 100, but when we came out of the room, everyone looked like they've been drowned at the lakefill 10 times.

His grading on exams are also terrible. They barely give partial credits, take more points off then they should, and the 2 TAs have vastly different grading policies (resulting in very different grades) but Kulkarni doesn't seem to gaf about that. The regrading policy is mysterious too. You just hand them the paper and pray to god. If you get points back, amazing! If you get more points off, they don't even explain why or give you the papers back.

Also, I swear this man is the slowest talking being on planet earth because even if you put the recorded lectures on 2x speed, he still talks at the speed of a sloth in monotone. The slowness makes the lectures completely not understandable and unnecessarily confusing. I mean his lectures are so bad that at one point only 3 people showed up to class. I WOULD feel bad for him if he had actually tried to teach us. But he seems so unpassionate about this class! Near the last 2 weeks of the quarter, he claims that there's some emergencies in the ME department to be taken care of, so bro just mysteriously disappeared and canceled class multiple times with no specific details on why. No lectures were recorded, no slides were uploaded, we simply just didn't learn anything in those days. It just seems so unprofessional to me.

In all fairness, I do think the material is totally self teachable, so you are not completely screwed. But then again, why do we even have a professor in the first place if im teaching myself? Atp, northwestern should pay me his paycheck. So if you are taking EA3 with Kulkarni, you better start reading the EA3 wiki page like its the bible, or better, just go to prof keys lectures.
Me and everyone else took this class for the easy exams but in every quiz we had the lowest mean by far.
This course itself was not too difficult, but my professor seemed lost during every lecture. He did not adequately explain concepts, and even did not teach some of the material in the course.

Prof Kulkarki is super nice but he's not the best lecturer. After the 1st week, there were max 20 students attending. However, if you go to office hours for the homeworks and read the webtext you'll be more than fine. His exams aren't exactly easy As but easier than the homeworks
Kulkarni is chill, but I kinda went to Key's as well. Kulkarni does cover some slightly different material than keys, so make sure to read Kulkarni's slides if you do choose to go to keys instead

Do NOT take this course with Kulkarni. He simply does not teach. Lectures consist of nothing but him absent–mindedly trying to explain pre–worked examples. Everything I learned in this course I learned by independently studying the web–textbook.

Kulkarni is not a good professor. Only about 8 people in the class went to lectures. I only know that from the one time that I went in the middle of the quarter.

Everyone else either goes to another section or relies on the textbook. Personally, I was able to watch Lynch's lectures on my friend's Panopto, which worked well.

However, the tests are pretty easy relative to other classes (mostly because his students lag behind).

The lectures were very haphazard and unstructured. They did not feel engaging and many people skipped class to go to other professors. Then the midterms also did not match the level of teaching as they were harder and did not connect well to what we did in class.
it was ok, lectures and homeworks were ok

Kulkarni is a really friendly guy, but you might not learn very much about EA from him. His tests are much easier than the other profs though; I highly recommend taking him but only if you can go to Keys' lecture.

This was not great. Kulkarni is a very nice person but his lectures were impossible to follow. His tests were fine but the administration of the course was awful thanks to him and the TA. On multiple occasions, the key to the exam was incorrect. More than half of the class had their exams graded wrong, and that has yet to be fixed. Kulkarni may be an easier teacher but that doesn't mean you can count on an A because of the incompetence with grading.
I learned how to think through mechanical and electrical systems.
Professor Kulkarni was kind of confusing and didn't seem to be on pace with the other instructors or the homeworks. He taught by going through a lot of conceptual true/false type questions.
i learned differential equations which is good

I attended about half of the lectures before I gave up going, and quite frankly it was a waste of time. I mostly learned off of the e–text and videos, asProf Kulkarni really didn't do much for me. I mean take him if you have to but try to get another prof if you can.

Don't review his CTECs from last year. He's still a terrible lecturer but instead of giving fair/"easy" exams he now just gives terrible exams. Unless this is the last spot left, don't take the class with Kulkarni.
I felt like the material learned in this class was easier than ea 1 & 2, but it was taught in a less fluent manner.
This course was hard. Especially with Kulkarni as a professor. The information is more difficult than EA2.

Took Kulkarni cause previous–year CTECs said he was easier, although a bad prof. First midterm of the year, Kulkarni's midterm was substantially harder than every other professors (average lower by 10–20%). Nice guy, not a great prof, bad exam writer. The "wiki" (textbook) teaches better than sitting in class, but it doesn't cover all the material that is discussed in class, so you have to pick your poison of which you use (class or textbook), unless you want to have a massive time commitment.
I found the content at the beginning fine, but when we got closer to the end they seemed to skip over fundamental principles and ideas but trying to compare it to what was previously taught.

so useless and annoying, was not even physics it was just math. the structure of the course feels all over the place and overall just not good. do not take with kulkarni bc he's a shit lecturer. also grading is so wack because the TAs grade so differently. like one was so much harsher than the other and my TA did double jeopardy so i had to go and argue back points with kulkarni.
pretty annoying class but there isn't a super large amount of material to learn

Take this course with anyone else. The previous CTECs said that the quizzes were easy so take the course with Kulkarni and go to a different lecturer like Keys for the instruction. The quizzes this year were actually pretty difficult and we had the lowest averages among any professors so take that as you will.
This course isn't bad, but the lectures did not help learn the content in the slightest. Read the webtext
Kulkarni

Kulkarni has the easier tests, but is not the clearest instructor. Go to another teacher's lecture if you have difficulty understanding the core concepts. This year the exams were fairly easy, however, the last exam was a little off script. But, overall don't regret taking this class with him.

source_file: Student Report for YIZE PAN (GEN_ENG_205-2_20  Engineering Analysis  II)_e7f81f2d-3451-468c-8571-e266ee6303f1en-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: Took this as a sophomore because of unclear major requirements. If you've taken any physics and are comfortable with mechanics, this will be a breeze. Its statics so everything has to equal zero. Make sure you take the time to learn trusses and frames though. Those can be deceptively tricky.

The class content itself statics is nothing special, but Professor Yize Pan made me rate the class much higher. Even though this was his first year teaching, and it was apparent more towards the beginning of the year as his teaching style would switch from the whiteboard one day, the projector another, the chalkboard the other, his passion for teaching and his students was the most I've ever encountered from any of my professors at Northwestern. He was very receptive to our feedback and I honestly felt like the quality of teaching to me at least had improved a lot by the end of the year. Even though this class was a large lecture hall type class Professor Pan made it feel like a very small and personalized class, learning many of the students names by the end of the quarter and connecting directly to students when teaching, instead of dryly giving lectures. The exams were very fair and if you did the homework fairly and enough textbook practice problems there were no major surprises.

This course really wasn't my cup of tea. First of all I have to say that Yize Pan is an amazing person. He comes across as a really nice guy that wanted us to do well and learn in the class. The amount of effort he puts in is evident and he wants everyone in the class to contribute and learn. Unfortunately, he isn't a good teacher and I spent a lot of time learning the material by watching Youtube videos. Most times he shows us a paper with answers on it that he worked on before. People just copy it down while he throws in some occasional explanations. Also he can have difficulty understanding questions sometimes which made me reluctant to ask questions.

I definitely enjoyed EA1 more than EA2. EA2 wasn't particularly exciting and sometimes it just felt like number crunching where you just plug in numerous values into a calculator to find your answer. The hardest part of this course was keeping track of the numerous calculations you had to do.

I was told that EA2 was the easiest EA coming into this class, but I was far better at EA1.

As an individual, Professor Pan is one of the nicest and most approachable professors I've met on campus. I've talked to him a couple times in office hours, and I can tell that he truly does everything in his power to help his students succeed. If you attend every single one of his lectures, you will have done very well in this class. My only regret this quarter was not attending more of Prof. Pan's lecture.

Nevertheless, I strongly believe that Prof. Pan is an incredible asset to Northwestern and should continue teaching more classes in the Gen_Eng / Engineering Analysis sequence, especially in the trailer sequence. Those who take the trailer sequence, often need additional support, including myself, which is why I appreciate the effort that Prof. Pan puts in to teaching this class.

To date, I've never bothered to write a CTEC that was longer than 1 sentence, but Prof. Pan is truly worthy of this praise.

If Prof. Pan is teaching again take it with him! Although he's only a post–doc I can say that he's definitely above average of all professors from my past 2 years at Northwestern. The lectures are extremely organized, and the way he introduces new concepts is intuitive and great for problem–solving. Professor Pan is also genuinely a nice person and cares about his students a lot. It'll be a shame if he stops teaching..

Absolutely disliked how the course was weighted. It made it feel like all the work was pretty much useless since the final was worth 45% of the entire grade. Even if you were successful for the entirety of the course up to the second midterm, you could lose it all in the final.
EA2 is definitely a challenging class and you have to put the work in to do well. Attend every lecture, go to office hrs, and practice practice practice
I was very surprised at the length faculty will go to aid in your success in the class.

Go into this course with the thought that everything needs to equal 0 or in other words be in equilibrium. A lot of the class, it is just solving for unknowns for which you will have equations. As long as you have as many equations as unknowns, you should be able to answer. Other than this general comment, it was very enjoyable class if you invest time in it.

Yize Pan was a fantastic professor – if you ever get the chance to take a class with him, definitely do. He explains things in a very understandable way, is very kind, and clearly wants you to succeed. Overall the class was tough because it's mostly exam based, but take it with Professor Pan if you can

Lectures are good, and worth going to. Practice problems are provided, but the answers are not unless you go to lecture, which effectively forces you or one of your friends to go to lecture. Though, I think this is not entirely a bad idea, as I feel like I get a much better handle of what we are covering when I attend lecture instead of reading lecture notes. The class gets curved to a B, which isn't the best, but the exams are fair, and representative of the content we learn in class. Yize seems like he cares about teaching the content to students, and he seems passionate about helping us succeed.

This course is rather challenging but there's no way around it since it's a McCormick requirement. My best recommendation is to take it with a good and enthusiastic professor who can provide constant support throughout the quarter.

I think that the homework are very helpful if you do them by yourself and try your best to figure out what to do.

Also, recitations are insanely helpful!!! I know they are necessarily required, but its really the best way to learn and get help during your problem solving process. I never went to office hours. I never felt that I needed them, because usually the recitations would cover everything I would've asked during OH.
We love Yize Pan. Best pan on google.
Ugh. If you do enough practice you will understand it.

This is a very doable course, but to do well, here are some things that you should absolutely do: – Get a calculator that can solve systems of equation, cross product, and cross product. Trust me, this will save you a lot of time on the exams. – Do as much practice as you can – the examples in class and the homework are extremely relevant to the material that shows up on exams. Other that, this was a good course. Professor Yize was extremely kind and straightforward in his teaching style.
I've always been weak in regards to physics so I didn't like it that much. I wish this course explained a lot more of why things are the way they are (like zero force trusses)

Prof Pan is so sweet and kind, but he is definitely new to the realm of teaching. This often just means that you pretty much just teach yourself all the material. Be prepared for the exams to be much harder than practice material. The final is very very hard and is where most students have their grades drop to a B.

EA2 goes quickly, but is satisfying to keep up with, as most of the concepts tend to lean on each other. This also means it's extremely important to keep up with every topic as they come along. Make sure to start the projects early, as the actual math/work is not difficult, but writing the report can be very time consuming. The course is really as difficult as you make it.
Pan is a really nice person. Explanations can be a bit too fast. He does answer questions in class and there are a ton of practice problems. The projects aren't very time consuming.

I was never a physics person. However, the way Yize Pan taught this class made it really memorable for me and gave me the extra motivation to try my best to succeed in this class beyond what I thought was possible. At the end of the class, I really learned a lot and am grateful for this opportunity. retook after a year because last time i got curved down below the cutline–

the grading scheme of this class is not great at all the fact that a single question on the final exam is worth AS MUCH AS a 10+ page paper does not make sense to me the disparity of the effort just does not make a lot of things in this class feel worth it

Professor Pan truly cares about his students. By the end of the course, he knew every single student's name in the class. He is dedicated and patient, and takes the time to ensure students understand the material thoroughly. His notes are also very readable. I am very grateful to have him as a professor. Even though the course is challenging, if you consistently read the textbook and attend his lectures, you will gain a thorough understanding of the material.
Personally, the math seemed a little easier to me, especially since most of it is so physics based. The matlab projects are long but simple code, so it really comes down to a time management thing.

I liked EA. Yize Pan was a great lecturer and made everything fair. The homework, projects, and midterms were all doable and it was not very hard. The only thing I did not enjoy was that we could not access lectures, you have to attend in person, but you could have asked to take pictures of the notes after.
Professor was amazing and if you go to class you will do well in the class
Yize is a great teacher! He is so sweet and ready to help with any questions.
tests were harder than what “trailer” was made out to be, but yize is great
It is pretty straighforward physics statics. Difficult exams but overall a solid class.
It's a great course. The professors and TAs tried their best in making this course more flexible and accessible for all. I really wants to thank them.
if you actualy do the homework and understand it the midterms and final should be easy and doable.
The grades being curved sucks, especially because so many horrid people just serially cheat, meaning that the midterms are harder to do relatively well on.

This class was just tough. The midterms and final were hard, and there was a ton of emphasis placed on the fact that the grade distributions in this class would be the same as the non trailer sections. This put a lot of pressure on students and I was constantly comparing myself to median. Class is curved to a B, so few students will get an A.

The grading system is incredibly odd, and honestly unfair:10% homework, 10% projects, 15% midterm 1, 20% midterm 2, 45% final. The midterms are only 50 minutes, which is not very much time. You have to know exactly what you're doing immediately, or you may suffer. The course itself is fine, just physics and linear algebra. Your professor matters A LOT.
Not bad, need to do well on tests though. Study!

In terms of the class difficulty, I found this class to be slightly easier than the main section of EA2. The midterms were extremely fair and straightfoward, and I highly recommend attending class because some of the problems in the exams are very similar to those done in class. What makes no sense about this class is the two design projects that take multiple days to complete, just for them to be worth only 10 percent of the grade, and the HW's also only being 10 percent. At the same time, theres a final exam worth 45 PERCENT of the grade, which is just ridiculous and can seriously knock your grade if you don't do well. In terms of the teaching, Yize Pan is an average professor, but he's probably the nicest professor I've had in this school by a wide margin and clearly cares for his students, though I think he's not teaching it next year. The other professor for this course was Shady Gomaa and I attended some of his lectures and thought he was fantastic. Overall though it's just another ea class; just get through it

This course was just fine. Prof Yize Pan always bought an enthusiastic and joyful energy to the classroom. The content was difficult to grasp at times, but the textbook is quite helpful. Also, videos from "Jeff Hanson – statics" are very helpful!

For an EA course, EA 2 really wasn't that bad. Prof Pan is a super sweet and funny guy. He definitely cares about his students, and he made an effort to learn the names of everyone that came to class. I'm not sure if he'll be teaching this class again, but if he does, take it with him! The exams could be challenging, but they are always super fair and similar to the homework. Super reasonable class.

source_file: Student Report for Ryan Bethel (CHEM_151-0_03  General Chemistry I)_02c406e1-3329-42c1-b2a1-162acc62fd1fen-US.pdf

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: Professor Bethel is such a good lecturer and his slideshows offer succinct summarizations of the textbook. If you need more explanation though of the content, I would recommend going to office hours of the TAs or of Professor Bethel, or reading the textbook.
I thought this course was hard but manageable. I had a lot of other difficult classes at the same time as this, so dedicating time to spend doing hw and studying was difficult.

Dr. Bethel is great, so I'd recommend taking it with him.

I took basic chemistry online in tenth grade and didn't remember any of it, yet I still got placed into this class instead of 110–131. I asked my advisor to ask the chemistry department about this they said they think their test is accurate so I should be in this class. That is such a load of crap. There is no way I should've been in this class. The only thing the placement test tests is whether you can do simple math. This class was incredibly hard for me. I switched from being a Material Science and Engineering major to an IE/Econ double because I realized I hate chemistry. I didn't drop the class because it's still a science credit, but it screwed up my GPA. Oh well, good learning experience to talk about in interviews I guess. It's probably not as bad if you took AP Chemistry. Good luck.
It's not like you have a choice for this class, its just gen chem so just take it

I thought the course was overall not too bad. It had a decent amount of work but it was manageable. If you took AP chem you should be in a good place. The lectures were probable the most important and useful thing about the course. I didn’t buy the textbook and was still able to get through the course. If you go to class and pay attention the class is honestly not that hard.

Taking Chem 151 as a freshman was formidable challenge, as expected due to its rigorous nature. Succeeding in this course demands a significant investment of time and effort, and even with diligent commitment, many students drop after the first midterm. Achieving a high letter grade is an uphill battle, but attaining an A is not an impossible.

The course structure encompasses quizzes, in–class participation questions, and homework (which is a helpful free 100). Additionally, there are two midterms and a final, each carrying escalating weight in the overall assessment. To excel in Chem 151, it's imperative to contribute substantial hours outside of class to thoroughly cover the material briefly summarized in lectures through slides. Professor Bethel is a funny dude, but you barely learn in actual class time. I would say that the majority of the

4/13 learning exists outside the classroom.

In summary, this class extremely difficult, and unless you genuinely like chemistry or need to take it to fill a requirement, don't take it. Plus, you will have to pay almost a $100 for a website subscription in order to do the homework.

Overall, Bethel is a decent teacher. Signing up for a study group was super helpful for me since they do go very fast in the lecture –– I downloaded the slides and wrote notes directly onto them. The achieve homework is typically not too hard, but I would recommend doing it by Wednesday so you can ask questions in recitation on Thursday.

This class is a review from AP chem at a quicker, faster, and less in depth way. This class takes a lot of time from your schedule and going to office hours were key in order to succeed in this class. Bethel recommends signing up for the Peer Study and honestly those session are a hit or miss depending on who your peer guide is. The class is very difficult and you will need to put in a lot of time and effort.

I thought the course was taught well until the last two weeks. We spent 2–3 weeks on quantum mechanical topics that I thought could have been used much more beneficially at the end of the course, instead of being inundated with way too much information that I couldn't really synthesize.
This class was hard, and takes a lot of outside work to understand the topics. I can’t say I enjoyed it because I honestly hate chemistry but it was okay.

This class wasn't as bad as I thought it was going to be. It's definitely fast–paced and challenging, but the workload during the week is not that bad. There's weekly quizzes, which aren't too bad as long as you pay attention during lecture, and the homework is a good practice tool. The midterms and final are definitely hard and often not exactly a great representation of what we practiced in class. There's not any way to study for them because a lot of times they involve types of questions we've never seen before. Professor Bethel is really great and does his best to make the content interesting. This class also takes attendance which can be annoying.
This course is hard yes, but it is also completely manageable if you go into it with the right mindset.

The course was very stressful and difficult. The course feels extremely jammed packed and doesn't really seem fit or in line with the quarter system. It is extremely "weed out" and difficult. It takes an abnormal amount of time and also is extremely stressful.
This course does a really good job of going into depth on all of the concepts taught. I especially enjoyed how we went into MO bonding theory as I had never heard about it before.

Professor Bethel is great. He is very knowledgeable, good at explaining/answering questions, and cares about the performance of his students. We complained about the iClickers (in–class 1 minute questions worth points) after the first midterm, and he made them only for attendance points which was nice. The class is a lot of information very quickly. Keep up with the homework and go to every one of his office hours.

Bethel is a good professor for chemistry in general. He covers the material pretty well but the course can be quite fast, but that's just the nature of a chemistry class. The iClicker questions were pretty useful and the quizzes aren't too bad either. Bethel is good at taking feedback from students as well regarding the structure of the course, so that was helpful. Overall, a very manageable class.

Professor Bethel was a nice guy and a good teacher, but the course is hard. I think that doing textbook problems and doing lots of review on the lecture materials specifically is important. The course is very fast paced and there are a lot of things skipped so I wouldn't come into it with no knowledge at all. Falling behind is not really an option, but being in a peer–guided study group really helped me.

This class is objectively hard. As someone who took AP Chem in high school, this class goes into more depth about topics that were simply touched on high school, and expects you to be able to apply the concepts that are discussed in lecture. You have to spend a lot of time practicing the application of different formulas, concepts, etc. Bethel does a good job not going too fast and covering everything in lecture, and giving plenty of example problems. Even so, you have to take the time outside of class to really understand the material, or you will do poorly on exams. There are two non cumulative midterms and one cumulative final.

This course is very very difficult and if you do not have a great background in chemistry and a natural strength, and are not putting in a significant amount of work before and after class to understand material, you WILL FALL BEHIND. This class is very very difficult, and if you are not confident in your abilities to do chemistry previously, take an easier course, or avoid this one entirely.
It is a lot of work but is doable. You have to focus and practice the content outside of class. It’s a difficult class overall

I think that this course in general will be difficult no matter what changes could be made to it. There is a tremendous amount of content and on top of that the quarter system makes it so that content is paced incredibly badly. Yet at the same time you're expected to have an indepth understanding of all concepts for the exam no matter how little it was touched on in class. There is a lot of time that would need to be dedicated to this class and even then it's quite difficult to excel. Other than that, the instructors care and try really hard to make the content digestable.

I felt like at points material was taught well and at other points taught very fast. I hated that aspect. Prof basically sped ran quantum before midterm 2 and thermodynamics and is currently whizzing through the last chapters before final. Midterms compared to last year were WAY better. very very fair. My only problem was really the time crunch during the exam but if you knew your stuff, it was good. They do take off points on little stuff so beware. Midterm 2 was awful though. they had us shoulder to shoulder for no reason and it got very hot in the middle of it all. office hours are helpful, i recommend. Super TA's are great. they lowkey sometimes mess

5/13 up when answering questions. I though bethel was good though in explaining. sometimes he doesnt answer questions very well in the sense that he'll answer with a more complicated but ig it is what it is. Acheive sucks in the beginning but gets better as quarter goes on; helpful for the quizzes but not so much the midterm. Lack of practice material too. Loved thursday discussions tho, the packet we worked through always helped.
Ryan was a good lecturer and his exams are written very fairly.

When approaching this course CTECs warned me to not take this course unless I absolutely had to and I would make the exact same comment. Do not take this course unless you absolutely have to. This course was extremely challenging. At first it appears as if the content is straightforward, but exam questions do not reflect this. There are weekly quizzes that I think help you stay on top of the material but took a lot of studying and time in office hours to prepare for. For midterms, we were given a practice exam before each one. This was nice to have, but we were not given a practice examination for the final exam, which I believe is where it matters the most. A majority of the final exam was on new material and yet we were not given material other than the homework that reflected what we would be asked on an exam. Each week we are given "suggested exercises," which I would do but I do not think were reflective of exam questions a majority of the time. Often, they would ask irrelevant questions with solutions that were ambiguous and not worked out in the book. I wish we were given more practice exams to study with because I often felt unprepared after hours of studying the suggested exercises and homework we were given. I think in class content was rushed over, which made comprehending the information difficult. I appreciated being given the slides in advance, but I think it is important to not that we learned 3 new chapters of content between the week of Thanksgiving and our final. Not to mention, we had required class during Weinberg's reading week where participation was worth points with iClicker questions. Overall, this course was extremely challenging and no matter how many hours of work I put in, nothing made it easier.
The course was challenging and the tests were hard.
Chem 151 is definitely a difficult course, even with an AP Chem background. Read the textbook, do practice problems, and sign up for a peer guided study group if you can.

This class is difficult. While a background in AP Chem helps immensely, there is still plenty of new content that you will have to learn multiple times over in order to be able to apply on exams. Doing the homework and staying on pace is the most important advice I can give.
I wish that there was slightly more points awarded for the weekly quizzes, but that's my only comment.

The course is extremely fast paced so it is hard to keep up with the material. It is up to the student to review the given material, which feels a lot like one teaching themselves. Though Bethel is extremely nice in office hours, he's not exactly honest about how to prepare for midterms and the final. It is up to the student to really prepare for the exams because they are often really hard.

This course starts off difficult if you have little to no Chemistry knowledge. The 1st midterm is a little rough but content gets easier. I can say that it is manageable to get a good grade even if you had no background knowledge. A lot of time needs to be put into it if you want to master the material.
The course is challenging, but if you keep up and go to office hours its bearable.

Really really hated this class, and I don't recommend if it isn't absolutely necessary for your major! Material and midterms were SO difficult and there's stuff on the midterms that wasn't even covered in class, so I was confused as to how I was supposed to know the answers. Really didn't like the professor because in the beginning of the quarter, the participation points are based on how accurate you are on the iClicker, which was the most stressful thing ever. He did eventually change it to just participation only which I was glad about. Professor talks a lot and it's really hard to pick out what is important and it's also hard to pay attention in class. Even quizzes were harder than what was covered. Only good thing about this course was that the lecture videos are posted on canvas so if you're confused during class you can just rewatch the lectures. Also, every Thursday they make you attend mandatory recitation practices where they give you practice problems. Problems are so so hard and aren't even taught in lectures and then those same problems show up on quizzes and exams. Super TAs are not helpful during recitations, and general TAs didn't even know anything even when I went to office hours. Another annoying thing was that there were students in class who asked the most random questions that were barely related to the content that just wasted a lot of time that could've been spent on covering more examples. Chem 151 is extremely difficult and convoluted. Take it with a different professor.

It was very well taught, I personally didn't feel the need to read the textbook and I did fine in the class. I really like the teacher he explains things well and goes over all of the necessary topics.

This class, to be blunt, is definitely a lot of work. At least if you hope to do well on the exams, there is no real way to not study and do well. I took AP Chemistry in high school as well as organic chemistry, so I came into the class with a good foundation of the topics covered. That said, I still committed a LOT of time outside of the classroom doing readings, completing homework, and doing my own outside study to fully grasp the material. Professor Bethel is a great professor and he kept his lectures somewhat fun (as fun as a morning chemistry lecture can be), and was always more than willing to answer any questions necessary. If you take this class, you should definitely be prepared to do a lot of work for it. That said, it is an interesting class, and with the right work is definitely maintainable.
This is a challenging course, particularly at the end of the quarter. Professor Bethel is a good lecturer.
Very challenging in the final parts of the quarter. Prof Bethel is a very good lecturer.

It is hard.

6/13

The course was pretty much what I expected. I had learned some of the chemistry that we worked on in high school, but some other topics were completely new to me. The lectures and notes slides really helped me when I went back to review information that I needed a better grasp on.
The course truly challenges you and I recommend spending time outside of lectures to make sure one has a solid understanding.
This course is terrible if you aren't already really interested in chemistry, but if you're in mech e you have to take it so have fun.

Yes, teacher is very supportive and has a fun approach to chemistry. The class could be a little fast paced but it's alright if you just pay attention and take the time to study. This course is not an easy A, if you want to do well you need to put in the work. But there are resources for you like office hours and study groups so take advantage of them

This course is very difficult and challenging! It is super fast–paced and we go through content very fast! Would not recommend it as a distribution course there are easier courses. The content in unnecessarily hard. Try and do pre–readings of the content before each class because it is easy to fall behind. Bethel is a good professor but the courseload is too much.

This class is organized well but if you've never taken chemistry before be prepared, it moves wayyy too fast. And anticipating exams, especially the second midterm and final is incredibly difficult. Figure out how you learn best early on because if you let questions build up it will be too late. Actual homework assignments (with the exception of a few weeks) was manageable but didn't do a lot to prepare for exams (it was however very helpful for weekly quizzes). There is also no reading week in this class (contrary to what sources will tell you) because you will still be learning content. This class is very intense to say the least.

Professor Bethel is such a nice professor. He is willing to help students understand the classroom material. This course was challenging but going to the office hours and spending time on my own made it doable. It would advise a student to always do the suggested exercises, challenge questions on the recitation worksheet, and the weekly homework to be successful in this course.

This is a tough class. The exams are extremely hard as well. That being said, the homeworks, although they are lengthy, really help you learn the material, and the lessons go at a good pace that allow you to grasp what they are saying.

Chem 151 is a much more sped up version of AP Chem plus certain new concepts that weren’t taught in high school. Since the course is only 10 weeks, entire chapters fly by in just one lecture, requiring you to spend a lot of time outside of class trying to fully learn new concepts. The class is pretty hard, so I would NOT recommend taking this course unless you have to for your major. Homework makes up about 15% of the grade, while weekly quizzes make up 15% of the grade. Midterm 1 and Midterm 2 are both around 20% and 25% of the final grade. While the remaining 30 ish% is based on the Final. There are 6 quizzes, but one quiz grade can be dropped. Professor Bethel is a nice professor who is passionate about Chemistry. He seems to care about his students and encourages them to attend his office hours, which are helpful. He teaches in a clear and straightforward manner and makes chemistry a tad bit more exciting to learn. I would recommend taking this course with Bethel if you can. Overall Chem 151 is a pretty difficult course which is by far not an easy A. A lot of work is required to get an A in the class. If you do take this course, take it with Bethel.

This class was definitely challenging as I think it's not enough just to learn from lectures. I did a lot of learning on my own which was the main point that was difficult to adapt to. Exams are also a big combination of problem solving and synthesizing concepts, which at times was difficult. Dr. Bethel is a good lecturer, but as expected from any big class, it's hard to learn from the professor as he can't tailor the class to a smaller group.
