Chemistry is a hard topic in general, but it's definitely not impossible.

The class was definitely difficult but not impossible in any sense. Bethel was a great professor, and there was not a lot of external work involved with this class outside of studying. The actual homework was minimal.
Although this course was fast–paced, I definitely learned a lot. Many concepts were breezed past but were included on the exam, so I did have to do some learning on my own.

This course overloads you with information. You are forced to learn all of it and be able to recall on it when necessary or else you risk not being able to pass. If the right organizational skills are employed then it is very possible to retain the information you receive from start to finish of the course, however, you must stay disciplined and engaged with your coursework, and don't be afraid to rely on your TA
TRY to avoid this course by all means. IT IS SOOO HARD. and the instructors are just meh. i dont know but even after studying for days straight and pulling all nighters my life was just terrible.

This course is very hard, and time consuming. The course definitely will take up a lot of space on your schedule, and you must be prepared to not always do the best on the exams given. I feel as though even though the quarter system makes learning the material so much harder. The pacing is a lot and you're learning new things literally everyday. To me my AP chemistry from high school only helped so much because I still struggled with keeping up with the work. The best thing is that the homework assignments is like one a week but the homework has a lot of questions. The quizzes aren't as bad but they do throw in trick questions to throw you off. Studying can only do so much. GOOD LUCK!

This course was challenging but I did learn a good amount. Professor Bethel is not bad at instruction or lecturing, but it was challenging as there were a lot of time restraints on the content being taught in class. I feel like going through a whole unit (unit 14) in one day on the last day of class (during reading week) was a bit unfair especially because it was a lot of the content put into the final exam. But overall Bethel was a pretty good professor for 300 students so I would say to take it with him.

Overall a very solid course, even if you have never taken AP Chem like me. Professor Bethel is great at teaching and shows his

7/13 interest in the subject, and I think that the class is at a perfect difficulty level. You will spend much more time doing work for the lab section than the lecture section, and the labs don't always help you understanding of what you are doing in class at the moment, but they aren't too bad overall.

i think that the course was well handled and well taught. the exact content that we would be tested on would have been useful to know on major exams, but overall the class was as enjoyable as a chem class can be.

Lots of thoughts on this class. I’m a sophomore so I had friends who took this last year and still have trauma from it. Like it was horrible. So I was very scared coming into this and knew I’d have to put in a lot to get anything out. Thankfully, I think the professors really listened to people last year and have restructured the course somewhat. It’s still difficult, but definitely not the horrible CHEM 151 it was before. Exams are more manageable for sure. I’m doing well in the class and it’s really about how much time you put in. I review the slides, achieve, do the textbook problems, and make sure I actually understand everything. If I don’t get it, I go to office hours. Make sure you understand the concept bc no matter how much you try to just memorize, they’ll throw something at you that you’ll have to use your understanding of chemistry to solve. Good luck, you got this!
I knew that this course would be challenging going into it but I feel like there were a lot of small concepts in the lectures that were discussed briefly that showed up on exams.

This course may have taught me more than all my other courses combined – there is no way that this is designed to be taught all in one quarter. That being said, Professor Bethel was very instructive, and I genuinely enjoyed going to lecture every day.

Overall the course is not bad if you have taken some higher–level chemistry courses in high school, but the pace is still very fast. Multiple topics are covered in each lecture, and sometimes it can be difficult to keep up. I did very poorly on the first midterm because I tried to learn the material through the book and past exams, but they were not always the most helpful. I found that watching YouTube videos was much better than trying to rewatch lectures and reading the book.

I had no chemistry experience before this class, but it was introductory enough that it helped explain things, giving me a good foundation of chemistry. Sometimes concepts are not valued as much as just finding the answers to formulas, which is the only thing I think could be improved.

Chemistry is hard. There is no other way to say it. Professor Bethel is receptive to feedback but could also use a lot of improvement. That does not mean he is a bad professor, however, his midterms and final do not reflect what we spend the most time in class. We did not even get a practice final and had to take it knowing anything was fair game. Suffice to say it did not end well for many of us. Going to his office hours does help a ton, and I recommend reading the textbook before class. I would not have taken this class unless I had any other choice. You start feeling what a weed–out class means by the end of it, and I have never felt like a single class has lowered my standards as much as this one. If you are forced to take it, however, Bethel is not a bad choice.
I had to teach myself much of the content this quarter but was able to do so, it was just a pain.

As someone that likes to be ahead especially with the busy and rapid quarter system it almost felt like this class was surprised that they were going to have students. By that I mean at least for the first midterm, we waited until the day before the Midterm before the key for the practice was posted and right now as we prepare for the final, there have been no mentions of whether they will post one or not.

The workload for this course was extremely high. The readings were very heavy (20+ pages of textbook material every day), and the textbook was often quite difficult to understand (language–wise); many times, I found it easier to look up my concepts in a different textbook or online (e.g. Khan Academy). In addition, Dr. Bethel moved very fast with the content. He would give an overview of an entire chapter within an hour of lecture, leaving it to the student to do the majority of preparation and/or learning on their own. Also, there were many students who had taken AP Chemistry during high school and indicated that they had placed in a higher chemistry course (e.g. CHEM 171), but chose to take this course. For them, it seemed much easier. Also, this course required us to take an additional course as a co–requisite (CHEM 161), which also had a very high workload. Many times, I felt like I was taking 2 separate chemistry courses, and the link between the two courses was pretty weak.

Professor Bethel was very engaging. He had fun examples, made jokes that uplifted the class mood, had well–prepared lecture slides, etc. This class, however, is extremely difficult. In order to truly do well, you must attend all lectures, read all textbook chapters, and complete both achieve problems and bonus practice problems. Having other challenging courses this quarter, I could not do all of these things. My grade reflects that. Ultimately, you need to put a lot of time into this class to be successful, since it's graded on a curve and there's bound to be some Chemistry–lovers who spend their entire weekends pouring over the textbook. Since this course is generally a degree requirement, best of luck to future students. The class is hard. Prof. Bethel makes it as enjoyable as possible, so take the class with him if you can.
They focused on all the topic of chemistry that I needed to learn

The course will be challenging, and you must put in a lot of work. If things are not going well for you at the start, you need to find different ways to prepare for the class. I overall didn't really enjoy using the textbook besides the practice problems. I usually just found taking notes from the slides and rewatching the lectures helpful. Redoing problems from recitation and practice exams and midterms helps immensely in preparing for all your exams, so you should definitely do that. The class is not easy at all, and I think it will take time to adjust. Give yourself room to make mistakes, and don't beat yourself up after one bad grade. The course is cumulative, and you have various different exams to help show your learning. Also, reviewing a bit each day helps. This sounds obvious, but you would be surprised how time can slip by.

8/13
Chem 151 was a very interesting class. Having peers in the class helped me as it is a good class to have a study group with. IClicker was sometimes unfunctional

It was a really interesting class and was structured and taught very well. There is a lot of material covered and I would suggest taking notes on the slides, from the textbook, and going to office hours to succeed.

I thought this was overall a pretty good course. I feel confident on all the material without too much requirement to self–study outside of class time or weekly homework assignments. It was taught well and intuitively, although some of the course organization was a bit odd. It felt like we started with very challenging topics and then moved to very simple topics that would have been useful as foundational knowledge for the beginning of the course. This organization did somewhat make me feel like we were set up to fail in the first part of the course. Overall, it was an engaging course.

If it could have been avoided, I would not have taken this class again. The slideshows were helpful, but Bethel rambles in class sometimes about things that we don't necessarily need to know for the sake of the course itself. Quizzes and midterms are heavily reliant on the TAs for the quarter but the quizzes were mostly manageable. I felt I learned the most from the weekly Achieve homework. Bethel isn't an awful teacher though; he takes a survey after the first midterm and slightly adjusted how he formatted class based on the overall results.
I felt the class served as a type of review of past chem concepts, but did not introduce too many new.

I heard absolute horror stories about Chem–151 before I enrolled in this class. The class is definitely a lot of work, but honestly did not feel nearly as difficult as it is made out to be. If you have taken AP Chemistry before, a huge portion of the class is review, with only a few new concepts. I can definitely see this class being incredibly difficult if you have not had previous chemistry experience. If you think you might struggle in this class, I suggest reading the textbook chapters covered in lecture before each class. That being said, it seems like the difficulty of exams was toned down this year, as the class average for the first and second exam was around a 70, while last year the average was in the low 60s. Overall, Chem–151 with Dr. Bethel is a manageable class, and Bethel does a great job helping students understand chemistry.
I passed it and needed to for my major

This course was exactly what I was hoping for. I wanted to get an overview of chemistry to decide whether it would be worth doing, and I feel I know a lot better now what chemistry, at least historically, is all about. I definitely feel that the course helped me to learn that because, while I had seen a lot of the material before, every single topic when to a level of depth that I found meaningfully insightful, while (usually) not being so far down in the weeds that I couldn't come up for air. I found the discussion of bonding theories and crystalline geometry particularly interesting and fun. The thermodynamic topics were by far the least interesting, but they were usually still very easy.

The class is hard no doubt, but not that bad!! Just study and do practice problems, and put time into your studying and you will pass! I think that getting an A is also achievable, given the nature of this course, but make sure to use your resources well. Also if you take it with Bethel and he says not to study something, STUDY IT (he lies). I think that this class wasn't AS bad as described and was pretty enjoyable, even though I am glad I am nearly done for the year!

Bethel is a good professor. It's clear that he's very passionate about chemistry. At times he breaks down the topics well and helps us understand complex topics. However, at other times, he rushes over the material, leaving you confused about what is going on conceptually. Not that this is out of the norm for the chem department.

The first practice midterm we had was so difficult that it kind of discouraged me from trying to study, but the actual exam was easier. However, the second practice midterm was way too easy compared to the actual exam. It made me think that I'd mastered the material when I hadn't.

Read the textbook if you want. Or don't. It doesn't really matter. As long as you attend lectures and do the homework, you'll be fine. Definitely do the suggested exercises Bethel recommends though.

Bethel likes to use iClicker, which is part of your grade. This means that you have to make sure you attend lectures so that you don't hurt your grade by missing the iClicker points. It's a bit frustrating at times because it feels like he doesn't give you enough time to answer the question when you need it the most. Honestly, I have a love–hate relationship with iClicker. While it definitely helps me learn how to solve the problems, it also gives me a bit of anxiety because I feel like I can't learn how to solve them at my own pace. The achieve homework is pretty easy, especially since you get unlimited attempts and there are hints. Sometimes I learned more from doing the homework than I did from the lectures themselves. Personally, the homework assignments typically only took me an hour or two and I would finish it all on Mondays so I wouldn't have to worry about it during the week. If you take this class, make sure to show up to class and turn in the homework on time. You'll appreciate the slight boost to your grade.

There is a quiz every week (when there isn't a midterm). These quizzes were pretty easy and the questions were similar to the iClicker questions. Bethel drops your lowest quiz grade, so it's not the end of the world if you do bad on one quiz. Sometimes, the image or the wording would confuse me a bit so I'd have to ask the TA for help.

This brings me to the TAs. Honestly, it felt like the TAs themselves didn't know what was going on most of the time. When we'd ask them questions during discussions, they'd have to consult the key. It would be nice if we had TAs that were actually knowledgable

9/13 about the content. Sometimes, you'd ask them a question during the quizzes or midterms and they'd give you the wrong answer (luckily you can request regrades for midterms if this is the case). It genuinely feels like they're trying to trick you half the time.

Some more things that irked me about this class: Bethel holds lecture during reading week. This means that while all your other friends get to stay in bed all day or go get good study spots at the library, you have to orient your morning around chem class. The exams are 7–9 pm, so if you get tired around then, you better drink some coffee in the afternoon. Sometimes, Bethel would start teaching the next week's material. He would tell you it wouldn't be on the quiz, and then put it on there anyways. Make sure you review everything you learn that week if you want to do well on the quizzes. Also, it seems they don't proofread the tests before they print them. There would always be some question with typos that you'd need to fix by hand before starting the exam. Sometimes he would put unsolvable question on the discussion packets where even the TAs would say that there is no way to know the correct answer.

Overall, if you unfortunately have to take this class, Bethel is a good option to take it with. He uses a lot of sarcasm and makes funny jokes that help the class become more bearable. You'll definitely learn a lot, even if you think AP chem taught you everything you need to know. Don't be fooled though. He may look nice and make jokes, but he will take you for all you've got and then some. This class is not for the faint of heart.

Honestly don't even bother torturing yourself by scrolling through the CTECs. If you're reading this, it probably means you have to take the class anyways. Just accept your fate and move on. This is one of those classes where you get out what you put in. Work hard and you'll be fine. Don't expect to get straight A's though–you will be thoroughly humbled if this is the case. It's time to adjust your standards. Being within one standard deviation of the mean/median (which is around 73 for midterms) is considered as doing well in the class.

Edit: Just took the final exam tonight and I am appalled. What I thought was going to be an assessment over the entire quarter actually turned out to be a test with 70% of the questions being over only the week's worth of content. Never have I ever taken a cumulative exam that bulldozes the definition of "cumulative" so thoroughly. This assessment was a joke. I feel that it did not test my knowledge over the content of the course as a whole, but instead set me up for failure by focusing heavily on the topics that we'd only had a few days to familiarize ourselves with. To make things worse, it seemed as though Bethel and the TAs were smirking at seeing all of us struggle. It seems those that are supposed to help us don't really care about us and that they thrive on schadenfreude. Apparently we should simply consider the final as a third midterm, not a final.

This course is significantly challenging but it's a manageable amount of work. Make sure to put in work throughout the course if possible, as the material is extremely difficult to cram. Don't put off studying for the midterms and finals. The bulk of the workload for Chem will come from the lab, not the lecture. Making sure to go to class every day will go a long way, as the mass amount of content will become much more manageable if you understand it during the lecture and focus on the participation examples.

The course content was difficult, especially since I had little to no prior chemistry experience. However, I appreciated the opportunities for grade boosting like in–class participation questions and completion homework.

I thought that this class was taught okay. You learn a lot more from Achieve assignments but I thought that some points were not explained as well. Like if you were knowledgeable about the subject before taking the class you might understand what Prof. Bethel is trying to say but if it's new content then it'd take some more time to grasp. Overall I didn't go into this course with the highest expectations being that it's gen chem but I think it was okay.

The fast–paced manor of the course barely allows for any absorption of the material, you might feel like you understand the homework questions but good luck because the test is nothing like the homework. The slide shows you could download from canvas did not have any of the practice problems that we would go through in class on them so it was harder to study at home based off of random scratch work from class. Bethel is a funny, passionate professor who truly wants his students to succeed. The issue is that it was very easy to lose track of what was being said in class as Bethel talks pretty fast. Homework is assigned and due at reasonable times and the homework is good practice for the quizzes. Bethel makes this class bearable, if you can, get him.

this class is definitely manageable especially if youve taken ap chemistry in high school because there are a lot of topics that overlap with it. lectures are recorded but you kind of have to attend class bc there are iclicker questions which i personally thought was helpful to make sure i understood what was being taught. the homework is not really similar to exam questions which is kind of frustrating. i thought the exam was fair and you really just have to understand all the concepts in order to do well, so read the textbook, do practice problems, etc. bethel is a decent professor, idk i dont really have much opinions on him
I enjoyed learning about chemistry (it interests me), however the exams were overly stressful and hard to feel fully prepared for.
This class was definitely more fast paced and difficult than I expected it to be and if you dont use your time well, you will struggle in it.

This is a good introduction to science classes at Northwestern! I'm a sophomore taking it because I didn't have the chance to last year, so I've got a few of classes in the biology sequence to compare with, and this chemistry class was definitely structured to be fairly forgiving as you're getting used to university sciences compared to the bio sequence, which starts in the spring quarter and generally expects you to have gotten the hang of things by that point. My favorite part about the coursework in this class was that the homework allowed for infinite attempts at each question, which meant a lot less stress as I was doing those assignments.

10/13
I think this class is your typical lecture, however I found that the exams were alot harded than what I expected given the clicker questions and material covered in class.

This class requires a huge time commitment per week on top of the lab (Chem 161). There are 6 weekly quizzes with the lowest score being dropped and there are 9 weekly homework assignments; Some of them can be time–consuming, but they are really good practice for the quizzes and exams and honestly you can't really complain about 1–3 hours of homework per week. The quizzes are generally easy if you finish the homework. You have to show up to lecture because the professor uses iClicker, which is a system for asking questions during class and they're graded. There are 2 non–cumulative midterms and a cumulative final being worth ~13%, ~20%, and ~33% respectively. These are actually killer. Make sure you know your stuff really well and the knit gritting topics because everything is fair game on the free response sections; make sure you do lots of practice problems before the exams. I really only did well in this class I think because I reviewed the lecture material after class and took notes outside of class because professor Bethel goes at a moderate pace, but it's pretty easy to get lost if you blank out. GO TO HIS OFFICE HOURS! They really do help. All in all, with enough time, this class is manageable to get a good grade in (like a B or more). Don't take this class if you don't need to do. If you do have to, suck it up, it's gonna suck, and you just got to deal with that. Try to take some easy classes with it.

Honestly this class wasn't even that bad. Professor Bethel is a pretty enthusiastic lecturer and the midterms are very reflective of the content covered during class. The class was also of an appropriate pace. Overall, it was a pretty straightforward class and if you took AP Chem, it's a free A/A–. HINT: go to ASLA Peer–Tutoring to clarify any questions because they are pretty good about that.

This is easily the most difficult course I have ever taken in my life. I spend a lot of time every single day dedicated to Chemistry and I still didn’t get the grades I was looking for. Not even close. I would never in a million years recommend this class to someone who wasn’t required to take it.

The structure of this class is a bit all over the place, you start with one unit, then jump to another unit that has little correlation to the previous unit. A lot of chapters are sped through, while some chapters take way longer than needed. The textbook does not help with the exams or quizzes, only the homework and posted lecture slides helped.

I felt that there definitely could have been more practice exam material during the class. I felt that the class itself was fine and actually pretty interesting to learn about, but there was no practice exam during the final. This isn't an easy class at all and should probably only be taken as a requirement. The achieve assignments were pretty reasonable. Just more practice would have been nice.

If you want a good grade be prepared to put in at least 15+ hours/week. An A/A– is doable, but it's not gonna be easy. Try not to take too many other difficult/time–consuming classes at the same time and u should be fine. Also, Bethel is a pretty solid teacher, I'd def recommend him over the other guy. Also, laugh at his jokes every once in a while I feel bad when it's silent.

11/13

source_file: Student Report for David Schieber (SOCIOL_110-0_20  Introduction to Sociology)_506777e1-d42b-439d-8f9a-8bf008fa8f6fen-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: Love this class! Prof Schieber keep eachlecture engaging. One of the best classes, as long as you do the work and show up to class, you will get an A.
This was a chill class. The TAs and Prof. Schieber are very knowledgeable and enthusiastic. The grading system is very forgiving as long as you do the work.

This is a really easy distro and content is interesting as well. Professor Schieber is a great lecturer and the slides and reading cover a bit. Overall, great class to take as there aren't any tests just assignment based.

This course is definitely very easy – as someone organized I was able to finish the course in 6 weeks or so while continuously attending lecture to get participation points. Sometimes grading is slow but its relatively nice grading so I would def recommend.
probably the easiest distro. You can basically be done with all of the work by week 7 if you want and then all you have to do is show up to every lecture.
Very light and easy foundational discipline requirement. Lectures were chill, and if you put in effort, it's easy to get a high grade.
Easy distro, recommend
BEST CLASS AT NORTHWESTERN! PROFESSOR WAS AMAZING AS WELL!

I took this class thinking it would be an easy A but it's a harder A than a lot of distros. I had to write a lot, a lot, of essays (think more than 8). Overall enjoyable lectures and interesting topics, though!
This class was very surface–level, and while interesting at times, it did not challenge me. The professor was very nice, and the TAs graded very easily.

This class is amazing with Professor Schieber! The topics are super interesting and engaging, and you can pace yourself for the assignments based on what best aligns with your schedule when submitting assignments for the most part. Very, very easy to achieve an A in this class as long as you submit everything timely and show a clear understanding of the readings and lectures. I loved this class and hope to take more sociology classes in the future, even though it is outside of my major.
Good, straightforward class to take. You get out what you put in.
Good class

This course if a really good introductory course. It covers a lot of topics and the readings, though may be long, are really insightful. I found them to be really interesting at times. The class is structured where all of the homework is on your own time. We have no in person exams or quizzes which I really like for an introductory course.

This is class is an easy A but attendance is mandatory now so don't listen to anyone else. You have to actually write the essays and short answers can be written or done in office hours but depending on your TA you might trying harder or less than others in the class to get good grades. But honestly, it's not that bad just make sure all of your assignments align with the rubric before you turn them in.
Very interesting, I would read the reading sin my free time

Amazing course!!! The lectures were fun to attend, very interesting to learn from, and Professor Schieber knows so much and is passionate about what he is teaching. The workload is very manageable with the right planning, and even when they were sometimes a lot, the readings made them very tolerable and enjoyable. I'm so glad I took this class, and I highly encourage that you take this class if you're interested in sociology or just want to learn something new!

Prof. schieber is an amazing lecturer take this class! He uses very relevant examples in his lectures and the workload is very light, with just a few reading responses each week that aren't graded too harshly. Also great for pre–med students looking to learn some socio content for the MCAT.

I really enjoyed this course overall and feel like it’s providing me a strong general foundation in sociology, and I definitely would like to take more sociology classes in the future to further my knowledge in specific realms of sociology.
Survey course, general idea of main topics
Easy, DO it

Take this class!!! The assignments consist of 8 Reading Response papers (about 2 pages) and 10 Short–Answer questions (250 words each). A lot of my peers and I completed all of our requirements by mid October and were pretty much finished with the class by then. While attending lectures is mandatory, it is honestly super fun and engaging. I also genuinely enjoyed the readings that we did for this class, they were interesting and quite relevant to college students. Great class!

I would recommend to everyone to take this, easy A and to be honest, No work at all. Overall for this course, I would say, very informative, relates to real life situations and gives you clear knowledge of societies impact in one's life.
too many reading responses and short answer questions compared to past CTECs, still great class, no need to go to lectures if you are willing to write 750 words missed lecture write–ups
2 sets of readings due every week and short answer questions. readings and lectures were extremely interesting and informing
Easy A class. Make sure to do assignments throughout the quarter so you do not stress yourself out during finals time.
Great class

I did all of the reading assignments in the beginning of the quarter. After that, there were just some post lecture questions that weren't too bad. By week 6 all I had to do was show up to class for attendance which was fine. Overall it was really easy and some of the conversations were interesting.

While grading for this course is easy, the workload is not. There are 10 short answer questions which must be answered before the end of the quarter between 250–500 words each. There are also 7 reading responses due throughout the quarter before their respective lectures. You can choose which of these you want to do, and they are longer than the short answer questions. The instruction and lectures are great, but attendance is mandatory with poll questions. Overall, this course does take a considerable amount of time, and it might be worth looking elsewhere to fulfill a distribution requirement.
This class was much different than I expected. The lectures are interesting. The assignments can take a while and there are way more of them now compared to previous quarters.
Learned a lot about society

This course was challenging to me since I hadn't done big reading of 40–50 pages in one day before. Reading is also not one of my strengths so this class was really hard for me since I struggled being able to express my ideas into paper. Not only that but getting only half points on some readings stressed me more and got me to spend more time on readings and on my summaries. This summary's where hard for me and I spend around 3hrs for each reading and summary.

This course is relatively straightforward. As long as you do what you are supposed to do, you will be just fine. The course is content–heavy in terms of papers and reading responses you have to write, but Professor Schieber gives you multiple opportunities to do these writing responses in case you don't get a satisfactory grade. The class is workload–heavy, but it's spread out over the entire quarter so you will be fine if you just manage your time effectively. Overall, the course is good and it's not difficult to get an A.

This is a very interesting course that covers many content related to sociology. Attendance is mandatory. You need to complete 8 reading response paper (750 words) and 10 short answer questions (250 words).
It's pretty boring ngl. But I like the instructor and it's a nice intro if it's been a while since you've done academic writing and reading.
It was fine

Very chill course. Didn't really have to pay attention to lectures, just review slides and research papers assigned to complete the writing assignments. I appreciated having access to all of the assignments and their due dates as soon as the class started. interesting prof and interesting topics

Very easy A and a chill distro! Professor Schieber is super nice and approachable. For the course work, there are 18 assignments total. You have 8 reading responses due before every lecture that should be roughly 750 words (if you do about 1 per week you'll be fine), and 10 short answer responses that are assigned after every lecture but due all at the end of the quarter (so, hypothetically you could do all of them right before the due date) which make up the entire course (no midterms or finals or anything). Very manageable, and the readings were pretty interesting as well. The only downside is that attendance is mandatory, and the lectures are pretty much unnecessary to go to so you'll see a lot of people just doing their other assignments during lecture time.
This is an amazing class. Professor Scheiber is awesome and is genuinely interested in teaching and research. He tries to make class fun and low stress which it completely is.

The readings were not that bad for me. At the beginning of the quarter, the professor gave a presentation on how to read and skim papers and why we do it. It was very helpful and made me think through my process for writing essays. I don't have any non– technical classes as a STEM major, so this class was so helpful for strengthening those skills. The TAs were also so helpful; the TA I had wasn't too brutal in grading and gave very good feedback that I knew exactly how to go about writing the next paper. I really appreciated that we only had to submit 8 reading responses and 10 short answer questions, and that we could choose which ones. It helped me structure my year around my busiest weeks so I wouldn't get stressed. Honestly, an awesome class.

I highly recommend taking this course! The grading is lenient, and the workload is very manageable, assuming you plan wisely. We had 8 required reading responses where you would read 3–4 articles (they could get dense at times) and write summaries/responses. There were 10 short–answer questions that had to do with lecture material, each of which was posted after a lecture. You only had to do 8 out of about 20 reading response options, so you could plan to do them based on when you knew your schedule would get busy. The short–answer questions were all due at the end of the quarter (about 10/20 required), but didn't take much time at all. Professor Shieber was very engaging in his lectures and also very approachable. Attendance was required, and we used an attendance app to participate in interactive polls, but I think they made the lectures more interesting. Once again, I highly recommend this course—it's great to take during a busy quarter and an interesting foundational discipline.
Great class, very manageable and good to take in a hard quarter
Easy. Fun lectures
The course readings are very dense and I would spend hours trying to summarize them, but over time the skills picked up allowed me to more efficiently summarize and think about the material.
It was a really interesting course, and I appreciated that it allowed me to work on my own schedule. It wasn’t too overwhelming and helped me maintain a much–needed balance in my schedule.

This course was the highlight of my week!!! If you are in a stressful major and need a chill class that challenges your view on our society, I really recommend taking this class. When a professor is passionate about their subject matter it makes the class more digestible... Professor Schieber was passionate and a very witty. The class has different themed weeks and both lectures correspond with the topic. You have to complete 8 Reading Response Papers over the quarter and 10 Short Answer Questions. If you pace yourself properly at the beginning of the quarter, you can most definitely be done with the entire class well before Thanksgiving break. The grading is super light as long as you show critical thinking in your papers. For attendance, it is taken through polls administered during every class. The only thing I wished occurred more was having discussions in class, but it is such a large lecture hall that people rarely spoke up. OVERALL, MUCH RESPECT FOR PROFESSOR SCHIEBER!
Kind of tedious but super easy. Lectures are required but very interesting. Reading responses become a burden at the end of the quarter, so stay up to date with assignments.

I loved this class. There are no midterms, instead your grade consists of attendance and participation, reading response essays, and short answers. If you are someone taking stem classes, I would totally recommend taking this class. Attendance is required, and you have to complete 8 reading response papers (750 words or more) from the reading provided before each class. Also, you have to complete 10 short answers (250 words). Overall, great class and the topics were very interesting to read about.

I think they made the course more difficult for this quarter than it was in the past. I did not appreciate having to write a thousand words a week for a distro, but it was not that bad. Some of the readings are interesting, and some of them are very dry.
straightforward class with reasonable number of assignments. attendance required in lecture
This class is kind of a pre–med requirement, and is an extremely easy A. It is also interesting, I suggest taking this as an easy class in a quarter when you have a hard workload.
Super easy and chill class. Lectures are actually pretty interesting if you go with an open mind listening to examples. Almost the entire class is done outside of class and it is super easy grading.

Good distro class! There aren't any midterms/finals for this course and you do the assignments on your own time. 20% of the course grade is just attendance/participation but you can just fill out a missed lecture writeup to max out the category. Aside from that, you have to do 8 reading responses and 10 short answer responses based on class lecture (lecture recordings are available as well along with slides) and you can choose which week's readings you want to do. Lectures are sometimes boring, but still a good intro class overall.

This course is flexible, probably best for students that have heavier workloads for other classes. Unfortunately, because this class is very large, many students do not pay attention to the lectures which is unfortunate because they are very interesting. This course makes you see everyday topics from a different perspective, I even learned some local history!

I thought the way the class is structured is interesting. You basically have to do 8 reading response essays in response to whichever readings you want throughout the course and then 10 short answer questions. I like that we were able to pick the reading responses however the short answer question were pretty dull and felt more like busy work than furthering our understanding of sociology.
Best prof ever, take this clas
Very kind and funny professor who is very passionate about the subject, interesting lectures. Material is not too difficult to grasp.
Fun class . You make friends. BUSY BUSY BUSY work. So much readings and writing but everyone gets As.

This class has transformed my perspective on life through the daily interactions I have, shaping my current self, and envisioning the future. Intro to Sociology is an extensive exploration of various aspects of our society, delving into the intricacies, reasoning, and consequences of social practices that reveal the deeper aspects of human existence. I highly recommend this class to anyone who is willing to engage with dense material and fascinating case studies.

This class was very interesting! All the lectures had a specific theme or topic to them which was reinforced with readings. I liked how the class had specifically laid out all the assignments at your own pace. It made the class much more manageable.
This course was great! Low stress. No exams!
The class itself is really easy. The homework instructions are outlined clearly and you get a lot of room for error and time management, so the homework was barely a concern.

This is an excellent introductory course/FD requirement. Professor Schieber is an enthusiastic lecturer and clearly very passionate about what he teaches. There is no final assessment, only short graded papers which can be completed over the course of the quarter. As long as you do not procrastinate, this is a very easy and interesting course that I highly recommend.
I really loved the topics in this course

Sociology included long readings that were very thought provoking and genuinely interesting to read. This class also helped me improve my reading and writing skills. If you put in the effort you will get an easy A!

Professor David makes this course so that you learn so much but willingly and not taking up an over amount of your time. You do put in time to do good but as long as your a good student and do what is asked – which is not a lot– you will do great!
Lots of writing for the short answer and reading responses but really not bad if you're fine with writing. Very easy to end with a high grade.
Professor Schieber is a great instructor! He is funny and clearly passionate about his work. This class was a great introduction to sociology.

This class is an easy A. As long as you complete the 8 reading responses and 10 short–answer questions and attend the lecture, you will get an A. Please get the essays done early so they don't pile up at the end.

Prof Schieber is the absolute best! I would definitely take another class with him. He is so engaging and clearly very passionate. He is approachable and extremely nice and understanding. The class is overall light work and he truly wants to see you succeed. On that note, I genuinely encourage anyone who takes this class to please not treat this class as your back burner class, it’s actually really interesting if you pay attention. That being said, a lot of students didn’t pay attention and did their work for other classes and did just fine, you don’t really need to take to take notes or anything. All of the work is outside of class but attendance points are required through polling (PollEv) during class. The class is composed of 8 reading responses. You have an opportunity to do these at any point in the quarter as you please, with 2 opportunities every week but each one is due/must be turned in before each class it is for. These can be a little tedious as they are minimum 750 words and require a summary of each reading (usually there’s 3–4 readings per class) and a response section so you want to be strategic and to get them done as soon as possible or on your off weeks. There’s also 10 short response answers that you can do at any point in the quarter, these are really easy to bang out and are only expected to be around 250 words. Overall this class is really fun and highly applicable for any major. Super interesting and a lot of thought into the readings and the structure of the class.
The course gave great freedom to pursue the topics I was most interested in.
Topics that related to Northwestern students.
I enjoyed having a lot of topics to discuss and learn about, reading responses in a good way forced me to understand each reading.

This class is so much fun and incredibly valuable for understanding people, our actions, and how our social situations shape the two. I seriously would enjoy if the lectures were twice as long. Prof. Schieber is so compelling and engaging. He has such an interesting perspective and is so intelligent, insightful, wise, thoughtful, and genuine. Sometimes, the findings he mentioned would literally drop my jaw because society is so shocking. He exposes society in such dramatic ways sometimes. He also really cares about his students doing well, he is very accommodating, flexible, approachable, and helpful. It's virtually impossible to fail this class or let it stress you out without a deliberate intention to. He makes the class as straightforward and reasonable as possible. Also, you can just tell he really cares about people in general, it shows often. His authenticity is stark, refreshing, and honestly inspiring. And he's funny too. I was a little sad about the class ending. I enjoyed even the readings.
Not a difficult class. Grade is dependent on 8 reading responses, 10 SAQ's, and attendance, all of which are pretty much based on whether you do it or not.

I was surprised at how interesting I found sociology. The patterns that people find in human behavior are fascinating to hear about. It was a pretty easy course, just be on top of your reading responses!
love it
The lectures were very related to the readings assigned, which focused on various aspects, and were interesting.

This course was an amazing intro course to sociology. I loved the freedom in the workload – you can do the work pretty much whenever you want which is helpful for planning around your other classes/life events. Also, Professor Schieber's lectures are so engaging.

This class is interesting but its more work than people say on most reviews. Assignments are 8, 750 word reading reflections and 10, 250 short answer questions which can add up. There is also an attendence requirement.

Great class to take if you want something engaging and with no exams! Easy to get an A if you complete all assignments, follow directions, and go to class. This was my "easy" class as an engineering major.

This class is well known for being quite easy, but it is also fairly interesting as well. The course requires participation and a number of short answer questions and reading responses, but the grading is intentionally set up to help you succeed. Should you choose to pay attention in lecture, the course information is quite interesting, and the professor does an excellent job demonstrating his knowledge but also spinning the story of each lecture. Take this course!
honestly some pretty interesting content but they made this quarter a lot tougher than past quarters :(
The class itself is incredibly chill and just has reading summary and lecture summary assignments for grading
Great course, great professor. Easy to do well if good effort and solid writing skills are put forth.
I loved this class, the professor, and the TA. Great class with great examples of real world issues.

A great, chill class to take alongside other courses. The course load is relatively light; it consists of eight (8) reading responses (750 words min), ten (10) short responses (250 words min), and optional lecture write–ups (for when you miss class). Attendance is MANDATORY as it counts towards your participation grade.

EASY A class!!! Take this class no midterms or finals, if you want to you can literally be done with all of your work by week 5. The only thing is attendance is mandatory which I don't really mind but just be aware. The grading is 8 reading responses (750 words), 10 short answer questions (250–500 words), and then attendance is worth 20%.
Easy class, just do the work and you’ll get the A. Interesting if you do all the readings and give it your all

source_file: Student Report for Carine Nemr (CHEM_110-0_01  Quant Problem Solving in Chem)_bfdaab6a-7ba0-44be-b9db-9831f4ff0e36en-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: The class is pretty hard. You really just can’t fall behind on material or learning and you should go to prof or your TA right away if you have problems. Address it sooner rather than later.

This class was definitely much harder than expected. if you have taken a chemistry course then this is going to be a generally easy class for you. However, if you have never taken chemistry then I would definitely recommend you make sure you have enough time to study for it. it is very topic heavy with very little time to catch up if missed. This is not an extremely challenging class however the exams are much harder than the content in class. I would not recommend this class to anyone who is not looking to pursue a course that has this as a major requirement unless they have done chemistry before and are quite comfortable with the subject.

Very fast paced and all the related topics build on each other, so don't procrastinate with leaning new topics. Prof Nemr is nice and good at explaining things. Averages were in the 50s for both midterms, so if you get above average you will pass. Getting an A is very difficult.
Chem 110 is somewhat of a weed–out course and it can be challenging at times but Nemr is definitely the best professor to take the class with.

I struggled a lot initially in this course. It only began to improve once i learned how to study (with friends and by myself) as well as made use of the resources offered to succeed in this course. I wish I joined a peer guided study group.
The course is fast–paced, and you must keep up with the work, especially studying. In saying that, I wasn't expecting anything else, but there are many different resources for all learning needs.

Professor Nemr is a really nice and understanding professor. I like how in class we did practice problems when we went over new concepts. The class can move fast after the first two weeks and I do reccommend going to office hours for both Professor Nemr and Professor Northrup they are both really helpful and nice.
The class is very difficult. I would recommend taking a lighter load when taking this class. I found class and recitation unhelpful. Youtube helped me best.

CHEM 110 is one of the most challenging courses in WCAS. Prof Nemr was nothing short of an amazing and approachable instructor. You can tell that she is very passionate about her students' educational growth. The course is very math intensive (more than what you would expect from a Gen Chem course) but becomes doable with extensive practice. It is very easy to get lost in Prof Nemr's lectures that's why I advise that rewatching the recorded lectures can help with learning the material.
It has taught me chemistry as well as how to study and manage time well being a college student

This course was mentally draining. I took chemistry during sophomore year of highschool, so I didn’t have much background coming into college. The chemistry course is very fast pace and if you fall behind it will be hard to catch up. I recommend reading the text book and working on pratice problems. This course was like learning ap chem in like 2 months. Professor Nemr is very nice and provides detailed slides.

This class certainly surprised me. As most people in the class, I had zero experience coming into this course, and had no clue what to expect. I was shocked to discover just how difficult and demanding this course was, especially for an “introduction” course. It felt like at times we were expected to know things we never really covered, and be able to combine concepts to each other when we never even learned their relationships in the first place. I also didn’t expect this course to be so math heavy (although that might just be my fault). There were barely any conceptual questions throughout the class, and the majority was focused on mathematical practice and not the “why” to chemistry. Overall, however, I am also very impressed with how much knowledge I’m leaving this class with. Definitely taught me a lot.

Tough class– you have to really work to stay up with it. Exams are way harder than practice exams and feel super confusing during, but everyone scores low and there is a curve so if you are around average you should be fine around a B i'd say. Grading system is a bit frustrating because you have no idea what your grade looks like until they give you a final letter grade, and you never get you final exam back or the class average. Nemr is really nice and has office hours that are helpful. Don't let the CTECs freak you out, just study and try hard and it will be fine.

I think it’s pretty obvious that this is a weeder class, so sometimes it felt too difficult for an intro to chemistry class. The first few weeks were somewhat slow, then suddenly the pace increased to the point where I was struggling to keep up. I also don’t think that the things we learn and practice in class are difficult enough to prepare us for the exams, which are a lot harder than you would expect

As a chronically ill and disabled student on Northwestern's campus, the Northwestern Chemistry department, specifically the 110 sequence, does not set you up for success, even when your quality of life is on the line. I took CHEM110 in the fall of my first year; however, I faced significant health challenges during my first quarter in Evanston, even ending up in the hospital on the day of the first of two midterms. When I emailed the department about the severity of my condition in the beginning of the year, they promised to ensure that my deeply personal and physical challenges did not impact my education. This was not the case. After my hospitalization, I reached out to the 110 team informing them of my physical state and letting them know that I would be unable to sit for the exam due to hospitalization and asking to take the midterm at a later date when I was discharged and back on campus. Thinking that the previous promise still held, I hoped for a contingency plan, but there was none. Instead, I was told that I would NOT be able to make up the exam and that their next idea was to "exempt" me from the midterm by deducting those points from the total amount of points that we are graded on, taking away the opportunity to improve my grade and making all of my other assignments, including my next final and midterm, weighted heavier than my peers. Not only was the department's lack of empathy extremely disheartening, I later found out that athletes who miss an exam for a sporting event get the opportunity to take the exam remotely or upon their return with a Northwestern–approved proctor (yes, you read that right)

I approached my second–year at Northwestern and my now second year working with the CHEM110 department hopeful that this time would be better, and to extent it was, however barriers were continued to be put in front of me. Luckily, I made it to the first midterm with little health complications. However, that soon changed when I broke a series of 3 bones, placing me in constant and excruciating pain, resulting in my long–term use of a wheelchair. After getting told no when I asked if I could take a quiz later in the day when I got off a three month waitlist for a specialist at a local hospital, I had to choose between my academics and my health, and because my grade would once again take a hit for it, I felt forced into choosing my academics due to the department's lack of empathy. To this day, I am still waiting for another appointment to open up. Additionally, I was denied to be zoomed into the second midterm's review sessions, despite enduring excruciating pain and expressing that I could no longer sit in my wheelchair for another two hours in addition to my 4 hour bio lab that I had that very same day. I also asked for the accessible entrance into the review session's room so that I could attend the session and the professor provided one. The entrance was NOT accessible, making me get up out of my chair to struggle to get to a seat with a fractured femur so that I can simply have the same quality education and a fighting chance to succeed on the next midterm since I was denied other accessible options.

After this, I made the executive decision to finish out the quarter in CHEM110 despite the odds stacked against me, and changed my major and my long–lasting career plans due to my experience with this team. Through the lack of accessibility and empathy for my undeniably unique situation, I became tired of working through this system, the system being this Chemistry department that was created to support the needs of non–disabled students and athletes. It is because of the Northwestern CHEM110 department that my love for science, the very tool that has saved my life, has significantly faded and that I will most likely no longer achieve that goal of being a doctor. Instead, now I am more determined than ever to become and ADA lawyer or policymaker to ensure the experience does not repeat itself. I am already in for the fight for my life, I should not have to fight for a place within a course at a university that preaches the accessibility of its campus, departments, and faculty.

Definitely take critical notes besides the slides in class, review the textbook, watch videos on the side going over the specific topics you want to review, and try practice problems as they contain language that makes you think more critically about the problems you might get on the weekly quizzes.
This class is hard. There is a lot of math involved in this class. The class starts off slow the first few weeks but it goes by really fast.
The midterms are hard and you really need to do all of the practice problems and homework questions. Go to office hours and get as much help as you need.

This was my first chem class ever and it was hard. I hate how fast it goes and how the average is around a 50% on all tests, its like they don't want you to actually know all of the material on the test. First midterm is a reality check, don't do the practice midterms the day before, memorize the polyatomic atoms and solubility rules and life is easy. Make sure to time yourself on the practice midterms because I didn't even get to the FRQs on the first midterm.

This course was decent. Homework was helpful, and the lectures were too. However, the practice for the midterm is not at all representative of how the actual exam would go. Many people did not do well on the midterms because of this. Quizes were fine as well, as long as you attended lecture. Discussion section in my opinion was too long. iClickers were very annoying.

It was a challenge to learn or be refreshed of all the things I learnt about in high school with chemistry in a year condensed to just 3 months, and with much higher difficulty of problems but I believe that, for the most part, it went well and I was able to get through it okay. I really liked the recorded notes and recitation problem solutions that were given so you could look back on old slides if you missed anything as well as get all the answers to problems that may have proved difficult during recitation periods.

This course moves very fast and tries to cram a lot of information in a short amount of time (which is what you'd expect from the quarter system, but still overwhelming nonetheless). As someone with little prior chemistry experience (only took honors chem sophomore year of high school), this course was very challenging. If you do not like math and solving lengthy equations, this course is not for you. This class definitely trumped other classes in regards to the amount of outside work and studying I had to do. I would recommend (if you have slides) going over the slides after lecture every day to make sure you really grasped the concept because they build off of each other and not understanding one concept can really snowball. GO TO OFFICE HOURS, ask questions. Prof Nemr wants to help you!

I think the course did a good job at providing a lot of study resources. They always posted solutions to the quizzes, homework assignments, midterms and practice midterms. I found it very helpful because it allowed me to check my work and improve on different areas that needed more work than others.
It's crazy how much people struggle with this introduction course. Good luck to those taking this course. It can be manageable if you have the determination to study for long periods every week.

Over chem 110 is a very difficult course and no professor will change that. Prof Nemr is a good instructor just sometimes it may be hard to keep up in class but you can always go over the notes after. She really cares about her students and wants you to succeed which is nice.
This course was good in focusing on the aspects that everyone should know in Chem

My reaction to this course was at first overwhelmingly negative. I thought it was structured very poorly with a very strange timeline with odd amounts of time spent for very simple topics. We spent a good two weeks covering the syllabus, logarithms, and extremely simple systems of equations whereas the end of the class felt extremely rushed–– covering thermodynamics in only two classes. Looking back Id say professor Nemr did a great job of lecturing, and each class was interesting ang engaging. Overall I think the structure of the class is lacking but having access to textbook problems, office hours, and TA recitations made studying much easier. DEFINITELY take advantage of TA's. Their office hours are extremely helpful as usually the professor's OH are much busier.

I think this course did teach me how to take a difficult course at Northwestern, but I think it was overly hard, and I think some of the tests are unfair as they don't give us what to expect. I think that it will get better from here, but I recommend one spends a significant amount of time really trying to understand the material as they will really test you on your understanding and make it very tricky. Make sure you are detailed when learning and make sure you start strong. They do have an improvement clause. I would definitely take Prof. Nemr over Northrup, as her slides are so much better to understand. Also, make sure that you understand all the practice and that you ask questions if you are confused. Make sure that you meet with your professor and discuss how you can improve. Office hours and the Chemistry Resource room are really helpful, even the Google Meet.

This is a much harder class than you would think. The material is not what's difficult but the questions are so confusing and not what we learn. The exams are hard and the questions are so confusing. Do not take this unless you absolutely must. Professor Carine Nemr is very nice and supportive so if you want to take this class take it with her.

CHEM 110 is arguably one of the hardest general chemistry courses at Northwestern. That is not to scare you, however. If you need to take this course for your major or if you are pre–med, you will survive. However, if you DO NOT need to take this course, please don't subject yourself to it. Lectures are every single day except once a week to take the weekly quiz. Nemr in my opinion is a good lecturer, and she is very willing to explain more during class and after, as well as in office hours or in individual meetings. The quizzes at first are a bit annoying, do not be surprised if you fail the first few, but honestly a good way to engage with what your learning in lecture every day so that you are not cramming for the midterms. The lowest two are dropped anyways. Homework assignments are hard, they are designed to be worked on with other people and outside help from tutoring or online sources is encouraged, you just have to report it when submitting the assignment. Recitations are LONG for no clear reason, but the problem sets they give you are helpful for studying when exam season rolls around. Now, the midterms are definitely difficult and are not at all representative of the types of questions given in lecture, but the TAs are very generous with partial credit when grading. If you study with other people and make sure you are good on all the concepts you are confused about during lecture, either from office hours or watching videos online, you will most likely be fine. The final this quarter was hard, but doable. As long as you are with the average or higher on all the exams, you will pass the class. There is also an improvement policy that the professors will take into account before assigning final grades.
It can definitely be challenging but feels like you can go through it if you push yourself to put effort into understanding the science.
Overall, this course really piqued my interest in chemistry, but was very difficult considering this is the first chemistry sequence.

Reading the textbooks more into depth allowed me to understand more of the tops from lecture. I wish for preparation of the exams, the quizzes would be similar to the questions asked as multi–concept questions, and if there were more time to do the quizzes.

This course is very challenging and time–consuming, but Prof Nemr brings a light, positive energy to class every day that makes learning the material feel fun. Prof Nemr did a great job despite the large amount of material that had to be covered over the course of the quarter. She helped break things down into digestible pieces and it was very clear that she spent a lot of time thinking about the material outside of class. Her organization and clarity in teaching was helpful to my learning of the material.

Prof Nemr is the best lecturer at NU. She is always very prepared for every class, her slides are easy to follow, and she leads lecture in a very interactive way with her students. This class is bad in itself and requires a significant amount of your time every day to truly understand the material and how it could be applied on quizzes and exams. Exams are extremely difficult with both midterm averages being in the 50s range. However, Prof Nemr made this class more bearable. She truly cares about her students and makes the effort to learn everyone’s name, even in a large lecture. She genuinely wants everyone to succeed and makes herself very available by email, office hours multiple times a week, and is always willing to set up individual appointments. You truly can not get better than her. My biggest advice is to go to her office hours right from the start of the course. Even if you don’t have questions, go there to work on chemistry problems and work with other people because that is how you are going to do well in this course. If you want to do well in this course here's how: go to both prof and TA office hours, email any questions you have, find a group of students and make a study group, do every problem from the homeworks and recitation worksheets, join a PGSG, and do extra– problems in the back of each chapter from the textbook.

It's definitely a class that requires a lot of time and effort so don't expect it to be easy because it is intro. As the quarter goes on it definitely gets better and you start to understand what the profs will expect of you. If you haven't taken chemistry in a while I would recommend starting with 110 instead of 151.

If you have a bit of chemistry learned from high school like honors chemistry or a basic khan academy introduction to chemistry, you'll do fine mostly in the beginning, but the class ramps up in difficulty quickly, so keep up as much as possible to not fall behind. It is not an easy A especially with the midterms testing on topics that you'd have to do much more studying outside of class to finish a question. Nemr does help as much as possible, making sure she doesn't give you the answer but help you arrive at it.

This course definitely is a hard first year class. If you do not put enough time and effort into it, your grade will reflect it. There is not too much work given out of class, one homework a week, which gives you time to study for tests. There is only so much class time, so professors tend to only go over surface level examples while the exam problems are much harder. Exams are very difficult. Nemr was a good professor, you can tell she wants her students to succeed and always helps if you ask.

This course needed a lot of work put into it to get a grade that was considered good. There was a lot of content that was taught in a short period of time ; however, I believe that is was necessary due to the chemistry department's requirements and the quarter system. The tests were pretty challenging if you want to get an 100 percent because the free response questions consist of many parts. I believe that office hours should be thought of as a necessary part of chemistry that one needs to attend because it is very helpful. However, one should come prepared with many questions because if you are not prepared ahead of time, you will be wasting your time.

source_file: Student Report for Deborah Brown (PHYSICS_135-1_01  General Physics)_ff20e6b8-a77c-4306-bf33-9df2362940e7en-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: Came in with only grade 11 physics and this course is a lot of self studying. Weekly quizzes are mostly based on the problem manual, so grinding it inside out is a good way to study. The same study method does not work for the exams as questions are no longer simply the problem manual questions but with the numbers changed. To study for the exam, you must expose yourself to as many question types as possible. The textbook has a lot of questions that you can practice on. Search on Reddit for the solution book.

This was a good introduction to mechanics. The course load was not too heavy, especially if one has previous knowledge of physics. Dr. Brown was an effective professor who answered questions well. The exams and quizzes were also very reasonable in terms of difficulty.

I enjoyed this course a lot, and I felt that the tests were fair. Dr. Brown has us buy a problem manual that she wrote, but it's only $20, and it is not absolutely required. She posts the problems from the manual, but the physical book also has answers in the back. I didn't use the textbook for this course, and I felt that the manual combined with lectures was sufficient to do well.

Professor Brown is very nice and she seems to be pretty lenient with grades/curves. However, the lectures were not helpful or engaging and the $20 practice problem solution book that you must buy for the class doesn't help much either. As someone with no physics background at all, this class was difficult, but it didn't strike me as being particularly hard. I would recommend this course if you have a solid physics background or you can capably teach yourself the entirety of the class's content with youtube and google.

I used an online pdf of the textbook because this textbook was not 100% helpful and neither were the lectures. Professor Brown is very nice but she takes a long time to get through material and is often just deriving the formulas on the board when it would be better to see examples. You need to know how to do the problem manual problems by heart for the quizzes each week, but it would be more valuable if major concepts were explained better.

Dr. Brown is incredibly sweet and has great humor, but I can't say that I've learned much in this course. I have taken physics previously, so a lot of this course was review for me. Dr. Brown's lectures seemed rather confusing in my opinion, and it was difficult to keep up. There are participation quizzes, weekly quizzes, optional homework, two midterms, and a final exam. The first two midterms were not too bad, but the final was brutal. The final exam was confusing to me, and I didn't feel prepared for it despite doing a lot of practice and having a strong background in mechanics. I love Dr. Brown as a person, but I didn't really enjoy her as a professor.

This course was pretty good for the first half of the quarter, but the last month of the quarter was pretty rigorous. The textbook was also helpful for learning until the last month, when the concepts became too difficult for the textbook to explain well. I also think that Professor Brown was kind and wanted us to learn, and I appreciated being in the lecture, though I wish she had focused less on proofs.

source_file: Student Report for Ryan Bethel (CHEM_151-0_02  General Chemistry I)_bc5bfc8e-bd6e-4b3a-b1ac-dd9b7716998aen-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
