Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: The course is fine, though needlessly hard. Quizzes are extremely inconsistent and will often swing from very very easy to incredibly hard from one week to the next. The quizzes also often had niche questions from the book/ homework that were barely mentioned in class. The content on the exams is fair, but many questions are also worded in ways to cause confusion. The practice exams help, but are often unfinished and for our final, the key was never released even though many exercises had incorrect answer choices.
This course is challenging but doable if you take the time to learn from the many mistakes you will make.
Bethel explains the course content well as well as introducing topics that appear in future chemistry courses and the MCAT.
The time was the biggest struggle in this class, attempting to stay up to date with lecture materials whilst working on other classes proved to be very difficult.
Gen chem is hard but if you're a pre–med it is a requirement so you just got to do it.
Class is a standard Gen chem course, can be slow at first. There are many resources for you to succeed so as long as you're not slacking you'll do good
Take this course with Professor Bethel, he's a great professor and is easily approachable if you have questions or are confused.

I actually enjoyed this course in the long run. It was challenging for sure, but I found it to be the class where I learned the most interesting things. Prof. Bethel was also really fantastic at answering my questions and giving me the deeper understanding of the content that I was looking for.

Chem 151 is a difficult class that will challenge both your intellect and discipline with weekly quizzes and hard midterms. If you are taking this course first quarter of freshman year, it will help you quickly adjust to the fast–paced environment and make you work hard to achieve desired results.

This class requires a lot of focus and studying from the beginning; make sure you do the homework because it genuinely helps so much on the quizzes. It's expensive but shilling out for the updated textbook is really helpful as you miss a lot of topics if you don't do the assigned textbook problems (also, odd–numbered problems have answers in the textbook, so you can check your answers before the key is posted to see if you understand!). And use the TA resource room frequently from the beginning; it's a lot more helpful than going to professor office hours where the room is completely full. iClickers are quite annoying, especially if you take longer to grasp the concepts, but they can help your grade as long as you talk to the people around you about your answers (befriend people who are good at chem!). The practice final was not representative of the actual final; much of the content was extremely similar to midterms on the actual final while there was a lot of new content on the practice final. Definitely don't just study using the practice final if it's mostly new content because the real final stresses older content much more. As for instructors, Prof. Bethel is generally better than Prof. Swearer as he doesn't do demos in–lecture, so you get more time on the content you'll be tested on.
Hard class! You'll need to put in a lot of effort studying and doing practice problems.

Overall, this course was stressful, but I don't think it was as bad as the CTECs had made it out to be. One of the best parts about this class is that the homework isn't required for you to turn in, though you should still absolutely do at least the problems Dr. Bethel created and go to office hours to work through confusing questions. Going into the class having read the CTECs, I was worried about the iClickers, which are graded questions during the lecture, but if you find a group of people to sit around and help you with them, I promise you'll be fine. I don't know how Swearer is as a professor, but I recommend taking this class with Bethel. His handwriting is pretty atrocious, but this is a man who breathes Chemistry and can answer your questions. There is a quiz every Friday morning, and I got a 33% on the second one, but you're allowed to get rid of your lowest quiz grade which is nice, and overall they're pretty fair. Someone in one of the previous CTECs said this was exposure therapy if you have OCD, so I was kind of dreading this class, but I have OCD and if you can accept getting a C or a B on a few quizzes every now and then, you'll be fine. Ultimately, I didn't think this course was as bad as my expectations, but maybe take that with a grain of salt since I took AP Chem in High School. Also, you have to use a potato calculator instead of a TI–84 which is annoying. You should go into it expecting to learn how to use one or two new equations everyday, and hopefully have some kind of intuition for each one.

Don't take this course if it is not required. It takes up a lot of time, and isn't well organized. The answer keys for practice problems aren't released until usually less than 24 hours before the quiz or test, if they are released at all. It is overall a lot of work and time spent to still not get high grades on tests.
Learned Gen Chem

This course was challenging, but definitely doable if you stay on top of the problem sets and review the material weekly. It was a little bit more work than my other classes because there is a weekly quiz to study for, but the quiz is helpful to make sure you don't fall behind. I also think the exams were very fair and there were very representative of what was covered in class. Some of the quizzes had some curveball questions, but also were pretty representative for the most part. I would definitely recommend taking this course with Bethel as he is very good at explaining the topics and letting you know what you are expected to know for the exam.
seems like a standard gen chem course; good if you've taken ap chem before

This course wasn't as miserable as I thought it would be from prior rumors, but that doesn't mean it was pleasant. iClicker is genuinely awful––it's absurd that thirty seconds after you learn a concept you have to answer a question that contributes to your grade if you get it wrong. Take the class with Bethel. We had it way easier than the Swearer section. If you have a chemistry background it's not going to be that bad content wise, but if you haven't reviewed it in a while it's not going to be pleasant. The curve is really nice though. Your entire grade is low key just the midterms and the final, so don't worry about the rest that much.
Bethel is great at teaching this course. The slides are very organized and homework and practice exam questions are also helpful for the midterms.
Lectures are fine, and the attendance questions are pretty chill. Pretty normal class all things considered.
Extremely challenging. Takes so much time.

For the most part the course felt well structured and the examinations and quizzes felt fair, especially considering that quizzes (not exams) were often based on homework and textbook problems. Professor Bethel is a good lecturer as well, so the only challenging part of the course is really just remembering all the content and the different ways the questions can be asked, as there is a lot to cover.

Quizzes should be longer so that a mistake on a single question doesn't automatically mean you're getting a C. Recitation section was not helpful at all. Exams were difficult –– I wish they were more similar to the format of Bio 201 exams instead. For other students – if you need to take chem, take it, Bethel is a good professor. If you don't need to take it though, it's a heavy time commitment for a very standardized, and not super rewarding, difficult course.

The homework is not mandatory but not doing it will put you at a large disadvantage on the weekly quizzes. Also, do not expect to check answer keys for anything and find work shown because it almost definitely isn’t there. If even after attending the lecture, reviewing notes, and reading the textbook you don’t understand how a question was solved, either attend Office Hours or hope the internet can help you. One of the very few positives of this class was the review sessions held by the TAs one night a week. I highly recommend either attending them or watching the recording. They are one of the most helpful resources for the quizzes and exams in my opinion.
This class had be studying hours and hours on end before quizzes too. The content came by so fast. You need to lock in for this class or you will fall behind.

This course was difficult but ultimately manageable. Definitely go to Bethel's office hours even if you don't have specific questions to ask. It's good to hear what other people are confused about. The TA's review sessions are also very useful and I recommend going to them to do well on the quizzes.

This course was not easy, but not unachievable. The topics just require a lot of practice and reapetitiation, and it doesn't always feel like you have the time to actually study with all of the quizzes and corresponding labs. To be honest, it kinda of killed all my free time for my first quarter, which I don't appreciate. Regardless, it's not impossible to pass, you just really have to lock in. Best of luck to those who take this course next year!

I took AP Chemistry in high school, but if I did not, I would be so cooked. The weekly quizzes help keep you in check: do not do the OD homework problems if you already have a solid foundation there are too many homework problems ain't no one is doing all of those. Focus on the notes and don't worry too much about how much conceptual detail about the most specific random stuff.

Class is pretty annoying and definitely challenging. Attend the TA review sessions and office hours. Try not to compare yourself to your classmates because some people are just really good at Chemistry, and it can be frustrating. A lot of time goes into this class. Allowing us to get points back on the midterms with the final was the best part of the grading. The quizzes were kind of annoying because you would study for hours for this 4–question quiz, and it would test on the most random parts of the chapter.

Here is how you succeed in this class, so that nobody has to endure what I did. 1. Attend every class for iClicker points. Never miss this lecture. 2. Do *every* homework problem. Yes, all of them. Finish them a little early so that you can go to Prof. Bethel's office hours and ask about concepts that you don't understand. 3. Go to every one of Bethel's Office Hours anyways, because his insight is helpful for the quiz. 4. Go to your TA's office hours and talk with them about anything you got wrong on the previous week's quiz/midterm.

I would call this the bare minimum work required to do well in this class if you haven't already completed an AP or IB Chem. That's at least 8 hours per week spent purely in office hours and lectures – not including the homeworks which easily can take 4 or more hours per week, and the work you do to review for the quizzes.

The only way to succeed in this class is for it to be your entire life – and I think that is unfair. There is undue pressure on quizzes and exams (as all homework is ungraded), and the amount of content covered is too much in too little of a time. The pace and scatter– brained organization of topics makes it feel like you leave this class without anything except a lot of memorized information which will not serve you in your future coursework in McCormick unless you're ChemE or BME or similar.

The class structure itself is the problem – Bethel (as a person) doesn't seem malicious. He's outright kind when I have talked with him. Despite this, I would argue he takes a little too much joy in giving difficult iClicker questions. I have seen him give such advice as "just figure it out!" (with a wry smile) when a student asked him a question. This attitude is perpetuated by the course structure, leading to a hostile–feeling learning environment.

Regardless of whether you take the class with Prof. Bethel or Prof. Swearer, this class' structure will be the same. I think Prof. Bethel is a better lecturer and explains things better (despite the aggressive pacing of the content). Be aware though, the lecture is not sufficient preparation for the homeworks and quizzes, they moreso serve as a primer for the rest of the course content. You are expected to teach yourself a good bit of content regardless of the professor.
I felt it was focused on the topics that would be key stepping stones for other classes, which I appreciated.

I feel like there is a lot of room for improvement for this course. While Bethel is a good instructor, I feel that he made a lot of things in this course unnecessarily difficult. He gives homework that isn't "mandatory", but the quiz and exams are based on things from there, so it's pretty much required. Also, he gives a lot of problems from the textbook, but sometimes I think that he isn't actually looking over the questions he gives properly, because we would go to ask him questions about the problems during office hours and he would tell us to not do certain questions or become upset at some of the questions and the textbook.

As for grading, as long as you really understand each week's materials and do the homework, I believe it shouldn't be too hard to do okay in the class. There are a lot of components to your grade, and I like that you can see your projected average after each midterm, but I would like it better if they released grades on canvas with their respective weights so we could have a better prediction of our grade throughout the quarter.

Basically, don't take this course unless you need to.
Class was good, Bethel was a great teacher, take him over Swearer.

The most important aspects were office hours, as someone who can't always formulate questions, going to sit and talk with the professor, and collaborating with others on a smaller scale is extremely helpful.

This course is certainly challenging at times, with some units being much more difficult than others. It is definitely possible to do well, but a large amount of time is needed every week to study for this course. The course generally becomes more challenging as the quarter progresses, with the final being the most difficult exam.

This may be an unpopular opinion but I don't think Gen Chem deserves the reputation that it has. Don't get me wrong, the course is very fast–paced, and you will need to do a lot of work on your own to stay on top of things. Some things that I'd recommend include reading the textbook chapters for the week over the weekend, so you aren't caught off guard in lecture. Also, review your recitation worksheets and do as many difficult problems from the textbook as possible. This also helps you to train your problem solving and critical thinking skills, both of which you will need in this class. I had Bethel as professor; nice guy and straight to the point. Not the most passionate lecturer but his explanations make sense.

Bethel expects you to know a big chunk of chemistry before you start. So if you didnt take chem before, the class will be very challenging. Also he talks really fast and teaches in a way where he expects you to fully understand what he is saying right away
I did learn a lot but it was really difficult and I struggled a lot especially at the beginning of the quarter.

As someone without any AP chem experience who'd only taken honors chem sophomore year, I came into the class pretty worried, but I ended up doing very well throughout the course and should finish with an A as long as I don't bomb the final. The course has two midterms worth 20% each, a final worth 30%, 8 weekly quizzes worth about 15%, and some iClicker points. Those quizzes are a very good way to make sure you know the material that will be on the midterm. It is so important to do the provided HW each week as those problems can show up directly on quizzes, and I do believe practice is the best way to understand the applications of the course subjects. The biggest problem with this course is the fact that it moves very quickly for a lot of material, some of which can be quite intellectually difficult to grasp. This means that you need to weigh up how well you know the material and determine how much work to do outside of class with the homework and textbook. In the first few units, I spent a lot of time in the textbook making sure I understand the material, doing lots of practice problems. There are also review sessions and office hours which can be another way to get great practice or clarification. In class, Bethel is a very good and clear lecturer, and there are practice problems in class (some are graded iClickers), so you should be at lecture as much as possible. Since there is limited time, you may need to use the textbook to boost your understanding. If you are willing to challenge yourself and put the work in to understand the material, then please don't be scared of this course, but be ready for a challenge. I'd definitely recommend this over 110 for the two quarter sequence rather than three (and honestly 110 sounds like it may be worse). While difficult, there are many resources for you to succeed in this class.

This class starts off really rough, but once you get the hang of it, it gets much better. It's important to not let seeing a bad grade get in your head, because that often prevents you from doing well on the next quiz or exam. Prof Bethel is really good at lecturing, and his practice questions in class are helpful.

Office hours are definitely extremely helpful and I'd recommend going to them as much as possible. Professor Bethel is very kind and will answer any questions you have about the material or that week's homework. The class moves quickly so just make sure you are always staying on top of the material!

This class can be difficult because of how much content there is to learn, but there are a lot of resources you can use to be successful. Office hours were especially helpful, and I would recommend finishing homework earlier in the week so you can get any questions you have answered before quizzes.

I just dont like chemistry at all, and the gen chem sequence at this school is for sure an interesting set up. The grade distribution is all over the place because they don't let 151 drop into 110, but many people dropped from 171 into 151, too.

Although the course was very difficult and required a lot of studying, I really enjoyed the learning process and Prof Bethel is also really funny sometimes. Sometimes the topics are very confusing but most of the time it's understandable from someone who's taken AP chem in HS. There aren't tons of HW due every week but there are quizzes, which in my opinion are much better than other chem classes that require weekly problem sets or both quizzes + problem sets. It encourages you to constantly study without forcing you to complete large amounts of workload. The midterms are hard as they are very tricky, but with practice comes perfect.
The tests often have questions that were barely covered in lecture, causing a lot of stress when studying. Don’t take this course if you don’t have to

This course is fine, it's a general chemistry course. What do you expect? It's honestly pretty hard but idk how curve is going to work yet which is really frustrating. Weekly quizzes can get really annoying bc they ask super niche things sometimes but overall if you pay attention and do the homework it's very manageable. Don't let all the people saying it's bad scare you, if you're okay at math and problem solving then you'll be good. Lab is a different story.

Do not take this class if you don't have to. It's horrible. I expected it to be bad but it was worse than I thought. The profs themselves taught well and were engaging, and I really liked Bethel because you could see his interest in the subject, but the main issue was the tests. The exams are nothing like the practice exams, quizzes, or homework, so it is very, very hard to study for them. Some of the questions are outrageous and unfair, and the TAs can grade harshly and also incorrectly. It was very common for people to put in regrade requests. The homework was helpful for the quizzes and the quizzes weren't too bad, just the exams. The iClicker questions did not help at all because they were too easy. Overall, this course takes up so much time and causes so much stress, so try and place out if you can and definitely don't take it for a distro. People are fighting for a B. Especially with the transition of coming to college, this is not a good course for freshman year fall quarter.
This course covers a lot of content very quickly. I'd recommend reviewing the content covered in AP Chemistry before the quarter starts.

Bethel is a good teacher, your average Chem prof. If this class is not a requirement, don't take it. If it is, take it with Bethel. Weekly quizzes can be annoying and sometimes hard, sometimes, the stuff you learn feels really random, but if you are a MechE like me, just take it, work hard, and be done with your one quarter of chem. Definitely not an easy class, but work hard, read textbook ,and study, you'll be fine.

iclicker is aight, a lil annoying to have participation and questions each class.

This course was really challenging and the way homework and quizzes were laid out was very frustrating. Solutions to homework problems would not be posted until Thursday night when the quizzes were Friday morning giving you absolutely no time to review your answers. You also can't check if you are doing it right so you could do all the homework wrong and then not even now until the night before the quiz and have to cram. They do the same with final exam practices and midterm practices. The answer keys are posted so late that it makes studying early difficult.

Highly recommend taking this class with Bethel as he writes the exams and seems to prepare his sections for them much better. Overall this class is fine, if you've taken AP Chemistry before, like I have, you aren't doing anything crazy beyond that just going more in–depth so its very doable. With the lab section is a pretty big time commitment with lectures, labs, lab lectures, and recitation. Definitely do the homeworks to prepare for the weekly quizzes, it will make your life a lot easier. Much much less conceptual than 171, bigger focus on knowing equations and how to use them in 151.

This course is definitely rigorous. It's known as one of the hardest courses you can take as a freshman in your fall quarter, however it's definitely far from impossible in doing well or exceeding 151. What's really good about the course are its support systems –– from PGSGs, to office hours, to drop–in peer tutoring, to TA review sessions, the TAs and instructors do a really good job in giving you a lot of options and resources where you can seek help. I used all of the ones I mentioned above and it definitely had a major positive impact on my experience in 151, so I would definitely recommend it. Aside from that, for me succeeding in this class mainly depended on building a strong weekly routine and focusing on doing the homework problems before quizzes. All in all, especially if you think you are interested in pursuing STEM, don't shy away from Chem 151, trust yourself to be on top of the work, and take advantage of the resources provided, and this will help you greatly throughout the quarter.
This class is just kind of a nightmare. But it's a nightmare you have to be in so buckle up and watch out for the second midterm.

if you took ap chem in high school, this class won't be too bad. definitely take it with bethel if you can because i thought he was a good professor and was really good at explaining the topics in detail. attendance is required and lecture are recorded

The beginning of the class is very rough as they expect you to know stuff which I did not already know and it was unclear what exactly I was already supposed to know so it was hard to learn the beginning content easily. However, Bethel was a very good teacher and I would recommend him as he explained the concepts very well.

This course required a lot of self–study. While lectures did touch on key points and formulas, the level of coverage was not very broad and often felt rushed. The example questions covered in class were basic and way lower in difficulty than what appeared on midterms and quizzes. This, paired with the constant cycle of a prelab, notebook, homework, and quiz every week, genuinely makes the class feel overwhelming, but putting in the effort can get you a good grade in the end.
A difficult course, but if you do the homework, you should be fine. Highly curved class !!

This course is awful. If you have to take it, be prepared to study and not do well because what is taught in class is not relevant to what you need to know on the exams. The exams were so much harder than the quizzes or practice material and a lot of the stuff on our exams did not even have any practice material on it. Office hours are kind of helpful but always packed; TA office hours are rarely helpful. Do every single problem in the textbook for homework because a lot of the times it is copied and pasted onto the quizzes or finals with different values. Recitation worksheets are super hard and do not really help. Its frustrating that the homework answers are posted at 8 pm on Thursday night before your quiz with no explanation, so you don't actually know how to do the problems and have to look to outside resources. The practice midterms and finals are challenging but do not reflect the actual exams, but still do them. Go to TA review sessions when they have them.
Enjoyed lectures. Weekly homework sets were super helpful for getting better grasp of new content. Overall well structured course. Professor Bethel breaks down concepts really well.

I enjoyed the course as it was moderately challenging. I liked the professor's teaching style too and I think that the homework was well formatted as it wasn't graded though very helpful for comprehension of content.

It's college gen chem. You'll want to scream and cry. This is one of the hardest and most frustrating classes you'll ever take regardless of teacher. There's so much material to cover that you can't actually absorb all the information they give you. DO THE PRACTICE HOMEWORK PROBLEMS. They're not required and you won't turn them in but they help so much in cementing the material and giving you practice to work through. It is a weed out class for a reason. Definitely go to the office hours for Bethel because you go over homework problems and he sometimes drops hints of what you will or won't need to know for the weekly quizzes.

The aspects that were most important to me were the weekly quizzes, and big exams. The course requires daily and committed work towards understanding the material. It should be stressed that I learned it my way along with how it was taught in the lectures. It was necessary to understand the diverse concepts in the practice problems.

Try to finish all the homework problems before each quiz, including textbook problems. I would also recommend supplementing lecture slides with the textbook chapters. Joining a PGSG and attending TA review sessions helped me a lot. I also found the example problems in the textbook chapters useful.

This was a hard course. The content goes very in–depth, a lot more than other schools' gen chem courses. Sometimes you'll think it's overkill, and the pacing is extremely fast. Great wake–up call to what the quarter system is like, though. I recommend taking easier/lighter load courses if you're taking this course, because you need a lot of time studying. There are weekly quizzes on Friday, which require you to have mastered each week's content. Personally, I appreciated it because I used to be a last–minute crammer, and this made midterm studying so much easier, especially since we learn content up until the day before midterms. The exams are difficult. Getting an A is quite challenging in this class, and I absolutely do not recommend you take this without a chemistry background in high school, preferably AP chem. However, due to the difficulty all around, I've found that students are willing to help each other out. I very highly recommend you find a good group of friends within this course, as there will be times when collaboration is necessary to understand the content. Also, ABSOLUTELY attend office hours. It is crucial. If you don't attend, find a friend who does. Bethel is objectively better for exam prep, and Swearer tends to leave out small bits of content, or so I've heard numerous times. Bethel simply does not have enough time in class to go over everything to the extent you need for the midterms. Thats what office hours are for. BTW if you're looking to love the college experience and go out, sacrifices must be made due to this course in conjunction with the ruthless hours from the accompanying lab. I have literally spent every single night in the library this quarter, excluding two due to purely chemistry. The 20+ hours are not a joke, only if you want to get better than a C in this course. Don't be too discouraged though, it's not impossible, its straight forward, work hard, use the resources, get results.
Disproportionally difficult

The pace of this course does not slow down. We meet every single day of the week for either lecture, lab lecture, or discussion, and there is a quiz every single week. The homework is not required but if you can figure out how to do the homework problems you can probably do just about anything because they are so much harder than what we do in class. Sometimes the alignment of the homework is not the same as what we cover in class and it's hard to know what you should know on the quizzes/exams.

I took AP Chem, and a lot of the information within this course is also in AP Chem, but the way you are tested is way different. The questions are purposely hard, and the FRQs are extremely niche. I don't feel like the results accurately reflect my knowledge in the class subjects because the information you need in the final only really tests you on niche topics. For example, the large majority of FRQ were drawing orbitals, which can be learned without understanding the rest of Chem. The most important things would be to go to the TA review sessions, since that is where you'll find the topics you need to actually know. The "optional" homework is not optional; if you don't do it, you will probably fail. Neither professor is all that great, but between Swearer and Bethel, Bethel is the better option.

Terrible terrible class. If you do not have to take this class, please avoid it at all costs. This class has a lot of work, will likely take up the most time out of any your classes, and is designed very poorly. It is not impossible to get an A, but you definitely have to put in a lot of work. While homework is not required, you should definitely complete all the questions to properly prepare for the weekly quizzes. However, the homework keys are posted extremely late only one day before and never include any work, so if you do not know how to do a problem, it is basically impossible to figure out how to solve. Further, many of the assigned problems are extremely difficult to solve and are far beyond anything that was covered in lecture so can just be a waste of time. Professor Bethel was a pretty good lecturer, and he was helpful during office hours, but be warned, his office hours are always packed. I found his lectures to be helpful, and they are required because of the iClicker questions. Usually, the questions were not too difficult, and you could always work with the people around you. This class had weekly quizzes, two midterms, and one cumulative final. The midterms were challenging, but as long as you adequately reviewed the homework and lectures beforehand, they were doable. The quizzes were not too bad as well, but sometimes, they would put one impossible question for no good reason. Overall, this class is not fun and might destroy any interest you had in chemistry. The amount of work and stress makes it too difficult to actually enjoy learning the content. Make sure to dedicate enough time and space out the homework throughout the week.

This class is definitely one that you're gonna remember when you leave NU. Unlike previous chem courses i've taken in the past, this class forces people to intellectually think and properly understand the covered material. You can't really "BS" your way through this class, and the material comes fast and in batches every week through lecture. Bethel is a good professor in my opinion and is always willing to answer questions and help students during office hours. I will say, if you want a good grade in this class, you have to be on top of your work and think deeply of the material, not just having a surface–level knowledge base.
This course is challenging, there is no doubt about that, but I think it is very possible to succeed in it as long as you use all of your resourced.
IT was quite bad, I didnt attend class onley

This is a difficult course as expected from general chemistry but putting in the work does pay out in the end. There where 8 quizes 1 dropped, so that relived some anxiety. However, the midterms are difficult and can sometimes come as a surprise taking it for the first time. 2 midterms and 1 final exam.

It is very possible to do well in this course as long as you are committed to setting aside time to study the material every day. Doing a lot of practice problems helped me the most. Do the homework problems, recitation worksheets, practice midterms, and redo old quizzes/exams. Professor Bethel's office hours were also very helpful and he is great at answering questions! Do not be afraid to ask for help!

The amount of prior knowledge expected of you in this course does not accurately represent what is tested on the placement exam. The first two weeks of class were incredibly stressful, essentially playing catch–up trying to learn five chapters of material that were expected to be known. Discussion quizzes are also on a very tight time constraint, making them more difficult than their corresponding exam content. The course also requires you to use the Ti–30X IIS, which can be difficult and even annoying to use. However, the course does teach you a lot and is intellectually stimulating after you overcome those humps.

I was challenged mentally in this course and found myself falling behind a lot. Only missed one lecture and my iClicker dropped from an A to a B+ and I had perfect attendance otherwise. I was always mentally present during lectures but still felt very confused. Overall, this is a course where you're going to have to self–study most, if not all of the material. Practice problems will be your best friend. And the exams are WAY harder than the quizzes.
This course is challenging but fair, and a good grade is definitely attainable.

I believe that lecture was sufficient, but I would not deem it anything too special. I believe that most of the important content necessary for general information and exams came from practice problems in the homework, and in itself the lecture slides but it was difficult to understand which were the most stressed and important topics necessary to do well in the class.

It's a general chemistry course: it's going to be difficult. Watch Bethel's lectures, (although he can by slightly dry), put your head down and get through it. I took AP last year and got a 5, so the class was a lot of review for me. However, if this is you, do NOT assume the class will be a cakewalk. There are new topics, and the in class iClickers are often made to trick you more than they're made to help you learn. It was not uncommon for an iClicker to be given followed by Bethel saying "ok now let me teach you the formula you needed to know to get that correct."

It is a very challenging course, even if you've taken some chem before. There are generally very little resources, like 1 practice midterm and the recitation worksheets do not correspond even remotely to what is on the quizzes/exams so you do not know what is being tested practically up until you are tested on it. You also take a quiz every week on topic covered that week, which has 4 questions and 15 mins for the exam so scoring higher than 75% is nearly impossible. Homework isn't a part of your grade so its only quizes, midterms and finals and iclicker points which are testing you on concepts that were explained 30 seconds ago every class. That being said there is a curve, you however don't know what grade to expect until the very end of the course which can be demotivating
Not as bad as everyone makes it seem. As long as you attend lectures, study for the weekly quiz, and attend office hours as needed it is a very manageable course.

I thought the class was challenging but well taught. The daily slides were always recorded and we always did sample practice problems in class that helped us learn. The weekly quizzes were also a good way to stay on top of material. Sometimes the midterms covered topics we barely talked about.
The course is understandable and clear. Professor Bethel is great at teaching what is expected of the students on examinations, and the homework problems are great practice.

If you’re not religious, pick one and pray. It really is as bad as people say. On the first day, the professor expects you to already know the first five chapters—and somehow they immediately become part of the material. Every week there’s a quiz, which isn’t terrible since it’s based on the assigned homework. This course takes a lot of time, and being “good at chemistry” or having taken AP Chem does not make it hurt any less. Enjoy!!
make sure to utilize office hours and read the textbook.

I would not recommend this course unless it is a requirement. The weekly quizzes were stressful and time consuming. Weekly review sessions were the most helpful tool. Pacing made it difficult to keep up. The class has a mix of students who had little chemistry knowledge and those who had prior chem knowledge which made the curves tough.

Lots and lots of content in this class but weekly quizzes make it somewhat more manageable. Bethel is a pretty good lecturer and helps you understand the content and he has interesting dry humor but he gets the job done. Either way gonna be a stressful class no matter when or how you take it. Grading is based on weekly quizzes, two midterms, a final, and iclicker participation.

This is one of the worst courses. The department is very unorganized and will literally quiz you on something completely random even after you spent hours studying the material. The weekly quizzes were good to keep you on pace, but often did not include material that was emphasized in lecture. To do well you need to watch the lectures and do practice for at least a week before each midterm and its very fast pace. Also the course jumps around a lot which is very disorganized. Professor Bethel is not very welcoming and the office hours are useless because there is always 100 people in there so you won't get a word in.
This class was too much too fast. The attendance policy was terrible, and having class everyday (which was mandatory) is so stressful for no reason/

PLEASE READ THIS! As a junior engineer who already knows chemistry and was simultaneously in a graduate level chem course who was forced into this class due to transfer credit shenanigans, and will be getting a good grade, I believe I have a little more perspective on this class than other freshman. The way this course is taught and examined makes 0 sense. First of all, the slides used in Prof. Bethel's lectures are comically unreadable, as the important information is almost never emphasized and there is always way too much or way too little context for each slide. Prof. Bethel teaches new concepts without any overarching context or introduction, leaving many in the class very lost at all times. Additionally, the examinations in the class often don't pull directly from lectures. Because the exams/quizzes are written by the TAs, there can be a disconnect between the course and exam content. To understand the exam content, one must attend the TA review sessions where they will often emphasize topic that were barely, if not at all mentioned in lectures, and put these topics into examinations. Finally, the course is also very cluttered and can feel extremely disorganized, both in its content and in its execution. A perfect example of this was the distribution of the practice final exam. Usually practice exams are accompanied with answers keys, but they often show up a few days after the exam itself is posted, which led to confusion as the day of the final came around and the final answer key was never posted. It was never communicated that it was never going to be posted, but when inquiring about this from the TAs, I learned that many of the questions themselves had errors.

The TAs mentioned 3 errors, but after realizing there were errors, I went back through the practice exam and found several more errors in the practice final that must have caused major confusion for students who had less chem background. None of these mistakes were publicly acknowledged to the class and TAs/lecturers allowed students to come into the final after reviewing a practice exam with MCQs that had 0 right answers and FRQs with major errors. A lot can and should be done to improve how this class is taught in the future.

Taught by Bethel, this course is doable, albeit quite difficult. The vast majority of the grade comes from quizzes, midterms, and the final, and no exam is very forgiving. That being said, every exam does reflect material learned in class. Bethel does a good job explaining concepts in lecture and does a very good job getting through material in time. The key to success in this class is doing the homework problems every week. The questions 100% reflect the kind of questions you can expect on the quizzes and exams. Office hours are also a great resource as Bethel gets through a lot of material in them and does a good job explaining through homework problems, etc. If you have to take this class, take it with Bethel.

This class is a lot of work, and very time consuming. Definitely take it with Bethel if you can– he does a really good job of breaking down complicated topics to make them much easier to understand. His office hours are also incredibly helpful. The homework is pretty time consuming, and the weekly quizzes are stressful. As someone who did not take AP Chem, the class is definitely doable, but I had to do a lot of catch–up and review during week 1 before the first quiz.
It was VERY stressful. I'm not sure if it was because I wasn't prepared to take this class or because it is stressful for everyone, but I was not expecting it to take up that much time this quarter.
This class made me hate chemistry. I came in planning to be a Mat–Sci major, but I have since dropped that along with any will to do a chem minor because of how terrible it was.

This course is a lot of work but it is manageable and interesting if you are willing to put in the effort. Professor Bethel is a good teacher but you need to put in a lot of work outside of class to get through the problems sets and learn everything necessary for the assessments.
It was hard and some quizzes were just plain bad. But it is very doable. It'll be okay.

Chem 151 seemed a difficult at first but to me, the contents learned in first quarter wasn't that bad. I have taken AP Chem in high school before so I already had a preliminary understanding of the content. However, many many topics taught in class was outside of the content in AP Chem, so it requires quite a lot of studying. The first mid–term was brutal but it gets better after. Overall the difficulty of class felt appropriate for a chem 151 course.

Professor Bethel is the type of professor who can only be understood by people who have already studied the topic beforehand. The problem is Professor Bethel is the best out of the 2 professors for chem 151 and his teaching is a little complicated. For some reason, he finds a way to explain the simplest concepts in the most technical jargony way possible which destroys the whole point. However the iclickers are very annoying but great at the same time. They are annoying because if you didn't understand what the professor said in that moment and he presents a iclicker on that topic, its almost a guaranteed game over at that point. Worst of all is that iclickers affect your overall grade so keeping them up is crucial. However, sometimes Bethel gives out free points which is great. The good side of the iclickers is that he keeps people engaged and doesn't have to worry about students sleeping in class because believe me nobody is even blinking for a second during lecture.
I don't really like the weekly quizzes, it was stressful. Most of my time this quarter is occupied by this course.

Gen Chem is exactly as expected. Under Bethel's instruction, you shouldn't have any trouble learning the content necessary for the exams, which were written simple enough to solve quickly if you have studied. There is no graded homework, but I recommend to do them for preparation for the weekly quizzes.

This course is very content heavy and fast paced. If you don't pick up on a topic the week it is covered, it is extremely easy to fall behind fast. Complete the homework assignments, iClickers, and read the textbook for extra practice. Quizzes on Friday aren't too bad, just ensure you are prepared as it is good practice generally and important for the quizzes, quiz grades are crucial. I advice to maintain an equation sheet that is continuously updated throughout the year, it will be very helpful for preparing for the final.

source_file: Student Report for Kevin Hunter (CHEM_131-0_01  Fundamentals of Chem I)_f8d18dce-84bb-4b21-a5eb-2f0359f9066ben-US.pdf

likert_questions.question_text: Provide an overall rating of the instruction.
Provide an overall rating of the course.
Estimate how much you learned in the course.
Rate the effectiveness of the course in challenging you intellectually.
Rate the effectiveness of the instructor in stimulating your interest in the subject.

categorical_questions.question_text: Estimate the average number of hours per week you spent on this course outside of class and lab time
What is the name of your school?
Your Class
What is your reason for taking the course?
What was your interest in this subject before taking the course?

comments: Hunter is very engaging and well informed on chemistry topics. I enjoyed his note taking lecture style
The lab for this course was awful

You really need to read the textbook to understand things because it feels like prof hunter just glazes over the topics. Sometimes it is helpful to just hear what we need to know, but other times he doesn't go into enough detail and it makes it confusing. He is very nice though. I did not enjoy the course overall and didn't love his teaching style (writing notes rather than slides), but if you read the textbook and study for HOURS when it comes to the midterms and finals you can do well.

I took this course because it is a prerequisite to several of my engineering major requirements. I do not feel that this class taught material that was relevant for those classes, rather I think it catered more towards those who were taking it as a prerequisite to organic chemistry. While Professor Kevin was a fun and chatty professor, he told anecdotes and stories in with the same tone and cadence as the important course material, sometimes making it difficult to notice when he switched from one to the other.

The course was challenging but Prof Hunter did his best to digest it with us by using his version of notes (handwritten). We were always welcome to ask questions during class and it was a good environment to do so without judgement. Felt very collaborative despite the large lecture size and that is in part due to Hunter's attitude while teaching.

This is a great course. This course is a perfect introduction to many important chemistry topics. I definitely feel like this course expanded my view of the chemistry world. Additionally, Professor Hunter is an excellent professor and lecturer. I would highly recommend taking a class taught by him.
This course was better than Chem 110 and a lot less math related which was nice. I think it is taught really fast but if you go to class and pay attention, it is manageable.

I found the instruction to be incredibly helpful. Professor Hunter's office hours were always beneficial, and his lectures were wonderful and amazing at helping understand content. The quizzes and midterms always really challenged me though, and I never quite knew what to expect. I felt like this course required an incredible amount of work and my grades do not reflect this. However, I do feel like I learned and largely understood a very significant amount of content in a short period of time.

This course is a continuation of the Chem 110 introductory course but leans more heavily on theory as opposed to Chem 110’s focus on math (computation). There are chapters that seem almost fictional, so I recommend watching additional YouTube videos and examining real–world scenarios. I advise reading the textbook before class and actively questioning during sessions. It’s crucial to grasp the concepts in depth rather than just repeatedly practicing problems from the textbook. This course demands less homework compared to Chem 110.

Professor Kevin is an excellent instructor who deeply cares about his students. I strongly recommend attending his office hours, as he shares many vital points not covered in class. However, I also suggest watching Professor Nemr's videos for some topics that Kevin might overlook.

This was sooo much better than chem 110!! If you can make it through 110, you can do this class. It was more actual chemistry (electrons and bonding etc), which made it engaging, and there was lots of stuff about *why* everything works the way it does, which definitely helped me. If you focus on getting the backstory and trying to reason through the logic of everything, that'll help you do well. Also, Hunter & Nemr were both incredible instructors; they explained things super well and were good about ensuring you get the concepts, not just memorizing random "rules" and their million exceptions. If you can't go to office hours, try to check their notes so you don't miss anything. Also genuinely the textbook does help clear some things up, so even though it's a pain to read, I'd say it's pretty helpful!
This class isn't that bad as people make it seem. Slightly harder than 110 but doable.
It was good, better than chem–110.
I think this course was effective.
I enjoyed this course. Professor Hunter is a great teacher. I think that the midterms tested our ability to remember information rather than our understanding of chemistry, which is a shame.
This was a great class — Kevin was super engaging and made it all really interesting, and although it was hard work, it was still a fascinating and enjoyable course.

This course was not bad, but you just need to stay studying. Make sure to know what classes your taking with it because I took certain harder classes that should not have been in the same quarter as this one making it very hard for me to pass them.
131 is very difficult, not as bad as 110 though. Kevin is an amazing professor and made the class worth coming to every day.

This class requires routine studying to be done if you want to do well week–to–week on the quizzes and homework. In my opinion, some of the material in this class is easier than some of the material taught in chem 110. It's also less math and problem solving based than 110 which is nice as frq questions on midterms are less multi step based and more based on knowledge of principals.

Way better than Chem 110, but still difficult. First exam is easy, so don't be fooled, the difficulty of the second midterm and the final exam will make up for the difficulty of the first midterm.

Professor Hunter was a decent professor and used fill in notes rather than the typical reading off powerpoint slides, so that was a nice change and it helped present the material in a much less intimidating way. More importantly, he truly cares about his students and their success, so if you choose Prof Hunter or end up in his section, you're in good hands
Very hard, but easier than Chem 110.

Its gen chem what do you expect. More interesting for sure than 110 and actual chemistry is definitely interesting but its just a lot of content and things to do. Like the weekly quiz, homework, lab checks, pre–lab, post lab was just a lot esp if you're doing a full course load. yadda yadda its supposed to be hard i guess but workload is just so annoying. LOVE KEVIN THOUGH
I feel that even though the course is nice, the content chapters do not connect. The course starts out with quantum theory, but it ends with the interaction of molecules within solution.

This course definitely requires a lot of outside reading/learning in order to do well. Although Professor Hunter takes a lot of time to accommodate student's needs, I sometimes feel like topics were taught too quickly and in not enough detail. I think sometimes it was assumed we knew information when we didn't.
The exams are really hard.

I believe that the instructor was great at explaining concepts. The illustrated hand written notes and diagrams were great to follow along in the lecture. I just found at times the lectures felt rushed, but that could definitely be a inherent aspect of the Northwestern quarter system that there's so much work to get through. Furthermore, referring to the filled in lecture notes sometimes were hard to follow outside of class. Professor is very kind though.
I really liked it with Hunter. He had a good teaching style where he explained the material well and kept it engaging with his personality.
Hunter is a great professor! He writes his notes alongside students so his lectures are very easy to follow and easy to understand. He is also very understanding and an all around great professor!

This is definitely a difficult class, so get a good study group and review the material well. I had Hunter, and while he definitely enjoys teaching and wants to engage with his students, he does it in a way that does not mesh well with the class. He will spend one second on stuff in class, telling us that we'll review it later, but then that info is on the quiz and no one knows how to do it unless you read the book. I would definitely recommend taking this class with Nemr.
I wouldn't take this course if it wasn't required but it is definitely better than Chem 110. The material can be interesting but you must do the work to stay on top of content.
I loved Hunter

This class was challenging, mostly because I had to synthesize most of the information on my own to make sense of the content. Exam and quiz questions require you to go beyond the information taught in lecture. It was a lot of outside of class work and big concepts that took a long time to grasp, but it was much better than 110. Kevin is a great professor that really cares about his students, and he actually teaches the content rather than flipping through slides during lecture.

Prof. Hunter teaches through simultaneously explaining and writing his notes out right in front of him. He does not integrate a lot of practice problems into his teachings, but when he does he writes out the steps right in front of you. Sometimes he may get off track, but for the most part he does a good job making sure the course stays relevant to what we're learning, and the exams are a fair reflection of what he's taught. For weekly quizzes be more careful if you have him, Prof. Hunter does sometimes skip over finer details that the other section may go over so sometimes what shows up in quizzes are not things that he's taught us, but are present in the textbook, or in the other sections' notes. I recommend if you have him, make sure to cross check your notes with other sections' notes. Although he has a slight tendency to skip over smaller details, his teaching of the concept overall, in my opinion, is through.

Do not take this class unless you have to, it is so difficult, BUT that being said.... PROFESSOR HUNTER IS THE BEST. Gen chem is difficult and always will be, but Prof Hunter is SO kind and cares so so much about teaching his students. He takes notes with the class as he lectures and stops throughout lectures to answer our questions. His office hours are so helpful and he is willing to set up time one on one to help if you are struggling. I just LOVEEEE him so much. That is all. He is the greatest. Take this class with him if you can.

it's gen chem idk what you're expecting. its a weed–out class but once you fully commit to putting your head down and getting through it it's semi–manageable. professors and content felt better/easier than chem 110. very conceptual and very technical at the same time, requires lots of visualization, but putting in the effort is rewarding. quizzes are HORRIBLY written so be aware.

This course is much better than chemistry 110 in my opinion, as it delves into more conceptual topics rather than mathematical ones and as a result it makes chemistry a more interesting topic overall. Part of what I look for in a course is how its teachings can be applied to real life, and this course has been by far the best example of that .

I mean it's genchem, it's going to be a difficult course, and the pacing is pretty fast but if you go to recitation sections, read ahead in textbook, and study a lot, it's doable. But it's still extremely frustrating and hard considering theres so many in depth dives of the textbook, they can throw any curveball question on the test, and there's just an iinsane amount of material.
I thought this class was interesting but also just a classic gen chem class. The material was not as difficult as Chem 110, and the assignments were formatted in a much more doable way.

This course is fine. You have to spend a lot of your own time teaching yourself the material. Office hours are crucial. Some of the homework is impossible unless you ask a TA or the professor which was frustrating sometimes. Kevin is very positive and helpful though so it is beneficial to create a good relationship with him so you can ask him questions and he can help support you through the course.

I think that it could have been more specific on how the concepts connect to each other because it goes so fast and can be easy to miss the big picture. Other than that, all the material that we covered was good because I am pre–med and will need to know all of this infomation.
Meh

Just like all gen chem classes, this class is hard. However, it is definitely not as bad as CHEM 110. The first midterm was very fair, but they got harder as time went on. This class does have a lot of resources to help students succeed, but it does sometimes feel like the chemistry department takes pride in making students miserable.

It can be a struggle for sure but the TAs are pretty good at explaining concepts and being readily available for answering questions. The TAs and instructors were available to help you when you needed it
It was what I expected upon taking the course.
It was fine, sometimes Prof. Hunter's notes did not cover everything, and he would be dismissive of material that would later show up on the exams.

Learning what quantum theory is and being able to distinguish principal quantum numbers was super important to me. Lectures on Periodic Trends, Lewis theory, Bond strength and hybridization , Molecular Orbitals, Intermolecular Forces, Entropy, and Spontaneity for a reaction helped me understand the fundamentals of chemistry I.

This is a pretty standard chemistry class, make sure to pay attention and attend lecture as falling behind can be very hard to recover from due to the fast paced nature of the class. I did not find most of the material that interesting, but it has value in explaining common phenomena well. Professor Hunter's lecture style is pretty good since he uses paper that he annotates. Professor Nemr uses powerpoints, so you can probably find a teaching style that works best for you.

This is a very hard class, but it is way easier than 110. If you did Chem 110, this will be much easier. I had a great professor (Prof. Hunter) and I enjoyed how he structured the lessons. The content is more conceptual for 131 than 110 and I find it more interesting and applicable. If you got through 110 you will be fine.
It was hard. And the tests were harder. It was hard keeping track of all the various deadlines and such however.
Prof Hunter is the goat. Doesn't just read off of slides

Honestly, Professor Hunter is a decent teacher, but his notes are unorganized and vague. I think Nemr's notes would be better, but Hunter is really open to questions. I only got through this class because of my PGSG though.

It was a difficult course but for me definitely not as challenging as 110 was. The material was mostly conceptual until the last couple of chapters and there were a couple of things to memorize but not as much as 110.

Overall, this course was significantly better than 110, although many of the shortcomings were still present. The averages for this course were comparatively higher, demonstrating that it is possible to do better in this class. I felt that I actually often understood the content of this course. Prof. Hunter was amazing and definitely made a difference in my learning this quarter. He is definitely dedicated to helping each person succeed and is very approachable. If you have the opportunity to take this class with Prof. Hunter, I highly recommend it!

The course has a tight schedule. We have weekly quizzes, two midterms, and a final within ten weeks, so we need to go on a swift schedule. However, having quizzes and homework problems every week helps us keep track of the materials and our studies. Moreover, Professor Hunter hosting office hours, allowing students to ask questions and work on their homework problems, also immensely helped.

comments: Like any other introduction course, Physics 135–1 is one of those courses you just have to get through. Lectures were often boring and repetitive with very little being taught at times. Luckily there are lots of example in the problem manual that allow you to learn the material yourself if you want. The weekly quizzes for the class were generally like the homework assignments (which are optional) but the exam problem are way tougher.

This course is demanding if you have not taken Physics before. You cover a new chapter every week, making the course incredibly fast paced. Brown is not a good lecturer, but she is incredibly kind. I think the midterms, quizzes, and final were very fair, she does not try to surprise you with anything.

Dr. Brown lays out a very organized progression of the course and it's easy to understand each concept based on each prior one. The homework assignments and detailed answer key was also very useful for preparing for the weekly quizzes.
Good course

Overall a great introductory Physics course, if not a little fast–paced towards the end of the quarter. Though Dr. Brown may not be the most enthusiastic lecturer, I found her lectures pretty easy to follow, and exams are a fair reflection towards her lectures.
I was really happy with the structure of the course. The canvas page was very organized and had great resources.
yes, professor is nice.
Dr Brown's problem manual and use of it is great and it makes this class challenging but doable

I've never taken a physics class before so there was definitely a learning curve in learning how to analyze word problems to understand what formulas to use and how. But there were weekly quizzes that helped keep me accountable for keeping up with the course content. Professor Brown tends to go through lecture notes pretty slowly so it was easy to keep up during lectures, but she spends a lot of time deriving formulas instead of doing examples. So you do have to figure out a lot of applications on your own.

don't take this class if you are just looking for a distro, but Prof. Brown is really nice and teaches everything in a clear and logical manner.

4/6

comments: I took the EA trailer sequence after not doing well in EA in the fall. This time I did much better because it was a smaller class and it was just Ilya. I do wish Ilya's office hours weren't in the auditorium because they were basically an extra lecture, not a time to ask questions, but the TA's office hours were so helpful and much of my success is from attending those. Definitely don't fall behind; it can be so tempting to skip class since Ilya posts everything, but if you depend on watching the videos instead of going to class, you'll probably end up retaking it like I had to. Also this class is just difficult in general, so just do your best and ask questions on Campuswire as you have them.

Ilya is an amazing professor, so kind and understanding. He really breaks down hard concepts and is always willing to go the extra step to help you learn concepts better. Definitely go to his office hours and never be afraid to ask for help!

as someone with no coding or linear algebra experience, the trailer sequence of EA 1 was definitely much preferred and a much better pace than the original. the course covered a lot of content at a good and helpful pace where you learned a lot.
Ilya was an amazing professor, you can tell that he really cares.
I loved Ilya as an instructor. Please take EA1 with him, will be the best experience!

The course helped me understand how to use MATLAB and basic concepts of linear algebra. I loved how case studies were used to teach the students how concepts that we were learning are being used today such as data encryption, weather prediction model, and page ranking. I always felt so rewarded when I completed the homework and lab assignments after putting in a lot of work to understand. This class is tough, especially if you have no prior coding experience and/or linear algebra knowledge, but your hard work will be rewarded if you put in the hard work.
go to office hours!!!
Go to office hours. Saved my butt.

I really enjoyed the course and the exampled the professor used when teaching the course, it made a very challenging subject easier for me to understand especially because I did not have prior coding experience.

This course was required so I was not super interested in taking it, but I think Ilya did a really great job of answering all the questions that we had and did anything he could to help us better understand the material.

Ilya is fantastic. He is fun and engaging, and it is really clear that he loves the subject he teaches. He also provides many resources, from his lectures, notes, tutorials, modules in Canvas, etc., that help you understand more concepts and get a better grasp on things. His office hours were helpful and insightful. The trailer sequence of EA is a really nice option, especially for those who felt like they didn't have as good of an education as others in high school. The class covered a lot of good information, but I was never overwhelmed with the pacing of it. If I was being, I could simply rewatch a lecture, or go through Ilya's tutorials, or schedule an office hour (the resources to succeed in the course were in abundance which I think should be the case for all classes in McCormick!). This was my favorite class this quarter. I learned a lot, had a great time, and felt confident in exams.

I really liked this course! The concept was really interesting (I personally really like coding and I found linear quite fun to learn) and Ilya is an amazing professor! He really cares about each one of us succeeding in this course and always offers the most he can to help us get the best grades possible. He makes himself very available through Campuswire and in office hours, and he's also very attentive during lecture to make sure no one's falling behind. He's also very enthusiastic about the subject, which I think made me like this course a lot more than I would've without him teaching it. As a side note though, if you don't have coding experience the learning curve to this course is probably gonna be quite steep, but it'll all be worth it in the end. I'm gonna miss taking this course with Ilya!
Good teacher, just make sure you don’t fall behind

This was a very difficult class. Meant to make you want to quit. Stick with it.

5/7

comments: I felt this was way easier than the main EA2. The averages were way worse too. The professor isn't amazing, but the material isn't super difficult to learn on your own. The homeworks were pretty tedious, though, and I did end up spending quite a bit of time on some of them–– same with the Matlab projects.

The long story short with this class is in order to master the material, use lots of practice problems found in the textbook and online. They are by far the best practice given the content of this course. If you do that, get help through tutoring or office hours when you are struggling with concepts, and keep on top of assignments, you will be fine. That being said, I still dislike the projects very much and find them a huge time commitment for such a small percent of the grade. I didn't find this class's material especially difficult, but it was not well run. Simply put, I found Professor Donmez less than stellar at teaching the material. He did not explain concepts well and took some feedback poorly. In class, I often left confused about simpler concepts, an example being trusses, not really understanding how I was supposed to approach them. Then, I would have a friend explain said concept in two minutes, and it all made sense immediately. This led me to using free online resources when understanding concepts more than his lecture material.

I got a D in EA2 the first time so this was my second time taking it so wasn't difficult at all. The projects take forever and are annoying; otherwise, homework isn't too bad, and exams could be worse.

This was my second time taking EA2, so I had a leg up in this class however Prof.Donmez did not help with the clarification on topics at all. On several occasions he did not seem to answer my questions and the distribution of the first midterm was insane and uncalled for. EA 2 should be one of the "easier" engineering courses and I guess he evened it out with the second midterm and final. To be fair, his exams are based on practice problems worked on in class. If you do not attend class you will not know what to expect on the midterms as his notes are never posted. It was hard to understand what he said in class at times and it was also his first year teaching. I believe he bases his lectures off of professor Alarcon's notes but that may just be for his first quarter of teaching.
I kind of forgot about this class because I was so busy with others, so I guess it’s an easily crammable class.

I think that this course was pretty easy as long as you put the time into completing the homework and attending a few TA office hours/discussions. I would recommend signing up for peer guided study group because that really helped me in succeeding in the class. Lecture wasn't the best, so utilizing resources outside of it makes a difference.

Not too difficult of a course, requires a lot of visualization

4/7
I feel like this class did not do a good job teaching me the subject i needed to learn from it. It was very difficult and painful.

the course teaches you a lot of new material that is integral to engineering, but you will be learning it yourself as opposed to being taught it and will have to rely heavily on finding your own resources
To be honest I don't really see the point of this course for CS/CE majors. Just get it over with. That's pretty much all it is. Nothing extraordinarily good or bad.
I really did not like the way the professor taught this course. It was hard to understand and had to rely on the book a lot to do my homework and pass exams.
I learned a lot of things regarding static mechanics
no comment
It was well set up and not overtly complicated or overloaded in terms of content
EA2 had reasonable amount of work; however, I wished the grading percentage is more reasonable (a final exam should not be worth 40%)

This was a good course. I found the pace to be pretty good and the assessments were fair as well. Professor Donmez is helpful and kind. Most of his lectures consist of us doing practice problems together, which I found fairly helpful. The TA's were also very helpful! The grading is based on weekly homeworks (10%), two MATLAB projects (10%), two midterms (each 20%), and a final (40%). All of the assessments were largely fair and reasonable. I don't really understand why the final is worth 40% but we'll see how it goes.
It's a crucial course for engineers, especial mechanical or civil.
Only three questions on each midterm. It is so easy to do badly, if you miss one question that is already a 60%. The design of the course structure makes it easy to fail.

Analysis and use of Compuatation. I disagree with this McCormick "requirement". Having a physics sequence as requirement(mixed with computation) will go a long way towards being a first year requirement towards making students learn actual concepts rather than blind, old school, and frank "unnecessary for some majors" techniques. There are certainly positive aspects to the course. However with an outlook towards the future, I see it as less priority topic. I say this as someone doing fairly good in this class.
Anything I learned in this class was self taught or from YouTube. This Professor is hard to follow and never makes much sense.
It was a fine course. Instruction could have been better but overall not too bad.
I loved this course! At first I thought physics to be my personal purgatory, but this course introduced me to an intro–level form of physics that I really enjoyed!
Donmez is not a good lecturer. I do not think I have learned a single thing from his lectures, which means I had to self study the entire course.

EA2 Trailer is fantastic. Professor Donmez is great. His pacing is fantastic, and often makes jokes. He's the best. The projects in this class take entirely way too long especially being only 5% of your final grade. They're interesting and doable. The homework was fairly easy in terms of how long I had to spend on it. I usually did the entire thing on a Saturday.
Keep up with homeworks, projects are very easy
This course is fine but be aware that the prof will not be accommodating of documented medical issues.
Although I did not take physics in high school, this class was not very overwhelming. I appreciated the pacing of the course.
The lectures were not the greatest because of our professor. He was very nice and passionate, however, he was hard to follow.

This course was overall pretty effective in teaching me the course content. Between the example problems done in lecture, the homework problems, and the problems done in discussion, I was able to feel like I had a really strong grasp on the content, so taking all this practice seriously helps give the necessary repetition to learn the course material. Going to lecture is highly recommended, and the professor is good at teaching the content, even if he is a bit hard to understand at times.

5/7
